actions.thought: UTG in a 6-max game, 85o is too weak and disconnected to open profitably. Limping invites multiway action with dominated equity and poor playability, and raising builds a pot out of position with a hand that rarely makes strong top pairs and has limited straight potential. Preserve stack and fold.

I am in Middle Position with Ace-Trey offsuit (A3o). While I have an Ace, the kicker is very weak and the hand is offsuit, leading to poor playability and potential kicker trouble if I am called. In a 6-max game, opening A3o from this position is generally unprofitable as there are four players left to act behind me. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand to wait for better opportunities.

Preflop, folds from UTG and MP, so first to act voluntarily in CO with 9s7c offsuit. This is a weak, gapped offsuit hand with poor playability. Standard CO opening range includes better suited connectors like 87s+ or 98o+, but 97o is too weak, especially early in tournament with deep stacks (100BB). Calling the 100 gives poor implied odds OOP against button and blinds. Raising risks playing OOP postflop with a marginal hand. Best to fold and wait for better spots to build stack.
K8o on the button with all players folding to me. This is a standard steal opportunity. K8o is well within a button opening range, and I should raise to try to take down the blinds.

Fold. 2♥7♥ is one of the weakest hands — very often dominated and has poor post‑flop playability out of position. Claude opened to 2.5x from the button; calling 200 to play OOP requires roughly 33% equity (200/(400+200)) which 2‑7s won’t provide against a reasonable button range, and a 3‑bet bluff from the small blind is thin (I’ll be out of position and can easily face calls or 4‑bets). Preserve the ~9.95k stack for better spots and fold the hand.

I hold T4o in the Big Blind facing a 2.5x open from the Button. While I get decent pot odds (calling 150 to win a total pot of 550, requiring ~27% equity), T4o is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability and low equity realization, especially out of position. It falls below standard defending ranges against a Button open. Therefore, folding is the most prudent decision to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the UTG (Under the Gun) position, which is the earliest position to act in a 6-max game. Holding Ace-Trey offsuit (A3o) is generally too weak to open from this position because it is easily dominated by better Aces and has poor post-flop playability. Standard strategy suggests folding this hand to avoid playing a weak range out of position against the rest of the table.

Preflop, middle position, facing no raise (only blinds and UTG fold). 2h3s is a speculative suited hand but extremely weak with poor high-card strength, minimal straight potential, and dominated by better suited connectors. Deep stacks (100BB), early in tournament (hand 2/150), no need to play marginal hands OOP. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for premium spots.
8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand. Even in the cutoff with two folds before me, this hand doesn't have good playability or equity. Not worth opening - easy fold.

On the button with Jc9d (offsuit) and three early folds, this is a standard open-raise spot: good position, decent hand to steal the blinds and take the initiative. I want a size that pressures both blinds and charges their wide defending ranges while preserving stack depth (~99 BB). I'll raise to 300 chips (3x the big blind). If I face a large 3-bet I'll fold J9o; if I get called I'll play postflop aggressively in position, using fold equity and my hand's connectivity to realize equity.

Facing a 3x raise from the Button, I hold KTs in the Small Blind. This is a strong suited broadway hand that falls well within a standard defense range against a Button open. While a 3-bet to isolate or take down the pot pre-flop is a valid aggressive option, playing a bloated pot out of position early in the tournament with a hand that can be dominated (by AK, KQ) carries high variance. Calling allows me to see a flop at a reasonable price, keeping my range somewhat uncapped and retaining the playability of my suited connector. I will call the additional 250 chips.

We’re getting a good price in the big blind: 200 to call into a 700 pot (need ~22% immediate equity) and we’re closing the action preflop. 9♠5♠ is not strong enough to 3-bet (and would play poorly OOP in an inflated pot), but it has suited connectivity for flushes/straights and can realize equity reasonably well in a multiway pot when we keep the pot controlled. Call and proceed cautiously postflop, especially on high-card, disconnected boards.

I hold the second nut flush draw (Kd Td) on an Ad 3d 2c board. The pot is multi-way with the Button (Minni) as the pre-flop aggressor. As the Small Blind, playing out of position, the standard optimal play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) into two players, one of whom likely has an Ace, is generally suboptimal. I will check to see how the action unfolds; if the Button bets, I have a strong enough draw to continue (likely via check-call or potentially check-raise), and if it checks through, I get a free card to realize my equity.

We’re 3‑way on A♦3♦2♣ holding 9♠5♠: no pair, no flush draw, and only a weak gutshot to a 4. This board strongly favors the button’s raising range (Ax, strong broadways, better wheel/diamond combos), and donk-leading into two players with minimal equity is spewy. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets the preflop raiser c-bet, and we can respond based on sizing (often folding vs meaningful pressure).

I opened from the button but on A♦3♦2♣ I have J♣9♦ — no pair and only a one-card diamond backdoor. Both blinds called pre and checked the flop, so I’m facing a multiway spot against wide but often Ax-heavy/paired ranges. C-betting into two callers on an Ace-high flop with essentially no fold equity is a marginal line; I want to control the pot and avoid getting called or raised by better hands. I’ll check to see a turn card and only invest further if I pair/jump to a clear draw or pick up a favorable action on later streets.

I have turned the effective nut flush (Kd Td on Ad 3d 2c 2d). Note that the Ace of diamonds is on the board, making my King of diamonds the highest possible flush card in a player's hand (barring the unlikely 4d5d straight flush). The flop checked through, suggesting the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) is weak or potting controlling, and the Big Blind likely missed. If I check again, there is a high risk the turn checks through, missing a street of value. I need to start building the pot. I will lead out (bet) for value, targeting Ax hands, trips (2x), or lower flushes. A bet of 400 (roughly 45% of the pot) is large enough to build the pot but small enough to get called by marginal made hands like a weak Ace.

Turn is Ad 3d 2c 2d and SB leads 400 into 1300. With 9s5s we have no pair, no flush draw, and only a gutshot to a 4 (about 9% equity with one card). Calling 400 to win 1700 requires ~23.5% immediate equity and we also risk the button continuing behind us, which further hurts realization. A bluff-raise has poor blockers and is unlikely to fold out Ax/2x/diamond hands multiway. Best play is to fold and preserve stack.

I call 400. On the button with Jc9d I have no made hand but I have four to a diamond (9 diamonds left) plus non-diamond J/9 outs — unique outs = 9 (diamonds) + 2 (non-diamond Js) + 3 (non-diamond 9s) = 14 outs. That’s ~14/46 ≈ 30.4% equity on the river. The price to call is 400 to win a pot of 1700 (post-call), so required equity ≈ 400/1700 ≈ 23.5%. My equity exceeds the price, so a call is +EV. Raising to 800 as a semi-bluff is possible but bloats the pot OOP with limited fold equity against Ax or 2x hands and risks commitment when behind; calling preserves position, keeps pot control, buys a clean river decision, and gives good implied odds if I hit the flush. If the river bricks and Pro barrels large, I can fold non-made hands; if I complete the flush I can extract value.

I hold the King-high flush (Kd Td) on a board with four diamonds (Ad 3d 2c 2d 7d). The Ace of diamonds is on the board, so my King is the highest possible flush card in a player's hand. The only hand that beats me is the 4d5d straight flush (Steel Wheel), which is extremely unlikely (1 combo). Effectively, I have the nuts. The pot is 1700. My opponent (Minni) called a bet on the turn, suggesting she has a piece of the board, likely a lower flush draw that got there, trips, or top pair. With four diamonds on the board, any diamond in her hand makes a flush. If I check, she may check back a low flush or made hand out of caution. I need to bet for value, targeting hands like QdX, JdX, or even smaller diamonds. A bet of around half the pot (850) is large enough to extract value but small enough to get called by worse flushes that might fold to a larger size on this scary board.

River: I hold Jc9d so I made the Ace‑high diamond flush (A‑9‑7‑3‑2). Pot is 2550 and Pro bet 850, so calling 850 to win 3400 requires ≈25% equity. Hands that definitely beat me are full houses/quads (AA, A2, 33, 22 and 3+2 combos) and any diamond in Pro's hand higher than my 9 (T/J/Q/K♦). However, a large portion of his continuing range (worse diamonds, single A/x, broadways, some pairs) still loses to my flush. Given the pot odds and that my hand is likely good vs most of his plausible hands, calling is +EV. Raising to the min (1700) is unattractive — it bloats the pot OOP, gives him the chance to jam with full houses/higher flushes, and doesn't gain much fold equity against value hands. Folding would be too weak with a made flush. I will call 850 and go to showdown.

4s8d is a trash hand from UTG in a 6-handed game. Poor playability, offsuit, disconnected. Limping or raising risks tough multiway pots OOP with a weak range. Early in tournament (hand 3/150), stacks deep (~100BB), preserve chips for premium hands. Standard fold.
5d Th is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity. From middle position, this hand doesn't have enough equity to open or defend. Easy fold.

Fold. 7♦3♥ offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands — poorly connected, unsuited and frequently dominated. It’s folded to me in the cutoff with the button and both blinds yet to act, so calling 100 to see a flop is an expensive speculative play and raising to 200 to “steal” is risky against players who can re‑raise or call and put me in tough multiway pots. With a deep 84BB stack I should conserve chips for better spots and avoid marginal, poorly playable hands. Fold and wait for a hand with real postflop playability or positional advantage.

I am on the Button with Q8o. In a 6-max game, this is on the borderline of an opening range (typically top 45-55% of hands). While GTO solvers often open this, it is the bottom of the range and can suffer from reverse implied odds against 3-bets or when dominated by Q9+ or Ax post-flop. Given that we are early in the tournament (Hand 3) and I have no specific reads on the Small Blind or Big Blind's defensiveness yet, I will take the lower variance line and fold. I recently won a large pot in Hand #2, so there is no urgent need to push marginal edges immediately.

Folded to me in the SB versus a single opponent. 7-2 offsuit is the weakest holding and plays very poorly in raised pots, especially out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a steal-raise that often gets defended. Completing keeps the pot small, realizes my equity when BB checks, and lets me comfortably fold if the BB iso-raises to a meaningful size.

I am in the Big Blind with 95o. The Small Blind has just completed the blind to 100 chips. With a weak hand like 95 offsuit, there is no value in raising to build a pot or attempting a bluff this early in the tournament against a single opponent who has already committed chips. Checking allows me to see the flop for free and potentially realize equity if I hit a favorable board, while keeping the pot small.

In a limped blind-vs-blind pot on 5♠-8♥-T♣ we have essentially no made hand, no real draw, and only thin backdoor clubs/overcard-to-2 type equity. Leading as a bluff here is high frequency to get called by any piece (T/8/5, straight gutters like 76/97, plus random floats), and we don’t have good turn barrels. Best line is to check and realize whatever minimal equity we have, and reassess versus a bet size from BB.

I have hit bottom pair with my 5 on a flop of 10-8-5. Since I am in position and the opponent in the Small Blind has checked to me, checking back is the most prudent play. Betting doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and checking allows me to control the size of the pot while potentially realizing my equity or catching a bluff on later streets. My hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable, so keeping the pot small is ideal.

Blind-vs-blind limped pot went check/check on a relatively dry flop, which heavily weights Flash toward air or marginal one-pair that didn’t want to build a pot. The K♥ is a good card for me to represent (Kx, Tx, 8x, or improved hands) and puts meaningful pressure on his flop-check range. My actual hand has essentially no equity and no useful blockers, so I don’t want to polarize large; I just want a cheap stab that folds out his complete whiffs and some weak holdings. If called, I’ll mostly give up on rivers unless I spike something meaningful or the runout becomes very favorable. If raised, I fold immediately.

The Small Blind has led with a half-pot bet of 100 chips after checking the flop. While the King on the turn is a scare card, my pair of fives still has some showdown value in a limped pot, especially given the small bet size. I am receiving 3:1 pot odds to call, which is sufficient to see a river card. I will call here but remain cautious, likely folding to further aggression on the river unless I improve to two pair or trips.

We picked up a small amount of showdown value with 7♠2♣ on the river (pair of 7s), and in a limped blind-vs-blind pot that’s often enough to win against all the hands that floated the tiny turn stab with ace-high/gutters and now miss. Betting doesn’t look attractive: worse hands (mostly pure air) will fold, while better hands (Kx/Tx/8x/flushes and some 7x) call, and we have no heart blockers to run a credible/value-protecting bet on a 3-heart river. Best is to check and realize our equity at showdown, avoiding getting called by better too often.

I have bottom pair (fives) on a board with multiple overcards. The action went check-check on the flop, FiveTwo bet the turn when the King appeared, and has now checked the river. My hand has very little showdown value, but in a small limped pot, there is no merit in turning it into a bluff as I'm unlikely to represent a hand strong enough to fold out a better pair like an eight or a ten. Checking back allows me to see the showdown for free.
J8o is a weak hand to open from UTG. This is a fold from early position - not enough equity or playability to justify raising, and definitely not worth a call or limp.

I have A♠ K♦ (Ace-King offsuit) in middle position with no prior raise — pot is 150 (only blinds) and it’s 100 to call. This is a premium, playable hand that wants to take the initiative preflop. I’ll open-raise rather than just call to build the pot, isolate the blinds, and get fold equity against wide defending ranges. With ~84 BB effective, a standard 3x BB sizing is appropriate: big enough to deny some speculative callers but still leaves room postflop. If I face a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on opponent and sizing (call or 4-bet as appropriate).

I am holding JTo in the Cutoff facing a raise to 3bb from Minni in Middle Position. While I have position on the opener, JTo is a marginal hand that is often dominated by a standard MP opening range (AJ, AT, KJ, QJ, TT+). Calling invites squeezes from the players behind me and puts me in a spot where I suffer from reverse implied odds (hitting top pair but being out-kicked). With stacks deep early in the tournament, there is no incentive to play a marginal, easily dominated offsuit hand. The disciplined play is to fold.

Button vs a middle-position 3x open, 8d4d is low but playable suited in position with deep stacks. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity well (flushes/straights/two-pair possibilities), and avoids bloating a pot with a hand that has poor blockers for a 3-bet bluff. We’ll take the price and play postflop with position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. Minni opened for 3x from middle position and was called by FiveTwo on the button. A3o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against two active players. Calling is unattractive because of the lack of board coverage and potential kicker issues, and 3-betting is too risky as a bluff against an open and a call. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack early in the tournament.

Preflop in BB facing a MP raise to 300 (200 to call into 750 pot) and a Button call. My hand Jd7s is offsuit, disconnected, and mediocre. Pot odds are decent (~3.75:1), but multiway OOP with a hand that plays poorly postflop (few strong draws, dominated often). Standard fold to avoid difficult spots deepstacked early in tournament. No value to raise, check not applicable.

I opened preflop with A♥K♦ and I’m first to act on a dry, rainbow 4♥-5♦-8♣. This board misses a large portion of the button’s calling range (broadways, weak suited connectors and singletons), so as the preflop aggressor I should continuation-bet to take down the pot and get value from worse high-card hands. I’ll bet about half the pot (375 into 750) — big enough to charge floats and protect my perceived range, but small enough to fold to strong aggression without committing too much with only Ace-high. Plan: if called, re-evaluate on the turn (fold to heavy raise unless I improve or get correct pot odds); if checked to on later streets I’ll control the pot and only continue with clear improvement or a cheap bluff opportunity.

We flopped top two pair (8♢4♢) on 4♥5♦8♣ versus a small c-bet. This board is dynamic (6-7, 7-9, A6, 66/77, etc. have equity), and Minni’s range contains many hands that will call a raise for value (overpairs like 99–AA, 8x, 55/88/44, combo draws). Raising now both builds a pot while we’re far ahead of most of their continuations and charges the numerous turn cards that can change equities. I’ll use a size that keeps worse hands in but meaningfully prices draws and sets up stacks for turn/river value.

I opened preflop and c‑bet 375 on the dry 4♥5♦8♣ flop with A♥K♦. FiveTwo check‑raised to 1,200 — a big, somewhat polarized action, but calling costs only 825 more into a pot of 2,325 (I’d be calling to win ~3,150 total; ~26% pot odds). AK is not a made hand here but has reasonable equity versus a check‑raise range that will include both value (sets, straights, medium pairs) and bluffs/semi‑bluffs (floats, missed connectors). Folding would be too tight given my preflop aggression and the Ace blocker; raising out of position with only Ace‑high is unnecessarily risky and can get me in trouble on later streets. I’ll call 825 (1200 total on the flop) to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn — if I pair up (A/K) I’ll pursue value; if a blank comes and he barrels big I’ll strongly consider folding; if he checks the turn I can probe for pot control or a second-barrel steal.

Opponent check‑raised my flop c‑bet very large (1,200 into 750), which polarizes him toward very strong value hands (sets, flopped straights, big pairs) and occasional big bluffs. The turn 5h pairs the board and significantly improves many of those value hands (pocket 4s/8s → full houses, pocket 5s → quads, straights remain), while I still have only A♥K♦ with no pair. Betting now would bloat the pot out of position with very little fold equity and a high chance of facing further aggression from a range that improved on the turn. I’ll control the pot and keep the river decision flexible: check back, fold to a large lead if he fires a strong bet, but re‑evaluate on the river if he checks or gives a small probe. Action: check.

Minni check-calls the flop raise and now checks the paired turn (4h5d8c5h). Our hand is still very strong (two pair: 8s and 5s) and Minni’s range contains many worse hands that will continue: overpairs (99–AA), 8x (A8/K8/Q8), and some straight draws like 67 that we should charge. At the same time, the turn pairing introduces some strong holdings (5x trips, 44/88 full houses), so I prefer a solid but not pot-committing value/protection bet rather than an overbet. This size builds toward a river value decision while keeping us from auto-stacking off if we face a big check-raise.

I checked the turn as planned with A♠K♦ (AhKd) and now face a 1,600 bet into a 4,750 pot. That call costs me ~25% equity (1,600/(4,750+1,600) ≈ 25%). Villain’s line — calling preflop, a very large check‑raise on the flop to 1,200, and now a turn bet — is heavily weighted toward strong made hands that either were already strong on the flop (sets, 6‑7 straights, 4‑5 two‑pair) or improved on the paired turn to full houses/quads. AK has very little showdown equity vs that polarized, value‑heavy range; the Ace blocker helps only marginally. Raising out of position with only Ace‑high would be reckless (it commits a large portion of my stack and will often be met by a shove or a call from better hands). Given the pot odds, the line, and the risk of facing a full house/quads, folding is the correct, pot‑preserving play.

UTG with 5♣6♦ offsuit is a marginal, speculative holding and I’ll be out of position for the entire hand with five players to act behind me, including several deep stacks who can 3‑bet or apply pressure. Limping/calling 100 to play a multiway pot is poor value — the hand is often dominated and has limited postflop playability. A raise here would be a thin bluff and likely to get shoved on or 3‑bet by better ranges. Preserve my 6,900 stack for better spots and fold.

I hold Kd 2d in Middle Position. While suited Kings have some potential, K2 is the weakest of them. With four players left to act behind me, opening this hand is too loose and carries significant reverse implied odds if I hit top pair. The standard play in this position at this stack depth (approx 117 BB) is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

We’re in the cutoff with position on both blinds and only four players left to act (BTN/SB/BB). A4o is comfortably inside a standard CO opening range: it can win unimproved with ace-high, flops top pair often enough, and has some wheel/straight potential. Limping gives up initiative and invites the button to isolate; folding is too tight given the dead blinds. I’ll apply pressure with a normal open size that still keeps my range wide and forces the blinds to defend with real hands.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit. While I have position, the cutoff (FiveTwo) has opened for 2.5x blinds. FiveTwo has shown aggressive tendencies in previous hands, including a check-raise in Hand #4. Ace-Six is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by the cutoff's opening range (A7+, medium pairs). Given the deep stacks and the fact that there are still two players left to act in the blinds who could squeeze, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid a difficult post-flop situation with a weak kicker.

Preflop in SB with 4d5d, suited connectors but low and speculative. Facing a cutoff raise to 250 after two folds (2.5x raise). Pot is 400, need to call 200 OOP, which is marginal pot odds (2:1). Postflop OOP vs likely strong range from CO, 45s plays poorly multiway or heads-up if I miss or get outdrawn on straights/flushes. BB still to act, but folding preserves stack early in tournament (hand 5/150, ~100BB deep). No strong history on FiveTwo yet, but aggressive in prior hands. Standard fold to avoid tough spots.
Facing a min-raise from cutoff with J8o in the BB. Getting 400:150 pot odds (~2.67:1). J8o is a marginal hand but defendable against a late position min-raise with these odds. Calling to see a flop.

Flopped top pair with J8 on Jd9h7s. This is a connected board with straight draws possible (T8, 86). I should bet for value and protection against FiveTwo who raised preflop from cutoff. A ~55% pot bet of 300 is reasonable to charge draws while getting value from worse Jx hands.

Claude’s donk-lead into the preflop raiser on J97 is a spot where his range can be a mix of one-pair (Jx/9x/7x), draws (T8/86/QT), and some stabs. With A4o we’re weak, but we do have position, an ace that can improve us to top pair, and a backdoor nut-flush possibility with Ac. Raising here would be a pure bluff on a board that interacts well with BB’s defend range and will get called too often; folding is a bit too tight given we can float in position and potentially win on turns that favor our perceived range (A/K/Q/T/8) if he slows down. I’ll call once and reassess versus a second barrel.

Turn brought 6s creating more straights (T8, 85 get there) and a flush draw. My top pair weak kicker is vulnerable on this very connected board. FiveTwo called my flop bet which could indicate draws or made hands. Best to check and control pot size - if they bet big I can fold, if they check I can reassess on river.

Claude donk-led small on the flop then checked the turn on a very dynamic board (J97→6♠). That check is often a range that contains a lot of one-and-done stabs, weak pairs (7x/9x), and some draws that are deciding whether to continue. With A♣4♥ we have essentially no real equity and very little showdown value if it checks through, so the best way to win the pot is to apply pressure now while we still represent strong hands (overpairs, Jx+, straights like T8) after calling the flop. I’ll take a medium stab that can fold out his air and some marginal pairs, while keeping risk controlled and folding comfortably if he check-raises.

I have top pair (Jacks with 8 kicker) on J976 with two spades. The turn 6s completed some straights (T8, 85) and added flush draws. FiveTwo bets 550 (half pot) after I checked. While this board is dangerous, folding top pair to a half-pot bet is too weak - FiveTwo showed aggression in hand 4 with a big bluff. Getting ~3:1 odds, I should call and reevaluate on river. Raising would bloat the pot with a vulnerable hand.

The river 5h completes my straight! With J8, I now have 9-8-7-6-5. This is a great river for me. The only straight that beats me is T8 (makes T-9-8-7-6). I should value bet to extract from Jx hands, two pairs, and sets that FiveTwo might have. Betting around 65% pot to get called by worse made hands.

River completes our wheel straight (A-4-5-6-7), giving us a strong made hand after barreling turn. Claude’s 1500 into 3750 looks like a value/block bet with a lot of one-pair/two-pair or some bluffs, but he also can show up with many 8x (and T8) straights that beat our 7-high straight. Because we’re near the bottom of the straight hierarchy, raising mainly gets action from better hands and folds worse, so we shouldn’t inflate the pot. With good pot odds and a hand that’s ahead of a large part of his range, the best play is to call.

I am in UTG position with 4s 6h, which is an extremely weak off-suit hand. Standard strategy dictates folding the bottom of my range from early position to avoid playing weak hands out of position against potentially stronger ranges. There is no strategic merit in playing this hand.

Q3o from middle position in a 6-max game is too weak to enter the pot. It plays poorly multiway, is frequently dominated when we make a queen, and doesn’t have strong straight/flush potential. With no dead money beyond blinds worth chasing, best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better open/3-bet spots.

I am in the Cutoff with 3s 2s, which is a very weak starting hand. Although it is suited, it lacks the high-card strength or connectivity required to be a profitable open-raise even from late position. With the action folded to me, there is an opportunity to steal the blinds, but 32s is at the bottom of my range and usually a fold in this spot to avoid being out-kicked or dominated post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with folds to me. Ts5h is an offsuit, gapped hand with poor post-flop playability, dominated equity against typical blind defending ranges, and low overall strength. While button stealing ranges are wide, T5o falls below standard profitable open-raise thresholds, especially early in the tournament with deep stacks and no immediate stack pressure. Folding preserves chips for premium spots to maximize long-term stack growth.

Heads up vs BB with suited connectors 65c. Everyone folded to me. This is a standard raise spot - suited connectors have good playability and I should take the initiative rather than limping OOP. Raising to 250 (2.5x) is a reasonable size.

Claude opened to 250 from the SB; I’m in the BB with A♥7♣. Pot is 350 and it’s 150 to call. A7o is a clear defend in the big blind versus a likely wide SB opening — I have an ace-blocker and good equity to hit top pair or favorable runouts. A thin 3-bet here (min-raise to 400) is unnecessary and would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. I’ll call to keep the pot controlled, realize equity and evaluate on the flop.

I flopped a flush draw with 65cc on Tc2h8c. I'm the preflop aggressor heads up against the BB. This is a standard c-bet spot - I have 9 outs to the flush plus some backdoor straight potential. Betting around 1/3-1/2 pot keeps pressure on and builds the pot with my draw while getting value if called by worse.

Call 175. I defended the BB preflop with A♥7♣ against a likely wide SB opener and this spot still calls for a defensive call rather than an OOP raise. The flop T♣ 2♥ 8♣ is dry and Claude’s bet is very small (175 into a ~675 pot) — I’m getting ~20% pot odds (175 to win ~850), so I only need ~20% equity to continue. A7o has >20% equity vs a wide c-bet range (three aces and three sevens to pair, plus backdoor club-runner possibilities and decent showdown value because of the Ace blocker). Raising here would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand and invite awkward decisions; calling keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

Turn paired the board with 8d. I still have a flush draw with 9 outs. The paired board reduces my semi-bluff effectiveness - if Minni has an 8 or a ten, they're not folding. Checking here to get a free card to the river makes sense. If I hit my flush, I can bet for value; if I miss, I can decide whether to bluff or give up.

Turn summary: I’m Minni (BB) with A♥7♣ on T♣ 2♥ 8♣ 8♦. Pot = 850. Claude (SB) opened pre, cbet a small amount on the flop and then checked the turn. With no bet to face, my main choices are to check behind or fire a bluff/value bet. 

Why check: I have only Ace-high — no pair, only a weak showdown candidate. Claude’s line (small flop c-bet then turn check) looks like either a wide missed range or pot-control with a made pair; he also could slow-play 88 but there’s no reason to build a big pot OOP with a marginal hand. Betting here would be thin value (very few worse hands will call) and a bluff is risky OOP against a likely sticky opener. Checking controls the pot, preserves stack depth for better spots, and lets me realize my (limited) showdown equity. 

Plan: Check the turn and evaluate river. If Claude bets the river I’ll fold most non-improving rivers; if checked to me on the river I can consider a small bet as a bluff if the card is favorable, or a check-back to take the showdown with my Ace blocker.

Missed my flush draw on a double-paired board (Tc2h8c8d2d). I have 6-high with no showdown value. Minni called flop then checked turn behind, suggesting a marginal hand like a ten or small pair. Bluffing into this double-paired board is risky - opponents often have trips/boats here, and Minni has shown willingness to call. Best to check and give up rather than burn chips on a bluff that's unlikely to succeed.

The river (T♣ 2♥ 8♣ 8♦ 2♦) leaves the board double‑paired. The five best cards are effectively the board (8‑8‑2‑2‑T) for anyone without an 8 or a 2, so my A♥7♣ has no showdown‑beating value — it doesn’t improve and the kicker is irrelevant. Claude’s line (SB open, tiny flop c‑bet, turn check, river check) is consistent with a missed wide range or a slowplayed made hand. Betting here would be a thin bluff: few hands worse will call and I risk getting called or crushed by full houses/quads. I’m out of position and the pot is modest relative to stacks, so preserving chips and taking the likely chop/showdown is best. I’ll check back.

UTG 6-handed with AA is a mandatory open for value. We want to start building a pot while keeping dominated hands (AQ/AJ, TT-KK, suited broadways) in, and a ~2.5x sizing plays well at these stacks while not over-inflating multiway risk. If we face a 3-bet, we can 4-bet for value.

I am facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo while holding Ten-Deuce of spades in middle position. Although the hand is suited, it is extremely weak and does not play well against a standard UTG opening range. There are several players still to act behind me who could squeeze or call, putting me in a difficult spot out of position or with a very weak holding post-flop. Given it is early in the tournament, there is no reason to defend such a marginal hand. I will fold.

J9s is a strong suited connector in the cutoff position facing an UTG raise to 250 (2.5x BB). FiveTwo's UTG range is relatively tight (top 12-15%), but J9s has excellent playability, straight/flush potential, and dominates many worse suited connectors/broadways. Deep stacks (~100BB) and early tournament stage favor calling in position to realize equity post-flop, where I can control the pot and exploit position. Pot odds are favorable: calling 250 into 400 (effective odds ~2.6:1, plus implied). Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks OOP play and higher variance OOP if shoved on; calling keeps pot manageable multi-way if others call. Past hands show FiveTwo aggressive post-flop, so position is key for value/bluffs.

I have pocket 6s on the button with an UTG raise and a call from the cutoff. This is a classic set-mining spot with good position and implied odds. The pot is 650 and I need 250 to call, giving me 2.6:1 immediate odds. With position and deep stacks, calling to try to flop a set is the standard play here.

Facing an open to 250 from FiveTwo with two callers (Elon and Claude) and me in the small blind with J♦7♥. Pot is ~900 and I must call 200 more. J7o is a weak, offsuit hand with poor equity and terrible post‑flop playability out of position against the opener and callers. Calling into a multiway pot is negative EV and a squeeze 3‑bet here is risky with players behind and likely stronger ranges to punish it. The 50 I already posted is sunk—preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Facing a raise from UTG and two calls, I am in the Big Blind with King-Three offsuit. The pot odds are very attractive (calling 150 to win 900, roughly 7:1), requiring only about 14% equity to call. However, K3o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position in a 4-way pot. My raw equity against a strong UTG range and two calling ranges is likely below the required threshold, and my ability to realize that equity OOP is very low due to reverse implied odds (domination on King-high boards). Folding is the disciplined decision to preserve chips in the early tournament stage.

Multiway 3-handed on Q-8-4 rainbow, AA is still a clear value/protection hand but we don’t want to bloat the pot with a large sizing when callers have plenty of Qx. A small c-bet targets 99–JJ, 8x/4x, and some floaty hands (JT/T9) while denying equity and keeping worse hands in. If we get raised in a multiway pot, it’s heavily weighted to strong Qx/two pair/sets and we can proceed cautiously.

Flop of Qh8d4c completely misses J9o. No pair, no flush draw (offsuit), no straight draw (neither open-ender nor gutshot; J9 needs a 10 for a mediocre top pair or unlikely straight, but currently just overcard equity). FiveTwo's UTG preflop raise followed by 25% pot bet into multiway pot (me and Claude behind) indicates strength: likely Qx, 88+, AJ+, sets, etc. Calling spews chips with no showdown value or fold equity realization postflop; position helps but can't overcome zero equity realization. Raising as bluff risky with Claude's button squeeze potential and FiveTwo's history of aggression (e.g., Hand 2,4,5). Pot odds ~4:1 but implied odds poor on dry board where villain slows down only with nuts. Early tournament, preserve stack for better spots. Standard fold.

I missed my set with pocket 6s on a Q84 board. FiveTwo c-bet 300 after raising preflop from UTG - their range likely contains Qx, overpairs, and occasionally AK. My underpair has only ~8% equity (2 outs to a set) and I'm out of position against the original raiser. This is a clear fold - I was set mining and missed.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with Queen-Eight offsuit. In a 6-handed game, this hand is significantly below the standard range for an opening raise. Playing weak hands out of position often leads to difficult situations post-flop. Therefore, the most disciplined and mathematically sound play is to fold.

46o is a trash hand with poor playability, low equity, and no position advantage. In middle position with multiple players behind in a deep-stack tournament (96BB), folding is standard to avoid speculative plays that rarely pay off. Preserve stack for better spots.
72 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Even with position in the cutoff, there's no value in playing this hand. Easy fold.

I’m on the button with 4♦6♠ — a marginal hand but great position and only the two blinds left to act. With ~72BB effective I can open a fairly wide steal range; a raise also gives me fold equity and the chance to play postflop in position. I’ll raise to 300 (roughly 3x the BB) rather than min-raise to discourage light defenses and keep sizing consistent with earlier opens. Plan: if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and fold to significant aggression or a sizable 3-bet preflop to avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand.

I hold Jc 7h in the Small Blind facing a 3bb (300) raise from the Button (Minni). J7 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position. While the Button's range is likely wide, calling requires investing 250 more chips into a pot of 450, offering odds that don't justify the disadvantage of playing a dominated hand from the worst position post-flop. The standard play is to fold.

Facing a 3x button open, I’m getting ~31% pot odds (200 to win 650 after calling), but 84o has poor playability and realization out of position: it’s frequently dominated when it pairs and has no meaningful blocker value for a profitable 3-bet bluff. With no antes and deep stacks, this is a good spot to conserve chips and defend with stronger offsuit/connectors instead.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 7s3d offsuit, one of the worst possible hands. No position, multiway potential, and deep stacks make this an automatic fold. Playable hands from UTG are tight; 73o has poor playability, low equity vs calling ranges, and no bluff value for raises. Preserve stack for better spots early in tournament.
Q2 suited is a weak hand in middle position. While it has some suited potential, Q2s is not strong enough to open from this position with several players left to act behind me. Standard fold.

Cutoff with K♥6♠ and 74 BB effective. This is a marginal but acceptable opener from late position — good fold equity and playability. Limping gives too much pot control to the blinds and invites multiway action; folding squanders position. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure on the button and blinds, so I open-raise. Size to 300 (≈3x the BB) is a standard, table-consistent raise that reduces callers and preserves fold equity. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, play cautiously to heavy 3-bets (fold to strong 3-bets from tight opponents), and continue against single callers on decent boards.

I am on the Button with King-Six offsuit (K6o). The player in the Cutoff (Minni) has raised to 300 (3 BBs). While I have position, K6o is a weak hand that is often dominated by the opener's range (e.g., KQ, KJ, KT, AK). The raise size of 3 BBs offers pot odds of roughly 1.5:1 immediately, but equity realization with this hand is poor, and reverse implied odds are high if I hit a King. Minni has been active, raising frequently, but without a stronger holding or suited connectivity, folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for better spots.

Facing a 3bb cutoff open from Minni with 5d6h in the small blind, we’d be out of position the rest of the hand and this offsuit connector has poor realization (no suitedness, dominated straight potential). Flat-calling invites the big blind in and creates a difficult multiway spot; 3-bet bluffing here is also low-quality since we have no blockers and Minni’s sizing suggests a reasonably strong/committed opening range. Best EV is to conserve chips and fold.

I have QJo in the Big Blind and am facing a raise to 300 from Minni in the Cutoff. QJo is a solid broadway hand that performs well against a typical late-position opening range. Given the pot odds (calling 200 more to win a total pot of 650), I need roughly 31% equity to justify a call, which QJo certainly has. I will call to defend my blind and see a flop.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker. On this board of Qd-Td-4d, my hand is strong but vulnerable to diamonds and straight draws. As the player in the Big Blind in a single-raised pot, standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor, Minni. This allows her to continuation bet with her wide range, including bluffs and semi-bluffs, while keeping the pot size controlled. I intend to check and will likely call a standard-sized bet to see the turn.

I opened preflop from the cutoff and this is a standard c-bet spot. The flop 4♦ T♦ Q♣ is somewhat dry for my raising range aside from diamond draws and Q/T combinations. I hold K♥6♠ — no pair and no diamond, so my hand has little intrinsic showdown value, but as the preflop aggressor I have fold equity against many of the hands the BB called with (weak broadways, small pairs, air). Betting ~1/2 pot (650 → 325) will fold a lot of those hands and deny equity to diamond draws while keeping the pot manageable if I get called. If I face a substantial check-raise I will give up because my hand plays poorly multi-street out of position. Bet size chosen to balance fold equity and pot control with ~11 SPR left to play post-turn.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker on a board with several draws. Minni has made a standard continuation bet of half the pot. Given my position and the strength of my hand, top pair is definitely strong enough to continue. I don't want to raise here because I might isolate myself against better queens like AQ or KQ, or sets. By calling, I keep her range wide, including her bluffs and semi-bluffs with diamond or straight draws, and I can reassess the situation on the turn based on the card and her further action.

On the turn 2c, which is a complete brick, I still hold top pair with a Jack kicker. Since I was the caller on the flop, it is standard to check to the aggressor (Minni) to see how she proceeds. Leading out here (donk betting) doesn't accomplish much as it likely folds out her bluffs and gets called by better hands. By checking, I allow her to continue bluffing with her diamond draws or straight draws (like J9 or KJ) while controlling the pot size. If she bets, I plan to call given the strength of my hand on this relatively safe turn card.

I c-bet the flop (325) and was called. The turn 2♣ is a blank for my holding K♥6♠ — I still have no pair, no diamond, and very little showdown value. Flash’s call on the flop indicates a range of Qx/Tx, pocket pairs, or diamond draws; many of those hands have decent equity or will call a turn bet. With position and a marginal hand, I prefer pot control rather than building the pot with air. Plan: check the turn to keep the pot manageable, fold to heavy turn/river aggression, and make a decision on the river (call small probes for cheap showdown or bluff/value bet only if the river strongly favors me).

On the river, the Ace of Clubs is a significant scare card. My top pair of Queens has now been demoted to second pair. While Minni checked the turn, which suggests she might not have a monster, the Ace fits her pre-flop raising range perfectly (hands like AJ, AQ, or even AK). Additionally, a straight (KJ) is now possible. Leading out into the pre-flop aggressor on this board doesn't serve much purpose as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I risk getting raised by better ones. Checking is the most prudent play to see how Minni reacts and to potentially get to a cheap showdown if she also checks back with a hand like a Ten or a weaker Queen.

I hold K♥6♠ on A♣ Q♣ T♦ 4♦ 2♣ — I missed entirely and have no club to block the three‑club board. Flash’s line (called my flop c-bet, then checked turn and river) is consistent with showdown‑seeking hands: Qx/TX, pocket pairs, or even Ax — many of which beat me. I have very little fold equity (I don’t block A/Q/clubs) and a river bluff is likely to be called by the better part of his range, so betting would be negative EV. With the pot at 1300 and ~74 BB effective, I prefer pot control and take the free showdown. If I face a river raise I will fold.
8h2h is a very weak hand, especially from UTG position. Suited but with terrible connectivity and no high card value. Easy fold from early position.

UTG folded and I’m in MP with 5♦8♦. Pot is 150 and it costs me 100 to see the flop. With ~68 BB effective, 5-8 suited is a speculative hand that wants to see flops and can realize equity multiway, but it’s not strong enough to open-shove or face a likely 3-bet from the aggressive cutoff/button. A min-raise here (200) is small and will just invite callers or a 3-bet; a standard 2.5–3x open is an option but against these players and this hand I prefer to keep the pot controllable. I’ll call 100 cheaply, fold to significant preflop aggression, and proceed postflop only with a pair or strong draw (flush/straight) or good pot-control opportunities.

Current hand is A5o in the Cutoff. Minni (Middle Position) has limped for 100. Previous history shows Minni raising to 300 frequently, so this limp likely indicates a weak speculative hand or a small pair, though a trap is possible. A5o is a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially off-suit. While an isolation raise could take down the pot of 250 immediately, playing a bloated pot with A5o against a sticky opponent or facing a 3-bet from the Button/Blinds is a negative EV situation. Given the early stage of the tournament (Hand 10/150) and the stack depth, preserving chips by folding this marginal holding is the prudent, low-variance play.

Button vs a single limp, we have position and can profitably attack the dead money. T6o isn’t a great multiway hand, so raising is better than overlimping to thin the field, take initiative, and often win preflop or with a simple continuation bet on favorable flops. If a blind or the limper plays back strongly, we can comfortably fold this hand.

I am holding Ten-Nine of clubs in the small blind, which is a very strong suited connector that plays well in multi-way pots. FiveTwo has raised from the button after a limp from Minni. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the pot odds are reasonable to see a flop, and T9s has great playability. I'll call the 400 and see how the flop develops, though I must be wary of a squeeze from the big blind (Elon) or the initial limper.

Ah4h is a strong speculative hand in the big blind facing a button raise over a limper and SB call. Suited ace offers nut flush draw potential, live ace blocker, and good implied odds deepstacked early in the tournament (hand 10/150). Pot odds are excellent: calling 350 into 1100 (3.1:1), needing ~24% equity which A4s achieves against a wide BTN opening range (~25-35%) and loose calls from SB and limper. Postflop playability is decent OOP multiway, with top pair potential or strong draws. Folding is too tight; raising OOP with marginal ace is -EV. Call to realize equity and implied odds.

I limped with 5♦8♦ and the button (FiveTwo) raised to 450; I’ve already put in 100 so it costs me 350 to call. 5♦8♦ is a speculative, suited hand that performs well multi‑way and against a button open I can realize a lot of equity on favorable flops. Calling 350 gets me roughly 350 to win ~1,800 (≈19.5% break‑even equity), which is a cheap price with ~68BB effective stacks and good implied odds if I hit a flush/straight or two‑pair. Raising to isolate would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal holding and folding is too tight given the price and multiple callers. Plan: see the flop and continue only with a pair or strong draw; fold to heavy action without improvement.

I have Ten-Nine of clubs on a board of Five-Trey-Seven. While I have two overcards, I lack any direct pair or strong draw in this 4-way pot. Since I am first to act, checking is the most appropriate move to see how the other three players react to this low board, especially since FiveTwo was the pre-flop aggressor on the button.

Multiway pot (4 players) on a drawy flop with two diamonds (3d7d) and uncoordinated low cards (5s3d7d). Ah4h is pure Ace-high bluff-catcher with no pair, no straight draw (gutshot 2-6 unlikely), no flush draw (hearts miss board). Pre-flop call was speculative for implied odds, but flop misses completely. No value to thin-value bet; Ace blocker to top pair Ace but multiway ranges from BTN raiser (FiveTwo), SB caller (Flash), and limper (Minni) include many pairs/draws/setmines. Bluff-betting OOP risky early (hand 10/150) with deep stacks (~90BB) and history of players calling stations (e.g., Hand #2 Minni calls big). Flash check suggests weakness, but checking preserves stack, controls pot OOP, induces bluffs later, and allows free card realization if behind. Folding equity low multiway. Check to BB/TN then re-evaluate.

I have 5♦8♦ on a 5♠ 3♦ 7♦ flop — that’s top pair (pair of fives) with an 8 kicker plus a four‑card diamond draw (9 diamond outs). Pot is 1,800 and there is currently no bet to me. Checking is the right play: I want to control the pot out of position, realize my decent equity (≈19% to hit the flush on the turn, ≈35% by the river, plus a couple of extra outs to improve to trips/two‑pair), and allow the button to bluff or c‑bet wide. Betting or raising here would bloat a multiway pot with a marginal hand and expose me to raises; instead I’ll check and see the turn. If faced with a reasonable bet on the turn I’ll call to realize my draw and protect my pair; I’ll fold to extreme pressure unless I improve, and if I hit the flush or two‑pair/trips I’ll extract value on later streets.

Multiway (3 opponents) on a low, connected, two-diamond flop, this board hits callers’ ranges hard (pairs, 7x/5x, 66-99, Axd, diamond draws). With Td6s we only have a small gutshot to the 4 and no backdoor flush, so a c-bet will get called too often and leaves us with weak barreling prospects. In position, taking the free card keeps the pot controlled and lets us stab profitable turns if it checks again or improve on a 4.

I am currently holding Ten-high with no pair and no draw on a board that now features a possible flush and a King. In a 4-way pot, betting here as a pure bluff is extremely risky, especially since I am out of position against three other players. The flop checked around, which suggests the other players might not have strong holdings, but the King of Diamonds is a likely card to have hit someone's range or completed a diamond flush. My best course of action is to check and see how the rest of the table reacts, as I have no showdown value and very little equity to continue if a bet is made.

Turn board Kd completes diamond flushes and adds a high card, improving some drawing hands and pairing king ranges multiway. Ah4h remains ace-high with no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw (hearts are clean but uncoordinated). Pre-flop speculative call realized no equity, and checking flop was correct pot control OOP. Flash's check suggests continued weakness, but betting ace-high bluff multiway vs. BTN raiser (FiveTwo who checked flop), limper (Minni), and SB caller is low-FE early in tournament (hand 10/150, ~90BB deep). Checking preserves stack, controls pot OOP, allows realization of any unlikely river improvement or inductions bluffs on river, while avoiding difficult spots. Ranges likely contain pairs/draws that can call or raise bluffs. Check behind to river.

Turn K♦ completes my diamonds — with 5♦8♦ I now have a made flush (K-8-7-5-3). This is a very strong hand in a multiway pot but vulnerable to any two-diamond holding containing a diamond higher than my 8 (A♦, Q♦, J♦, T♦, 9♦). The table has been relatively passive (lots of check/calls), so heavy raises are less likely but possible. I want to extract value from hands like Kx, single 5x, and medium pairs while keeping my commitment manageable if someone shows strong aggression. A half-pot value bet (~900 into 1800) accomplishes that: it charges worse hands and fold equity is limited (so it won’t bloat the pot catastrophically if I’m behind). Plan: bet 900 now, call or re-evaluate a small raise but fold to a large, unmistakably strong raise that represents a superior flush.

Minni’s 1/3-pot lead into a 4-way pot looks like a lot of range (some value/flushes, but also plenty of single-diamond probes and Kx). With Td6s we have meaningful equity: any diamond on the river gives us a flush (Td), and any non-diamond 4 gives us a straight (3 additional outs), for ~12 river outs before discounting. Getting 3:1 (900 to win 3600) in position makes continuing preferable to folding, while raising risks getting jammed on and burning chips with a marginal draw. Plan: realize equity in position; fold most missed rivers versus significant pressure, and value cautiously when we improve given our flush won’t always be the nuts.

I currently hold Ten-high with no pair and no draw. The board now has three diamonds and a King, and both Minni and FiveTwo have shown strength by betting and calling, respectively. Given that I have no equity in this pot and am facing significant action from two opponents, there is no justification for continuing with this hand. Folding is the clear and optimal decision.

Facing a 900 bet into 3600 pot (~4:1 odds, 20% equity needed) on a turn Kd that completes the diamond flush draw on a already draw-heavy board (5s3d7dKd). Ah4h is pure ace-high with no pair, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), no flush draw. Preflop speculative call was for set-mining/flush potential or top pair, but missed completely both streets. Minni (limper who checked flop) donk-bets turn after double check, strong range: completed flush (AdXd, KdXd), two-pair, sets, or strong Kd. FiveTwo (preflop raiser) calls 900 after checking flop, assigning strength or draw. My checks both streets show weakness; calling OOP multiway deepstacked (hand 10/150, ~90BB) with zero equity and poor showdown value vs. villains' ranges is -EV. No fold equity to raise ace-high into two players likely to call/raise. Fold preserves stack for better spots, realizes preflop plan of pot control when missing.

I have 5♦8♦ on a 5♠ 3♦ 7♦ K♦ T♠ board — I’ve made a K‑high diamond flush. The only realistic hands that beat me are nut diamonds (A♦) or a disguised full house (very unlikely given the passive action). The pot is 3,600 and the lone caller on the turn (BTN) is likely to have Kx, second pairs, or medium pockets that will pay a value bet. I want to extract value but avoid overcommitting to the one obvious nut combo. A half‑pot value bet (~1,800) charges worse Kx and pairs and keeps the pot manageable if I face strong resistance. Plan: bet 1,800 for value; if I face a large, unequivocal raise (overcommit/overbet shove) I will fold since that line strongly indicates the nut flush or full house, but I’ll call small raises and re‑evaluate.

River pairs us (T) after we called the turn lead. Minni’s 1/3-pot sizing (1800 into 5400) looks like a lot of blocker/thin value in a spot where she can have many medium hands from a limp-call line (7x/5x, some Tx, occasional air turned into a cheap stab) in addition to the strong hands (Kx, two-diamond flushes, 46 straight). We only need to be good ~25% (1800 to win 7200), and folding top pair to this small sizing is too tight; raising is bad since we’re rarely called by worse and often get snapped by Kx/flush/straight. Take the price and realize our showdown value.

UTG with J♥7♠ is a marginal, poorly-playing hand. In 6-max UTG I need a much stronger, higher-playability range — J7o is frequently dominated by better jacks and broadways and performs poorly out of position. Facing five players behind (and aggressive callers/raisers observed at this table), limp/calling 100 will often lead to multiway pots and difficult decisions; a tiny open to 200 is thin and invites 3-bets or calls from better hands. With a healthy 108 BB stack I should wait for a better spot rather than invest chips with a weak hand out of position. Fold and conserve stack for more profitable opportunities.

I am in middle position with 2h 9d, which is an extremely weak hand (unconnected, off-suit, low cards). With players left to act behind me, including the cutoff and button, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot. The standard play is to fold immediately to preserve chips.

We’re in the cutoff with 8♦2♣ in an unopened pot (just blinds). Even in a 6-max format this hand is too weak to profitably open: it’s dominated when we make top pair, has poor playability, and won’t realize equity well versus the button/blinds. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. Since the action has folded to me, this is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds or take control of the pot. A7o is well ahead of the average range of the blinds in a 6-max game. I will raise to 3 big blinds to put pressure on Elon and Claude.

Preflop from the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x raise). My hand is Jc4d offsuit, which is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, no suitedness, and poor high-card strength. Button ranges are wide but still contain many hands that dominate J4o or have better playability. Defending SB with such a weak hand leads to difficult postflop spots where I'm often out of position and crushed. Effective stacks are deep (90+ BB), so no need to gamble. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots in a long tournament.

4h7c is a weak off-suit hand with poor playability. Facing a button raise of 300, I need to call 200 more. While pot odds are 200:450 (~2.25:1), this hand plays poorly out of position against a button raising range. It's not suited, has no high card value, and the gap between cards limits straight potential. Easy fold.

I am holding 9s 6h in the UTG position. This is a very weak starting hand (unsuited, unconnected gapper) and I am in the earliest position with 5 opponents left to act behind me. Playing this hand would be a significant mistake given the high probability of being dominated or facing a 3-bet. With a stack of 116 BBs, there is no pressure to make desperate moves. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Pocket 4s are strong enough to open from middle in 6-max. Limping invites a multiway pot where we’ll often be dominated postflop and gives up initiative. By raising we can (1) pick up blinds immediately at a good frequency, (2) realize set value when called, and (3) comfortably fold to larger 3-bets from the aggressive stacks behind without torching chips. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open to keep ranges wide and sizing consistent.

I am in the Cutoff with 8d 5d. FiveTwo has raised to 250 from middle position. While FiveTwo has shown a tendency to play aggressively and open wide, 85s is a very weak hand to defend against a raise, even with the benefit of position. There are still three active players behind me (Elon, Claude, and Minni) who could 3-bet or call, further reducing my equity and making it difficult to realize my hand's potential. Given the tournament stage and my healthy stack, there is no need to take a high-variance, -EV spot with a speculative one-gapper. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

AhJh is a strong suited broadway hand on the button, with excellent playability, position, and equity against a middle position opening range. FiveTwo's raise to 250 (2.5x BB) from middle position suggests a wide range, as they've shown aggression in prior hands (e.g., frequent preflop raises and postflop betting). Calling keeps the pot multiway with blinds to act, but 3-betting isolates, applies pressure, and builds the pot with position. Standard sizing: 3.6x their raise to 900 total. This has good fold equity while getting value from worse broadways and suited hands. FiveTwo has 6050 (~60BB), so committed little; blinds are deep and may fold. Folding or flat-calling is too passive here.
Holding 76o facing a 3-bet to 900 from Elon on the button. This is a clear fold - 76 offsuit plays poorly out of position against a 3-bet range. No implied odds justification here.

Facing a button 3-bet to 900 after an earlier open to 250, I hold Kd‑2s — a weak, dominated offsuit hand with a poor kicker and minimal postflop playability. Calling 800 leaves me out of position against a likely strong 3-bet range (and with the original opener still to act I risk a multiway pot). 4‑betting would be a thin bluff with poor blockers and little fold equity. Preserve the stack for better spots and fold.

We opened 2.5bb with 44 and face a button 3-bet to 9bb. Calling 650 more creates a ~1,950 pot with only ~5,150 behind (SPR ~2.6) and we’ll be out of position, so the implied odds to set-mine aren’t there (we need much closer to ~10–15x the call in effective stacks, and we have ~7.9x). A 4-bet bluff would be high-variance and risks running into a strong range/jam. Best is to preserve our 60bb stack and wait for better spots.

UTG in a 6-max game, 43o is too weak to open or even limp profitably: it makes dominated low pairs/weak two-pair, has poor playability, and will often realize equity badly out of position versus multiple players. With no compelling dynamic to attack (and plenty of stacks behind), the best EV is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I have 2c 3h, which is an extremely weak starting hand with very little potential. Given I am in middle position and there are several players left to act behind me, there is no reason to play this hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here to conserve my stack.

Preflop in cutoff with folds from UTG and MP, leaving dead money in the pot (SB limp/post + BB). 6d7h is offsuit but has straight potential and playability postflop, especially in position against button, SB, and BB. Deep stacks (95BB) allow for aggressive play. Standard to open-raise wide from CO to steal blinds; limping is weak and invites multiway pots. Raising ~2.25x BB builds pot and takes initiative. Opponents have shown tendency to fold or call light but not overly sticky preflop recently. Minraise to 200 is too small; sizing to 225 applies good pressure while keeping pot manageable.

K4o is a marginal hand facing a raise from the cutoff. While I have position on the button, K4o doesn't play well postflop - it's dominated by better kings and the 4 kicker is weak. Calling opens me up to squeezes from the blinds, and 3-betting as a bluff with a hand that has some showdown value isn't ideal. Standard fold here.

I’m in the small blind with pocket 3s facing a cutoff open to 225. With ~10700 behind (deep stacks) calling 175 more to make 225 total is ideal for set-mining — excellent implied odds and I avoid bloating the pot out of position. A 3-bet here risks squeezes and 4-bets from the big blind or the opener; calling keeps weaker hands and bluffs in and disguises my range. Plan: call to see the flop; if I hit a set I’ll play aggressively, otherwise proceed cautiously out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 5h, facing a raise to 225 from the Cutoff and a call from the Small Blind. The pot is 550 and I need to call 125, giving me direct pot odds of 4.4 to 1 (requiring ~18.5% equity). While 85o has roughly 21% raw equity against reasonable opening and calling ranges, it is a disconnected, offsuit hand that will play very poorly out of position in a multi-way pot. My equity realization will likely be low (around 60-70%), making the effective equity (~13-15%) insufficient to justify the call. Folding is the standard and prudent play here to preserve chips.

I have pocket 3s and flopped a set on J♦ 7♣ 6♣. It’s heads-up against the preflop raiser (Elon). The board has two clubs, so there are flush draws and some straight/connector draws in his likely raising range, but many of his hands (Jx, mid pairs, suited connectors) will call a bet. I’m out of position, so I want to both protect my hand and build the pot for value. Bet sizing around half to two-thirds pot keeps worse hands and draws calling while denying free cards. I’ll bet ~60% pot to extract value from top pair/medium pairs and to charge club draws. Plan: if called, reassess on the turn — continue extracting on safe turns and be cautious if a club or a Jack shows up; I’m unlikely to fold a set to a single large raise.

Preflop raise with 67o from CO got a loose SB call from Minni, isolating well heads-up in position. Flop Jd7c6c hits perfectly: two pair (7s and 6s) with no club in hand, disguising strength. Board is draw-heavy (clubs flush draw, open-enders like T8/89/45/58), warranting a raise for value and protection against draws. Minni's lead (1/3 pot) into preflop raiser is standard with pair+ or draws, but her history shows aggression with made hands (e.g., Hand 10 turn/river bets, Hand 2 calls). Deep stacks (~95BB), position allows raising to build pot OOP opponent's range. Call too passive, risks giving free cards to draws or check-jams. Fold absurd with top-two effectively. Raise sizing ~3x her bet (1050 total) balances value from worse pairs/top pair (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, pairs/draws) while protecting. Avoids minraise (too small, invites calls); pot-sized too large early.

I flopped a set of 3s on J♦ 7♣ 6♣ — a very strong hand but the two clubs on board give many flush draws and there are a few rare sets/trips (77, 66, JJ) that beat me. Elon, the preflop raiser, check‑raised my 350 to 1050 — this line is consistent with either a made top pair (Jx), a protected pocket pair, or a semi‑bluff with a club draw. I’m out of position, so my priorities are to build the pot for value and charge/price out club and straight draws. Calling keeps draws cheap and gives them a free turn; folding is incorrect with a set. I’ll raise to a size that forces most draws to pay dearly while still getting called by Jx and medium pairs. Raise to 4000 total: that prices many club draws and extracts value from worse made hands. Plan: if called, reassess on the turn — continue extracting on safe turns and be prepared to commit to a large turn/river if the board doesn’t complete obvious draws.

Facing a massive flop 3-bet from Minni (SB, OOP) after her donk lead into my preflop raise range on a draw-heavy J76cc board where I hold the effective nuts with top two pair (76). Her line is highly polarized: nuts (sets JJ/77/66, combos J7/J6), strong draws (flush draws like AcXc/QcXc, combo draws 89cc/T9cc, etc.), or air/bluffs to exploit my wide raising range. Direct straights unlikely preflop. Calling 2950 into 5600 (~2:1) gets decent odds with ~85%+ equity vs her range, but risks turn completion (flush ~9% if she has it, str draws), free cards to missed draws, and low SPR (~0.76 with her 6525 behind) favors aggression. Raising all-in to 9275 total (committing my 8225 remaining + prior 1050) maximizes pressure: high fold equity vs her bluffs/draws (35-50% of range), value from trapped weaker value (overpairs like QQ/TT, top pair Jx she assigns strength), denies equity, and simplifies. If called, I have position and crush her calling range (e.g., equity >70% vs sets/draws). Minni's history shows aggression but folds to big pressure (Hand 4 river fold, Hand 10 value bets but not invincible). Tournament stage early (hand 13/150, ~82BB effective), ICM neutral, jamming exploits her likely overplay. Passive call/check-fold too weak with monster.

I flopped a set of 3s on J♦7♣6♣. Elon check‑raised my c‑bet to 1050, I 3‑bet to 4000 to charge club/straight draws and extract value, and he shoved to 9275 (all‑in). I must put in 5275 more into a pot of 13,825, so I’m getting ~27.6% break‑even equity. A set is ahead of almost all of his plausible shove range (club draws, Jx, medium pairs); only a narrow range of higher sets/trips (JJ, 77, 66) beats me. Given the pot odds and the hand strength, folding would be a mistake. I’ll call the all‑in — the price is correct and I expect to have the best hand vs enough of his range to make this +EV. I’ll have ~1,250 left behind after the call.

I am in the UTG position with 9c 7c. While this is a suited connector, it is generally too weak to open-raise from the earliest position in a 6-max game. The risk of being dominated or facing a 3-bet from the five players acting after me is high, and playing this hand out of position post-flop is not ideal. I will fold to preserve my stack.

TJ offsuit is a marginal hand at best from middle position in a 6-handed tournament with deep stacks (191 BB) and players behind, including short-stacked Minni on the button (12.5 BB) who could shove wide. Preflop, after UTG fold, standard play is to fold this hand rather than open limp (which invites multiway pots) or raise (lacks strength and playability postflop). Preserve stack for stronger spots to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

Q2o is a weak hand that's not in my standard cutoff opening range. Even though I'm in late position with only 3 players behind, this hand has poor playability and will often be dominated when called. Better to fold and wait for a stronger spot.

I’m on the button with Kc7s and only 1250 (≈12.5BB). Action folded to me; pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call. With this short stack I should be in shove/fold mode — open-shoving maximizes fold equity vs the blinds and avoids getting squeezed or playing a marginal postflop spot OOP. K7s is plenty good to shove from the button at ~12BB. Calling 100 or a small raise leaves too much dead money and gives the blinds cheap odds to see a flop; all-in simplifies the decision and gives me the best chance to double up or steal the blinds. I shove for my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Kd 9d. The blinds are 50/100. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 1250, which appears to be an all-in given her stack is listed as 0 in the opponents list (implying the 1250 is her remaining chips). The pot before my action is 1400 (1250 + 100 BB + 50 SB). I need to call 1200 to win a total pot of 2600, offering me pot odds of approximately 46%. K9s is a decent hand but against a desperate shove range (likely top 30-50% of hands), it has about 43-49% equity. This makes the call marginally EV neutral or slightly negative, not accounting for the Big Blind left to act behind me who could wake up with a premium hand. Given I have a healthy stack of 11,450 (114 BB), there is no need to take this high-variance, marginal spot. Folding is the prudent play.

Minni’s raise appears to be an all-in (min-raise N/A and her stack showing 0), so my decision is strictly fold vs call. I’m being laid ~45% pot odds (call 1150 to win 2550). A8o is comfortably ahead of a typical button shove range at this depth (many Kx/Qx, suited hands, and smaller pairs), and the Ad blocks strong Ax combos that dominate me. Even if her shove is somewhat tight, this is still close enough that calling is +EV versus most reasonable ranges.

K8o is a marginal offsuit king from UTG in a 5-handed game. Standard opening ranges from early position do not include K8o due to its poor playability out of position and vulnerability to domination by better kings. With deep stacks (191 BB), patience is key early in the tournament (hand 15/150). Folding preserves stack for premium opportunities. No relevant opponent tendencies override this.

A9o in UTG+1 is a solid opening hand. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x BB) as a standard open. This hand plays well heads-up or in small pots, and I can take down the blinds or continue with top pair if called.

Current hand is 5-handed. Claude (CO) opens to 2.5bb. I am on the Button with A9o. While I have the positional advantage, A9o is a marginal hand that often suffers from reverse implied odds against an opening range, as it is dominated by AT+, AJ, AQ, AK. Flat calling invites squeezes from the blinds and leaves me navigating a dominated hand post-flop. 3-betting is an option as a bluff with the Ace blocker, but A9o is generally too weak to sustain a call or 4-bet. The most standard and profitable play with deep stacks (100bb+) in this spot is to fold and wait for better opportunities.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG+1 open, out of position the rest of the hand and with the big blind still to act behind. 7-4 offsuit has poor playability, makes dominated pairs, and can’t profitably defend versus a reasonably strong opening range. A squeeze/3-bet bluff is also too thin with this holding and stack depth. Best EV is to let it go and preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. Claude has raised to 2.5bb from UTG+1. While I am getting a decent price to call (150 more into a 400 pot), J6o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an early position raiser. I prefer to fold here and look for better opportunities to enter pots.
4s9h is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity. From UTG position with 4 players behind me, this is a clear fold. No reason to open raise with such a weak holding.

I am in the Cutoff position (UTG+1 in a 5-handed game) with 75s (Seven-Five of Clubs). While this is a playable speculative hand in late position, opening it from the Cutoff is slightly loose, especially with a deep-stacked Big Blind and active players behind. My hand falls just outside the standard opening range for this position (typically top 25-30%). Given the early stage of the tournament and the desire to avoid marginal spots out of position against the Button, folding is the disciplined and standard choice here.

Folded to us on the Button with KTo, which plays well in position and is strong enough to open/steal versus the blinds. Raising applies immediate pressure to the SB/BB, denies them a cheap flop, and lets us take initiative with a hand that can make strong top-pair holdings. Use a standard small-ish open size to risk little while still generating folds.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd Jd, a premium suited broadway hand that performs well in short-handed play. FiveTwo raised from the Button, which is a wide range. I will 3-bet to 850 to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, and discourage Elon in the Big Blind from entering the pot with a wide range.

Holding 2d8s, a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. Preflop action: Folds from early positions, FiveTwo raises to 250 (likely button), Flash 3-bets to 850 from SB. Pot is 1200, to call 750 OOP gives ~1.6:1 odds (need ~38% equity), but 28o has abysmal equity vs. any 3bet range (~10-15% at best). No fold equity for a 4bet bluff with this hand. Standard fold to avoid spew and preserve stack for better spots.

Facing Flash’s small-blind 3-bet to 850 after our button open, KTo is a bit too marginal to 4-bet (we’d inflate the pot and often get called by dominating Kx/Qx broadways). However, the sizing gives us a good price (600 to win ~1200) and we have full position, plus SB 3-bets are often wider than value-heavy ranges. Calling keeps the pot manageable while letting us realize equity and outplay postflop; we can continue on K/T hits and favorable textures, and comfortably fold versus heavy aggression on boards that smash a typical 3-bet range.

I am the pre-flop 3-bettor and the board has come Ace-high (Ad 6c 2c). This board significantly favors my perceived range, which includes strong Aces like AK, AQ, and AJ. Even though I haven't hit the board with Qd Jd, I have a backdoor flush draw and the initiative. A small continuation bet of 600 (1/3 pot) is standard here to represent the Ace and put pressure on the opponent's pocket pairs and non-Ace broadway cards. If I get raised, I will likely have to fold, but a bet often wins the pot immediately on such a dry, high-card board.

Flash’s c-bet is very small (600 into 2400), giving us strong immediate pot odds and position. Even though we have no pair on A-6-2, KTo still has some equity (two overcards + backdoor club/straight possibilities), and—more importantly—we can realize that equity and apply pressure on turns when Flash checks or when scare cards (clubs/broadways) roll off. Raising here would mostly get action from Ax/overpairs and fold out the hands we’re already doing fine against, so calling keeps his range wide and preserves our ability to outplay later streets.

The turn card is the 6s, which pairs the board. Having 3-bet pre-flop and continuation-bet the Ace-high flop, I have represented a strong range. However, FiveTwo's call on the flop indicates they likely have an Ace, a pocket pair, or a flush draw. With Queen-high and no real draws, my hand has very little equity if called again. I will check to control the pot size and see how FiveTwo reacts. If they check behind, I might be able to realize some equity or evaluate a bluff on the river. If they bet, I will likely have to fold as I am currently behind most of their calling range.

Flash’s turn check after a tiny flop c-bet in a 3-bet pot caps his range a bit (a lot of whiffed broadways / pocket pairs and some pot-control Ax). With K-high we have essentially no showdown value, so checking back just concedes too often. The paired 6 is a good card to apply pressure because our flop-calling range contains plenty of Ax and some 6x, and my Kc blocks some natural club floats. I’ll fire a medium bet to fold out his non-Ace hands and set up profitable river barrels on good cards; if he check-raises, I can comfortably fold.

I am currently holding Queen-high with no draws on a board of Ad 6c 2c 6s. I was the pre-flop aggressor and continuation-bet the flop, but FiveTwo called, indicating they likely have a piece of the board (most likely an Ace or a flush draw). When I checked the turn, FiveTwo bet 1,350 into a pot of 3,000 (now 4,350). I have no showdown value and very little equity to improve to a winning hand on the river. Continuing with a bluff or a call is negative EV in this situation, as the opponent's range is heavily weighted towards hands that will not fold to further pressure or are already far ahead. I will fold and preserve my remaining stack.
