I am in UTG position with King-Seven offsuit (K7o). In a 5-handed game, this hand is significantly below the opening range threshold for early position. It is easily dominated by callers who will likely have better Kings or pocket pairs, and being out of position makes it difficult to play profitably. Given the deep stacks (114 BB) and early stage of the tournament, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. The standard and correct play is to fold.

5-handed with Pro folded, this is effectively an early/middle position open. A7o has good blocker value and dominates plenty of worse Ax that will continue, while limping invites multiway pots and lets the blinds realize equity too cheaply. I’ll open-raise to apply pressure, build the pot when ahead, and keep initiative; if I face a large 3-bet from the deeper stacks I can comfortably fold.

I am on the button with Ten-Deuce suited. FiveTwo has raised from UTG+1. Even though I have position and the hand is suited, T2s is too weak to play against an early position raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot to use my stack.

J2s is a weak suited hand with significant gaps, facing an early position raise from FiveTwo who plays aggressively but this is still a fold from the SB out of position. Poor equity against an UTG+1 range (~12-15% hands), bad postflop playability OOP, and no strong implied odds justification given the hand's weakness. Pot odds 2:1 require too much equity (~33%), and J2s realizes far less vs strong range. Preserve stack early in tournament.

Pocket 4s in the BB facing a min-raise from UTG+1. Good set-mining opportunity with 80:1 effective stacks. Pot odds of 400:150 (2.67:1) plus implied odds make this an easy call. If I miss the flop, easy to fold. If I hit a set, potential to win a big pot.

I flopped a set of 4s on a 4s-5h-3s board - this is a monster! However, the board is wet with spade flush draw and straight draws (A2, 67, 26 all have draws). I need to bet for value and protection rather than slow-playing. A 2/3 pot bet (~350) should build the pot while charging draws.

Claude is donk-betting a board (3-4-5 with a flush draw) that generally hits the BB calling range well, so blasting a bluff-raise with A7o (no spade) is high-variance and likely to get played back at by pairs/6x/2x/sets. That said, folding is a bit too tight because we have position, an overcard (A) and a gutshot to the wheel (any 2), plus we can apply pressure on certain turns when he checks. The price is reasonable and calling keeps the pot manageable while we realize equity and keep his stab range wide.

I still have bottom set on 4s-5h-3s-Ac. The Ace completes A2 for the wheel, but that's a small part of villain's range. More likely FiveTwo has Ax that just hit top pair, flush draws, or other hands. I should continue betting for value - my set is still very strong. Betting ~65% pot to charge draws and extract value from Ax hands that improved.

We turn top pair (Aces) with a mediocre kicker plus a wheel gutshot (any 2). Claude’s flop raise then turn barrel can be value (6x straights, sets/two-pair, some Ax) and also some pressure with pair+spade draws; raising now mostly isolates us versus his strongest continues, while folding is far too tight getting a good price in position. Calling keeps his bluffs/draws in, controls pot size, and lets us re-evaluate on the river based on sizing/runout (especially spades/2/paired boards).

I have a set of 4s on 4s-5h-3s-Ac-9s. The river 9s completed the spade flush, which is concerning. FiveTwo called flop and turn - could have flush draws that got there, A2 for the wheel, or Ax hands. However, I can't check and give up value from all their non-flush hands. Many of their calling hands are Ax that paired up or missed draws. I'll bet around 60% pot for thin value - if they have a flush they'll raise and I can fold, otherwise I extract value from worse.

River completes the front-door spade flush on a board where Claude has led flop, barreled the Ace turn (a card that heavily favors my UTG+1 range), and now fires a ~60% pot third barrel. With Ah7c I’m basically at the bottom of my turn-calling Ax bluff-catchers and I don’t hold a spade to block the large portion of his flop/turn semibluffs that are now made flushes. His value range (two spade hands, flopped straights like 67/26, sets, and Ax that improved to two-pair like A5/A4/A3/A9) is very plausible, and the river card removes many natural bluffs. Needing ~27% equity to call, this is a spot to let the weakest Ax go and avoid paying off a value-heavy line.

5-handed from UTG I want to play a fairly tight opening range. Q4 offsuit is dominated by many continuing hands (Qx, Ax, broadways) and plays poorly multiway; limping just invites isolation and tough postflop spots. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for stronger opens.

I am in UTG+1 (essentially the Hijack in a 5-handed game) with King-Four suited. While suited Kings have some playability, K4s is generally too weak to open from this position, especially with several aggressive players behind me who could easily 3-bet. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play to preserve my stack for better spots.

2d3s is one of the weakest possible hands, offsuit low cards with poor connectivity and no high card strength. On the button with folds to me, stealing blinds is tempting due to position, but this hand has terrible post-flop playability: low equity against calling ranges, reverse implied odds if we hit, and vulnerable to 3-bets or calls from aggressive blinds like Claude. Pot is 150, calling 100 gives poor odds for limping into a multiway pot potentially. Raising risks commitment with trash. Deep stacks (18k effective), early in tournament (hand 18/150), better to fold and preserve stack for premium spots. Opponents' prior aggression (e.g., Claude's raises, Pro's calls) reduces fold equity for a steal.

Holding 3h8d in SB with everyone folded to me. This is a weak hand, but I'm getting 3:1 odds to complete against just the BB. Not strong enough to raise, but completing to see a cheap flop is reasonable. If I fold I give up 50 chips with some showdown potential.

I am in the Big Blind with 2h 5s, one of the weakest starting hands. The Small Blind (Claude) has limped (completed to 100). Since there is no raise to call, I can see the flop for free. Raising with 5-2 offsuit would be over-aggressive and unprofitable. Checking maximizes my EV by allowing me to realize any potential equity without investing more chips.

I flopped bottom pair (3s) with an 8 kicker on a 5h6c3d board. This is a marginal holding - I have some showdown value but betting would be thin. The board has some straight draw potential (47 makes a straight). Checking is best here to pot control and see what Pro does. If he bets small I can consider calling, if he bets big I can fold.

I hold bottom pair (5s) with a weak kicker (2) and a gutshot straight draw (need a 4) on a 5-6-3 rainbow board. The pot is small (200), and the Small Blind checked to me. Betting here has little value as worse hands (air) will likely fold, while better hands (better pairs, 6x) will call or raise. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, potentially improve on the turn, and keep the pot control manageable with a marginal hand. I am in position and want to get to showdown cheaply.

Turn brought the Ad which is a bad card for my bottom pair. The Ace could easily have hit Pro's BB range. My hand has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet - I'd only get called by better. Check and hope to get to showdown cheaply.

I hold a pair of Fives (third pair) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). The pot is small (200 chips). The turn card is an Ace, which connects well with the Small Blind's limping range. Since my opponent checked to me again, I have the opportunity to take a free card. Betting has little value as worse hands will likely fold and better hands (like any 6 or an Ace) will call. Checking allows me to realize my equity and try to hit my straight or get to showdown cheaply with my pair.

River 9h doesn't change much. I have bottom pair with 3s. Pro checked back flop and turn, suggesting weakness. My hand has marginal showdown value but betting would be thin - I'd only get called by better. Best to check and try to win at showdown with my pair of 3s.

I hold a pair of Fives (4th pair) on a board of 5-6-3-A-9. The pot is small (200), and the action has been checked down on all streets. My opponent (Small Blind) has shown weakness by checking three times. Betting has no value as worse hands (air) will fold, and better hands (6x, 9x, Ax) will call. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value for free; I might win against King-high or Queen-high hands.

I am in the UTG position with 7c 4s. This is an extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand that does not meet the requirements for an opening range from any position, especially early position. Standard poker strategy dictates an immediate fold to conserve chips for better opportunities.

Preflop in UTG+1 position with J3o, an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor playability and no high card strength. Standard play is to fold trash hands from early position to avoid difficult postflop situations and preserve stack for better spots. No limpers or raises yet, but entering the pot with this would be a mistake given the multiway potential and our deep effective stacks relative to blinds.

7h3s is a very weak hand, even for a button steal attempt. While I have position and only the blinds remain, 73o is bottom of the range and not worth risking chips. With a healthy stack at 13475, I'll wait for better spots.

Action is folded to me in the Small Blind with Q3s. We are deep-stacked (100BB+). My opponent in the Big Blind (FiveTwo) has shown tendencies to defend blinds and play aggressively post-flop (e.g., Hand #4, #16). Q3s is a marginal hand that benefits from seeing a cheap flop due to its flush potential, but plays poorly in a bloated pot out of position against a sticky opponent. Raising might isolate me against a range that dominates me or induce a 3-bet. Getting 3:1 odds on a call, completing the blind (limping) is a low-variance play to realize equity while keeping the pot manageable.

Small blind limped, which caps their range and gives us a good spot to take initiative. A6s plays well as an isolation raise (can make top pair with good kicker dynamics in blind-vs-blind, has nut-flush potential, and blocks some strong Ax continues). Raising also picks up dead money immediately a decent amount of the time and lets us c-bet favorable flops. I’ll use a modest but firm iso size out of position to deny equity and avoid giving a cheap flop.

I previously limped from the Small Blind with Qc3c hoping to see a cheap flop. The Big Blind (FiveTwo), who has been aggressive, raised to 350 (3.5BB). I now have to call 250 chips into a pot of 450, offering roughly 1.8:1 odds. Although Q3s has flush potential, it plays poorly out of position, especially with such a weak kicker that is easily dominated by the BB's raising range (KQ, QJ, A3, etc.). Given the aggression of the opponent and my positional disadvantage, realizing equity will be difficult. The prudent play with this specific holding against an isolation raise is to fold.

9s 3c is a trash hand: offsuit, low cards, no straight or flush potential, dominated by any playable hand. UTG position requires a tight range to avoid playing out of position post-flop with a weak holding. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament (hand 20/150). No reads from history suggest otherwise; opponents play varied hands but this is unplayable.
78o is a weak hand in early position (UTG+1). With 4 players behind me, the risk of running into a stronger hand is high. This hand plays better in late position or multiway pots. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with [Tc 2h] and action has folded to me. While the Button is an advantageous position to steal the blinds, T2o is an extremely weak hand with very poor post-flop playability. Although I have been folding frequently and might have a tight image, the risk of getting called or 3-bet by the blinds (especially FiveTwo in the SB who has shown aggression) outweighs the potential reward of stealing the 1.5BB pot. The standard and prudent play here is to fold.

Folds to us in the SB, so this is a heads-up blind battle where we can profitably apply pressure with a wide opening range. Even though 5♠3♦ is a weak offsuit hand, the immediate pot is 150 and a small raise risks little while generating fold equity versus BB’s weakest holdings. Using the minimum raise keeps our loss small if Flash responds with a 3-bet (we can comfortably fold), while still denying BB a free realization when we complete.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ac 7d. The action folded to the Small Blind (FiveTwo), who raised to 2x (200 total). In a Blind vs. Blind scenario, a 2x raise represents a wide range of hands. Ac 7d is significantly ahead of an average Small Blind opening range and is too strong to fold. Since I will have position for the remainder of the hand, I will call the additional 100 chips to see a flop. This keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to utilize my positional advantage on later streets.

Heads-up blind battle in a raised pot. Flop gives us a very strong made hand (two pair: 5s and 3s) on a dynamic texture with a hearts flush draw and some straight possibilities (e.g., 4-6, 6-8). We want to start building the pot for value versus plenty of worse hands that will continue (7x, 5x, 3x, overcards with a heart, straight/flush draws) and deny equity rather than giving a free card. A ~75% pot c-bet accomplishes that while keeping stacks deep enough to respond appropriately if raised.

I have top pair with an Ace kicker (Ac 7d) on a 7s 5h 3h board. The Small Blind (FiveTwo) has led out for 300 into a 400 pot. In a Blind vs. Blind battle, top pair is a very strong hand. While the board has heart draws and straight possibilities (4-6, 6-8), raising here might force out the opponent's bluffs or weaker hands that I currently beat. By calling, I maintain a manageable pot size, keep their range wide, and use my positional advantage to see how the turn develops. I am likely ahead of their range, which includes many semi-bluffs or middle/bottom pairs.

Turn brings the 4♣, making the board much more dynamic: any 6x now has a made straight (3-4-5-6-7), 4x picks up a pair + additional equity, and two-heart holdings still have a live flush draw. We still have a strong value hand (two pair) and want to keep building the pot versus worse made hands (7x/5x/3x/4x) and charge draws, rather than giving a free river that could kill action or beat us. A ~2/3 pot barrel accomplishes that while keeping our sizing small enough that we can respond rationally if Flash check-raises (big raises heavily weight 6x/sets).

The 4c on the turn is a coordinated card that completes several possible straights (any hand containing a 6) and improves two-pair combinations (74, 54, 43). However, in a Blind vs. Blind battle, FiveTwo's range remains wide, including many semi-bluffs like heart draws or smaller pairs. With top pair and an Ace kicker, my hand is too strong to fold to a single turn bet, but it is not strong enough to raise for value given the board texture. I will call to see the river, keeping the pot controlled and allowing the opponent to continue bluffing with missed draws.

River K♦ doesn’t change our hand strength much (we still have two pair, 5s+3s), but it does shift Flash’s range: missed hearts are still missed, and some Kx floats (K-high with a heart / backdoors) can improve to top pair. After calling flop and turn, Flash very often has a one-pair type hand (7x/5x/4x) and some draws; the main concern is 6x (made straight on the turn) and occasional sets (77/44). 

Checking risks losing value when Flash checks back a lot of those one-pair hands, while a big bet would fold out the exact hands we want to get paid by and only get action from straights/sets. So I want a thin value bet with a small sizing that gets crying calls from 7x/5x/4x and some Kx, while keeping my bet/fold clean versus a raise (a raise here is heavily weighted to 6x/boats).

In this Blind vs. Blind hand, I have a pair of sevens with an Ace kicker on a highly connected board (3h-5h-7s-4c-Kd). The turn 4c was a dangerous card that completed straights for any hand containing a six, and the river King is an overcard to my pair. However, FiveTwo's river bet of 800 into a 2300 pot offers me roughly 4-to-1 pot odds (needing to be correct about 21% of the time). In a Blind vs. Blind scenario, the opponent's range is wide and contains numerous semi-bluffs that missed on the river, particularly heart draws. While I am losing to a King, a straight, or two-pair, the small sizing of the bet combined with the wide range of missed draws makes a call mathematically profitable over the long run.
A7 suited from UTG in a 5-handed game is a standard open. Suited aces have good playability with flush potential. I'll make a standard raise to 250 (2.5x BB).

I am holding 3h 2d in UTG+1, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. The player in UTG (Claude) has opened with a raise to 250, indicating strength. Calling or raising with 3-2 offsuit in early position against a UTG open would be a significant mistake with negative expected value. The correct and only standard play is to fold immediately.

Facing an UTG open from Claude, 9♠2♠ is too weak to profitably continue even on the Button: it’s dominated when we make a pair, has poor straight potential, and doesn’t bring useful blockers for a light 3-bet. With a healthy stack, we preserve chips and wait for better spots rather than defend this far down versus a strong opening range.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Nine suited, a strong speculative hand. Claude has raised to 250 from UTG. While Claude's range from UTG is typically strong, the pot odds (200 to call into a 400 pot) are favorable enough to see a flop. I am out of position for the rest of the hand, so I will need to play cautiously post-flop, but the potential for hitting a straight or flush justifies the call.

Preflop in the big blind with Q9o facing an UTG raise to 250 from Claude (a frequent raiser but UTG suggests strength: likely premium hands like pairs, AK, AQ+), followed by a fold from FiveTwo and a loose call from Flash in the small blind. My hand is offsuit, non-connected, and dominated by typical calling/raising ranges. Pot odds are ~4:1 (600 pot, 150 to call), but equity against two opponents' ranges is poor (~25-30% vs. raiser strength), and multiway pot postflop is unprofitable with this hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a tournament setting with deep stacks and 130 hands left.

I have flopped middle pair (Tens) on a board with a King. My opponent, Claude, raised from UTG pre-flop, which typically represents a range of strong hands including Kings (AK, KQ, KJ), high pocket pairs, and some suited connectors or Broadways. While I have a piece of the board, I am out of position. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they respond. If Claude bets small, I have enough value to call; if they check back, it gives me a better sense of where I stand without inflating the pot with a marginal hand.

I have Ac7c on Ks Ts 6h - completely missed the flop with just ace high. Flash checked from SB. I have the preflop initiative and position. This board has a flush draw but my clubs don't connect. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot can take this down against Flash's missed hands while keeping the bet size small when I have no equity beyond ace high.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a Nine kicker. Claude, who raised from UTG, has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a 600 pot (1/3 pot). Given the pot odds (4:1), it is too early to fold middle pair. While the King on the board is well within an UTG opening range (AK, KQ, KJ), I have enough equity to see a turn card. I will call the bet and re-evaluate on the turn; if the board becomes more coordinated or Claude continues with heavy aggression, I may have to let the hand go.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a Nine kicker. The turn card, the Four of Hearts, is a brick that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Claude), I will continue with a check-call strategy. Leading out (donk betting) here would likely only get called by better hands and fold out worse ones. By checking, I allow Claude to continue bluffing or to check behind with hands I beat. If Claude bets, I will evaluate the size, but for now, the plan is to check.

I have Ac7c on Ks Ts 6h 4h - complete air with just ace high. Flash has check-called preflop and check-called my flop c-bet, showing some interest in the pot. The turn 4h is a blank but now there's a backdoor heart draw available. With no pair, no draw, and Flash showing continued interest, I should check back to control pot size. Barreling here with ace high against a player who's called twice is burning money. I'll take a free card and reassess the river.

I have second pair (Tens) with a 9 kicker. On the river, the board has paired fours, which doesn't significantly change the hand's value. Claude, the pre-flop aggressor from UTG, bet the flop but checked the turn. This check-back on the turn often suggests either a marginal made hand (like a weak King or a mid-pair like JJ/QQ) or a missed draw. I have decent showdown value here, but betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands and might force me to fold to a raise. Checking is the most prudent play to see a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from missed draws like Ace-high spades.

I have Ac7c on Ks Ts 6h 4h 4c - just ace high. Flash has checked to me on the river after check-calling preflop and the flop. The board paired with 4s which doesn't help me. Betting here would only get called by hands that beat ace high (any king, ten, or pocket pair). I should check back and potentially win at showdown against missed spade draws or other floats. No value in betting air here.

I am in UTG position in a 5-handed game with Q8s. While suited queens have some playability, Q8s is generally below the opening threshold for the first position, even at a short-handed table. There are four players left to act, including active players and large stacks who could 3-bet or call with dominating ranges (KQ, AQ, QJ). Opening here creates a reverse implied odds situation if I hit top pair. Therefore, the disciplined and standard play is to fold.

5-handed from UTG+1 (effectively cutoff), A4o is within a reasonable open range: we block strong Ax, can win blinds often, and benefit from having initiative rather than limping into a multiway pot where our kicker plays poorly. A standard raise size pressures the blinds while keeping risk controlled versus potential 3-bets from the deeper stacks.

I am on the button with Qh4h. FiveTwo has raised from UTG+1 (effectively the cutoff in this 5-handed game) to 2.5x the big blind. While suited queens have some playability, Q4s is too weak to call a raise against an early/middle position range, even with the benefit of position. It's a hand that is easily dominated and lacks significant straight potential. I will wait for a better spot.

Preflop in SB facing an early position raise to 250 (2.5BB) from FiveTwo after UTG fold. Holding K7s suited, which is a speculative hand but marginal for defense out of position. Pot is 400, calling 200 gives pot odds, but OOP multiway potential with BB behind, and FiveTwo shows aggression in history (frequent raises and postflop betting). K7s lacks strength to 3-bet effectively here and is not strong enough for a loose call OOP against EP range. Standard fold to preserve stack and avoid difficult spots postflop.
72o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even getting 150 to win 400, this hand has terrible playability and will rarely make anything strong. Easy fold from the BB.

5-handed UTG, TT is a clear value open. Raising builds the pot versus dominated pairs/broadways, denies equity to overcards, and avoids going multiway out of position with a hand that plays best heads-up. I’ll use a standard ~2.5bb size to pressure blinds and keep worse hands in while still charging calls.

I am holding King-Four offsuit (K4o) in UTG+1. FiveTwo has raised from UTG. K4o is a weak hand to play against an early position raise, and I am out of position relative to several players still to act. This is a standard fold.

On the button with QsTd facing an UTG raise to 250 (2.5BB) from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive in recent hands. QTo offsuit is a marginal hand but plays well multiway due to suited broadway potential and position. UTG opening range is strong (~15-20%: pairs, broadways, suited aces), but button position justifies a wide defending range. Equity ~35% vs typical UTG range, and postflop position allows maneuvering. SB and BB behind may call/3bet light, but pot odds are good (400:250). Prefer calling over folding (too tight deepstacked early) or 3betting (poor blockers, dominated often). Realize equity in position while keeping pot manageable.

I have Ad5d in the SB facing a UTG raise and button call. A5s is a playable hand - suited ace gives me nut flush potential and decent equity in a multiway pot. Getting 3.25:1 on a call of 200 into a 650 pot. This hand plays well multiway with implied odds if I hit a flush or two pair. Calling is the standard play here.

I am in the Big Blind with a low pocket pair (4h 4c). Facing a standard open from UTG and two callers, I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 850, roughly 6.7:1). This is a prime spot for set mining in a multi-way pot. The implied odds are high if I hit a set against 3 opponents. Raising is unnecessary and turns my hand into a bluff, while folding is too tight given the price. I will call to see a flop.

I completely missed this flop with Ad5d. The board is 7c6cJs - all clubs and I have diamonds. I have just ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (need an 8). In a 4-way pot with no made hand and no backdoor flush draw, I should check and see a free turn. Betting into 3 opponents with air is -EV.

I hold pocket Fours (4h 4c) on a flop of 7c 6c Js. This is a multi-way pot with four players. My hand is currently an underpair to the board and vulnerable to many draws and higher pairs. Given the wet board texture and the fact that I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (UTG) and the Button, leading out would be ill-advised. My best course of action is to check and evaluate the action behind me. If there is significant betting, I will likely fold.

Multiway (3 callers) on J-7-6 with two clubs is a poor c-bet spot for TT from OOP: the board connects well with caller ranges (Jx, 98/87/76, club draws) and we’ll get called frequently and sometimes raised, forcing us to inflate a pot with a marginal underpair. TT has solid showdown value and benefits from pot control; checking lets us realize equity, potentially induce bluffs from missed overcards, and keeps worse hands in without committing to a bloated line. We can reassess versus a bet based on sizing and bettor tendencies.

Flop is 7c6cJs, a somewhat coordinated board with two clubs for flush draws and possible straight draws like 78 or 89 for broadway straights. My QsTd is queen-high with no pair, no flush draw (offsuit to board), and no straight draw (gutshot unlikely). Pre-flop, I defended wide in position multiway against UTG raise. Now checked to me on button in 1000-chip pot. UTG (FiveTwo, aggressive) checking first suggests possible weakness or trap, but multiway with deep stacks, betting a pure bluff risks calls from draws or better hands. Queen-high has minimal showdown value long-term. Best to check back, control pot, take free card, and reevaluate turn with position. Bluffing here polarizes range poorly without equity; prefer check to realize thin equity or fold to aggression later.

Turn brought 2s, adding a spade backdoor flush draw to the board. I still have just ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (need an 8). Flop checked through 4-ways, suggesting no one has a monster, but betting into 3 opponents with pure air is too risky. If called, I'm in terrible shape. Check and see a free river - maybe I spike an ace or 8.

I hold a pair of Fours on a board of 7c 6c Js 2s. The flop was checked around by all four players, suggesting nobody has a very strong hand, though draws are likely present. The Turn 2s is a relatively blank card, though it brings a second flush draw. Being out of position in a multi-way pot with an underpair, betting achieves little; better hands like 6x or 7x won't fold, and flush draws won't fold. My hand has marginal showdown value against missed overcards, but not enough to value bet. Checking is the safest play to control the pot size and evaluate action from the players behind me.

Flop checked through multiway on a board that hits callers well, so pot control was best. After two more checks on a total brick (2s), ranges are now heavily weighted to underpairs, 7x/6x, straight/club draws, and some slow-played Jx. A small delayed c-bet here accomplishes a lot: it denies equity to overcards and club draws, can get thin value from worse pairs (7x/6x/88-99), and keeps the pot manageable when we’re behind to Jx. I’ll size down to avoid getting blown off equity and to make calling mistakes common. If I get raised in this multiway spot, I’m folding TT comfortably.

Facing a turn bet of 400 into 1400 from FiveTwo (UTG raiser, aggressive history) after a checked flop on a draw-heavy 7c6cJs2s board. QsTd is queen-high with no pair, no straight draw (no gutshot or open-ender), and only a weak backdoor spade flush draw (3 spades total: Qs + Js + 2s, needs runner-runner). Multiway pot deepstacked (my stack 18.5k, FiveTwo 9k). FiveTwo checked flop (possibly weak/medium strength or trapping strong), now leads turn for ~1/3 pot, which could be thin value (J7/J6/T9 pairs + draw, sets), delayed continuation, or bluff (missed draw). Pot odds ~4:1 (22% equity needed), but realized equity low: queen-high rarely good, position allows floating but risks worse spots river. Raising bluff risky multiway with worse hands behind (Claude/Pro could wake up); calling induces river bets from value and commits us further without improving outs (~6 outs to Q max, blockers poor). Folding preserves stack early in tournament (hand 23/150), exploits position by not bloating pot OOP later, waits for better spots vs aggressive FiveTwo. Standard fold marginal air lacking equity.

FiveTwo bet 400 into a 1000 pot after the flop checked through. I have ace-high with only a gutshot straight draw (need an 8 for ~8.5% equity). Getting 3.5:1 pot odds but need ~22% to call profitably. With no made hand, no flush draw, and weak showdown value, this is a clear fold.

I hold a pair of Fours on a board of 7c 6c Js 2s. The action checked around on the flop, but now the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) has bet 400 into a pot of 1000 on the turn. Although the bet is small, my hand is very weak (an underpair to the board). I am losing to any pair he might have (including lower pairs like 55/66/77) and even against overcards like AK/AQ, I am vulnerable to river cards. Being out of position with little showdown value and only 2 outs to improve to a set, floating here is not profitable. The range of hands I beat is too narrow compared to the hands that beat me.

I am in the UTG position with 9s 5d, which is an extremely weak hand. In early position, I need to play much tighter as there are four players behind me who could have much stronger holdings. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

78o is a speculative offsuit connector. In UTG+1 position in a 5-handed game with deep stacks and multiple players to act (Claude, Pro SB, FiveTwo BB), this hand has poor playability out of position. Standard play is to fold early position trash like this to avoid difficult spots postflop. Table history shows aggression but doesn't justify opening weak offsuit hands here. Preserve stack for better spots.

I have 56o on the button with only the blinds left to act. This is a marginal hand but an excellent position to attempt a steal. Both Flash and Elon have folded, giving me good fold equity against just the blinds. Standard button raise to 250 to try to take it down.

I hold 3d 9h in the Small Blind, facing a raise to 250 from Claude on the Button. This is a very weak hand (93o) and playing it out of position is highly unprofitable. Despite the wide range of a Button raiser, my hand has very poor equity and playability. The pot odds of roughly 3:1 are not sufficient to justify a call with this holding, and a 3-bet bluff is ill-advised given the hand quality. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Button open to 2.5bb in a 5-handed game is wide, and we’re getting a good price closing the action from the BB (150 to win 400). K5o is not strong enough to 3-bet for value and plays poorly in a bloated pot, but it’s a reasonable defend as a call versus this sizing and position, especially with position disadvantage already baked in. Plan postflop: proceed cautiously on K-high boards (often dominated by better Kx) and continue more on favorable textures (paired/low boards) where our range is strong and we can realize equity.

We’re out of position in a single-raised pot versus a wide BTN range on a Q-high dry board. K5o has essentially no equity beyond two overcards to the 5 (and a weak K that’s often dominated when it improves), with no backdoor flush and very limited backdoor straight potential. Leading doesn’t credibly represent much here and just bloats a pot with a hand that can’t stand pressure. Best is to check and respond to sizing: mostly fold versus a standard c-bet, and only consider a very small-stab defense if villain uses a tiny range-bet (still generally a low-frequency continue).

I raised preflop with 56o and got called by FiveTwo in the BB. Flop is Q42 with two spades. I have a gutshot (need a 3) but missed otherwise. FiveTwo checked to me. This is a good c-bet spot - queen-high board often misses BB's calling range, I have position, and a small bet can take it down. Betting around 1/3 pot as a continuation bet.

Claude’s 200 c-bet into 750 is small, but our specific hand (K5o) is near the bottom of our BB defend range on Q-4-2 with no spade, no meaningful backdoor flush, and only a very thin runner-runner straight possibility. Our overcard “outs” are often dirty versus his many Qx/AQ/KQ hands, and calling OOP just to face frequent turn barrels will realize poorly and bleed chips. Best is to give up and preserve stack for better spots.

5-handed game with active players. Position is UTG but in short-handed play, opening range widens to include suited connectors like 98d for strong post-flop playability, straight and flush potential, and good implied odds with deep stacks. Standard open-raise size of 2.5x BB (250 total) to build pot and take initiative. Folding would be too tight; limping poor. Will play post-flop dynamically.

AQo is a premium hand facing a UTG open to 250. This is a standard 3-bet for value. Elon has a big stack at 18300 and has been active. I'll 3-bet to around 750-800 to build the pot with my strong holding and isolate.

I hold 8s 5h on the Button. The action started with an UTG raise to 250, followed by a UTG+1 3-bet to 750. My hand is garbage (offsuit, gapped, low rank) and fares terrible against the strong ranges indicated by early position aggression. With 750 to call, the pot odds do not justify playing this speculative hand. This is a standard fold.

We’re in the small blind with 9♠4♠ offsuit facing an UTG open and a UTG+1 3-bet. Both ranges are strong and we’ll be out of position against at least one player (often two if BB comes along). 9-4o has poor playability, is dominated when it makes a pair, and doesn’t have the suited/connected qualities needed to defend or to justify a 4-bet bluff at these positions. Best EV is to cut losses and preserve stack.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. There was an open-raise from UTG (Elon) followed by a 3-bet from UTG+1 (Claude). K3o is far too weak to continue against two strong early-position ranges, especially out of position. I will fold.

98dd is a speculative suited connector that performs well multi-way or in position with implied odds, but facing a 3-bet from Claude (UTG+1) after my UTG raise leaves me out of position heads-up. Claude's 3-bet range is strong (QQ+, AKo/s, some bluffs possibly), and my equity realization OOP is poor against it (~30-35% max). Pot odds to call 500 into 1650 offer ~3:1, but without strong post-flop playability edge, folding preserves stack for better opportunities in deep-stacked tournament. Raising is overplaying; standard fold.
QJd is a solid suited broadway hand. Even from UTG, this is a standard open. I'll raise to 2.5x (250) to build the pot with my playable hand while maintaining fold equity.

I am in MP (UTG+1) facing a 2.5BB open from the UTG player (Claude). With 5 players left, UTG ranges open up slightly, but A9o is still significantly dominated by a standard UTG opening range (typically AT+, AJ+, AQ+, AK, and pocket pairs). While I would have position on the raiser, A9o has poor playability and reverse implied odds (often dominated when hitting top pair). Calling invites squeezes from players behind, and 3-betting is an overplay. The standard and most profitable line is to fold.

5-handed on the Button, T9o is a reasonable defend versus a 2.5bb UTG open thanks to position and decent playability (straights/two pair potential). A 3-bet here risks running into a stronger continuing range and getting 4-bet off our equity; calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes our positional advantage, and lets us outplay postflop. If a blind squeezes, we can comfortably fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. Claude (UTG) has raised to 250 and FiveTwo (Button) has called. The pot is currently 650, and it costs me 200 more to call. While A8o is a marginal hand to play out of position against an UTG raiser, the pot odds (3.25 to 1) make it a viable call to see a flop. I will need to play cautiously post-flop, especially if an Ace hits, as Claude's UTG raising range likely contains several stronger Aces. Folding is an option to preserve chips, but calling allows me to realize equity in a multi-way pot with a decent starting hand.

Pocket Tens is a premium hand, ranking high against an UTG raise range from Claude, who has shown aggression in prior hands. Two callers (loose Button call from FiveTwo and SB complete from Flash) make a multiway pot likely if I just call, which is undesirable OOP in BB. 3-betting isolates against Claude's strong range while denying equity to speculative calling hands. Sizing to 750 (3x the original raise) balances value, fold equity, and pot control. Excellent implied odds and direct odds (pot 850, call 150), but raising builds a bigger pot OOP with a strong hand. Stacks allow: mine deep at 18k, opponents covered.

QJd is a strong suited broadway hand getting 3:1 pot odds (500 to win 1500). Elon's 3-bet to 750 is a standard sizing. With two callers already and good implied odds if they stay in, QJs plays well multiway with straight/flush potential. Too strong to fold here, but not strong enough to 4-bet OOP. Calling to see a flop.

We’re facing a BB squeeze after an UTG open and an UTG flat of the 3-bet, which heavily tightens the ranges involved (BB is weighted to strong value/strong suited hands; UTG continuing is rarely weak). While the price is tempting (500 more to see a flop in position), T9o is often dominated when we make one-pair, and in a likely multiway 3-bet pot our implied odds and maneuverability drop (lower SPR, more reverse implied odds). A 4-bet bluff here is too high-variance and likely burning chips versus these continuing ranges. Best EV is to cut losses and preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. Claude (UTG) opened, and after FiveTwo and I called, Elon (Big Blind) squeezed to 750. Claude has called the 3-bet. While I am getting 4:1 pot odds (500 to call into a 2000 pot), A8o plays very poorly in a 3-bet multi-way pot, especially out of position. Elon's squeeze from the Big Blind into three players suggests a very strong range (likely TT+, AQo+). If an Ace hits, I will likely be dominated by Elon's or Claude's range. Given the high reverse implied odds and my positional disadvantage for the rest of the hand, I will fold to preserve my stack.

Preflop 3-bet with TT as a premium hand was correct to isolate Claude's UTG range and build the pot, reducing multiway chances OOP. Now heads-up on Ad3sJs flop, which is very wet and hits Claude's strong calling range hard (Ax, AJs, KQs, broadways). TT is an underpair to A/J, vulnerable to top pair or better, with two spades for flush draw potential. OOP first to act, no strong reason to bet into this range for value or protection, as he can easily have showdown value or better. Pot control and realizing equity by checking is optimal; prepared to check-fold to aggression or check-call lighter if he bets air/missed draws. Stacks deep (17.5k effective vs his 12.6k), but don't want to bloat pot OOP with a marginal hand here.

I flopped middle pair (jacks) with queen kicker on Ad 3s Js. Elon 3-bet preflop and checked the flop. His check is concerning - could be slowplaying AA, AK, AJ, or checking back with KK/QQ that don't love the ace. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value against his 3-bet range. If he has an ace I'm crushed. Check for pot control and see what develops.

Turn card Qs completes the spade flush draw (3s-Js-Qs) and opens up Broadway straight possibilities (e.g., KTs, ATs, KQs in Claude's UTG 3-bet calling range). The board is now extremely draw-heavy and coordinated, heavily favoring his strong preflop calling range (Ax, AJs+, KQs, broadways, sets). Pocket TT remains a non-improving underpair with no flush or straight draw, vulnerable to any pair, flush, or straight. Claude's flop check-back suggests he may not hold a strong top pair (A/J) but could have medium strength, backdoor draws, or checking to pot control. OOP first to act with 2000 pot and deep effective stacks (12.6k Claude), betting thin value or bluffing is poor: value bets get called by better, bluffs face sticky range. Optimal is to check for pot control, realize showdown equity cheaply, and induce bluffs from missed draws or weak hands while prepared to fold to significant aggression.

The turn Qs gives me two pair (Queens and Jacks) - a significant improvement. However, the board now has three spades (3s Js Qs), making flush draws a concern. Elon 3-bet preflop but has checked both flop and turn, which is curious - either he has a middling hand like KK that hates this board, a weak ace, or he's slowplaying. My two pair is strong but vulnerable to the flush completing. I should bet for value and protection against flush draws. Betting around 50% pot (1000) targets worse hands while charging draws.

Facing a 1000 bet into 3000 pot on a Qs turn that completes the spade flush draw and broadway straights on an already coordinated Ad3sJs board. Pocket TT is a non-nutted underpair with no draws, OOP heads-up vs Claude's strong UTG 3-bet calling range (QQ+, AKo/AJs+, KQs, sets). His flop check-back suggested possible medium strength or pot control, but turn bet after Qs hits his range hard (AQs/AJs flushes, KQs straight/flush, ATs/KTs flush, AQ/KQ pairs+draws). Pot odds ~3:1 but poor implied odds OOP with reverse implied disaster if behind (vulnerable to 1-2 outer); calling bloats pot with marginal equity (~25% vs polarized range) and no fold equity if raise. Consistent with pre-turn plan: check-fold to aggression on scary board to preserve deep stack (17.5k) early in tournament (hand 26/150). Folding realizes EV by avoiding spew.

I am in the UTG position in a 5-handed game, which plays similarly to the Hijack in a full-ring game. My hand, King-Jack offsuit (KJo), is a standard opening hand from this position given the deep stacks (111 BB). Opening allows me to potentially steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds (Elon and Claude, who act last pre-flop but first post-flop). While KJo can be dominated, my tight image and the positional advantage against the deep-stacked blinds make opening a +EV play. I will raise to 2.5x BB to assert initiative.

We’re 5-handed but still facing an UTG open to 2.5bb from Pro, and we’re UTG+1 with Q5o. This hand is heavily dominated by Pro’s UTG range (better queens, better kickers), plays poorly multiway with players left to act, and has significant reverse implied odds when we make a weak pair. With no suitedness or connectivity to compensate, there’s no profitable call or 3-bet frequency here. Best decision is to preserve stack and fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. Pro has raised from UTG, which typically indicates a strong opening range. J7o is a weak hand that does not play well against an UTG open, even with the benefit of position. Calling would likely put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff with this specific hand is suboptimal given the lack of blockers or connectivity. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

Pocket Aces is the best starting hand in Hold'em, dominating Pro's UTG raising range which includes pairs, broadways, and suited connectors but rarely AA itself. With FiveTwo and Flash folding, the field is narrowed. As SB, I'm OOP post-flop, so I 3-bet to isolate Pro, build the pot with equity advantage, charge draws, and apply fold equity. BB (Claude) may defend wide but stacks are deep (my 17.5k, Pro 10.9k). Sizing to 900 (3.6x the raise) is standard aggression: large enough to pressure without overcommitting early. Calling is too passive, folding insane.
K2o is a weak holding facing a 3-bet. Elon 3-bet to 900 from SB over a UTG open, indicating strength. K2o plays poorly against a 3-betting range and has poor playability post-flop. Clear fold.

I opened KJo from UTG and am facing a large 3-bet (to 900) from the Small Blind (Elon). Although I would have position post-flop, KJo is significantly dominated by a standard SB vs UTG 3-bet range (which includes AK, AQ, JJ+, KQs). The price to call is 650 chips into a pot that will be 1900, requiring about 34% equity, which KJo struggles to realize against a strong range. Elon has shown aggression previously, but defending KJo here is generally -EV due to reverse implied odds and lack of playability. Folding is the disciplined decision.

5-handed UTG plays much wider than full ring; QJo is strong enough to open for value and initiative. Folding would be too tight, and limping invites multiway pots where QJ offsuit is easily dominated and hard to realize equity. I’ll use a standard open size that fits the table’s prior sizing (most opens 2.5–3x) to pressure blinds and keep my range consistent.

I am holding 97 offsuit in UTG+1. FiveTwo has raised from UTG. 97o is far too weak to call a raise from early position, especially with three active players behind me. Folding is the standard play here to preserve my stack.

Preflop on the button facing an UTG raise to 250 (2.5BB) from FiveTwo, who has shown aggression in prior hands but from early position likely has a strong range (top ~10-15%). My 9c4c is a suited connector but very weak overall, with low playability multiway and poor equity (~25-30% heads-up at best) against a tight opening range. Pot odds to call are ~27% (250 to win 650 post-call), but position advantage doesn't compensate for the hand's weakness, especially with SB (Claude) and BB (Pro) yet to act who may call/raise. Folding preserves my deep 179BB stack for better spots.
5T offsuit is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. Poor playability and I'm out of position. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Q2o facing a 2.5bb raise from FiveTwo in UTG. FiveTwo has been active, but UTG raises still command some respect regarding range strength. Even though I am getting decent pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot that will be 550, roughly 27% equity needed), Q2o performs very poorly against an opening range, is easily dominated, and plays terrible out of position. The reverse implied odds are high if I hit a Queen. It is a standard fold.

I have been dealt Ace-King offsuit (Ac Kh) in the Under-the-Gun (UTG) position. This is a premium starting hand, especially in a five-handed game. I will make a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind to build the pot and thin the field. Given my stack size and the strength of my hand, I am happy to play for a larger pot.

9d6s is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against a UTG raiser's range. Facing an early position raise from Flash to 3x BB, folding from UTG+1 is standard to preserve stack and avoid difficult postflop spots OOP. No reads suggest Flash is overly loose, and with deep stacks, speculative hands like this aren't justified.

8h5s is a weak offsuit hand. Flash raised from UTG which typically represents a stronger range. While I have position on the button, this hand doesn't play well postflop - it's not suited, not connected enough, and easily dominated. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Ten offsuit (KTo). The player in UTG (Flash) has raised to 300 (3BB). This indicates a potentially strong range given the early position open. My hand, while containing high cards, is easily dominated by a standard UTG opening range (which includes AK, KQ, KJ, AA-88). Furthermore, I would be playing the entire hand out of position against the aggressor, and calling invites the Big Blind to come along or squeeze. The pot odds are not attractive enough to compensate for the poor playability and reverse implied odds of this hand. Therefore, folding is the prudent decision.

Facing a 3x open from UTG, K2o is near the bottom of defendable BB hands. Even getting a reasonable price, this hand realizes equity poorly out of position and is frequently dominated when we make top pair (reverse implied odds), especially versus an early-position range. With no strong postflop playability, the higher-EV line is to preserve chips and fold.

UTG with Tc3d, a complete trash hand lacking connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. In early position against 4 opponents with deep stacks, this hand has zero equity for opening. Standard fold to avoid spew and preserve stack for better spots in a tournament setting where position and hand selection are crucial.

I have pocket 2s in UTG+1 with Elon already folding. Small pocket pairs have set-mining value with deep stacks (144 BBs). Standard play is to open raise to ~2.5x to either take down blinds or play postflop with set potential. Limping is weak and folding is too tight for a pocket pair at this stack depth.

I hold 4h 2h on the Button. While suited, this is a very weak hand (low one-gapper). Facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG+1 (early/middle position), my hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify a call, even in position. The implied odds are not sufficient to overcome the frequency with which this hand misses or makes a second-best hand. Folding is the standard and most profitable play.

We’re 100bb deep in the small blind with KK versus an UTG+1 open in a 5-handed game, with the big blind still to act. This is a mandatory 3-bet for value: we want to build a pot against worse (QQ/JJ/TT, AK, some AQ) and deny equity/initiative while avoiding a multiway pot OOP. A larger sizing is appropriate from the SB; raising to 900 pressures Claude’s opening range and makes it costly for BB to peel, while still allowing dominated hands to continue and giving BB room to jam worse. If Claude 4-bets, we’re happy to continue (often shove) given stack depths and how strong KK is here.

I am in the Big Blind with Q7o. Claude opened from UTG+1, and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Small Blind. Q7o is a weak hand that is significantly behind the ranges of an early position opener and a 3-better. Given the stack sizes and the strength shown by both opponents, there is no reason to put any more chips into this pot. I will fold.

Facing a 3-bet to 900 with pocket 2s. I need to call 650 into 1250 (about 2:1). With 22, I'm purely set-mining - hitting a set ~12% of the time. Stack-to-pot ratio after calling would be ~7:1 with about 8100 behind for FiveTwo. Implied odds are borderline, but being OOP with the worst pocket pair against a 3-bet from SB (typically strong range) makes this a marginal spot. The 3-bet sizing is substantial and I'll be playing guessing games without a set. Fold is the disciplined play here.
Q2o is a weak hand, especially from UTG position. Even at a 5-handed table, opening Q2 offsuit from first position is too loose. I should fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG+1 (effectively Cutoff since UTG folded) with Kd 2d. In a 5-handed game, King-low suited is a marginal hand. While suited kings have some playability, opening from early/middle position is loose and risks domination by better kings or being 3-bet by the button or blinds. Given the stack depths and the fact that I have aggressive players behind me, the standard and disciplined play is to fold.

With both UTG players folded, this is a button steal spot, but 6♢3♥ is too far down the distribution: it has very poor playability when either blind defends and we’ll often face squeezes/isolations (especially from the big stack in the BB). The raise needs a high fold rate to show immediate profit, and against two competent blinds that’s unlikely. Limping is also unattractive because it invites the BB to attack and we realize equity poorly. Best is to pass and keep our stack for better steal candidates.

It's folded to me in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. While I only need to commit 50 more chips to see a flop, this hand is quite weak and I will be out of position against Elon, who is the chip leader, for the remainder of the hand. I'll choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 3d 2c (3-2 offsuit) in the UTG position. This is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em, with no high card strength, no flush potential, and very poor straight connectivity. Being in early position (UTG) with over 100 big blinds, there is absolutely no incentive to play this hand. Opening the pot would be a significant mistake, and limping is equally bad. The standard and only correct play is to fold immediately.

5-handed and first in after UTG folds, but J3 offsuit is too weak to open from UTG+1: poor high-card strength, dominated when we make a jack, and no suited/connected playability. Limping is also unprofitable and invites isolation from position. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am on the button with 9-4 suited. While suited cards have some value, 9-4 is a very weak hand with poor connectivity. Given that both Elon and Claude in the blinds have shown aggression in previous hands, I will fold this hand rather than attempting a steal with a sub-optimal range.

Folded to me in the small blind with K6 suited, a speculative hand with good playability due to suited connectors-ish nature and king high. Deep stacks (179BB), no action before me, so excellent fold equity against Claude's BB. Raising steals blinds often or builds pot when called, and I can play post-flop OOP with flush draw potential or king value. Standard size ~3x BB (300 total) to balance value/steal.

I have pocket 4s in the BB facing a min-raise from Elon in the SB. Small pocket pairs play well as set-mining hands. With deep stacks (140+ BB effective), I have good implied odds to call and try to flop a set. Folding is too tight, and 3-betting 44 isn't necessary here.

Heads-up on a drawy flop Ts6d9d after my SB steal-raise with K6cc. I have middle pair (6s) with strong K kicker, which blocks some of villain's value hands like K6/K9. Board has diamond flush draw and straight draws (78, T7, 87), so protection/value bet is warranted. As preflop raiser OOP, my range is perceived stronger; c-bet to continue representing value, deny equity to draws, and build pot. Half-pot size balances thin value vs floats/draws without overcommitting deep stacks (~170BB effective). Folding equity against Claude's BB calling range, which includes Ax, suited broadways, pairs, connectors. If raised, can evaluate based on sizing/range.

Missed my set on T96dd board. My pocket 4s are an underpair with no real equity - no diamond draw, no straight draw. The board is coordinated and Elon's c-bet suggests he has at least some piece or draw. Calling to float makes no sense without equity or position. Folding is the disciplined play here.

5-handed but still first to act and will be out of position versus multiple opponents. 32o has extremely poor equity, is dominated when it makes a pair, and realizes equity badly OOP. Limping invites isolation; raising is burning chips with little fold equity against blinds/button. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in UTG+1 with 9d 2c, which is a very weak starting hand. Given the table dynamics and my position, there is no strategic value in playing this hand. Folding is the most prudent action to conserve my stack.

On the button with QsTd after two folds (UTG and UTG+1), this is a prime stealing spot. QTo offsuit plays well multiway or heads-up due to position, straight potential, and domination potential. Raising builds the pot, isolates weaker blind ranges, or steals dead money. Standard sizing ~3x BB in this deep stack unopened pot. Minraise too small, so pot-committing 300 total.

I have 7h9h, a suited connector in the small blind facing a button raise to 300. While I'm out of position, suited connectors have good playability and implied odds. The pot is offering me 450:250 odds. I'll call and look to hit a straight, flush, or two pair type hand on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five offsuit (Jh 5d). The Button (Elon) raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Claude) called. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 to call, offering pot odds of 3.5:1 (requiring ~22% equity). However, J5o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially in a multi-way pot where I will be out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. The hand lacks flush potential and has poor straight connectivity. My equity realization will be low, and I am easily dominated. Despite the attractive price, the correct long-term play with this specific holding is to fold.

I flopped middle pair (9s) with 97hh on Q94 board. I have a backdoor flush draw with one heart on board. Against Elon's button raise range, checking makes sense - I can call a reasonable c-bet or check back and realize equity. Leading out would put me in an awkward spot if raised, and my hand has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to build a big pot.

Top pair with Ten kicker on a dry rainbow flop (Q-9-4). Board favors my preflop raising range significantly, as it misses much of SB's calling range (which includes broadway cards, pocket pairs below QQ, suited connectors). Claude checked, giving me free opportunity to c-bet for value against worse Qx (KQ, AQ unlikely given preflop call sizing), floating Ax, and to charge gutshots/draws (T8, JTs type). Position allows control postflop. Pot 700, betting ~half pot builds it without overexposing to check-raises from sets (unlikely). Avoids check-back realizing equity.

I have middle pair (9s) with a backdoor heart draw. Elon bet 400 into 700, a standard c-bet sizing. I'm getting 1100:400 (2.75:1) which is good odds with a pair. My hand has showdown value against his wide button range that includes many overcards and bluffs. Folding is too weak, raising bloats the pot unnecessarily. Calling keeps pot manageable and lets me evaluate the turn.

Elon checked behind on the turn after c-betting the flop. This shows weakness - he likely doesn't have a strong Queen or overpair. My middle pair of 9s is probably good here. I could bet for thin value but checking keeps the pot controlled and lets me evaluate the river. If I bet and get raised, I'm in a tough spot. Checking allows me to either call a river bet or value bet myself depending on action.

Turn card 6d is a blank for most draws; possible str8 draws like T7s,75s, but low equity and unlikely vs my range. Board Q-9-6-4 rainbow, top pair T kicker very strong, ahead of Qx worse kicker (AJs,KQs less likely preflop), 9x, lower pairs, Ax floats. Claude double-checked, capping range lower; unlikely sets (QQ,99,44 preflop calls thin). Value bet half pot extracts from worse, denies equity to draws. Pot control secondary with position/strong hand. Sizing 800 maintains pressure, pot-committing him if behind.

I have middle pair of 9s with 97hh on Q946 board. Elon raised pre, c-bet flop 400, and now double-barreling 800 on turn. This shows consistent aggression. Getting ~3.9:1 pot odds (3100:800), but my hand is just a bluff catcher against his range which includes Qx, overpairs, and some draws. Calling puts me in a tough river spot where he'll likely barrel again with value and I can't improve much. Middle pair weak kicker facing double barrel OOP is a fold.

Holding K9s UTG in a 5-handed game is a standard opening hand. With a stack of 68 BBs, I have enough depth to play this suited King profitably. I will raise to 300 (3x) to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or play a controlled pot in position if called.

J4s is a marginal suited connector/gapper with some post-flop playability due to suitedness and straight potential, but facing an early position (UTG) raise from Flash, whose range is likely strong (top ~15-20% like pairs, broadways, suited aces, etc.). I'm in UTG+1, which is still early position, meaning out of position post-flop against the raiser. Pot odds are 450:300 (~1.5:1), but implied odds are poor OOP with a weak hand that rarely makes strong hands and is often dominated. Flash's stack (6525) is medium, mine deep (19050), but no need to speculate here when better spots abound in a long tournament (hand 34/150). Folding preserves stack and avoids tough OOP decisions.
Holding 3s7h on the button facing a UTG raise of 300. This is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity. Even with position, calling or raising here against a UTG open is -EV. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Four offsuit (K4o). The player in Under-The-Gun (UTG) has raised to 300. An UTG opening range is typically strong, often consisting of pocket pairs, strong broadways, and suited connectors. K4o is poorly playable, especially out of position against a strong range. I am dominated by many of the hands in their range (AK, KQ, KJ, KT, KTs, etc.). Calling would be a losing play in the long run, and 3-betting as a bluff is too risky given the position and hand strength. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Flash’s UTG 3bb open (5-handed) represents a reasonably strong range, and 2h4h is at the very bottom of suited connectors. Although the pot odds (200 to win 650 if I call) look close, being out of position with a hand that realizes equity very poorly makes this a negative-EV defend. A 3-bet bluff is also unattractive with this specific combo due to poor playability when called. Best decision is to cut losses and fold.

T5o is a complete trash hand from UTG in a 5-handed game. It has no high card strength, poor playability post-flop, and is easily dominated. Even with deep stacks (190BB), raising would require too wide a range and bleed chips. Limping is weak and invites multi-way pots where this hand performs poorly. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.
9c2c is a very weak hand, especially from UTG+1. Suited but with no connectivity and poor high card value. Easy fold from early position.

The action has folded to me on the Button in a 5-handed game. I hold A5o, which is within the standard opening range for the Button, aiming to steal the blinds. With only the Small Blind and Big Blind remaining, and having position post-flop if called, raising is the optimal play to capitalize on fold equity and the positional advantage. A standard open size of 2.5x big blind (250 chips) puts pressure on the blinds while not committing too many chips with a marginal hand.

We’re in the small blind, out of position, versus a Button open. AKo is a clear value 3-bet here: it dominates the Button’s opening range, plays much better with initiative, and we also want to discourage the big blind from coming along and realizing equity cheaply. With ~100bb effective, a larger OOP sizing is appropriate; raising to 900 (9bb) pressures the opener, builds a pot for value, and sets up straightforward decisions versus a 4-bet.

I am in the Big Blind with KQo. The Button (Pro) opened for 2.5bb, and the Small Blind (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 9bb. While KQo is a strong hand in a vacuum, it is often dominated by the 3-betting range of an aggressive Small Blind, especially one who has shown significant activity like FiveTwo. Calling 800 more (roughly 11.6% of my stack) would put me in a difficult spot post-flop out of position against a 3-bettor and potentially the original opener. Given the stack sizes and the 3-bet, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chips for better opportunities.

I opened to 2.5bb from the Button with A5o, a standard steal attempt. The Small Blind (FiveTwo) has 3-bet to 9bb (900 chips), a sizable raise (3.6x my open). While I have position, A5o is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a 3-betting range (which usually includes AQ+, ATs+, 88+). I am getting roughly 2:1 pot odds, requiring ~33% equity to call profitably. Against a standard strong 3-bet range, my equity is often lower (around 28-30%), and the reverse implied odds are significant if an Ace flops. Furthermore, FiveTwo has shown capability to 3-bet previously (Hand #30), suggesting they aren't afraid to play back, but without a suited connector or a stronger Ace, continuing is negative EV. Preserving the stack of >100bb is prioritizing over a high-variance spot.

I have 9c8c suited connectors in UTG. While UTG is typically a tight position, suited connectors have good playability post-flop with straight and flush potential. With 132 BBs, I can afford to open raise and play this hand. Standard open to 2.5x.

I hold Pocket Sevens (7c 7d) in UTG+1 facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG. With effective stacks over 100BB (10,350 for me, 12,975 for Claude), I have excellent implied odds to call and 'set mine'. The 'Call 20' rule suggests I need about 20x the call amount in effective stacks (250 * 20 = 5000), which I easily satisfy. Folding 77 to a single standard raise is too tight given the depth. 3-betting is an option but isolates me against a stronger range or invites 4-bets. A flat call allows me to realize my equity cheaply and potentially win a large pot if I hit a set on the flop, while being able to fold easily on dangerous boards if I miss.

UTG open is from a typically strong range, and the cold call behind further weights the field toward hands that dominate J8o (AJ/KJ/QJ, JT, big pairs). Even on the button, calling invites reverse implied odds in a multiway pot with an offsuit, non-connected hand that flops dominated top pairs and weak draws. A squeeze is also poor because we block very little and will get called/4-bet too often. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket eights. Claude raised from UTG and Pro called. Pocket eights is a strong hand but difficult to play out of position if I 3-bet and get called by either opponent. Calling the 200 allows me to see a flop cheaply, look for a set, and evaluate the board texture. The pot odds are favorable (200 to win 650), and my stack is healthy enough to set-mine.

A♠T♣ is a premium hand that dominates much of Claude's UTG opening range (e.g., Ax weaker, KQ, suited connectors) and the calling ranges of Pro and Flash, who have shown loose tendencies from hand history. Preflop pot odds are favorable (150 to call into ~1000 pot), but calling OOP multiway risks difficult postflop spots if we miss (61% equity needed long-term). 3-betting leverages fold equity against a capped range, isolates vs weaker aces/pairs, and builds a pot with our equity advantage (~65-75% vs calling range). Deep stacks (18k effective) support aggression. Sizing to 900 (3.6x the raise, accounting for two callers) pressures without overcommitting.

Facing a 3-bet from Elon to 900. I have 9c8c suited connectors. Pot is 1650, need 650 to call - getting about 2.5:1 odds. With Pro and Flash still in, this could be a multiway pot with good implied odds. Suited connectors play well multiway. My stack is healthy at ~130BB. Calling is reasonable here given pot odds and playability, but folding is also fine given my OOP position. I'll call and see a flop - if I miss, I can get away cheaply.

I am facing a squeeze to 900 from the Big Blind after originally calling a raise to 250. The UTG player has already called the squeeze, creating a multi-way pot. The price to call is an additional 650 chips. With a stack of over 10,000, the call represents about 6.5% of my remaining stack, giving me an effective stack-to-call ratio of roughly 15:1. While pure set-mining usually prefers 20:1, the excellent pot odds (over 3.5:1) and the fact that I will have absolute position on all opponents (SB, BB, and UTG) post-flop make this a profitable call. My plan is to 'fit or fold'—looking to hit a set on the flop to crack big pairs held by the raisers, or folding if I miss and face aggression.

I am in the Small Blind holding pocket eights. After my initial call, Elon (Big Blind) 3-bet squeezed to 900, and both Claude and Pro called the raise. The pot is now 2950, and it costs me 650 to call. I am getting roughly 4.5-to-1 immediate pot odds. Given that there are three other players in the pot and we have significant stack depths behind, the implied odds for set-mining are excellent. Pocket eights play well in multi-way pots if they flop a set. Folding would be too tight given the price, and 4-betting into three opponents (one of whom showed strength with a squeeze) is unnecessarily risky. I will call to see the flop.

I am first to act on a flop of Qc-9h-6h in a 4-way pot. My pocket eights are currently an underpair to the Queen and the Nine. Given that Elon showed significant strength with a pre-flop squeeze and there are two other opponents in the hand, leading out into this field is extremely risky and unlikely to result in a fold from better hands or a call from worse hands. The board is also wet with straight and flush draws. My plan is to check and see how the action develops behind me. If there is a bet and a call, I will likely have to fold, but checking allows me to potentially see a cheap turn or evaluate the strength of my opponents' ranges based on their betting patterns.

Preflop 3-bet with ATs (AsTc) gave us initiative and a strong perceived range (AA, AK, AQ, sets). Flop 6h-Qc-9h is somewhat dry but hits calling ranges with top pair (Qx, 9x), straight draws (JTs, T8s), and flush draws (hearts). We have ace-high with no pair/draw, but excellent fold equity against Flash's weak check (SB calling range often weak), Pro's loose calling history, and Claude's UTG range which misses frequently (e.g., AJo+, KQs). Multiway pot (3600) with deep effective stacks (Flash shortest at ~6k). Half-pot c-bet (~1800) charges draws, targets floats/marginal hands, and maintains pressure without overcommitting. If called/raised, we have outs (A, runner-runner str8), blockers, and can shut down. Checking risks giving free cards to draws and losing initiative OOP. Long-term EV positive aggression in positionally advantaged spot post-3bet.

I flopped middle pair (9s) with 8 kicker on 6h Qc 9h. Elon 3-bet preflop and is now betting 1800 into 3 opponents - this is a very strong line. His 3-bet range heavily includes hands like QQ, AA, KK, AQ, KQ - many of which have me crushed here. With the Q on board, he likely has top pair or an overpair. I only have 5 outs to improve (two 9s, three 8s for trips/two pair). Getting 4:1 but I'm likely way behind. Folding is correct - middle pair weak kicker OOP against a 3-bettor continuing strong multiway.

I called the pre-flop squeeze with Pocket Sevens purely to set-mine, adhering to a 'fit or fold' strategy. The flop comes Queen-Nine-Six, missing my set completely and presenting two overcards to my pair. The pre-flop aggressor (Elon) is betting half-pot (1800) into three opponents, signaling strength likely in the form of a Queen, an overpair, or a strong draw. With only two outs to improve and a player still left to act behind me, I do not have the equity to continue. My hand is effectively dead against Elon's likely range (AQ, KK, AA, KQ, JJ, TT). Therefore, I must fold.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket eights on a board of Qc-9h-6h. Elon, who squeezed from the Big Blind pre-flop, has led out with a half-pot bet of 1800 into three opponents. This show of strength into a multi-way field, combined with his pre-flop squeeze, strongly suggests a range of AA, KK, QQ, or AQ. My pocket eights are now an underpair and are likely behind. While I am getting 4-to-1 odds to call, the board is very coordinated with straight and flush draws, and calling would leave me with only 4175 chips in a 7200 pot, making it extremely difficult to play the turn profitably without improving. Given the strength Elon has shown, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my remaining stack.

I am in UTG position with 6d2h, which is an extremely weak hand (bottom 5% of range). With 5 players at the table and 4 opponents left to act, the probability of someone having a better hand is very high. There is no strategic merit in limping or raising with this holding given the stack depth (94.5 BB) and tournament structure. Folding is the only standard play.

We’re effectively first in from early position in a 5-handed game and facing only the blinds. 6♠3♥ offsuit has very poor playability (dominated top pairs, weak draws, reverse implied odds) and doesn’t justify entering the pot without position or initiative. With a healthy 105BB stack, the best EV line is to preserve chips and wait for stronger open/steal candidates.

I am on the button with 4h 5d, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have positional advantage, both the small blind (Elon) and the big blind (Claude) have been active and aggressive throughout the session. As the shortest stack at the table, I want to avoid chip-bleeding on marginal hands and look for better opportunities to commit my chips. Folding here is the most prudent play.

Folded to me in the small blind with 87o, a speculative hand with straight potential and decent playability. Positionally, this is a standard spot to raise and attempt to steal the blinds or play postflop from in position if called. BB stack is 122BB deep, and my stack is even deeper at 217BB, allowing for implied odds if we connect. History shows passive limping but as an aggressive pro player with the biggest stack, raising small maintains initiative and builds the pot profitably. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) is a good size to get folds or build pot.

7d3d is a very weak hand, even suited. Elon min-raised from SB and has been aggressive throughout. While pot odds are ~2.3:1 (need 30% equity), 73s plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an aggressive big stack. Folding is the disciplined play here.

5-handed UTG ranges widen significantly, and A3o is strong enough to open as the aggressor but not a great hand to limp/call and go multiway out of position. The ace gives good blocker value versus opponents’ strongest 3-bet/call ranges, and raising pressures the blinds and keeps initiative. If we face a large 3-bet from later position we can comfortably fold given dominated-ace risk and poor playability.

I am in UTG+1 with Q3o, which is a very weak hand. FiveTwo has opened from UTG with a standard raise. Given my position and the strength of my hand relative to an early position opener, I have no reason to enter this pot. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

82o is one of the worst hands in poker, completely uncoordinated, offsuit, low cards with poor playability and dominated by any raising range. FiveTwo's UTG raise to 2.5BB indicates a strong range. On the button with deep stacks, but this hand has zero equity against a calling range from blinds or value. Standard fold to avoid spew, preserve stack for better spots. Goal is long-term stack maximization over 150 hands.

I have 9s8s suited connectors in the SB facing a min-raise from FiveTwo. This is a playable speculative hand with good straight and flush potential. Getting decent pot odds (200 to win 400) and the hand plays well postflop. Calling is the standard play here - not strong enough to 3-bet but too good to fold.

I hold Pocket Sixes (6c 6h) in the Big Blind facing a raise to 2.5bb from UTG and a call from the Small Blind. The pot odds are excellent: I need to call 150 chips into a pot that will be 750 total (including my call), giving me 5:1 odds (20% equity required). Pocket pairs are ideal for calling in this spot to 'set mine,' especially given the deep stacks (over 90bb effective), which offer massive implied odds if I hit a set against UTG's likely strong range or the SB's calling range. Squeezing (3-betting) is risky out of position with a small pair that plays poorly post-flop without improvement. Calling is the most profitable low-variance play.

I completely missed this flop with 9s8s - no pair, no flush draw (board has diamonds, I have spades), and no straight draw. The Ace-high board is unfavorable for my hand. With nothing and an Ace on board that likely hit someone, I should check and give up if there's action.

I have flopped a set of Sixes on a board of [6d Ah 2d]. This is a very strong hand. The presence of the Ace connects heavily with the UTG aggressor's range (likely AK, AQ, AJ), making it very probable that FiveTwo will make a continuation bet. Leading out (donk betting) would likely cause weaker hands to fold and prevents the aggressor from putting money in with bluffs or value bets. The optimal play is to check to the raiser to trap them. If they bet, I can look to check-raise to build the pot against strong Ax hands and flush draws, extracting maximum value.

We’re multiway as UTG raiser on A♠-high (A♥ 6♦ 2♦), a board that strongly favors our range. With A♦3♥ we have top pair but a weak kicker, so we want thin value/protection versus worse aces, 6x/2x, and diamond draws, while keeping the pot controlled to avoid getting blown off our equity by a check-raise line. Holding the A♦ is a big plus (removes the nut flush draw from opponents), making a small range c-bet higher EV. A ~1/3 pot sizing accomplishes all of this and keeps our turn decisions flexible.

I have 9s8s on a 6d-Ah-2d board. I completely missed - no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), and a gutshot at best with the 7 giving me a straight. The Ace-high board favors the preflop raiser. With essentially no equity and facing a c-bet, folding is the clear play.

I have flopped bottom set on a board that contains an Ace and a flush draw.
