This texture is excellent for me because the UTG opener's range is heavily weighted towards Ax hands (AK, AQ, AJ) which will find it very hard to fold to a raise. Additionally, there is a flush draw present, so I want to charge any diamond draws rather than giving a cheap card. The opponent bet 250 (1/3 pot), a standard c-bet size. By check-raising, I start building a big pot for value while my equity is robust. Calling would under-represent my hand but risks a scare card killing the action or a flush completing. A raise to 850 gets value from Top Pair and charges draws appropriately.

Pro’s check-raise on A♥6♦2♦ can certainly be strong (A6s/A2s/sets), but in the BB he also has plenty of semi-bluffs (diamond draws, 34, some A-x “protection” raises) and he waited until it was heads-up (after Claude folded) to spring it, which increases the bluff/probe frequency. With A♦3♥ we have top pair plus the A♦ blocks the nut flush draws, and we’re getting a good price: 600 to win 2450 (~24.5%). Raising would just isolate ourselves versus his strongest value range and fold out the bluffs we want to keep. Best is to call and re-evaluate turns (continue happily on any diamond; be cautious on bricks versus big barrels).

I hold a set of Sixes on a board of 6d Ah 2d Qh. The turn card brings a Queen and a second flush draw (hearts) to the board. This is an action card that likely connects with the UTG opener's range (improving AQ to two pair, giving Qx diamond draws a pair). After check-raising the flop and getting called, my opponent's range is defined as strong Ax hands (AK, AJ, AQ), sets, or strong flush draws. Checking here is risky as it gives a free card to two different flush draws and allows Ax hands to check back for pot control. I need to bet to extract value from worse hands and charge the draws while the board is still relatively safe. A bet of 1600 (approx 65% pot) builds the pot effectively, setting up an SPR of roughly 1.2 for a river shove (remaining stack ~6750 into a potential pot of ~5650).

We have A♦3♥ on A♥6♦2♦Q♥ facing a flop check-raise and now a substantial turn barrel. Key point: with A♦ in hand and two diamonds on board, we still hold the nut flush draw in addition to top pair (weak kicker).

Pot is 4050 and it costs 1600 to continue, so we need ~28% equity. Even versus a very value-heavy range (sets/two-pair), we often have ~9–10 diamond outs to the nuts on the river (~20–22%), plus we’ll also be ahead of his semi-bluffing range (diamond draws/straight draws) a meaningful amount of the time. Given his line can still contain plenty of continuing draws and we’re in position, calling realizes our equity well.

Raising (especially to put him in) mostly folds out the bluffs we want to keep and isolates us versus hands that crush our one-pair portion, so the best play is to call and re-evaluate river (happy on diamonds; cautious versus big shove on bricks).

I hold a set of Sixes on a board of [6d Ah 2d Qh 9c]. I have taken a very strong line: check-raising the flop and barreling the turn. The river 9c is a blank that misses the flush draws (diamonds and hearts). My opponent (FiveTwo) has called a check-raise and a large turn bet, narrowing their range to strong Ax (AK, AQ, AJ), sets (AA, QQ), or stubborn flush draws. 

I lose only to AA and QQ (6 combos total). I beat AQ (9 combos), AK (12 combos), and AJ (12 combos). Given the missed draws on the board, my All-In shove (roughly 1.2x pot) looks extremely polarized—representing either the nuts or a busted flush draw bluff. This polarization maximizes the chance of getting a 'hero call' from top pair hands like AQ or AK, which might check back if I check. Shoving extracts maximum value from the majority of the opponent's range. With an SPR of ~1.2, getting the stack in with a set is the clear +EV play.

River is an all-in sized bet after Pro took a very strong line: flop check-raise on A♠-high texture, turn barrel, then shove on a blank river. With A♦3♥ we’re essentially bluff-catching (we don’t beat any of his thin value; we only beat missed diamonds/straight bluffs).

Key factors pushing to a fold:
- Holding A♦ removes Pro’s strongest/most natural semi-bluff (nut flush draws with A♦X♦), reducing the number of credible bluffs that take this exact line.
- His value range is quite plausible and dense here (A6s/A2s/66/22, plus some AQ/A9 that can still choose to polarize).
- Pot odds require ~35% equity (6750 to win 19150), meaning he must be bluffing at least ~35% of the time, which is unlikely given the line and the A♦ blocker.

Since we’re near the bottom of our continuing range versus this sizing and we preserve a playable stack by folding, the best decision is to let it go.

I am in the UTG position (first to act) in a 5-handed game. Holding Queen-Ten offsuit (Qd Th) is a marginal hand for an early position opening. Given that Elon and Claude have both shown significant aggression and have larger stacks, opening with a mediocre hand out of position risks a 3-bet or being forced to play a bloated pot with a dominated hand. At this stage of the tournament (Hand 39 of 150) with 60 big blinds, I prefer to maintain a tighter opening range from early position to avoid unnecessary chip loss.

Preflop, UTG+1 in a 5-handed game with K3s. This is a weak suited hand with poor playability and low equity against likely calling ranges. Position is early, and while stacks are deep (218 BB), folding preserves stack for premium spots rather than limping into a multiway pot where K3s struggles postflop. Opponents have varying stacks, but no reason to enter with this hand. Standard fold.

On the button with 6hTs after two folds. This is a good steal spot - only the blinds left to act. While 6Ts is a marginal hand, position is excellent and a standard button raise should take down the blinds often enough to be profitable. Raising to 250 (2.5x).

I hold Pocket Deuces (2c, 2s) in the Small Blind facing a standard 2.5x raise (250) from Claude on the Button. While 22 has equity against a wide button opening range, playing the lowest pair Out of Position (OOP) is difficult. I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, but my equity realization is poor due to being OOP and the risk of the Big Blind squeezing behind me. Set mining is an option with deep stacks (100bb+), but generally, 22 is often a fold in this spot to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots OOP against aggressive openers. Folding is the disciplined decision.

Claude is opening the button to a standard 2.5x and we’re getting a very good price in the BB (150 to win 550 total). Even though K6o is dominated when we make a weak king, it still has enough raw equity vs a BTN range to defend at this sizing, and folding would be over-tight 5-handed. Plan is to realize equity with check/call on favorable boards and be disciplined on K-high textures vs heavy pressure.

We defended BB vs a BTN open and flop middle pair (6x) on Q-6-5 rainbow. This is a solid bluff-catcher but not strong enough to build a pot out of position via donk-betting, especially with K-high kicker that doesn’t improve our value much. Checking keeps Claude’s range wide (lets him c-bet his air and worse draws), controls pot size, and sets up a straightforward check/call vs normal sizing while folding to heavy multi-street pressure on bad runouts.

Flopped middle pair with 6h on a 5d 6s Qh board. As the preflop aggressor with position, this is a standard c-bet spot. I have a pair and the Queen-high board favors my perceived range. Betting around 1/3 pot for value and protection against overcards.

Claude’s 200 c-bet into 750 is very small and will contain a lot of range (overcards, backdoors, pure air) in addition to value hands. With middle pair (6x) we have enough equity and showdown value to continue very profitably at this price. Raising would mostly fold out the hands we beat while getting action from Qx/overpairs/65, and it builds a pot out of position with a marginal hand. Best is to defend by check-calling and re-evaluate turn cards and future sizing.

We’re out of position with a marginal but decent showdown hand (pair of 6s, K kicker) after defending BB vs BTN and check/calling a tiny flop c-bet. The turn Tc is a dynamic overcard that improves parts of Claude’s range (Qx continues, some Tx picks up pair equity, and broadway/straight draws like KJ/J9 gain equity), but it doesn’t significantly strengthen our hand. Donk-betting would mostly fold out worse (air/low equity hands) while getting called/raised by stronger (Qx, overpairs, two pair/sets) and it risks bloating the pot with a bluff-catcher. Best is to keep ranges wide, control pot size, and induce bluffs by checking, then respond based on Claude’s sizing.

Turn gave me two pair (tens and sixes). This is a strong hand that I should bet for value. FiveTwo has shown weakness by check-calling flop, and I now have a hand that beats most of their calling range (Qx, pocket pairs, draws). Betting around 65% pot for value.

Claude’s turn sizing (625 into 1575 ~40% pot) is still moderate and can easily be done with a lot of his range after the tiny flop c-bet: Qx for value/protection, some Tx that picked up a pair, and plenty of floats/bluffs/straight draws (J9, 98, KJ, etc.). With 6x we have decent showdown value and are getting a good price (need ~28% equity), and raising would mostly isolate ourselves vs stronger hands while folding out the hands we beat. Best is to continue as a bluff-catcher and re-evaluate river based on card and sizing.

River brings the As, which improves a meaningful chunk of Claude’s barreling range: Ax now makes top pair, and hands like KJ/J9 complete Broadway/9-to-A straights on A-Q-T. Our hand (one pair, 6s with K kicker) has showdown value but is not strong enough to value-bet, and a blocker bet risks getting raised off our equity while mostly getting called by better (Ax/Qx/straights). Best plan is to check, allow bluffs to fire, and then make a decision based on Claude’s sizing.

I have two pair (tens and sixes) but the river Ace is a terrible card. Any Ax hand that FiveTwo might have been floating with now beats me (AQ, AT, A6, A5 all make two pair that beat mine). FiveTwo has been check-calling passively which could indicate a drawing hand that just got there or a medium strength hand. Given the scare card, betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me while folding out worse hands. Best play is to check back for showdown value.

UTG with K3 offsuit in a 5-handed game is a clear fold. This hand has poor playability, dominated by better kings, and no strong equity against a raising range. Position is the worst, and with effective stacks around 60-200BB, folding preserves stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV. History shows aggressive opponents, but limping or raising this is -EV.
I have Ah8s in UTG+1, a decent ace-high hand. Elon folded from UTG. This is a standard open-raising hand from early-middle position. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x BB) as a standard open.

I hold Qs4c on the Button. This is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Facing a raise to 250 from Claude in early position (UTG+1), my hand has poor equity and playability against a standard opening range. Even with position, calling is negative EV due to the hand's weakness, and 3-bet bluffing is not optimal with this holding. Folding is the clear best decision.

We’re in the small blind, out of position versus a UTG+1 open. 63o has very poor playability and reverse-implied-odds issues, and it doesn’t make good 3-bet bluff candidates (no blockers, not suited). Even getting a price to call, we’ll realize equity badly from the SB and often face a squeeze/overcall from the BB. Best long-term EV is to release.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. Claude has raised to 250 from UTG+1. While the price to call is only 150 more, T6o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially when playing out of position. Given my stack size of approximately 59 big blinds, there is no need to defend with such a wide range. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve chips for better opportunities.
4d 2h is one of the worst starting hands in poker. From UTG position with 5 players still to act, I need a strong range. This is an easy fold.

I am in the Cutoff position (UTG+1 in 5-handed play) with Ace-Jack offsuit. This is a strong starting hand that is well within the standard opening range for this position. The player to my right (UTG) has folded. I have a healthy stack of 123.5 BB. The Big Blind has a very large stack, so I should be prepared for potential defense, but AJo plays reasonably well post-flop. A standard open raise to 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) puts pressure on the Button and Blinds while building the pot with a good hand.

5-handed on the Button vs a UTG+1 open, A3s is too playable to just fold: we have strong nut-flush potential, wheel-straight equity, and key blockers to premium Ax (especially AA/AK/AQ), which makes it a good candidate to 3-bet as a semi-bluff. Calling keeps dominated-A problems in and invites the blinds; 3-betting leverages position, can win the pot preflop a lot, and sets up favorable SPRs when we do get called. I’ll use a standard in-position sizing that pressures the opener while still allowing an easy fold to a 4-bet shove/large 4-bet given stack depths.

I am in the Small Blind holding Qc 8d. Pro raised from UTG+1 to 2.5bb, and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Button to 8bb. My hand is very weak and would be playing out of position against two potentially strong ranges. There is no reason to get involved in this pot with a marginal hand like Queen-Eight offsuit.

2s3c is a complete trash hand with no straight potential, no suitedness, and dominated in any multiway pot. Facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo on the button after Pro's UTG+1 open raise, both players have strong ranges. Pot odds are about 1200:700 (~1.7:1), but equity against their ranges is negligible (<10%). Deep stacks but no reason to speculate with this hand; preserve stack for better opportunities.

I opened with AJo from the Cutoff, and the Button (FiveTwo) put in a substantial 3-bet to 800 (3.2x my raise). While AJo is a strong opening hand, it becomes a marginal bluff-catcher or trap hand when playing out of position against a 3-bet. Against a tight value range (TT+, AQ+), AJo is dominated. Against a wider aggressive range, I will still struggle to realize equity out of position without hitting the flop hard. Given the large sizing of the 3-bet and the stack depth (effective ~59 BB), calling creates a bloated pot with a hand that has reverse implied odds. Folding is the disciplined decision to preserve chips.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 5-handed game with Kd9d. With 5 players, UTG is effectively the LoJack/Hijack. While K9s has flush potential, it is generally considered slightly too loose to open from the earliest position, as it is easily dominated by hands like KQ, KJ, AK, and AT which opponents often call or 3-bet with. Given the active players behind (FiveTwo and Elon) and the depth of stacks (121bb effective), playing a marginal suited King out of position is likely -EV. Folding is the disciplined play here.

5-handed and with Pro folded, 66 is comfortably ahead of the blinds’ random holdings and plays well as an open-raise: we can win the pot pre, realize equity post with position on SB/BB often, and have strong implied odds when we flop a set. Limping invites multiway and lowers fold equity; folding is too tight at ~70bb. I’ll open to a standard 2.5bb to pressure the blinds while keeping the pot manageable if we face a 3-bet (we can evaluate based on sizing/opponent).

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit. The raiser, FiveTwo, has shown a pattern of opening extremely wide from early and middle positions in previous hands (opening with Q3o in Hand #38 and T2d in Hand #17). Given this history, my Ace is likely ahead of their range. While A5o is marginal, the button position provides a significant advantage for post-flop play. I will call the 250 to see a flop, keeping the pot manageable while retaining the ability to outplay a loose opponent in position. I must remain cautious of the big stacks in the blinds (Elon and Claude) potentially squeezing, but the price to see the flop is attractive.

K4o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability and dominated often by stronger kings. Facing an UTG+1 raise from FiveTwo (who shows aggression in history) and a button call from Flash, their ranges are strong (FiveTwo: pairs, broadway, suited connectors; Flash: wider but still decent). Pot odds ~3:1 (200 to call into 650+potentially more with BB acting), but equity too low (~20-30% vs range). SB OOP squeeze not ideal with caller and BB to act. Deep stack but no fold equity advantage here. Clear fold to preserve stack for better spots.

T4o is a weak hand, but I'm getting 4.3:1 odds (150 to win 650) and I'm closing the action. With position being the BB and decent pot odds, I can see a flop cheaply. Not a strong spot but the price is reasonable to defend.

Flopped bottom pair (4s) with backdoor hearts. Against two opponents on a low coordinated board, my hand has showdown value but is vulnerable. Checking makes sense here - I can evaluate based on their actions, and my pair of 4s isn't strong enough to bet for value against two opponents.

We flop top set on a very dynamic board (two hearts + straight connectivity). In a 3-way pot we want to start building a pot for stacks while charging the many draws (heart draws, 5x/7x straight draws, combo draws) and still getting called by worse made hands (44/33, 64/65, A6, overpairs that peeled pre). Checking gives a free card that can kill action or create bad turn/river runouts. A larger c-bet also reduces the chance Flash can realize equity cheaply in position.

On a flop of 3h-6d-4h, I hold Ace-Five offsuit, giving me an open-ended straight draw (any 2 or 7) plus one overcard (the Ace). FiveTwo has bet 600 into a pot of 800. This is a sizeable bet (75% pot), but my open-ended straight draw provides roughly 32% equity by the river, and I am getting approximately 2.33:1 on a call (requiring 30% equity). Additionally, I have the button position, which allows me to see how Claude (the Big Blind) reacts before I act in future streets. While FiveTwo has been aggressive, my hand has significant drawing potential and some showdown value if I hit an Ace. Raising here might be too risky against a player who has shown a willingness to bet large, so I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate based on the next card and Claude's action.

I have bottom pair (4s) on a 3h6d4h board. Two opponents - FiveTwo bet and Flash called. Pot odds are 600 to win 2600 (~23% needed). However, bottom pair multiway OOP is weak. FiveTwo showed aggression preflop and on flop, Flash called showing interest. My pair is easily dominated by any 6, overpairs, or even other 4s with better kickers. Turn will often bring overcards or completing draws. Folding is correct here - the hand is too weak to continue multiway.

Turn brings the third heart, so a chunk of Flash’s flop-calling range has improved to made flushes, but he also still has many one-heart hands (pair+heart, A-high with a heart) and non-heart straight/2p type hands that will continue versus a reasonable sizing. With top set we’re still a value hand and we must charge draws/one-heart holdings while also setting up a clean river shove for the remaining effective stack. I’ll use a medium sizing to keep worse continuing hands in (and not bloat the pot too hard versus the strongest part of his range).

I am currently holding the nut flush draw with the Ace of Hearts (Ah) on a board featuring three hearts, alongside an open-ended straight draw (any 2 or 7). With the 8 of Hearts appearing on the turn, the board texture is highly coordinated. FiveTwo is betting 1100 into a 2000 pot. I have 14 clean outs (8 remaining hearts for the nut flush, plus three non-heart 7s and three non-heart 2s for a straight). This gives me approximately 30% equity to improve to a very strong hand on the river. The pot odds offered are 1100 to win a total pot of 4200, which is roughly 26% required equity. Since my actual equity exceeds the required pot odds and I am in position with a stack that allows for a potential value bet or a bluff on the river if the board misses his range, I will call the 1100.

River is a brick that doesn’t change the main story: the turn completed the heart flush. After I bet flop and then bet again when the 3rd heart hit, Flash’s river continuing range is heavily weighted toward made flushes (plus some stubborn pair+heart and the occasional set/two-pair). If I bet again—especially for a size that meaningfully grows the pot—I’m likely to get called mostly by flushes (that beat my set) and fold out missed straight draws/bluffs, which is a bad trade. Checking keeps in his missed hands that may take a stab, and also allows worse made hands to check back so I win at showdown without value-owning myself into the flush portion of his range.

On the river, I have completed a 6-high straight (2-3-4-5-6) using the 5c in my hand. The board is 3h-6d-4h-8h-2c, which includes three hearts. My possession of the Ace of Hearts (Ah) is significant because it blocks the nut flush, meaning FiveTwo cannot have the highest possible flush. FiveTwo has been aggressive on the flop and turn but has now checked the river. While my straight is strong, the presence of three hearts and the possibility of a 5-7 (which would make a higher 8-high straight) makes betting for value slightly risky. If I bet and get raised, I would be in a difficult spot. By checking back, I ensure a showdown and likely win against FiveTwo's range of sets, two-pair, or missed semi-bluffs. This is the safest way to realize my equity and win a substantial pot without risking a check-raise.

5-handed UTG with K6o is too dominated and plays poorly postflop versus the likely calling/3-betting ranges behind (especially Button big stack). With 51BB effective, there’s no need to take a thin open/limp spot that will often realize negative equity and create tough decisions. Best is to preserve stack and wait for stronger open/steal candidates from later position.

I am holding Jack of Hearts and Six of Hearts in UTG+1 position. This is a marginal suited gapper. In a 5-handed game, opening this hand from early position is generally too weak, especially with aggressive players like Elon and Claude still to act behind me. Given my stack size of 8075 and the tournament stage, I prefer to fold and wait for a stronger opening hand.

83c is a very weak suited hand with poor connectivity (large gap between ranks), low playability postflop, and minimal showdown value. Even on the button in an unopened pot, standard opening ranges do not include 83s due to its lack of equity against calling ranges and poor implied odds. Blinds have deep stacks (12k+ BB) and are active players based on history, likely to defend or 3-bet. Best to fold and wait for stronger hands to maximize long-term stack growth.

Heads up against the BB with J3o. While weak, it's playable in this spot. I'll raise to put pressure on Pro and potentially take down the pot preflop. If called, I have position for the rest of the hand.

I hold a premium hand, KcAh, in the Big Blind facing a standard raise from the Small Blind. We are deep-stacked (120bb). While a 3-bet is a standard option to build the pot and assert dominance, the Small Blind's opening range is very wide. Flat calling has several advantages: 1) It keeps the opponent's range wide, including many dominated Ax and Kx hands that might fold to a 3-bet. 2) I retain the advantage of position post-flop with a disguised strong hand. 3) It avoids bloating the pot and facing a potential 4-bet, which would be a high-variance spot deep-stacked. Given the opponent's history of folding to significant aggression later in hands, keeping them in the pot to extract value on favorable boards is the superior exploitative play.

I have J3o on a very wet 8h7c9h board - completely missed with no pair and no meaningful draws. This board hits Pro's BB calling range hard (connectors, suited connectors, flush draws). With just jack-high and no real equity, c-betting into this coordinated texture is unprofitable. Check and give up.

The flop [8h, 7c, 9h] is very wet and coordinates well with the Small Blind's wide range. Although I have strong showdown value with Ace-King high, I have missed the board completely. Betting here has little utility: worse hands (complete air) will fold, while better hands (pairs, two pairs, straights) or strong draws will call or raise. Checking back allows me to realize my equity with two overcards, keeps the pot small on a dangerous board, and gives me the option to re-evaluate on the turn. I hold the Ah, which acts as a blocker to the nut flush draw and gives me some bluff-catching potential or bluffing equity on later streets.

Pro checked behind on a coordinated flop, showing weakness. The 7d pairs the board and doesn't complete draws. I have jack-high with a gutshot to a straight (need a T). Pro's check on the flop suggests they likely have a weak hand - maybe a small pair or nothing. I can take a stab here with a small bet to pick up the pot since my hand has little showdown value anyway.

On the turn, the board is [8h, 7c, 9h, 7d]. The opponent (Small Blind) bets 200 into a pot of 500 (40%). This small sizing often indicates a marginal hand, a draw, or a blocker bet rather than a nutted hand. I hold [Kc, Ah], giving me two overcards and the nut flush blocker. I am getting favorable pot odds (calling 200 to win 700, requiring ~22% equity). Against a wide SB range that includes semi-bluffs (straight draws, flush draws) and random air, my Ace-King high is often currently the best hand. Even if I am behind a pair like 8x or 9x, I have 6 outs to improve. Folding is too weak given the price and range, and raising risks isolating myself against better hands. Calling allows me to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the river.

I have J3o - complete air with just jack-high. Pro called my turn probe bet, showing they have something (likely a pair or better). The river 5c doesn't change much. Bluffing again into a player who's already shown willingness to call is burning chips. I should check and concede the pot - my jack-high has no showdown value, but a bluff here is unlikely to succeed against someone who called the turn.

On the river, the board is [8h 7c 9h 7d 5c]. I hold AK high. The opponent checked to me. The board contains a pair of sevens and 4-to-a-straight possibilities (6 makes a straight). The opponent's small turn bet and river check suggest a marginal hand or a missed draw. While I lose to any pair, my AK high has decent showdown value against missed flush draws (hearts) or other broadway hands (AQ, QJ) in the opponent's wide blind-vs-blind range. Turning AK into a bluff here is risky as it likely only folds out hands I already beat, and a pair like 8x or 9x might look you up given the checking line. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and win against weaker high cards without risking more chips.

I am in the UTG position with Queen-Six offsuit. This is a very weak hand to play from early position, especially with four active players behind me, including the chip leader Elon. To maintain a healthy stack and wait for better opportunities, folding is the standard and most prudent play.

4s8s is a speculative suited hand with low cards and a gap, unsuitable for opening from UTG+1 with 4 players behind (Claude on button likely to defend wide, blinds can squeeze). Deep stack allows waiting for better positions or stronger hands. Standard fold to preserve chips for value.

K9o on the button with two folds in front. This is a standard steal opportunity - K9o plays well enough from the button against the blinds. Raising to 250 (2.5x) to pick up the blinds or play in position postflop.

I hold 7c 3s in the Small Blind, facing a raise to 250 from the Button (Claude). This hand is statistically very weak (garbage offsuit) and performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. The pot odds (calling 200 to win ~400) do not justify playing this hand, as I would likely need ~30% realized equity which is hard to achieve with 73o out of position. With a solid stack of 125 BBs, there is no pressure to take risks with marginal holdings. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Facing a 2.5x button open, BB can defend wide, but T3s is near the bottom: it’s frequently dominated (by Tx, suited broadways) and has poor reverse-implied-odds when we make a weak pair. Out of position with ~50bb and as one of the shorter stacks, I’d rather avoid a marginal, hard-to-realize-equity spot and preserve chips for better defend/3-bet candidates.

7s3c is one of the worst possible starting hands in Hold'em: offsuit, low cards, no connectivity or suited potential for straights/flushes. As UTG in a 5-handed game, position is unfavorable for speculative hands. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 (~1.5:1) are poor for this trash, and raising is suicidal without strength. Folding preserves stack for premium spots, aligning with long-term ICM maximization in tournament.
Suited connectors 98cc in early position. Good playability with straight and flush potential. Standard open raise to 250 to build the pot or take down blinds.

I hold Jd2d on the Button. While suited, this is a very weak hand (low kicker, unconnected). Claude raised to 2.5BB from UTG+1, indicating a reasonably strong range that likely dominates my hand (better Jacks, higher flush draws). Although I have position, the hand strength is insufficient to call, and it is a poor candidate for a 3-bet bluff against an early position opener. Folding is the disciplined and correct choice.

Calling from the small blind with A3o is awkward: we’ll be out of position, invite the big blind in at a good price, and our hand has reverse implied odds when we make a weak ace. This is a better candidate to 3-bet bluff because the Ad blocks strong continue/4-bet hands (AA/AK/AQ) and Claude has shown he can open/fold and play straightforwardly versus aggression. I’ll use an out-of-position sizing that pressures his opening range and discourages the BB from coming along. If Claude 4-bets, we simply fold; if called, we can leverage initiative on many flops (especially low/medium boards) and proceed cautiously on ace-high boards.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten-Three offsuit. The action involves a raise from UTG+1 (Claude) to 250 and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (FiveTwo) to 900. FiveTwo is short-stacked, and a 3-bet in this position typically indicates a very strong range. My hand is extremely weak and does not play well against the likely ranges of either opponent. Folding is the only sensible play here to preserve my stack.

FiveTwo 3-bet to 900 from SB with a short stack (~4125). I have 98cc which has decent playability. Pot odds are 650 to win 1250 (~2:1). Against a short stack, implied odds are limited, but suited connectors play well if I connect. FiveTwo is committed with their stack size. Calling to see a flop.

We 3-bet pre and get a flop that gives us top pair (3s) but with a weak kicker and no heart. On 3h-9h-6d, Claude’s calling range has plenty of 9x, medium pairs, and strong draws/sets, so we don’t want to inflate the pot with a big c-bet. However, we still benefit from betting small for value/protection: it folds out a lot of unpaired overcards (KQ/KJ/QJ/Ax that missed), denies equity to random overcards, and keeps initiative.

I’ll use a small c-bet around 25–30% pot. If we face a raise, this hand is too thin to continue against a range that’s often value-heavy on this texture, so we can comfortably fold and preserve stack.

I flopped top pair with 9c on a 3h9h6d board. FiveTwo's small c-bet of 550 into ~1900 pot looks like either a weak made hand or draw continuation. With heart flush draw out there, I need to charge draws. FiveTwo has ~3025 behind after this bet - if I raise to 1500-1600, they're basically committed. Top pair good kicker is strong here against their 3-bet range on this low board. Raising for value and protection.

We don’t have “top pair” here — with 3s on 3h-9h-6d we have a set of threes. Given the bloated 3-bet pot and our remaining stack (3575) versus a pot of 3950, the SPR is < 1, so this is a standard stack-off spot.

Claude’s flop raise can be made with plenty we crush or want to charge: 9x, overpairs (TT-QQ), and especially heart draws / combo draws. Flat-calling gives those hands a cheap turn and creates awkward turn decisions out of position. The best play is to jam now for max value and protection; if he has 66/99 it’s a cooler we accept.

FiveTwo is all-in (0 behind) for 4125 total. Pot is 7525 and I need 2625 to call. Getting ~2.87:1 pot odds, need ~26% equity. I have top pair with 98cc on 3h9h6d. FiveTwo could have overpairs (TT-AA), sets (33,66), flush draws (AxHh), or weaker 9x. My 9 blocks some of their sets. Against a mixed range of overpairs and flush draws, top pair has enough equity to call given the odds. Can't fold top pair getting these odds.
I am UTG in a 4-handed game with a very weak hand, Jack-Six offsuit (J6o). With 125 big blinds, I am deep-stacked and have no incentive to play garbage hands out of position. This is a standard fold.

On the Button in a 4-handed game, 98o is a standard opening hand. My goal is to win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position against the blinds. While I am the shortest stack at the table with approximately 80 big blinds, I still have plenty of room to play. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to 250.

Preflop in SB facing a button min-raise to 2.5BB from Flash, who tends to be active and raise wide from late position based on hand history. A5o is offsuit but features a strong ace blocker, blocking premium hands like AA, AK, AQ. Deep stacks (200+ BB effective vs Flash's 77BB). Pot odds to call are attractive (~2:1), but OOP postflop playability is marginal. 3-betting provides fold equity against a likely wide button range, isolates if called, and takes down the pot frequently. Standard 3-bet size ~3x the open from blinds OOP. Raising to 750 balances value (live ace can make top pair top kicker or nut flush draws) with bluffs. Folding too tight; calling risks multiway pot OOP.
K8o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet. Elon has been aggressive and his 3-bet range likely dominates my hand. I'd be out of position postflop and K8o doesn't play well against a strong range. Easy fold.

I opened 98o from the Button as a standard steal attempt in a 4-handed game. However, Elon has 3-bet from the Small Blind. 98o is a weak hand that lacks the playability and equity to comfortably call a 3-bet, even while holding position. Calling 500 more would commit nearly 10% of my remaining stack to a pot where I am likely dominated or facing a range with much higher equity. Folding is the most prudent and disciplined action to preserve my stack for more favorable spots.

I am in the UTG position with Jc 5d. This is a very weak starting hand, especially in a 4-handed game where position and card strength are important. Although it is a 100-chip commitment to enter the pot, this hand has very poor playability and I should simply fold and wait for a better opportunity to protect my stack.

Ts7s is a strong speculative hand on the button in an unopened pot (UTG Flash folded). Position is excellent for postflop playability with suited connector potential for straights, flushes, and two-pair combos. Blinds are deep-stacked (Claude 177BB, Pro 124BB effective), and my stack is massive at 220BB+, allowing aggression without risk. Standard button open-raise size vs blinds is 2.25-2.5x BB (225-250). Raising to 250 applies pressure to steal the 150 dead money, builds pot when called, and maintains initiative. Calling risks multiway limp pot OOP for blinds; folding is too tight for this spot. Opponent history shows Claude and Pro defend wide but fold often to button raises (e.g., recent hands). Optimal to raise here to maximize EV.

4d8d is a weak suited gapper. Facing a button open from Elon for 250, I need to call 200 more. While suited hands have some playability, 48s is on the bottom of my defending range. Given the gap between cards and marginal strength, folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ks5s facing a 2.5x open from the Button (Elon). The Small Blind has folded. The pot is 400 and I need to call 150, offering me pot odds of roughly 27%. K5s is a standard defend against a Button open due to its suitedness and high card potential, and fits well into a calling range at this stack depth (124bb). Folding would be too tight given the wide range of the opener and the favorable price.

I have flopped trips with [Ks 5s] on a board of [As Kc Kh]. While this is a very strong hand, my kicker is weak. The board texture (A-K-K) heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, as they have all the strong Ax and Kx combos. Leading out would likely fold out his bluffs and isolate me against better Kings or Full Houses. Checking is the standard line to allow the opponent to c-bet with their bluffs or value hands (like Ax) that I beat. I intend to check-call to keep his range wide.

Flop of AsKcKh is a dry, paired board that smashes villain's BB calling range (many Kx, Ax, suited broadways, mid pairs). Hero's button raise range has few hits (AK, AA rare; sets unlikely), representing mostly medium strength and bluffs. T7s has zero equity: no pair, no straight draw (needs 89 or J8), only backdoor spade flush draw. Betting air here gets no value from worse (villain unlikely to have missed) and risks villain folding weaker hands or calling with monsters we dominate. Pro checked, indicating possible weakness or trap, but with deep stacks (effective ~122BB), better to check back: pot control, free card to realize backdoor equity, keep pot small for future bluffs or if we improve. Maintains balanced range, avoids spew. Preflop raise stole SB dead money successfully; now play fit-or-fold postflop discipline to maximize EV long-term.

The flop was checked through on an A-K-K board. I have trips with a weak kicker (Ks5s). The turn is a 9c, bringing a flush draw. The opponent's check-back on the flop typically indicates a capped range, often consisting of Ax, underpairs, or air, though a slow-played monster or better King is possible but less likely. With a weak kicker, I do not want to inflate the pot significantly against a better King. Checking allows the opponent to bluff with their air or value bet an Ace, from which I can easily call. Leading out likely folds out his bluffs and isolates me against a range that crushes my kicker or chops. I will check to induce action or control the pot size.

Turn board brings 9c, completing no strong draws for my range but adding club flush draw possibility, which villain unlikely to have given preflop call and double check. Hero's Ts7s remains pure air: no pair, no straight draw (possible gutshot runner-runner but negligible), weak backdoor spade flush draw. Villain (Pro in BB) defended wide vs button raise (typical: Ax, Kx, suited connectors, pairs), but double-checking flop and turn on paired kings board suggests medium strength (e.g., mid pairs, weak Ax, missed draws) rather than monsters (KK/AK would often bet for value/protection). Deep effective stacks (~122BB) favor pot control with nothing: betting risks folding out worse hands (Ax, 77-TT) while getting called/raised by better (sets, AA/AK). Checking maintains balance (mixes bluffs/value), realizes equity cheaply if runner-runner miracle, induces bluffs on river if villain has draw. Consistent with flop check discipline: steal preflop dead money succeeded (+EV), now avoid spew postflop to preserve massive stack (218BB) for premium spots. Checking extracts thin value on river if villain bluffs or shows down worse.

The opponent has checked back both the flop and the turn on a board of [As Kc Kh 9c Jd]. This line is indicative of a capped range, likely consisting of weak Ax hands, underpairs (QQ-TT), or perhaps a rivered pair of Jacks. It is unlikely the opponent holds a King or better, as they would almost certainly have bet the turn to extract value or protect against draws. With Trip Kings, I am significantly ahead of his checking range. However, given the scary board texture (Straights possible with QT, Boats possible), checking might allow him to check back worse hands that would call a bet. I believe Ax or Jx will call a small value bet but will unlikely bet themselves. Therefore, a small lead for thin value is the best play to extract chips from Ax/Jx. I will bet slightly less than half pot.

Facing a small 250 bet into 800 pot on the river (1050 effective pot odds ~4:1, needing ~19% equity to call profitably). Ts7s is complete air: no pair, no straight (board A-K-K-9-J misses entirely), no flush draw, negligible runner-runner outs. Preflop button raise + double check-back represents balanced range with value (AK, AA, KK, KQ, AJ) and bluffs, but repeated checks narrow it to medium strength or missed draws. Pro's BB defense range (Ax, Kx, suited broadways/connectors, pairs) hits this board hard: frequently has Ax (beats our air), Kx (two pair or better), QJ/AJ (two pair), sets, or even Q10 straight. Double-checking flop/turn on paired kings suggests caution with monsters (possible trap but unlikely OOP), favoring showdown value like mid Ax/KT or thin value bets on river (e.g., K9, A9, J9). Small bet size exploits pot odds but unlikely pure bluff: Pro's history shows passive play postflop but value bets when ahead (e.g., Hand 38 aggressive with Ah2d); deep stacks (120BB eff) reduce bluff frequency. Calling spews chips long-term, eroding massive 218BB stack. Consistent with prior discipline: stole preflop dead money (+EV), checked air postflop, now fold to preserve stack for premium spots in tournament with 104 hands left. Optimal EV: fold.

Preflop UTG in a 4-handed game with Qh Ks, a strong Broadway hand with good playability. Standard play is to raise for value and to take initiative. Opponents have decent stacks, but Flash in BB is relatively shorter at ~76BB. Raising 2.5x the BB (to 250 total) is a standard tournament sizing early on, building the pot while denying equity to weaker hands. Limping is too passive, folding too tight given position and hand strength.

Pocket 3s on the button facing a UTG raise to 250. Small pairs play well in position for set mining. With deep stacks (Elon has 21.5k, I have 17.7k), implied odds are good if I hit a set. Calling to see a flop is the standard play here.

I hold QTs in the Small Blind. Facing a standard 2.5x open from UTG and a call from the Button, I am getting very attractive pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be at least 850, roughly 4.25:1). Although playing out of position is a disadvantage, QTs is a hand that plays well in multi-way pots due to its flush and straight potential. A 3-bet squeeze is risky against a UTG opening range which is typically strong. Folding is too tight given the price and hand strength. Therefore, the best option is to flat call and evaluate the flop.

I am in the big blind holding 8s 2c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Elon raised from UTG, and both the button (Claude) and the small blind (Pro) have called. Although I only need to put in 150 more chips to see a flop in a pot that will be 1000 total, the hand's playability multi-way and out of position is extremely poor. The risk of losing more chips on later streets with a marginal pair or being dominated outweighs the potential reward of hitting an unlikely miracle flop. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I have flopped trips with a strong kicker (Queen) on a Th Td 7h board. Being out of position in a multi-way pot, leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal as it folds out opponent's bluffs and allows them to play perfectly. Checking is the standard line to allow the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) to make a continuation bet or for the button to stab with draws or weaker made hands. My hand is strong enough to check-call or check-raise, but the first step is to check and observe the action.

Flop Th Td 7h gives me the Qh flush draw (9 outs to flush, ~18% on turn, 35% by river) plus two overcards for gutshot/straight potential and showdown value with queen-high. Board is paired, but dry with no straight draws obvious except broadways. Pro checked SB first to act, showing weakness. As preflop raiser, I have range advantage UTG. Betting semi-bluffs to build pot with equity, deny equity to draws/one-pair hands, and take initiative. Half-pot sizing (550 into 850) balances value from weaker pairs/draws and protection. Claude on button may call/raise lighter, but deep stacks allow fold equity and fold to raise if needed. Checking risks giving free card to draws or letting Claude rep the paired board.

Missed my set with pocket 3s. Board is TT7 with two hearts. Elon bet 550 into 850, representing a ten or overpair. My 33 is basically worthless here - I'm behind any ten, any 7, and any overpair. No reason to continue without hitting my set.

I have flopped trips with a Queen kicker, a very strong hand. The UTG player (Elon) has c-bet 550 into a pot of 850 after checking to him. This bet size suggests strength or a semi-bluff with a flush draw. Raising here would likely fold out his bluffs and isolate me against hands that beat me (like AT, 77) or strong draws that have equity. Calling allows me to keep his range wide, including overpairs (JJ-AA) that might continue betting on the turn, and bluffs. Since I am out of position, pot control and trapping is preferable to inflating the pot immediately. I will call to see the turn.

I hold Trip Tens with a Queen kicker on a Th-Td-7h-6c board. The 6c is a relative brick, though it connects with 89s for a straight. My hand remains very strong. I am out of position against the UTG aggressor who c-bet the flop. Leading out (donk betting) here is not optimal because it allows the opponent to fold their bluffs and weaker hands while continuing with hands that beat me (like AT or a full house). Checking is the best play to allow the opponent to fire a second barrel with overpairs (JJ-AA), weaker trips (JT, T9), or semi-bluffs (flush draws). If they bet, I can then decide whether to call or raise, but my default action first is to Check to the aggressor.

Turn board 6c is a relatively blank card that doesn't complete obvious draws except possibly some weak straight draws, but primarily doesn't improve my hand. I have QhKs for queen-high with a weak backdoor flush draw (only three hearts total, needing runner-runner) and a king overcard, but no made hand or strong equity against Pro's check-call flop range, which likely includes pairs like 77, weaker tens, ace-high, or heart draws. As the preflop raiser and flop bettor, my range is perceived as strong (overpairs, tens, sets), but continuing to bet here with pure air would be overbluffing into a player who already demonstrated interest by calling the flop. Deep stacks (210BB effective with Pro at 120BB) mean I don't need to force the issue; checking preserves my stack, controls the pot, realizes equity safely if Pro has a weak hand he checks behind, and sets up potential river bluffs or value if I hit. Betting risks stacking off light or folding out better, with low fold equity given Pro's prior call. Tournament stage mid-early (hand 48/150), prioritize survival and selective aggression.

I hold QcTc on a board of Th Td 7h 6c 7c. I have a Full House (Tens full of Sevens). On this double-paired board (T-T-7-7-6), the kicker for the Ten does not play; my hand is composed of the three Tens and the two Sevens. Thus, I chop with any other Ten (Tx) and lose only to Quad Sevens (77), which is extremely unlikely given the board and action. I beat all pocket pairs (JJ-AA) and air. 

The opponent (Elon) checked back the turn, capping his range and suggesting he likely holds showdown value (like JJ-AA) or a draw that missed, rather than a strong Ten. If I bet the river, it is difficult for worse hands (JJ-AA) to call on this texture, as they beat nothing in my value range. They would likely fold. If he has a Ten, we chop, so betting gains nothing extra. 

By checking, I give the opponent an opportunity to bluff with his missed draws (like hearts) or value bet a Ten (which I would call for the chop). Checking extracts the maximum value from his bluffs while losing nothing against his value range.

River pairs the sevens (7c), creating a paired board with tens and sevens, favoring hands like trips, full houses (e.g., TT, 77, T7), or two-pair combos. My QhKs is pure queen-high bluff-catcher with no pair, straight, or flush; it loses to any pair or better, which dominates Pro's check-call flop range (likely includes weaker Ts, 7x, Ax/high cards, missed heart draws). Pro checked turn and river, showing weakness or trap, but after calling my flop c-bet (550 into ~1200 pot), unlikely air—probably medium strength without initiative. Deep effective stacks (Pro 120BB) and mid-tournament stage (48/150) favor checking: realizes showdown equity against possible worse high-card hands or missed draws; avoids thin value calls or spew bluffs into sticky SB range with low fold equity on scary paired board. Bluffing risks bloating pot unnecessarily when better spots exist; checking preserves stack and range integrity as preflop raiser who can rep monsters but shows discipline by checking behind thin here.

J3o from UTG is a clear fold. This hand is far too weak to open from early position in a 4-handed game. Even with reduced players, J3 offsuit has poor playability and will often be dominated when called.

I am on the Button in a 4-handed game with K9o. Action has folded to me. In a 4-handed setup, the Button opening range is quite wide (typically 50%+ of hands), and K9o falls well within this range. While the Big Blind (Elon) is the chip leader and capable of aggression, having position post-flop is a significant advantage. A standard raise to 2.5BB (250 chips) allows me to attempt to steal the blinds or play a pot in position if called, while being small enough to easily fold to a 3-bet. I choose to open-raise to exert pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five suited. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. While the hand is suited, it is quite weak and I will be out of position for the entire hand. Calling 200 more into a pot of 400 (getting 2:1 odds) is generally not profitable from the SB against a likely wide button opening range, especially with a large-stacked Big Blind still to act behind me. To preserve my stack and avoid difficult post-flop situations out of position, I will fold.

9s4h is a very weak offsuit hand with little playability, no high card strength, and poor postflop potential. Facing a button raise after UTG and SB folds, Pro's range is wide but still contains many strong hands. BB defense charts call for folding this bottom-tier hand. Pot odds of ~2.7:1 require too much equity (~27%), and 94o realizes far less against a polarized button range. No blockers for bluffs or 3-bets. Preserve stack for better spots in tournament.

I am in UTG position with King-Three offsuit (Kc 3h). This is a very weak hand with poor playability and reverse implied odds. With three opponents left to act and adequate stack depth (141BB), there is no incentive to open the pot or limp. Folding is the standard and optimal play.

I am on the button with 6h 4d, a very weak hand. While the button is a good position to steal blinds, 64 offsuit is too weak to open with, especially when the blinds (Elon and Claude) have significantly larger stacks and have shown a willingness to defend or play aggressively in previous hands. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

Preflop, folded to me in the SB heads-up vs BB with 8d2s, one of the worst possible hands. No playability, unsuited gapper, low cards. While pot odds are 150:50 (3:1), completing the blind OOP with trash leads to difficult postflop spots where we have no initiative and minimal equity. Standard play is to fold and wait for better spots in a deepstack tournament to maximize EV long-term.

I am in the UTG position with T3o, which is a very weak hand. In a four-handed game, standard opening ranges are wider, but T3o is still well outside the range of hands worth playing, especially from the first position to act. Folding is the correct strategy to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

9c2h is one of the worst possible starting hands offsuit with no connectivity or high card value. Even on the button with fold equity, raising into two players (SB and BB with deep stacks around 170xBB and 140xBB) is spewy; they defend wide. Calling 100 into a 250 pot offers poor implied odds and postflop playability is disastrous. Folding preserves our deep 208xBB stack for premium spots in this tournament phase (hand 51/150). Prioritize discipline to maximize long-term EV.

Heads up against BB with 38o - a weak hand. Two options: fold or complete. With position and only 50 to call into a 150 pot, I'll complete to see a cheap flop. Not worth raising with this hand but the pot odds are decent for completing.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit facing a limp from the Small Blind. This is a reasonable hand, but not strong enough to strictly mandate a raise for value, especially with deep stacks (140 BB). Raising might bloat the pot with a hand that can be dominated. By checking, I take advantage of my position post-flop to realize equity and control the pot size. It is a low-variance standard play in this spot.

I have 3d8c on Ac 6d 9h - complete air with no pair, no draw. Pot is 200 and action is checked to me. I could bluff representing the ace, but with no equity if called, a check is safer. Free card might help - could catch an 8 or backdoor draws. No reason to risk chips here with such a weak holding.

I am in the Big Blind in a limped pot against the Small Blind. The flop is A-9-6 rainbow. I hold J-T offsuit, which is just Jack-high with very little immediate equity (no flush draw, backdoor straight possibilities). The Small Blind checked to me. This board texture hits a limping range reasonably well (weak Aces, 9x, 6x), and betting would likely only fold out hands that I am already beating (worse air) while getting called by any pair. There is no incentive to bluff at this moment. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card which could improve my hand (e.g., an 8 gives me an open-ended straight draw, a J or T gives me a pair). Therefore, checking is the best option.

I have 3d8c - complete air with no pair, no draws. Board is Ac Jc 9h 6d. Pro checked flop and turn, showing weakness, but I have zero equity. Betting would be a pure bluff with no backup plan if called. Small pot, not worth risking chips. Check and see a free river.

I hold Second Pair (Jacks) on an Ace-high board (A-J-9-6). The opponent (Small Blind) limped pre-flop and has checked to me twice. His range is likely capped and consists of weak pairs or air, though a weak Ace is possible. Betting here is thin value as it likely folds out worse hands and isolates me against better hands (Aces). Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce a bluff on the river or call a value bet from a worse hand. It is a standard pot control line with a marginal made hand in position.

River with 3d8c - complete air on Ac 6d 9h Jc 4s. Pro checked every street showing weakness but I have literally nothing. Pot is only 200 - not worth risking chips on a bluff when Pro could easily have any piece of this board (weak ace, jack, nine, pocket pair). Check and concede this small pot.

I hold second pair (Jacks) on a board of 6d-Ac-9h-Jc-4s. The action has been checked down on the flop and turn, and the opponent (Small Blind) has checked to me on the river. The pot is small (200 chips). My hand has decent showdown value, beating 9x, 6x, 4x, and pockets pairs lower than Jacks. However, betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands, as most would fold, while better hands (weak Aces) would call. There is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff or bet for thin value in such a small pot where I risk being check-raised or called by better. Checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity.

UTG with Qc6c in a 4-handed game. Q6s is a marginal suited hand at best, but from early position, it's too weak. Standard play is to fold pre-flop to avoid playing out of position with a hand that plays poorly post-flop, often dominated by better queens or live cards. Ranges tighten UTG even short-handed to preserve stack for better spots. Pot is small (blinds only), no need to speculate.
KQs on the button is a premium hand. With Elon already folded, I have position and should raise to build the pot. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with J5s facing a standard raise from the Button (Claude). While my hand is suited, J5 is a weak speculative hand that plays poorly out of position. The button's opening range is wide, but likely still dominates J5s (better Jacks, better flush draws, stronger high cards). Calling puts me out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal holding that suffers from reverse implied odds. The cost to call (200) offers decent pot odds, but the playability disadvantage makes this a negative EV call in the long run. Folding is the standard and prudent play here to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 9c, facing a Button raise of 250 from Claude. The pot currently stands at 400, and it costs me 150 to call. This gives me pot odds of approximately 3.6:1 (requiring about 21% equity), which is well within the threshold for defending with a hand like 89o. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the playability of medium connectors makes a call the most mathematically sound decision here.

On a flop of As Jh 8d, I have bottom pair with an 8. The board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Claude), as it contains both an Ace and a Jack. I will check to the aggressor to see how they react. If they bet, I will have to evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the standard play with a marginal hand out of position.

I have KQd with a gutshot straight draw (need a T for the Broadway straight). Board is Js Ah 8d - no flush draws for me. Flash checked to me. I can c-bet small to represent the Ace and put pressure. A 1/3 pot bet of ~175 is efficient - if called I still have 4 outs to the nuts, and I may take it down now.

I have bottom pair (eights) with a 9 kicker on an Ace-high board. Claude, the pre-flop raiser, has bet 175 into a pot of 550. This is roughly a 1/3 pot continuation bet, which is a standard sizing for an aggressor on this board texture. Given the pot odds (calling 175 to win a total pot of 900), I only need about 20% equity to justify a call. Folding bottom pair to such a small bet would be overly tight and exploitable. I will call to see how the turn develops and potentially improve to two pair or trips, or reach a cheap showdown if the action remains small.

I have bottom pair (eights) with a nine kicker on a board of As-Js-8d-2c. The 2c turn is a complete brick. Given that I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Claude), checking is the most appropriate action. Leading here would likely only be called by better hands and doesn't serve much purpose for protection or value. I will check and see if Claude continues his aggression or checks behind, which will provide more information about the strength of his hand.

I have KQd with a gutshot to Broadway (need a Ten). Flash has been check-calling passively - likely has a weak made hand like a weak J or 8x. The 2c changes nothing. I can either: (1) bet again to apply pressure, or (2) take a free card to try to hit my gutshot. Given Flash's passive play pattern suggesting a made hand that won't fold easily, checking makes sense - I get a free look at the river with 4 outs to the nuts, and I have K-high for some showdown value if Flash is bluffing or very weak.

I have a pair of eights on a board of As-Js-8d-2c-2d. The river double-paired the board with deuces, so my final hand is two pair, eights and deuces, with an Ace-Jack kicker (As-Js-8s-8d-2d). Claude, the button raiser, checked back on the turn, which often indicates a medium-strength hand like a Jack or a weak Ace, or potentially a complete air hand giving up. By checking the river, I am allowing Claude to check behind with hands I beat (like small pocket pairs or high-card hands) or potentially bluff if he has nothing. Betting into him is unlikely to get called by worse hands and only risks getting raised by better ones that were pot-controlling on the turn.

River completed with 2d pairing the board. I have K-high with a missed gutshot - no showdown value. Flash has been check-calling passively, suggesting a made hand like weak Jx, 8x, or possibly an Ace. Bluffing into someone who's been calling stations throughout this hand is -EV. Check back and accept the loss - no point risking more chips when my bluffs likely won't fold out their made hands.

K8o from UTG is too weak to open. Standard UTG range should be much tighter - pairs, suited broadways, strong aces. K8o is easily dominated by better kings and doesn't play well multiway. Folding is the correct play here.

I hold Pocket Sevens (7s 7d) on the Button, which is a strong starting hand, especially in late position. With the action folded to me, this is a clear opening opportunity to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. The stacks are deep (I have ~141 BB), which makes set-mining valuable if 3-bet, but my primary goal is to open the pot for value and positional advantage. A standard opening size at this table has been around 2.5x, so I will raise to 250.

I am in the Small Blind holding King-Ten offsuit. The Button has opened for 250. In a 4-handed game, this hand is well within the range of hands that should defend against a button steal attempt. While being out of position is disadvantageous, the pot odds are 2:1 to see a flop. I prefer calling over 3-betting here to keep the pot size controlled and avoid being forced to fold to a 4-bet from the Button or an aggressive squeeze from the Big Blind.

In the big blind with 4s5d, an offsuit low connector with poor playability. Facing a button raise to 250 by Pro (stack 13900) and a small blind call by Flash (stack 7800), the pot is 600 and I need to call 150 more for 4:1 odds. While pot odds are decent, my hand has low equity multiway, poor post-flop potential especially out of position against the button, and doesn't block strong hands. Pro's button raise could be wide, but Flash's call suggests some strength or speculative hand, making it unlikely to be a pure steal. Folding weak hands like this preserves stack in a deep tournament (hand 53/150, my stack 20750), avoiding speculative multiway pots where 45o struggles. Standard fold to avoid realizing equity issues.

The flop [Qs, Ah, 3c] heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Button). Holding Kh Tc, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) but currently only King-high. Checking is the standard and most prudent play here to see how Pro responds to the board. Leading out into the aggressor on an Ace-high board is risky without a made hand or a stronger draw.

I hold Pocket Sevens on a dry Q-A-3 rainbow flop. After opening pre-flop, the Small Blind called and checked to me. This board texture hits both ranges, but arguably hits the caller's flatting range (Ax, Qx, Broadway hands) reasonably well. My 77 is essentially a bluff-catcher here; betting likely folds out worse hands (lower pairs like 44-66) and gets called by better hands (any Ace or Queen). There are very few draws to protect against. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially call a bet on later streets if the opponent bluffs, or get to a cheap showdown.

The turn 6h is a blank. After Pro checked back on the flop, it's less likely they have a strong Ace or a Queen, as those hands typically continuation bet on such a board. However, I still only have King-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). Checking is the most disciplined play here to either see a free river card or evaluate the price if Pro decides to bet.

On the turn, the board reads [Qs Ah 3c 6h]. My opponent (Flash) in the Small Blind has checked to me again after checking the flop. My hand, Pocket Sevens, is currently a marginal made hand with showdown value. The presence of the Ace and Queen on the board makes it likely that if I bet, I will only get called by hands that beat me (Ax, Qx) and fold out hands that I beat (lower pairs, air). Checking back allows me to keep the pot small, realize my equity, and see a free river. I can re-evaluate on the river if facing a bet, but essentially I am trying to get to showdown cheaply.

The board is Ace-high and has been checked through on the flop and turn. Pro's decision to check back on an Ace-high flop as the pre-flop aggressor suggests they likely don't have an Ace or a Queen, or they are playing a very passive game with a marginal hand. With the river pairing the board (3h), my King-high has almost no showdown value against any pair. However, in a small pot where the action has been so passive, I will check. If Pro checks back, I see a showdown for free. If Pro bets, I will likely fold as it's difficult to represent a strong hand here given my own passive line.

I hold Pocket Sevens on a board of [Qs Ah 3c 6h 3h]. The action has gone check-check on the flop and turn. My opponent (Flash) checks the river again. My hand has showdown value against lower pocket pairs or total air, but will lose to any Ace, Queen, Six, or Three, which are definitely in the opponent's range. Betting here serves no purpose: worse hands will fold, and better hands will call. Therefore, the optimal play is to check back and take the free showdown to potentially win the pot against a weaker hand without risking more chips.

I am in UTG position with 5s 2h, which is statistically one of the worst hands in Texas Hold'em (unconnected, unsuited low cards). With a stack of 145 BBs, there is absolutely no incentive to get involved with this hand from early position. The correct and only standard play is to fold immediately to preserve chips.

I am on the button with 7-5 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand. While I have the benefit of position, the hand strength does not justify a raise to steal the blinds, especially against active opponents like Elon and Claude who have significantly larger stacks. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Folded to me in the small blind with As7s, a strong suited ace hand with good playability, blockers to strong aces, and flush potential. SB vs BB is a profitable spot to raise and steal the dead money (150 in pot). Calling 50 is passive and allows BB to check-raise or play poorly postflop. Raising takes initiative, builds the pot OOP with a strong hand, and applies pressure on Claude's BB range. Standard sizing around 3x the BB (300 total) balances value and fold equity without overcommitting deep stacks. Minni raise not aggressive enough.

Holding 3s8c offsuit in the BB facing a 3x raise from Elon. This is a weak, disconnected hand with poor playability post-flop. Even getting 2:1 pot odds, 83o is too weak to profitably defend against a standard raise. Folding is the correct play.

J7o is a weak hand to open from UTG, even in a 4-handed game. My stack is the shortest at the table, so I should look for better spots to commit chips rather than playing marginal hands out of position.

On the button with A5s after UTG fold, this is an excellent spot to raise. A5s is a strong suited ace hand with position, nut flush potential, straight possibilities, and ace blocker. Deep stacks allow for post-flop playability. Blinds are tight in history but vulnerable to steals. Standard raise size of 3x BB to build pot and take initiative. Pot 150, raising to 300 (3x) is optimal for fold equity and value.

2d5h offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a 3x raise from the button, I have no reason to defend this hand - poor equity, hard to make strong hands, and playing out of position. Easy fold.

I hold Ace-King offsuit in the Big Blind, a premium hand. Facing a raise from the Button (Elon), who likely has a wide range, I have a significant equity advantage. Since I will be out of position post-flop, 3-betting is superior to calling as it allows me to take the initiative, build the pot for value, and extract chips from dominated hands like AQ, AJ, or KQ, while folding out lower equity holdings. A 3-bet size of roughly 3.5x to 4x the open is standard when out of position. Raising to 1100 achieves these goals.

Preflop on the button with A♦5♠ facing a 3-bet from Pro in the BB to 1100 over my 300 open. Pot is 1450, to call 800 more for immediate odds of about 1.8:1, but with position postflop and deep stacks (my 20k, Pro 13k), calling realizes equity well. A5s blocks AA/AK, has nut flush/straight potential, and plays profitably vs Pro's likely 3-bet range (JJ+, AK, AQs+, broadway suited, some bluffs). Pro has shown aggression but also calls stations in history; folding wastes fold equity from my open, 4-betting risks cooler or forces off weaker hands we dominate OOP. Calling maintains pot control, uses position to outplay postflop.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AsKc) on a very wet and dynamic board (Kh Th 9c). While my hand is strong, this texture (high cards, connected, flush draw) connects heavily with the Button's calling range, which includes sets (TT, 99), two pair (T9s), and straights (QJs), as well as many draws. Being out of position in a 3-bet pot, c-betting risks getting raised, which would put me in a difficult spot. Checking allows me to control the pot size, induce bluffs from missed hands or semi-bluffs from draws, and comfortably call a bet. Solvers typically prefer checking this specific texture (K-T-9) from the Big Blind in 3-bet pots due to the caller's nut advantage.

On the flop of Kh Th 9c, checked to me on the button after calling a 3-bet preflop with Ad5s. This board is highly coordinated: two hearts (nut flush draw), open-ended straight draws (QJ, J8), and pairs like KT, K9, T9, plus sets (KK, TT, 99). My hand is pure Ace-high with no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (A-high blocks top of range slightly but no gutshot). Preflop, my call was correct for implied odds and playability vs Pro's polarized 3-bet range (value: JJ+, AK; bluffs: suited broadways, Ax suited). Pro's check OOP suggests either a trap with top pair/top ender (KQ, KJ, ATs), monster (set/straight), or missed hand planning to check-raise bluff. With deep stacks (19.75k effective vs Pro's 13.4k) and position, checking back is optimal: realizes equity for free on turn, controls pot size with no showdown value, avoids bloating pot OOP if Pro check-raises, and keeps initiative for turn betting if safe card. Betting as a bluff (e.g., 1500 into 2250) risks check-raise from draws/strength, and my blockers (Ad blocks AK/AQ) don't justify it on this wet board where Pro's range hits hard. Folding impossible since checked to me. Check preserves stack and sets up turn flexibility.

The flop action went check-check, which typically caps the opponent's range, suggesting they likely don't have the nuts (straight QJ) or a set, as those hands usually bet this wet texture to build the pot and deny equity. The turn 7c makes the board even more coordinated, bringing a second flush draw and connecting with lower straight cards. While I have Top Pair Top Kicker, betting out of position risks getting raised or folding out the weaker hands/bluffs I dominate. Checking is the prudent play to control the pot size, induce bluffs from missed draws or floats, and safely navigate to the river with a strong bluff-catcher. I plan to call a reasonable bet.

Turn board brings the 7c, adding a club flush draw to the already draw-heavy Kh Th 9c board (hearts flush draw, multiple straight draws like QJ, J8 possibilities). Pro checks OOP again after checking flop, which could represent weakness (missed draws, air), medium strength (top pair weaker kicker, pocket pairs below sets), traps (sets, two-pair, straights), or waiting to check-raise. My Ad5s is still ace-high no pair no draw, with Ad blocking some AK/AQ value. Preflop calling range realization was for position and playability vs Pro's 3-bet range (JJ+, AK, suited broadways/bluffs). Betting here as a bluff (~40-50% pot, 1000-1100 into 2250) risks check-raise from improved draws (flush now, combos), monsters, or even floats; my blockers don't strongly protect against calls. Effective stacks deep (13.4k Pro), so checking back realizes equity for free on scary board, controls pot size with marginal showdown value (beats pure bluffs/air), maintains position for river flexibility (bluff safe cards, value thin if hits ace/5 unlikely). History shows Pro aggressive but folds to pressure sometimes (e.g., Hand 4,17); no need to force value now. Check preserves stack for better spots in tournament.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AsKc) on a board of [Kh Th 9c 7c Jc]. The river card is extremely dangerous as it puts three clubs on the board (flush possible) and four cards to a straight (7-9-T-J-K), meaning any Queen or Eight makes a straight. Although I have the Kc, I only have four clubs total, so I do not have a flush. Betting here is likely negative EV; worse hands (like AJ or AT) will almost certainly fold given the scary board texture, while better hands (Straights, Flushes, Two Pair) will call or raise. Checking is the prudent play to realize my equity at showdown if the opponent checks back, or to evaluate a bet if they make one. I hold the Kc which blocks the second nut flush, giving me some information for a potential bluff-catch decision, but initiating a bet is not viable.

River J♣ completes a club flush (board has 9♣ 7♣ J♣, enabling any two clubs for a flush) on an already draw-heavy board (Kh Th 9c 7c Jc: heart flush draw, straight draws like Q8/J8 gutshots filled or semi-bluffs possible). Pro has checked OOP for the third street heads-up, representing either nuts (sets, two-pair, flush slowplay), showdown value (top pair/weak kicker, underpairs like 88-QQ), or total air giving up (missed draws, ace-high worse than mine). My Ad5♠ is ace-high (blocks some AK/AQ), no pair/draw/straight, but beats pure bluffs/air. Preflop call was for position/playability vs Pro's BB 3bet range (JJ+, AKo/s, AQs+, suited broadways/connectors, some bluffs); postflop checks keep us in ranges. Deep effective stacks (~13.4k Pro), but pot small (2250). Bluffing risks check-raise (flushes/straights/sets) or calls from medium strength (Kx, Tx, Jx now, pairs) that beat me; no strong blockers to nuts (no clubs, Ad irrelevant for clubs). Checking realizes equity for free vs bluffs/air (~10-20% of range?), controls pot, shows down to chop vs worse aces or win small pots. History shows Pro aggressive pre/post but folds to pressure sometimes; no need to risk stack when showdown value exists in position. Check optimal for tournament EV preservation.

UTG position with 87o offsuit is a standard fold. This hand has poor playability, low equity against typical calling ranges, and no strong post-flop potential from early position. Deep stacks allow waiting for better spots to maximize long-term EV without risking chips unnecessarily.

Pocket 3s on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a standard open situation - small pairs play well from the button as I can win the pot preflop or potentially flop a set. I'll raise to 250 as a standard 2.5x open.

I hold 8s 5c in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand (off-suit trash). Facing a standard Button raise to 250 from Claude, I am out of position and my hand has poor playability and low equity. Although I get 2:1 pot odds, my realized equity will be very poor. Defending here is a losing play in the long run. The correct action is to fold.

I have been dealt Pocket Kings (Ks, Kc), which is the second strongest starting hand in Hold'em. Claude has opened with a raise to 250 from the button, a position from which players typically raise with a wide range of hands. With such a premium hand, I must raise to build the pot and extract value. A 3-bet to 850 is a standard sizing from the Big Blind against a Button open, forcing Claude to either fold his weaker holdings or pay a premium to see a flop with hands that I likely dominate.

Facing a 3-bet with pocket 3s. I opened to 250, Flash 3-bet to 850. I need to call 600 into a pot of 1150 (about 1.9:1). With a small pocket pair, I'm essentially set-mining. I'll hit a set ~12% of the time. Flash has ~6100 behind after this 3-bet, giving decent implied odds if I hit. The math works: need to win ~8x my call (4800) and Flash's remaining stack covers that. Calling to set-mine is the play here - folding is too tight with position and implied odds, and 4-betting would be spew.

I have an overpair with Pocket Kings on a board of 5d-9d-7s. While this board is somewhat coordinated with straight and flush possibilities, my hand is currently very strong. I need to bet for value against Claude's likely range of mid-pairs (88, TT, JJ), top-pair holdings (like A9s or T9s), and various draws (flush and straight draws). A bet of approximately half the pot (850) is a standard sizing in a 3-bet pot to extract value and protect my hand without over-committing against potential sets.

Missed my set on a 5d-9d-7s board. I have an underpair to the entire board with no draws. Flash 3-bet preflop and is now c-betting. Against a 3-bet range, my pocket 3s have very little equity here. No diamond for a flush draw, no straight draw. The set-mine failed - time to fold and cut losses.

3c 4s offsuit from UTG is a clear fold. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker - low cards, offsuit, no connectivity worth playing for. From early position with 3 players to act behind me, I have no reason to put chips in with this hand.

I hold AJo on the Button, which is a strong starting hand in a 4-handed game. With the UTG player folding, I have a prime opportunity to open the pot to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds. A standard open raise size of 2.5x (250 chips) is appropriate here to build the pot while keeping the range of calling hands wide enough to include dominated hands. Folding is too passive, and limping forfeits the initiative.

I am in the Small Blind holding 3d 2d. Pro has opened with a raise from the Button to 250. Although these are suited connectors, they are at the absolute bottom of the range and perform poorly out of position. Calling would require me to play the rest of the hand first-to-act against the Button and potentially the Big Blind. Given the low equity and poor playability of this hand, folding is the standard and safest move to preserve my stack.

Pocket Kings is one of the best starting hands, especially in the big blind facing a button raise after two folds. Pro's button opening range is likely wide, including many Ax, suited connectors, broadway hands, and bluffs.
