Calling would allow multiway pots or postflop playability issues against overcards, so 3-betting isolates, builds the pot for value, and pressures their wide range. 900 is a standard 3.6x raise size over their 250 open, denying equity to worse hands and setting up ICM-friendly postflop dynamics with deep stacks. Fold equity is high, but we get called by worse often enough.

I hold AJo on the Button and face a 3-bet to 900 from the Big Blind (Elon). In a 4-handed game, opening ranges and 3-betting ranges are wider than in full-ring games. Folding AJo here would be too conservative given my positional advantage and deep stacks (over 150 BBs). A 4-bet would likely only get action from better hands and fold out worse ones that I dominate. Calling allows me to play a pot in position against a range that likely includes suited connectors, smaller pairs, and dominated broadway hands, in addition to premiums. I need to call 650 chips to play a pot of 1850, and my hand has sufficient equity and playability to justify a call.

The flop of 9h-5d-5c is excellent for our KK. It's a paired board with no flush draws and limited straight draw possibilities (mainly 67 or 78, unlikely in Pro's button 3-bet calling range). KK is an overpair, likely best hand against Pro's range which includes Ax, broadway cards, mid-pairs like 88-TT, and some bluffs or suited connectors that called the 3-bet. Betting extracts value from these one-pair or draw hands, protects our equity, and builds the pot when ahead. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards or draws. A 2/3 pot bet (~1200) balances value and protection, inviting calls from worse hands while not overbetting into potential sets (55/99) or two-pair, though those are a small part of range. Pro has ~14.7k behind, plenty for further action.

The opponent (Elon) 3-bet pre-flop from the Big Blind and is now continuing with a large C-bet of 1200 into a pot of 1850 (approx. 65% pot) on a paired board of [9h 5d 5c]. While I have position and a reasonable starting hand (AJo), this board texture favors the 3-bettor's range which includes many overpairs (TT+) and strong Ax hands (AK, AQ) that dominate me. The large bet sizing suggests strength or a desire to deny equity immediately. Floating with just Ace-high and no draw is difficult because I likely won't realize my equity if he fires a second barrel on the turn, and I am drawing very thin against his value range. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve chips.

I am in UTG position (first to act 4-handed) with Js 8s. While suited connectors and gappers gain value in short-handed play, J8s is on the very bottom edge of a playable range from the first position. With active players behind me (Elon and Claude) who have deep stacks, opening this hand risks facing a 3-bet or playing a bloated pot out of position with a marginal holding. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here to preserve my healthy stack of 147 BBs.

I have a strong suited Ace (Ad8d) on the Button in a 4-handed game. With the UTG player folding, I have a clear opportunity to open. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot, put pressure on the blinds, and maximize the value of my positional advantage.

KJo offsuit is a strong enough hand from the small blind facing a button raise, which is typically a wide range in a multiway pot potential but button stealing often. Folding is too tight given position and pot odds. Calling commits us OOP with a marginal offsuit broadway hand that plays poorly postflop. 3-betting takes the initiative, builds the pot when ahead, and applies pressure on a wide button range. Standard sizing around 3x the open to 900, given deep stacks and history of aggression from late positions.
74s is too weak to defend against a 3-bet. Facing a raise and re-raise, I'm getting poor odds with a marginal suited connector. Out of position with no fold equity makes this an easy fold.

I am on the Button with Ad8d, a strong suited Ace in a 4-handed game. I opened for 300 and Elon 3-bet to 900 from the Small Blind. Based on previous hands, Elon is an active and aggressive player who frequently uses 3-bets to apply pressure. Folding such a high-equity hand in position would be too tight. While I could 4-bet, calling allows me to use my positional advantage post-flop and keep Elon's bluffs in his range without over-committing my stack pre-flop. I have over 80 big blinds, so I have plenty of room to play a post-flop game.

Heads-up OOP vs button who called my preflop 3bet with KJo. Flop 9d Jc Qh gives me second pair (Js) with top kicker (K), a strong made hand on a connected board. My 3bet range is strong, and button's calling range includes QQ+, AK, AQ, AJ, KQ, QJ, suited broadways/connectors, pairs. I block KJ/QJ, and value bet vs weaker Qx (QTs+), Jx (AJs/JTs), Ax, smaller pairs/draws like KT/AT/TT. Betting builds pot when ahead, protects vs OESD (T8, KT, AT), and denies equity to floats. Check risks giving free card to draws or letting him rep QJ. Size 2/3 pot (1300) balances value/protection without overbetting deep stacks. Flash has 7750 behind, pot odds unfavorable for calls.

The flop of 9d-Jc-Qh is highly coordinated and hits a typical Small Blind 3-betting range very hard, including hands like AQ, KQ, QJ, JJ, and KTs. My hand, Ad8d, has completely missed this board, offering only a marginal backdoor nut flush draw and an overcard that may not even be clean given my opponent's range. Facing a lead of 1300 into a pot of 1900, the pot odds do not justify a call with only Ace-high on such a wet board, and a bluff-raise would be poorly timed against a range that likely connected. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my remaining stack.

Holding 9-2 offsuit in the UTG position is extremely weak. With a stack size of 7750 (77.5 BB), there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a poor hand, especially out of position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop on the button with 2h4h after UTG (Flash) folds. Excellent position with initiative against two blinds (Claude SB 15.8k, Pro BB 14.6k). 24 suited is speculative but has strong playability: potential for wheel straights, flush draws, and two-overcard potential postflop. Button opening range is very wide (~40-50% of hands) in this spot to steal dead blind money (pot 150). Folding is too tight; limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots without initiative. Raising builds the pot when called and applies pressure. Standard sizing folded-to-button is 2.2-2.5x BB (100 BB), so raise to 250-300. Choosing 250 to keep it standard and consistent with observed raise sizes in history, maximizing fold equity while keeping SPR reasonable for postflop play.

I have 8c9h in the SB facing a button open to 250. This is a connected hand with reasonable postflop playability against a likely wide button range. Getting 2:1 odds to call 200 into a 400 pot. This is a standard defend - hand can make straights and has enough equity to continue. Calling rather than 3-betting to keep the pot small with a speculative hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 5s, facing a Button raise to 250 and a Small Blind call. The pot odds are attractive (calling 150 to win a pot of 600, giving 5:1 odds), requiring only about 16-17% equity to break even on a raw equity basis. However, 85 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability. It is disconnected and unsuited. Additionally, playing a multi-way pot out of position against the Button aggressor creates significant reverse implied odds and difficulty realizing equity. Even with deep stacks (146 BBs), avoiding marginal spots with trash hands out of position is prudent to preserve the stack for better opportunities. The correct play is to fold.

Missed the flop completely with 8c9h on 2s Kd 7h. I have a double gutshot (6 or T completes a straight) but no pair. As the preflop caller OOP, I should check and let the aggressor continue. No reason to lead into him with air on a K-high board that likely connects with his button opening range.

On the flop of 2s Kd 7h, I have bottom pair with 2h4h (pair of twos, poor kicker but decent showdown value), plus backdoor heart flush draw and gutshot straight draw potential. Board is relatively dry: king-high, rainbow suits, no completed draws, limited straight possibilities (e.g., 3-5, 8-9-10). As preflop raiser in position vs. SB caller (Claude, who checked), I have strong range advantage. His check suggests weakness (missed K/7/Ax, floats, or traps), but c-betting exploits this: gets value from worse hands (weaker pairs, Ax), charges draws (e.g., 65s, 89s), and denies equity to air. Checking back risks giving free cards to overcards/draws and loses initiative. Standard c-bet size ~50-60% pot (300-360 into 600) balances value and bluffs from my button range. Betting 350 builds pot controllably while deep-stacked (200+BB), maintaining fold equity without overcommitting marginal hand. Maximizes EV in tournament by applying pressure heads-up.

I have 8c9h on 2s Kd 7h - complete air with only a backdoor straight draw. I need a 6 or T to pick up an open-ender, giving me 8 cards to improve to a draw (not a made hand). Pot is 950, need 350 to call = ~2.7:1 odds requiring ~27% equity. Against Elon's likely Kx heavy range on this board, I have maybe 15% equity at best. This is a clear fold - I'm not getting the right price with a weak backdoor draw out of position.

UTG position with 7c2c, a weak suited hand with low cards and significant gap. This is a standard fold pre-flop from early position to avoid difficult post-flop situations, especially with deep stacks and multiple opponents behind. Limping or raising would be exploitative but incorrect given the hand strength and position. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

Pocket 9s on the button is a premium hand in this spot. Elon folded UTG, leaving only the blinds. I should raise for value - this hand plays well heads-up or multiway. Standard raise to 250 (2.5x) to build a pot while in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s5s, facing a raise to 250 from Claude on the Button. While my hand is suited, it is a weak two-gapper. Playing this hand out of position against a button raiser is generally -EV due to the difficulty of realizing equity and the positional disadvantage. I am getting roughly 2:1 pot odds, but this is not sufficient to justify a call with this specific holding OOP. A 3-bet bluff is possible but risky without better blockers or a stronger read. The most prudent and standard play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. Claude has raised from the Button to 250. While the price to call is only 150 more into a pot of 400, Jack-Seven offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against a button open. I am currently the short stack at the table with about 76 big blinds, and there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand when I can wait for better spots to commit chips.
ATo in UTG at a 4-handed table is a solid opening hand. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

I am on the Button with 8h6h, facing a raise to 250 (2.5bb) from Claude in UTG. We are playing deep-stacked (approx 145bb effective). While suited one-gappers have playability and implied odds in position deep-stacked, playing 86s against a UTG opening range is generally loose and risks reverse implied odds (dominated flushes/straights). Claude has been active but UTG ranges should still be respected. Without a specific read that Claude is opening very wide from UTG or is exploitable post-flop, the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot or a weaker opening position to attack.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 7s, which is a very weak hand. Claude has raised from UTG (effectively the Cutoff in a 4-handed game). Calling out of position with such a weak hand against an active player like Claude is not profitable, especially with the chip leader, Elon, still to act in the Big Blind. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

3c8d is a complete trash hand offsuit with no high card value, connectivity, or suited potential. Facing an UTG raise from Claude, who has been aggressive in prior hands, in the big blind with 219 BB effective stack (my 21950/100). Standard play is to fold this unplayable hand preflop to avoid spew. No position, pot odds don't justify calling (calling 150 into 400 pot for ~3:1 but hand equity is abysmal vs UTG range), and raising would be a bluff with no blockers or fold equity vs a competent raiser. Preserve stack for better spots in tournament with 90 hands left.

I am in UTG position with Queen-Deuce offsuit (Qh 2s). This is a statistically weak hand with poor playability, often dominated by better Queens or pairs. In a 4-handed game, while ranges widen, Q2o is still below the threshold for a profitable opening raise. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately to conserve chips.

I am on the button with 2d 4h, which is a very weak starting hand. While being on the button offers a positional advantage, the hand itself has almost no equity and the blinds, Elon and Claude, have been active and possess larger stacks. There is no reason to risk chips on a bluff or a low-probability steal with such a poor hand at this stage of the tournament.

Small blind vs big blind heads-up preflop with K7s, a suited king with good playability, equity against a wide defending range, and blocks top pair. Raising steals the dead blinds money (pot currently 150), applies fold equity, and puts me in position to c-bet postflop effectively. Standard raise size of 3x BB (300 total) balances aggression without overcommitting deep stacks (mine 21.9k, villain 15.8k). Limping risks multiway pots or giving free flops to BB; folding is too tight given position and hand strength.

Jc9d is a reasonable hand to defend in the BB against a SB min-raise. Getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 into a 400 pot). J9o has decent playability and can flop well with straights, pairs, and draws. Calling is the standard play here.

Preflop as SB, I raised a suited King which is standard for stealing blinds and position. Flop 8h-2h-8s is a paired board with a heart flush draw, which misses my K7cc completely (no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw; just King-high with club backdoors). I'm OOP heads-up, pot 600. My range as preflop raiser includes overpairs, Ks, some bluffs, but villain's BB calling range (loose) hits this board decently: 88 for trips, 87/65 suited connectors for draws/straights, heart flush draws, Ax. Betting risks getting called by better and stacks deep (21k vs 15k). Check pot-controls, induces bluffs from villain's missed draws/ace-highs, allows check-call/raise if villain weak, or check-fold to bet. Favorable spot to realize equity passively; don't bluff into paired board OOP without strong equity.

I have J9o on an 8h2h8s board. Elon raised preflop and checked flop. I have J-high with a gutshot straight draw (need a T). This paired board is hard to connect with for both of us. Betting as a bluff could work, but checking back keeps the pot small with my marginal hand and lets me see a free card to potentially improve. If I bet and get check-raised, I'm in a tough spot. Checking is the safer play here.

Heads-up pot OOP on turn with Kc7c, pure air (king-high, no pair, no draws) on a paired 8h2h8s6d board that favors villain's BB calling range (sets/trips with 88, two-pair like 86s/82/68, stubborn flush draws, some straight draws like 75/54). Preflop raise was standard isolation/steal, flop check pot-controlled effectively after both checked. Turn 6d is relatively neutral (brings some straight draws but pairs board further, less scary for value). Betting commits more chips OOP with no equity, folds out better aces/draws, gets called by worse rarely (villain floats/calls wide). Checking maintains pot control, realizes thin equity passively, induces bluffs from villain's missed draws/A-high floats, allows cheap fold to bet or check-call if villain weak. Stacks deep (21k vs 15k), no need to bluff into sticky spot; live to fight another hand in tournament.

Elon has checked both flop and turn on this 8h2h8s6d board, showing weakness. I have J-high with a gutshot straight draw (need a T). With no showdown value but some fold equity given his passive line, a small probe bet makes sense to try to take down this pot. If he has an 8 or is trapping, I'll find out cheaply.

Facing a small 225 bet into 600 pot (~38% pot) OOP with Kc7c (king-high, backdoor clubs, king blocker) on paired 8h2h8s6d board. Pot odds 825:225 (~3.7:1) require only ~21% equity, which king-high provides vs villain's BB range for this line: thin value like 86s/86o/A8s/88 (40-50% equity) mixed with bluffs like Ax floats, missed heart draws (KhQh/JhTh), A-high (60%+ equity). Preflop raiser range checked twice, villain's check-call flop then small bet turn polarizes to bluffs/thin value. Deep stacks (217BB eff.) allow cheap call to realize equity passively, control pot OOP, block villain's top value (KK/AK), and take showdown or induce river bluff/check-behind. Folding too tight given odds/live cards; raising OOP with air folds out calling range bluffs and gets called by value. Standard call light OOP tournament spot.

Heads-up on river OOP with Kc7c (king-high, suited but no realized draw) on 8h2h8s6dJd board (paired eights, missed heart flush draw, jack brings minor straight possibilities like 97/75 but unlikely). Pot 1050, deep effective stacks (~140BB). Preflop as SB raiser checked flop pot-control air, check-called small turn bet realizing equity vs polarized range (thin value like 86/88/A8s or bluffs like Ax/missed hearts). River Jd neutral, doesn't improve me, favors value slightly (J8/J6) but villain's check-flop small turn-bet line thin value or bluff-heavy. First to act OOP: betting thin with king-high risks folding out worse (Q-high/draws) and getting called/raised by better (88 trips+, two-pair); check induces bluffs/check-behinds from missed draws/A-high, realizes equity passively, pot-controls perfectly after history. Tournament mid-stage, no need to force action with marginal showdown value; check maximizes EV long-term.

The river Jd dramatically improved my hand - I now have top pair Jacks with the paired 8s on board (effectively two pair J8 with 9 kicker). Elon has been very passive: checked flop, called my small turn probe, and checked river again. This screams weakness - likely a missed draw or weak showdown hand. I should value bet here. Around 60% pot (600-650) is a good sizing to get called by worse pairs or Ax hands that might look me up.

River OOP facing 650 bet into 1050 pot (~62% pot size) with Kc7c (king-high, clubs blocker/suited but unrealized, no pair/draw). Pot odds excellent: 650 to call for ~2350 pot post-call (~2.6:1, needs ~28% equity). My preflop SB raise standard isolation, checked flop OOP air pot-control on paired 88xx board, check-called small turn 225 probe realizing equity vs villain's polarized BB range (thin value 8x/86/A8 or bluffs Ax/missed hearts/floats). River Jd neutral-brings Jx two-pair/top pair/Jd blocker but misses main hearts flush draw, improves some floats (Jx str draws unlikely). Villain's check-flop / small turn-bet / river bet line polarized: value (trips 88/A8/J8/86 two-pair ~25-35% of range) but bluffy-heavy (missed Ahxh hearts ~20%, Ax floats/A-high ~30%, Q-high etc. ~15%) given loose BB defend and history. King-high crushes bluffs (100% equity), live vs most (beats Q/J/10-high, ~40-50% vs A8/J8 thin value, ~20% vs trips/86), blocks AK/KK. Deep eff stacks (~140BB Claude), tournament mid-stage favors cheap call realize equity/passive OOP after pot-control line, induces check-behind bluffs historically. Calling > folding (too tight, misses value), > raising (air OOP vs sticky range, folds better bluffs). EV+ call maximizes stack variance control.

K9s is a strong starting hand in a 4-handed game, especially from UTG which acts as the cutoff in this configuration. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially take the blinds or force better hands to make a decision.

7s2d is one of the worst possible hands: uncoordinated, low cards, no straight or flush potential. Facing an UTG raise from Flash, who has a strong range in early position. On the button with a deep stack, but this hand has zero playability or showdown value. Folding is mandatory to preserve chips for better opportunities in the tournament.

65o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. I'm out of position in the SB against Flash who opened from early position, indicating strength. This is an easy fold - not getting the right implied odds to continue with this offsuit weak connector OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with [Js Ah] (AJo). The UTG player (Flash) raised to 300 (3BB) in a 4-handed game. With only 4 players, UTG is effectively the Cutoff, so his range should be reasonably wide, likely including many hands I dominate or flip with (lower Ax, broadways, pairs). AJo is strong enough to defend. 3-betting is an option but risks isolating myself against a stronger range or getting 4-bet off my equity while out of position. Calling closes the action, gives me good pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 450, requiring ~30% equity), and keeps his range wide while keeping the pot manageable out of position. I will call.

I have flopped Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) on a board of [Jc 2h 4c] with [Js Ah]. The pot is 650. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Flash), the standard and most profitable line is to check. This allows the opponent to make a continuation bet with a wide range, including worse Jacks (KJ, QJ), flush draws, and pure bluffs that might fold if I were to lead out (donk bet). My hand is strong enough to check-call comfortably or even check-raise later, but leading out likely folds out the hands I want to extract value from or induces folds from air. I will check to the raiser.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with King-high and two overcards on a relatively dry Jack-high board. My opponent in the Big Blind checked to me, which is a standard defensive play. By placing a continuation bet of approximately half the pot, I can represent a Jack or a strong pocket pair, forcing my opponent to fold hands that missed the flop or weak draws. If I am called, I still have potential to improve to top pair on the turn with a King.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with [Js Ah] on a [Jc 2h 4c] board. The opponent (Flash), who raised pre-flop from UTG/CO, has made a standard continuation bet of 325 into a pot of 650. My hand is currently very strong against his range. Raising here has significant downsides: it isolates me against sets and overpairs, and it folds out his bluffs and weaker hands that I want to keep in the pot. By calling, I keep his range wide, allowing him to potentially bluff again on the turn or value bet a worse Jack. I am getting excellent odds to call and see the turn.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AJ) on a board of Jc-2h-4c-2s. The turn card pairs the board with a Deuce, which is generally a safe card for me as it reduces the likelihood of the opponent having a set of Deuces and doesn't complete any draws. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor who c-bet the flop. My hand is strong enough to continue, but leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out his bluffs and weaker hands while only getting action from hands that beat me or have high equity. Checking is the optimal play to induce further bluffs or value bets from worse hands (like KJ, QJ, or underpairs) and to keep the pot manageable. If he bets, I will likely call. If he checks, I can evaluate betting the river for value.

The turn card is a Deuce of Spades, pairing the bottom card of the flop. Pro has checked to me for a second time. While I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, my King-high currently has no direct draw and limited showdown value. However, Pro's range after calling the flop likely includes Jacks, middle pairs, or club draws. By checking back, I control the pot size, avoid a potential check-raise, and get to see a free river card where I might improve to a top pair with a King or win at showdown if Pro was on a draw.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AJ) on a board of Jc-2h-4c-2s-7h. The opponent checked back the turn, which caps his range and suggests he likely holds a marginal made hand (like KJ, QJ, TT) or a missed draw, rather than a monster. Since the opponent appears passive based on previous hands (check-calling lines), he is unlikely to bluff missed draws if I check, and he will likely check back marginal made hands that I beat. Therefore, betting for value is the best play to extract chips from weaker Jacks or pocket pairs that might hero call a reasonable sizing. I will bet slightly under half pot to encourage a call.

I raised pre-flop and c-bet the flop on a Jack-high board. Pro called the c-bet, suggesting they have a piece of the board or a draw. When the turn paired the deuce, we both checked. On the river, a seven appears, and Pro leads out for 600 into a 1300 pot. While King-high might occasionally win against a missed club draw, Pro's line (calling a flop bet and then leading the river after the turn went check-check) often represents a weak pair or a Jack looking for value from Ace-high. Given I have no pair and limited evidence of a pure bluff, I will fold and preserve my stack.

Preflop as UTG in 4-handed play with Ah6o. This is a weak offsuit ace with a low kicker, not suited, and vulnerable to domination by better aces. Even in a short-handed game, UTG ranges should be tight to avoid difficult postflop situations out of position with marginal equity. Folding preserves stack for better spots, aligning with long-term ICM and stack maximization in tournament.
AKo on the button is a premium hand. With only Elon folding and action on me, I should raise for value. Standard button raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to build the pot and potentially isolate.

I am in the Small Blind with 2d 8c, facing a raise to 250 from the Button (Claude). This is a 2.5x open. My hand is 8-2 offsuit, which is one of the worst hands in poker. Despite being in the Small Blind and facing a likely wide button opening range, this hand has very poor equity and extremely poor playability out of position. I am also not closing the action, as the Big Blind is still to act. There is no strategic merit in calling or 3-betting with this holding. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I am in the Big Blind holding 6 of Clubs and 3 of Clubs. Claude has raised to 250 from the Button. To call, I need to commit 150 more chips into a pot of 400, giving me roughly 2.7 to 1 odds. While 63s has some connectivity and flush potential, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against a stack that covers me. Given the 2.5x open and the fact that 63s flops poorly most of the time, I will choose to fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.
JTs is a strong suited connector, easily playable from UTG in a 4-handed game. Standard open raise to 2.5x BB.

I am on the Button with A3s (Ace-Three suited), facing a raise to 250 from Claude in the UTG/CO position. In a 4-handed game, A3s is a strong candidate for either a 3-bet bluff or a flat call. Given the deep stack depths (~150 BB), flat calling allows me to utilize my position to realize equity with a hand that has nut flush and straight potential without inflating the pot unnecessarily against a potentially strong range. Calling keeps Claude's range wider and avoids facing a 4-bet. I will call to see a flop in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Spades and Four of Diamonds (T4o). Claude (UTG) raised to 250 and Pro (Button) called. This is a very weak hand to play out of position against a UTG raiser and another caller. Calling would commit more chips with a hand that is likely dominated and difficult to play post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Pocket 77 is a strong hand preflop, roughly top 10% equity, crushing typical UTG opening ranges except for the top pocket pairs. Facing an UTG raise from Claude (who has been aggressive in prior hands) and a button call from Pro, calling OOP multiway offers poor implied odds for set-mining and risks difficult postflop spots. 3-betting thins the field, takes initiative, and leverages fold equity. Sizing to 900 (about 3.6x the open) is standard for positionally disadvantaged 3-bets deepstacked; builds a pot while denying equity to weaker calling ranges. Stacks are deep (~200BB), so no shove. Excellent spot to apply pressure.

JTs is a premium suited connector with excellent playability. Facing Elon's 3-bet to 900, I'm getting about 2.2:1 pot odds (650 to win ~1450). JTs has strong implied odds with flush and straight potential. Calling is standard here - folding is too tight with this hand, and 4-betting would be overplaying it. Pro behind me may call as well, adding to my implied odds.

I am in position on the Button with A3s. After calling the initial raise, the Big Blind squeezed to 900 and the original raiser called. I now face a decision to call 650 more into a pot of 2100, giving me roughly 3.2:1 pot odds. Given the deep stacks (~150bb), my position, and the nut potential of my hand (flush and straight capabilities), I have sufficient implied odds to call. I must be cautious on Ace-high flops due to kickers domination, but the price and position justify seeing a flop.

Pocket 77 remains an overpair on a highly coordinated 4-5-6 rainbow flop, vulnerable to straights (e.g., 67, 35 for wheel) and two-overcard straights (78, 23). Claude's UTG open-call range includes strong pairs (88+), suited connectors (56s, 67s, etc.), which connect well here. Pro's button call is wider but still favors speculative hands that hit. As preflop 3-bettor OOP multiway (~100BB deep), I have initiative and rep polarized range (sets, two-pair+, bluffs). Betting ~60% pot (1650) extracts value from worse pairs/draws (e.g., 88-TT, gutshots, overcards), protects against overcards/SDs, denies equity to floats, and builds pot when ahead (~60% equity vs combo of worse + draws). Check risks giving free cards to draws or facing aggression; check-raise multiway OOP is clunky. Thin value c-bet exploits positionally advantaged callers.

I have JcTc on a 6d5c4h board - completely missed. I have two overcards but no pair, no straight draw (would need 7-8 or 2-3), and only a weak backdoor flush draw with one club. Elon 3-bet preflop and is c-betting 61% pot on this connected low board that heavily favors his 3-betting range (overpairs, sets). My equity is terrible here - even hitting a J or T could be dominated. Pro is also behind me. Easy fold.

I hold Ad3d on a 6d5c4h flop, giving me an open-ended straight draw (any 2 or 7 makes a straight), a backdoor nut flush draw, and an Ace overcard. The pot odds require roughly 27% equity to call (1650 to win a total pot of 6050). Against a strong 3-betting range from the BB (likely overpairs like TT-AA or sets), my draw has approx 32% equity, making a call profitable. My position on the Button allows me to navigate future streets effectively, and the deep stacks (SPR ~2.6) provide good implied odds if I hit my straight. Raising likely isolates me against a stronger range, while folding is too weak given my equity.

Pocket 77 remains an overpair on a turn of 9c, which doesn't drastically change the board dynamics. The flop was highly coordinated (456 rainbow), and Pro's IP call of my large 1650 c-bet (~50% pot) into a 2700 pot indicates strength: likely overpairs (88-TT), straights (78xx), or strong draws (e.g., suited connectors like 78s, 35s for wheel). The 9c adds two-tone club flush draw potential and gutshots (e.g., T8, J8, 78 already straight), but doesn't complete obvious straights or nut flushes, bricking many pure draws while possibly hitting speculative 9x or floats. My 3-bet preflop OOP rep range is polarized (sets, two-pair+, premium overpairs, bluffs), and 77 blocks sets/TT. Equity remains strong (~55-65% vs. Pro's calling range: ahead of draws, Ax/high cards, behind only 88+/straights). OOP first to act heads-up, checking risks giving a free river to draws or facing aggression from value; check-call/raise is clunky deepstacked. Thin value bet ~60% pot (3600) extracts from worse pairs (88-TT? unlikely full strength call pre), incomplete draws, and Ax floats, denies equity, builds pot when ahead, and retains fold equity vs. missed draws. Pro's stack (12625) and pot (6050) allow sizing without overcommitting (~20% of my stack). Continues initiative from flop, standard vs. sticky IP caller. Avoid pot/overbet to keep villain in with worse.

I am holding Ad3d on a board of 6d-5c-4h-9c. I have an open-ended straight draw (needing a 2 or 7) but no flush draw, as the turn was a club and there is only one diamond on the board. Facing a bet of 3,600 into a pot of 6,050, I am getting approximately 2.7:1 odds, requiring ~27% equity to call. With only one card to come, my 8 outs give me roughly 17% equity. Additionally, I am drawing to the low end of the straight; hitting a 2 or 7 completes a very coordinated board where higher straights (held by hands like 87 or any 8) are possible, presenting significant reverse implied odds. Given the poor direct pot odds and the risk of being dominated even if I improve, the disciplined play is to fold and preserve my remaining stack.

I am in UTG position with Q6 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. With 126 BBs, I have a comfortable stack and no reason to get involved with marginal holdings from early position, especially with aggressive players like Elon and Claude behind me. Standard play is to fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey suited (Js 3s). While it is a suited hand, the kicker is extremely weak. In a 4-handed game, the button range is wider, but with aggressive opponents like Elon and Claude in the blinds, opening such a weak hand is risky. I have 68 big blinds and can afford to wait for a better opening or defense opportunity. I will fold this hand.

2d7c is one of the worst possible hands in poker: offsuit, unconnected low cards with no high card value or straight/flush potential. In the small blind facing a big blind who will defend wide, I have negligible equity even if called (dominated by any pair, ace, king, or better connectors). While pot odds are favorable (3:1 to call 50 into 150), post-flop playability is disastrous—no strong draws, bluffing is tough OOP, and implied odds are poor due to reverse implied odds if I hit. Deep stacks don't help this trash hand. Folding preserves my stack for premium spots in a tournament where survival and chip accumulation matter over 150 hands.

I am dealt 9d 3c in the UTG position. This is a very weak hand, and in a four-handed game, opening from UTG requires a much stronger range to be profitable. 93 offsuit is not a hand I want to play, especially out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with 87 suited connectors, a speculative hand with good playability due to suitedness and connectivity. Deep effective stacks (mine 24k, blinds around 12-16k). UTG folded, so heads-up or multiway vs blinds possible. Standard play is to raise for value/isolation and leverage position postflop. Pot 150, to call 100 (pot odds 2:1 but position warrants aggression). Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is balanced, folds out worse hands, gets value from blinds who defend wide based on hand history showing loose play and frequent calls. Avoid limping to maintain initiative.

I have pocket 8s in the SB facing a button open from Elon. This is a solid hand that plays well against a wide button opening range. With deep stacks (160+ BBs effective), calling to setmine is standard. 3-betting is also viable but calling keeps the pot manageable and gives me good implied odds to hit a set. I'll call and see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with a small pocket pair (4d 4s). Facing a Button raise to 300 and a Small Blind call, the pot is 700 and it costs me 200 to call. I am getting excellent pot odds (3.5 to 1 direct) and the stacks are deep (125BB+), providing high implied odds to set mine. While being sandwiched between the SB and Button is not ideal post-flop, the low price to see a flop makes calling the standard optimal play. I will look to hit a set or fold on most boards.

I have pocket 8s on a J76 board - middle pair below the jack. Elon was the preflop aggressor from the button. With two opponents, my 88 is vulnerable - any Jx has me crushed, and both players could have hands like JT, J9, KJ that hit this board. Checking here allows me to control pot size and see how the action develops. If it checks through, I can potentially extract value on later streets or fold to aggression.

I hold pocket fours on a J-7-6 board, which is an underpair to the board. In a multi-way pot with the pre-flop raiser on the button yet to act, leading out (donk betting) has negative expected value as I have very little equity and fold equity. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If facing a bet, I will almost certainly fold given my hand strength relative to the board and multiple opponents.

Preflop raise with 87 suited got called multiway by SB and BB, who have shown loose defensive tendencies in hand history. Flop 7d Jc 6h gives me middle pair (sevens) with an open-ended straight draw (5 or 9 makes straight), a very strong combo hand (around 25-30% equity multiway). Board has straight and flush draw potential, checked through to me on button with position and deep stacks. Betting for thin value against weaker pairs/draws/floats, to build pot with my equity, deny equity to opponents' draws. 2/3 pot size (600) is standard, balanced, gets calls from worse like 6x, Jx weak, draws. Checking risks giving free cards to improve past me.

I have 88 on J76 board - middle pair plus an open-ended straight draw (any 5 or 9 gives me a straight). That's 8 outs (~17% on turn). Getting 3.5:1 on a call (600 into 2100). With my pair equity plus straight draw, I have enough to continue. Elon could be c-betting a wide range here. Folding would be too tight, raising commits too many chips with a vulnerable hand.

I hold pocket fours on a J-7-6 board, which is an underpair. Facing a substantial bet of 600 (about 2/3 pot) from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, my hand is significantly behind their likely ranges. I have effectively only 2 outs to improve to a set, and even then, a set of fours might not be good on this connected board. I have no immediate straight or flush draws (only a backdoor straight draw needing a 5 on the turn). The pot odds of roughly 4.5:1 are insufficient to chase a 2-outer out of position against two opponents. The only profitable move is to fold.

The 9s completes my straight! Board is 6-7-J-9, I have 88, so I have 6-7-8-9 for the nut straight (only T8 ties). This is a great spot to check-raise for value. Elon was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the flop - he'll likely continue betting with Jx, overpairs, or draws. Checking allows him to fire again, then I can raise for maximum value.

Turn 9s hits one end of my flop OESD, improving to pair of 7s (matching the board 7d) plus a gutshot straight draw to the 10 for the 6-7-8-9-10 straight (4 outs), plus backdoor club flush draw (Jc on board). Strong combo hand with showdown value and equity heads-up vs Claude's SB calling range after flop c-bet call. Board texture brings straight completions (e.g., 5-8, 4-5? but mainly 5-8 now has nuts), spade flush draw possible, but my range as button preflop raiser/c-bettor is strong. Claude checks into me again, likely weak/medium strength/draw. Betting maintains initiative, gets value from Jx, 9x, weaker pairs, floats/draws that missed; denies equity to villain's remaining draws. Pot 2100, bet 2/3 pot (1400) is standard size for value/protection deepstacked (~150BB effective vs Claude), balanced, allows multi-street play while building pot. Checking risks free river cards or losing initiative.

I have the nut straight (6-7-8-9) with my 88. Only T8 ties me. Elon bet 1400 into 3500. This is a clear value raise spot - I want to get paid by his Jx hands, overpairs, or draws. Raising to around 3800 extracts value while keeping him in with hands like JT, AJ, QQ, KK that he'd continue with.

Facing a check-raise on the turn from SB Claude after a flop check-call. Board: 6h-7d-9s-Jc. Hand 8c7c gives pair of 7s + gutshot to 10 for 6-7-8-9-T straight (4 outs ~8% equity). Also backdoor club flush draw. Claude's range for OOP check-call flop c-bet then check-raise turn: polarized strong - value like JJ, J9/J6/97 two pairs, 88/99/66 sets, T8 nuts straight (flop gutshot now hit); bluffs like missed SD/HD (spades/hearts), overcards/air but less likely OOP twice. Pot odds 2400 to call into 7300 (~4:1, 20% equity needed), but vs value-heavy range equity ~12-18% (pair often dominated, gutshot clean but thin). Raise too thin with vulnerable hand (effective stacks his 11k); call risks stack commitment deep (~150BB), bad rivers if miss. Tournament mid-stage (hand 67/150), fold medium-strength hand to credible strong line, preserve stack for superior positions/exploits vs loose field shown in history.

UTG position pre-flop with 6s8c offsuit, a weak speculative hand with poor playability, low equity against ranges, and multiple players to act behind including deep-stacked opponents. Standard tight strategy dictates folding trash hands from early position to preserve stack and avoid difficult post-flop spots out of position. No reason to limp or raise.

On the button with Ah3d after UTG folded. A3o is a standard button open - I have position and an ace. Will raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down or play in position postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit (A5o) facing a Button raise to 250 from Claude. Claude has been very active and opening wide from late position. While A5o has an Ace blocker and some straight potential, it is a difficult hand to play out of position, especially against a potential Big Blind squeeze or post-flop aggression. Calling invites a multi-way pot where I am out of position with a marginal hand that often has kicker issues. 3-betting is a viable counter-strategy to a wide opener, but typically suited wheel Aces are preferred for that role. Given the 2.5x raise size and my stack depth, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot or a suited hand to defend is the prudent, lower-variance play. I fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit (Jc 3s). Claude has raised to 2.5bb (250) from the Button. While the pot odds are roughly 2.7 to 1, this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly post-flop out of position. Given the stack sizes and the fact that Claude is an active and capable player, defending with such a marginal hand is a losing strategy in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.
6h7d is a weak offsuit connector from UTG. This hand lacks the playability of suited connectors and is too weak to open from early position in a 4-handed game. Folding is the correct play here.

I am on the Button with [Tc, 8c] in a 4-handed game. The action has folded to me. Ten-Eight suited is a reasonable speculative hand to open-raise from the Button, aiming to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. With effective stacks over 60BB, this hand has good playability post-flop. A standard open size of 2.5x BB (250) balances the risk/reward, putting pressure on the blinds while keeping the pot manageable if called.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Ten of spades, which is a strong starting hand in a four-handed game. The Button (Pro) has opened for 2.5x the big blind. While calling is an option to see a flop, 3-betting allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately, or at least thin the field. Given my stack of approximately 67 big blinds, a 3-bet to 850 is a standard size that puts pressure on the Button and the Big Blind. If I am called, I have a hand that plays well post-flop with both high-card and flush/straight potential.

Preflop in BB with T2s (suited but low and gappy). Facing a button raise to 250 from Pro (wide range possible) and a 3-bet to 850 from short-stacked SB Flash (5875 behind, likely strong or polarized). Pot 1200, to call 750 OOP multiway would commit ~3.5% of my deep stack to a marginal hand with poor playability and equity vs 3-bet ranges (est. <30% heads-up, worse multiway). No strong draws or straight potential justify calling; fold preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 69/150 hands played.

I opened with Tc8c from the Button to 250, and Flash 3-bet to 850 from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 1200, and it costs me 600 to call, offering 2:1 pot odds (33% equity required). While I have position, T8s is a speculative hand that suffers from reverse implied odds against a strong 3-betting range (dominated by AT, TT, 88+, etc.). Flash's stack is effectively ~58BB, creating an SPR of about 3.2 post-flop, which reduces my maneuverability and implied odds for hitting straights or flushes compared to deeper stacks. The 3-bet sizing (3.4x) is on the larger side, making the price less attractive. Given the marginal nature of the hand and the risk of domination, folding is the disciplined decision.

I am in UTG (which is effectively the Cutoff in 4-handed play) holding 8s5d, a very weak off-suit unconnected hand. With 120BB, there is no need to loosen my opening range to include trash hands like this. The expected value of playing this hand is negative. I will fold.

I am on the button with Ten of Hearts and Four of Diamonds. This is a very weak offsuit hand. While I have position, T4o does not play well post-flop and is not worth a raise or a call against the two large stacks in the blinds (Elon and Claude). I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to improve my stack.

AKo is a premium hand, top of my range. Folded to me in the small blind with only BB behind. Raising steals the dead money in the pot (150) frequently or builds a pot heads-up where I have position and strong equity against BB's calling range. Standard raise size of 3x the BB (300 total). Claude has been aggressive historically, but AKo performs well post-flop. Deep stacks allow for flexible play.
T4o is a weak hand to defend from the BB. Elon has been active and raised from SB. Out of position with a marginal hand - better to fold and save chips.

I am UTG with Queen-Deuce of Hearts. While suited, this is an extremely weak hand to open from early position, even in a four-handed game. My stack is healthy at approximately 70 big blinds, and there is no reason to risk chips on a marginal hand when I will have better opportunities to accumulate chips later in the tournament. I will fold and wait for a stronger opening range.

On the button in an unopened pot after UTG fold, J6s suited is well within my opening range. Suited connectors play well multiway or heads-up postflop due to implied odds and potential for straights/flushes. Deep stacks support speculative hands. Standard raise size around 3x BB to 300 total to apply pressure on blinds and take initiative. History shows frequent small raises preflop, fitting aggressive tournament play to maximize stack growth.
Q3o in the SB facing a button raise. This is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Not strong enough to 3-bet or call profitably. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with T5o facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Elon). The pot odds are roughly 3.25 to 1 (calling 200 to win a pot of 650), requiring about 30.7% equity. T5o is a very weak, disconnected, unsuited hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against the chip leader. It dominates very few hands in a button opening range and suffers from reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and prudent play here to preserve chips for better spots.
UTG position with 4h6d, an offsuit, unconnected low-card hand with poor playability multi-way. Standard fold to avoid risking chips pre-flop with trash. Preserve stack for better spots in tournament.
On the button with A2o after UTG folds. This is a standard button steal spot - ace-high has decent equity and I have position. Raising to 2.5x to take down the blinds or play in position postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d Ts facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Claude). Claude has been opening frequently from late position, so his range is likely wide. However, T9o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600), requiring about 33% equity realization. While T9o has raw equity against a wide range, realizing that equity OOP is difficult, and I risk being squeezed by the Big Blind or dominated. Folding is the most prudent long-term play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. Claude, the chip leader on the Button, has raised to 2.5x. While the odds to call are 2.6:1 (150 to win 400), T3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an aggressive big stack. Conserving my 70BB stack for better spots is more prudent than defending with one of the worst hands in the deck.
UTG with 4h9h - this is a weak suited hand that doesn't play well from early position. Not a profitable open from UTG in a 4-handed game. Easy fold.

I hold 8d 2h on the Button, which is a very weak starting hand (8-high, unsuited, disconnected). Although I am in position, the hand has very little playability or equity. With two players left to act in the blinds, including the chip leader in the Big Blind who has been active, attempting to steal here is not profitable. The standard play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 97o. The action has folded to me. While standard play often involves completing or raising in a blind-vs-blind scenario, my opponent in the Big Blind (Elon) is the chip leader and has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament. With a stack of approximately 69 big blinds, I am currently the shortest stack at the table. I choose to fold this marginal hand to avoid playing out of position against a deep-stacked, aggressive opponent, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the UTG position with T8o (Ten of Clubs, Eight of Hearts). With 4 players left, UTG is the cutoff position, but T8o is still a very weak hand to open with. It is dominated by many hands in opponents' ranges (Tx, 8x, pairs) and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. My stack is healthy (approx 119 BB), so there is no pressure to make loose moves. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

On the Button in a 4-handed game, 78o is a marginal opening hand. While it is often included in a wide Button opening range, both blinds (Elon and Claude) have significantly larger stacks and have demonstrated aggressive play in previous hands. Given the current table dynamics where the blinds are frequently defending or 3-betting, I will opt for a disciplined fold to preserve my stack for more favorable spots, as 78o has poor playability when facing resistance from the big stacks.

Unopened pot preflop with folds from UTG and Button. In SB vs BB with deep stacks (~200BB effective), 86o is a standard min-raise hand. It has straight potential, some playability, and good implied odds if flopped well. Raising applies pressure to steal the dead money (150 in pot) and takes initiative. Min-raise to 200 total is GTO-ish for SB steal range (~40-50% of hands include this). BB (Claude) calls wide in history, but we can fold to 3-bet. Completing or folding misses value.

I have 3h7c in the BB facing a min-raise from Elon in the SB. This is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity. While I'm getting 3:1 odds (100 to win 300), 73o is at the bottom of any reasonable defending range. The hand has minimal potential to make strong holdings, and playing it out of position against a larger stack is -EV. Folding is the disciplined play.

In a 4-handed game, Queen-Seven suited (Qs7s) is a marginal hand from Under The Gun (UTG). While the table size justifies a wider opening range, I am currently the shortest stack at the table with roughly 69 big blinds. The players behind me—Elon, Claude, and Pro—all have significantly larger stacks and have shown a willingness to be aggressive. Opening with a weak Queen suited out of position often leads to difficult post-flop scenarios or losing chips to a 3-bet. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous spot or better position.

On the button with 7cTc (suited connectors), multi-way potential or steal opportunity after UTG fold. Deep stacks (over 100BB), position allows profitable play post-flop. Standard open-raise size of 3BB (300) to apply pressure on blinds, generate fold equity, or build pot for implied odds if hit draws/straights. Excellent speculative hand in position; calling too passive, folding too tight.

Jc8c is a decent suited connector in the SB facing a button open. Good playability with straight and flush draw potential. Against a button steal, this is a reasonable defend. Calling to see a flop rather than 3-betting out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit (2h Ts), facing a Button raise to 300 and a call from the Small Blind. The pot odds are attractive (calling 200 to win a total pot of 900, requiring about 22% equity), but T2o is one of the worst hands in poker. It has very poor playability, reverse implied odds (dominated by better Tens), and I would be playing out of position against two opponents, one of whom is the aggressor on the Button. Despite the deep stack depth (117bb), defending here is negative EV due to the hand's weakness and lack of realization equity. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Board is 3h 9c 3s - completely missed my Jc8c. I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. This is a dry, paired board that favors the preflop raiser. With no made hand and only backdoor equity, checking is the best option. If Elon checks back, I get a free card to improve. If he c-bets, I can evaluate based on sizing whether my backdoor draws justify a call.

Preflop raise with suited connectors in position was standard. Flop is 3h9c3s, a dry paired board that misses most of Claude's likely SB calling range (which is wide and weak). I have no pair, but backdoor club flush draw and straight potential. As preflop aggressor on the button heads-up, with Claude checking, this is a prime spot for a continuation bet to exploit fold equity. His check suggests weakness; betting takes it down often while protecting against improves. Half-pot sizing balances value from any weak pairs/draws and bluffs well without overcommitting deep stacks.

Facing a 450 c-bet into ~700 pot on 3h9c3s. I have Jc8c - completely whiffed with only backdoor club flush and overcards. This paired dry board heavily favors the preflop raiser. My equity is poor (~15-18%) and I need ~28% to call. No real draws to justify continuing. Easy fold.

KQo offsuit is a premium hand UTG in 4-handed play, warranting a raise for value and initiative. Standard sizing around 2.5x BB given deep stacks and aggressive field history. Builds pot, denies equity to weaker hands, and plays well postflop from any position. Limping or folding too passive; opponents like Claude and Pro defend wide, Flash shorter stack may react dynamically but hand crushes typical calling ranges.
J4o is a weak hand. Facing an UTG raise from Elon, this hand doesn't have the strength to profitably call or 3-bet, even with button position. Easy fold.

I hold AQs in the Small Blind, a premium hand. Elon in UTG has been very active recently, opening frequently (hands 67, 70, 71, 74, 75), suggesting a wide range that I dominate. While flat-calling is an option to control the pot size against the chip leader, playing AQs passively out of position often leads to difficult spots and allows the Big Blind to realize equity cheaply. A 3-bet is the superior play to isolate the loose opener, extract value from his weaker holdings (dominated Aces, Kings, Queens), and take the initiative. Given the 250 open and being out of position, a 3-bet to ~3.5x-4x is standard. I will raise to 900.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8c 6s. Elon raised from UTG and Pro has 3-bet to 900 from the Small Blind. 8-6 offsuit is far too weak to call a 3-bet, especially when facing action from two players who likely have strong ranges in this configuration. Folding is the only sensible play to preserve my stack.

KQo offsuit is a premium hand UTG, and facing a 3-bet from Pro in the SB after Flash folds. Pro's 3-bet sizing to 900 (3.6x my open) is large but SB often 3-bets wide vs UTG opens in 4-handed play, including Ax hands, broadway, suited connectors, and pairs that we dominate or have live cards against. Calling OOP heads-up is suboptimal as KQo plays poorly postflop without initiative, vulnerable to floats and c-bets. Folding is too tight given our equity (~40-45% vs typical 3-bet range) and blockers to AA/KK. Best is to 4-bet for value/thin value from worse broadway/Ax and to deny equity/put pressure. Sizing to 2250 (~2.5x the 3-bet) is standard, builds a pot we can continue aggressively, represents strength, and Pro's stack of 10k allows fold equity if bluffing. Effective stacks deep enough postflop but initiative crucial OOP.

I am in the Small Blind with AQs, facing a 4-bet to 2250 from Elon (UTG). Elon has been extremely active, raising almost every recent hand, which widens his range significantly. While a 5-bet jam is an option, my stack depth (~100BB starting) makes shoving ~108BB over a ~22BB raise an overplay that isolates me against his strongest hands (AA, KK, AK). Calling is the best line: it keeps his range wide (including bluffs and dominated hands like AJ/KQ/lower pairs), offers a reasonable price to see a flop, and sets up a manageable SPR of roughly 1.9 post-flop, where I can play fit-or-fold or check-call depending on the texture. Folding is too weak given his hyper-aggressive profile.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with AsQs on a very dangerous monotone board (Td Qd 9d). The pot is large (4600) and the SPR is roughly 2. The opponent, Elon, 4-bet pre-flop and has been very aggressive. This board texture heavily favors ranges that contain sets (QQ, TT, 99), straights (KJ, J8), and flushes. As the pre-flop caller, this board hits my perceived range well, but my specific hand is vulnerable as I have no diamond and am drawing nearly dead against a flush or set. Leading out (donk betting) has little merit as it likely folds out his bluffs and gets called/raised by hands that crush me (overpairs with a diamond, sets, flushes). Checking is the standard play to the pre-flop aggressor to see what they do. If they check back, I control the pot size. If they bet, I will evaluate based on sizing, but caution is paramount given I have no redraws.

Preflop: 4-bet KQo OOP vs Pro's 3-bet was correct for value/isolation as it crushes broadway/Ax in SB 3-bet range and has blockers. Pro's call suggests QQ+, AK/AQ, AdXd, or occasional bluffs like suited connectors but tighter post-4bet. Flop TdQd9d hits my Q top pair top kicker perfectly for value, while board is extremely draw-heavy (any diamond flush draw, J8/JT open-enders, straight flush draws). Pro checks SB first-in postflop, showing potential weakness (missed draws, Ax no pair, or trapping slowplay monster). With pot 4600, Pro 9525 behind (SPR ~2), betting ~2/3 pot extracts value from worse (AJ/KJ/AQ, flush draws, gutshots), protects vs draws realizing equity, and denies cheap turn cards. Checking risks giving free card to draws or check-raise bluff. Standard sizing 3000 balances value/protection without overbetting vs possible monsters; deep effective stacks allow continuation if raised.

I am facing a large continuation bet (3000 into 4600) from an aggressive opponent who 4-bet pre-flop. The board [Td Qd 9d] is extremely dangerous, being monotone and highly connected. While I hold Top Pair Top Kicker with AsQs, I have no diamond in my hand. This is critical because I am drawing dead against any made flush, and I have very poor equity against sets (TT, 99, QQ), straights (KJ), or overpairs with a diamond (AdAx, KdKx). Even against semi-bluffs like AdKx (which contains the nut flush draw and a gutshot), I am often statistically behind or flipping. Given the wetness of the board, my lack of redraws, and the opponent's strong sizing indicating either the nuts or a high-equity draw, my hand is effectively a bluff-catcher that cannot withstand further pressure. Preserving my healthy stack (~95BB) is superior to calling in a spot where my range is crushed.
KTo is a solid opening hand in 4-handed play from UTG. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

I hold 5h4h on the Button. The player in UTG (effectively the Cutoff in a 4-handed game) raises to 2.5BB. While 54s is a suited connector with playability, it is on the weaker end of the range. Furthermore, the Big Blind (Elon) is sitting with a massive stack and has shown aggressive tendencies (squeezing/3-betting), making a flat call unprofitable due to the risk of being squeezed out of the pot. A 3-bet is a possible bluffing option, but given my image and the depth of stacks, simply folding is the most prudent and disciplined play to avoid a high-variance spot with a marginal hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 5s. Claude has raised to 250 from UTG. While 85s is suited, it is a very weak hand to defend from the Small Blind against a raise from an early position player. Playing out of position for the rest of the hand against a large stack like Claude makes this a clear fold to preserve chips.

Facing a UTG raise to 250 from Claude in a 4-handed game, heads-up now after folds. My K7s suited in BB has excellent playability, good equity (around 35-40% vs typical EP opening range), and pot odds are favorable (150 to call into 400, ~27% needed). Deep stacks allow realizing equity post-flop. Claude appears aggressive from history, but defending wide in BB vs EP is standard. Folding too tight; 3-betting risky with dominated king. Call to see flop.

Heads-up on the flop after calling Claude's UTG raise pre-flop with K7s. Board: 9h 2d 7c. I flopped middle pair top kicker (7s pairs 7c, Kc kicker), which is a strong but vulnerable hand on this dry rainbow board. No flush draws completed really, straight draws minimal (T8). Pot 550, checked to me (assuming Claude checked first). Deep stacks (my 24k, his 18k). Strong showdown value, protects against overcards (K, A, Q, J, T likely in his range). His UTG range: overpairs (88+), AKo/AQs+, broadway. I have ~35-45% equity vs that typically. Betting for value/thin value vs worse pairs/draws, protection, and to deny equity. Don't want to check and let him free-roll or check-raise bluff. Size 2/3 pot (approx 367) to build pot while keeping fold equity. 350 is fine integer. Claude aggressive historically, may fold worse or weaker.

I have KTo and completely missed this 972 rainbow flop. Elon donk-bet 350 into a 500 pot. I have two overcards but no pair and no draw on this dry board. While I could float with position, the dry texture favors made hands, and Elon's lead suggests he connected with something (likely a 9 or pocket pair). With only ~12% equity to hit an overcard and no other draws, the pot odds aren't there. Folding is prudent here.

I am in UTG position with 6h 4c, which is a very weak offsuit hand. With 95 big blinds, there is no pressure to play marginal hands, let alone trash hands like this. Standard strategy dictates a fold from any position, especially UTG. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a four-handed game. Since the action folded to me, I will put in a standard open-raise of 3x the big blind to 300 chips. This allows me to take control of the pot in position and potentially win the blinds immediately, or play a post-flop game with a range advantage against the blinds.

KJ offsuit is a strong hand from the small blind facing a button open-raise. Button ranges are very wide in this spot, including many Ax, broadway cards, suited hands, and pairs that I dominate or have good equity against. Calling puts me out of position multiway potentially, which is suboptimal with this hand that plays better postflop when I have initiative. A 3-bet applies fold equity against Flash's wide range and gets value from worse broadway hands. The BB (Claude) may fold or call light, but my deep stack allows me to continue postflop if necessary. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is standard for value and protection. Flash's shorter stack (6475) adds pressure but doesn't change the play.

Holding 3d4h in BB facing a 3-bet from Elon. This is one of the weakest possible hands and doesn't play well even with position, which I don't have. Getting ~1.6:1 odds but 34o is crushed by any 3-bet range. Clear fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit and opened for 300. Elon, the chip leader in the small blind, has 3-bet me to 900. While I have position for the rest of the hand, A-8o is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a standard 3-betting range (like A-9+, or pocket pairs). Given that I am currently the shortest stack at the table with about 65 big blinds, I prefer not to commit a significant portion of my stack to a 3-bet pot with a mediocre Ace against the table's most aggressive player. I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot to use my position.

I am dealt Jack-Three offsuit in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is a very weak starting hand with poor playability and low showdown value. Opening or calling from early position with this hand is unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a stronger hand or a more advantageous position.

2s5c offsuit is among the worst starting hands in poker, with negligible equity against typical calling ranges from blinds. Position on button provides some flexibility, but this hand lacks suitedness, playability post-flop, or straight potential without significant gaps. Pot odds of calling 100 into 150 are poor given multiway potential and reverse domination risks. Preserving stack for superior hands in late tournament stage maximizes long-term EV.

AJo is a strong hand heads up against only the big blind. With Flash and Elon folding, I should raise to put pressure on Pro. Standard 2.5x raise to 250 is appropriate - this hand plays well heads up and I want to build the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with K6o facing a raise from the Small Blind to 250. The pot is currently 350 (100 BB + 250 SB bet), and I need to call 150 more to see a flop. This gives me pot odds of 150/500 or 30%. Against a typical wide Small Blind opening range, K6o has significantly more than 30% equity (often 45-50%). Additionally, I will have position on the raiser post-flop with a deep stack (94 BBs). Defending here is standard to prevent the SB from profitably stealing too often. A 3-bet is risky with a weak King, so a call is the most prudent play.

I flopped top pair with a strong Jack kicker on Ah-2h-7c. This is a great flop for my range as the preflop raiser. I should c-bet for value and to charge the heart flush draw. Betting around 60% pot (300) is appropriate.

On the flop of [Ah 2h 7c], I hold King-high (Ks 6h) with no pair and no significant draws. The opponent (Small Blind) has bet 300 into a pot of 500 (60% pot). This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, as they hold all strong Ax combinations. Although I have position, my hand has very poor equity and playability against a range that includes top pairs, flush draws, and better high cards. The price of 300 is too steep to float with just King-high on this wet, Ace-high board. Folding is the standard and correct play.

UTG in 4-handed play with K3o (King-Three offsuit). This is a marginal hand at best, offsuit, disconnected, poor kicker, and terrible post-flop playability. Even in short-handed games, UTG opening range typically starts with better kings like KQo+, suited connectors, or pocket pairs; K3o is firmly a fold. No limpers ahead, pot is small (150), but risking chips with such a weak hand pre-flop is -EV. Opponents have varying stacks, with Flash shortest (~63BB), but domination risk and lack of equity make folding correct to preserve deep stack (~256BB) for premium spots. Goal is long-term stack maximization over 150 hands; discipline avoids spew.
7s4d is a very weak hand. While I have position on the button with only blinds left, 74o has poor playability and limited high card value. Not worth a steal attempt with this weak of a holding.

Action is folded to me in the Small Blind. I hold 9c 8c, a strong suited connector which is well above the median hand strength for a Blind vs Blind encounter. With 4 players left, playing aggressively is key. I have a stack of ~92bb and cover the Big Blind (Flash, ~63bb). Limping is an option to keep the pot small out of position, but raising puts immediate pressure on the Big Blind to fold equity or defend with a worse hand. 98s plays well post-flop if called. I will raise to 300 (3bb) to charge the Big Blind for seeing a flop and to maximize fold equity.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Clubs and Six of Spades. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. My hand is quite weak and does not play well post-flop, even though I will have position. At 63.75 big blinds, I need to be selective about which hands I defend. T6o is generally at the bottom of a defending range against a 3x open. Given the current tournament stage and the strength of the raise, I choose to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.
T9o from UTG in a 4-handed game. This is a playable hand with 4 players - UTG plays more like cutoff. I have a healthy stack at 185BB. Standard open to 2.5x.

I hold 85o on the Button facing an UTG open raise to 250. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity. There are two players left to act behind me in the blinds. This is a standard fold.

I am in the Small Blind holding Jack of Hearts and Deuce of Diamonds. Claude has raised from UTG to 250. My hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position, especially against an UTG opening range. As the shortest stack at the table, I need to be selective about the pots I enter. Folding here is the most prudent move to preserve my stack for better starting hands.

Preflop in the big blind facing a UTG raise from Claude to 250 (needing 150 more to call). My hand is 2s Qh, which is extremely weak: offsuit, disconnected, low equity against a strong UTG raising range. No suitedness, straight potential, or pair value. Pot odds are about 2.67:1, but with such poor post-flop playability and reverse implied odds, folding is optimal to preserve stack. No history suggests Claude is overly loose UTG. Raising is suicidal with this hand.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 4-handed game with Ace-Queen offsuit (Qc As). This is a premium hand, especially short-handed. My stack is healthy at ~94 BB. Limping is passive and allows the blinds to realize equity for free. A standard open raise is the correct play to build the pot and seize the initiative. Recent table history shows standard opens are typically 2.5x (250) or 3x (300). I will open to 250 to value-bet my strong hand while keeping the pot manageable if 3-bet by the larger stacks behind me.

I am on the button with King-Five offsuit. Pro has raised to 2.5x the big blind from UTG. In a 4-handed game, K5o is a weak hand to play against an opening raise from early position, as it is frequently dominated by better Kings (K8s+, KTo+) and Ace-high hands. While I have position, calling would likely put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a marginal hand. Folding is the most prudent action to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Qd7d is a marginal suited hand at best. Facing a UTG raise from Pro (9125 stack), who typically raises with a strong range including pairs, broad aces, and broadway cards. In SB position, out of position post-flop, equity is low (~25-30% vs strong open range). Pot odds are ~3:1 but not enough to justify calling with poor playability OOP. Deep stack (25k) allows waiting for better spots. 3-betting has poor equity and Pro may call/4bet light. Standard fold to preserve stack in tournament.

Holding 9c5c suited in the BB facing a UTG min-raise of 250. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 400, getting about 2.7:1 odds. 95 suited has some playability with flush potential and can make straights. While not premium, the pot odds combined with closing the action make this a reasonable defend. Pro's UTG range should be tighter, but the price is right.

Flopped backdoor flush draw with 5c9c on 2c-6d-3s. I have no pair, no straight draw, just backdoor clubs. The board is low and dry, missing my hand entirely. Pro opened UTG and likely has overcards or a pocket pair. With no immediate equity and position, checking is standard here - I can re-evaluate on the turn if I pick up equity or fold to aggression.

The board comes 2c 6d 3s, which is a very dry, low texture. As the pre-flop raiser with AQo, I have two strong overcards and good showdown value. This board texture hits the Big Blind's defending range (low pairs, sets, connectors) reasonably well, perhaps better than my broadway-heavy range, although I retain the advantage of having all high overpairs. Given the opponent Claude's history of being sticky and capable of check-raising (e.g., Hand #52, #67), betting risks getting raised off a hand with decent equity or isolating myself against better hands. Checking back allows me to realize equity, pot control, and keep the opponent's range wide, possibly inducing bluffs on later streets or catching an Ace or Queen. It also protects my checking range.

Turn brought 3h, pairing the board. My backdoor flush is now dead. I have a gutshot straight draw (need a 4 for 2-3-4-5-6). Pro checked back the flop showing weakness - likely has overcards or a weak pair. With only 4 outs and position disadvantage, I'll check to see a free river. If I hit my 4, I'll have the nuts. No need to bluff into a pot when I can see a free card.

On the turn, the board is [2c 6d 3s 3h]. The 3h pairs the board, which is a fairly safe card that reduces the combinations of trips the opponent might have, though a 3 is in the BB defending range. My Ace-Queen high still has significant showdown value. Claude has checked to me twice now. Betting here has little value as worse hands (like K-high) will likely fold, and better hands (pairs of 2s, 6s, 44, 55, 77-99) will call or raise. By checking back, I control the pot size, realize my equity for free seeing the river, and induce potential bluffs from Claude on the river or allow me to call a small bet/make a thin value bet if I improve. The pot is small, and there is no need to bloat it with a marginal made hand.

River completed a double-paired board (2c6d3s3h2h). I have 9-high with 5c9c - essentially playing the board with 9 as my kicker. Pro checked back both flop and turn, showing significant weakness. However, my 9-high loses to any pocket pair or any hand with a card higher than 9 as kicker. Bluffing here is unlikely to work against an UTG opener who might call with any pair or high card. Checking and accepting a showdown is optimal - my 9 kicker might occasionally win against complete air like KQ/QJ that gave up.

The board has run out 2c 6d 3s 3h 2h, putting a double pair on the board with a 6. My hand (As Qc) plays as Two Pair (Threes and Deuces) with an Ace kicker. This is a strong showdown hand that beats all King-high or worse holdings in the opponent's range. However, if I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands (like K-high), and I will likely only be called by better hands (any pocket pair 44+, any 6, or a full house) or chopping hands (Ax). Since betting extracts no value from worse hands and folds out nothing better, checking back to realize my showdown value is the optimal play.

T5o is a very weak hand to play from UTG (under the gun), even in a 4-handed game. Given my position and the fact that I have the shortest stack at the table, there is no reason to enter the pot with this hand. I will wait for a stronger opening opportunity.

4-handed, unopened pot after UTG Flash folds, on the button with excellent position deepstacked (~255 BB). J7o is a playable stealing hand from the button due to high fold equity against blinds. Pro (BB, 95 BB) and Claude (SB, 184 BB) have shown defending tendencies in history but fold preflop often in unopened pots. Raising builds the pot OOP or steals blinds outright. Limp weak, fold too tight. Standard 3x raise to 300 for good size postflop if called.

9h5d is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity. Against a button raise from Elon, this is a clear fold. The hand lacks suited potential, has poor high card value, and doesn't connect well enough to call profitably even with position on the BB.

I am in the Big Blind with Js 3d, facing a 3x raise (300) from Elon on the Button. The pot is 450, and I need to call 200, offering me pot odds of roughly 30.7%. While J3o has some raw equity against a wide button opening range, it suffers significantly from poor playability and being out of position. The 3x sizing makes the price worse than a standard min-raise, and realizing equity with this hand will be difficult. Against an aggressive big stack, defending such a weak holding is likely negative EV. Therefore, the best course of action is to fold.

UTG in a 4-handed game with 34 suited connectors. While ranges widen short-handed, this is a weak hand with poor high-card strength and limited playability out of position against three opponents. No dead money or steals justified here pre-flop. Folding preserves our deep stack for better spots.

On the button with K2o after UTG folds. This is a prime stealing spot with only the blinds to act. K2o is weak but playable from the button for a steal attempt. Standard 2.5x raise to pick up the blinds.

The current situation is 4-handed, and the Button (Claude) has opened to 250 (2.5BB). Claude has been very active, implying a wide opening range. As the Small Blind with 5s6s (Suited Connectors), I have a hand with decent equity and playability against a wide range, despite being Out of Position. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 into a pot that will be at least 600, requiring ~25-33% equity). Given the stack depth (~95BB) and a previous successful defense against Claude in a similar spot (Hand #79), calling to see a flop is a reasonable, lower-variance play compared to 3-betting or folding. I aim to realize equity on favorable board textures.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc 8c. The Button (Claude) opened for 250 and the Small Blind (Pro) called. I need to commit 150 more to a current pot of 600, giving me 4:1 odds on a call. King-eight suited is a strong hand to defend in this spot as it has good equity against a wide button opening range and plays well in multi-way pots due to its flush and high-card potential. Calling is the standard play here to see a flop and realize my equity without over-inflating the pot out of position.

I have completely missed the flop on an Ace-high board (Ad Th 3h) with 5s 6s. The texture strongly favors the pre-flop raiser (Claude) who has a range advantage. Being out of position in a multi-way pot with just 5-high and very weak backdoor draws, I have no equity to justify a lead or a call. The standard play is to check and fold to any aggression.
