I have King-high with no draws on an Ace-high flop. The pre-flop aggressor (Claude) is still to act, and the Small Blind (Pro) has already checked. This board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. I have no reason to bet here, so I will check and likely fold to any significant aggression unless I improve or the action stays checked around.

Flop is Ad Th 3h. I have K2o - complete air with no pair, no draw. Both opponents checked to me. While this is a decent c-bet spot as the preflop aggressor on an Ace-high board, I have two opponents and zero equity. If I bet and get called, I have no backup plan. Checking back for pot control is the safer play here - I can potentially win at showdown if it checks through or fold to aggression.

On the Turn, the board is Ad Th 3h 8h, putting three hearts on the board. I hold 5s 6s, giving me no pair, no draw, and no relevant blockers to the flush. The flop was checked through, suggesting weakness or pot control from the pre-flop raiser (Claude). However, with two opponents and a board that connects heavily with calling ranges (flushes, pairs with heart kickers), bluffing with zero equity is ill-advised. My hand has no showdown value. The standard and correct play is to check and fold to any bet.

I have turned second pair (8s) on a board that now features three hearts and an Ace. The flop was checked around by everyone, which suggests that the Ace may not have hit the pre-flop aggressor (Claude) or the Small Blind (Pro). However, the 8h is a coordinated card that completes flushes. Since I am out of position and my hand has moderate showdown value but is vulnerable, checking is the most prudent play to see how the other players react and to avoid bloating the pot.

Board now has three hearts (Ad Th 3h 8h). I have Ks 2c - no pair, no heart for a flush draw. Both opponents checked again. While I could try to steal, I have zero equity if called and the flush draw on board makes it risky. If someone has a heart, they're not folding. Checking keeps the pot small and I might win at showdown with King-high if they're on air too.

On the River, the board is [Ad Th 3h 8h Qc]. The pot is 750. The action checked through on both the Flop and Turn. I hold 5s 6s, giving me just 6-high, which has absolutely no showdown value. While the passive action suggests weakness from my opponents (Claude and Flash), attempting a bluff here is risky. The board contains an Ace and a Queen, and there are three hearts, meaning opponents could easily have checked down a weak Ace, a Ten, a Queen, or even a low flush hoping to induce or just pot controlling. Bluffing into two opponents with zero equity and low fold equity on this texture is negative EV. The pot is small, so preserving chips is prioritized over a high-variance bluff. I will check and give up the pot.

I have a pair of eights (fourth pair) on a board with an Ace, Queen, and Ten, as well as three hearts. The pre-flop aggressor (Claude) and the Small Blind (Pro) have checked on the flop, turn, and now Pro has checked the river. This sequence indicates that no one likely has a strong hand like a flush or a top pair. While my hand has some showdown value, it is not strong enough to bet for value, and bluffing into two opponents on this board is risky. Checking is the most prudent play to see the showdown for free and minimize potential losses.

River brings Qc, making final board Ad Th 3h 8h Qc (3 hearts). Both opponents checked again. I have K-high with no pair. After checking through all streets, betting now only gets called by hands that beat me. K-high might actually win at showdown since both players showed zero interest. Check for free showdown.
K9o from UTG at a 4-handed table. This is a reasonable opening hand short-handed. My stack is healthy at 182 BBs. Standard open to 2.5x.

I hold KdQc on the Button. The player UTG (Claude) has raised to 250. Claude has been opening very frequently throughout the tournament, suggesting a wider range than typical for UTG. KQo is a strong broadway hand that plays well in position. While a 3-bet is a viable option to isolate, flat calling keeps Claude's dominated hands (like KJ, QJ) in the pot and avoids bloating the pot against the top of his range (AA, KK, AK) or facing a 4-bet. Calling also allows me to navigate post-flop with the advantage of position. Therefore, I will call the 250.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 3h, an extremely weak hand. Claude raised from UTG and Pro called from the Button, indicating strength from both players. Calling 200 to win a pot of 650 with such a low-equity hand while being out of position for the rest of the hand is a losing play. Folding is the only sensible option here to conserve my stack.

J7o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the big blind facing an UTG raise from Claude, who often shows strength in early position, and a button call from Pro. Pot odds are about 4.3:1 but not justifying calling OOP with this hand against a strong range. Folding preserves stack for better spots with 66BB effective depth.

Flop is 2d4d8h - low, disconnected board with a diamond draw. I completely missed with K9o, but this board likely missed Pro's button calling range too (except for pocket pairs or diamond draws). Standard c-bet of ~1/3 pot to apply pressure and take it down. Pro has position on me so I want to keep the bet small.

I hold KdQc on a low board of 2d-4d-8h. I have two overcards and the King of Diamonds provides a backdoor flush draw. Claude's continuation bet of 200 into 650 is small (approx. 30%), offering me excellent pot odds of roughly 5.25:1 (requires ~19% equity). My hand likely has enough equity against his wide c-betting range to continue, especially given the advantage of position. Folding is too weak for this price, and raising turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily when I have showdown value potential. I will call to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

Turn brought Qd completing the flush. I have K-high with no diamonds. Pro called preflop and flop, showing interest. This is a terrible card for my range - it completes flush draws and could have hit Qx hands in Pro's calling range. I have no showdown value and barreling into a 3-diamond board out of position is -EV. Check and give up unless Pro checks back.

The turn brings the Queen of Diamonds (Qd), making the board 2d-4d-8h-Qd. This improves my hand significantly to Top Pair (Queens) with a strong kicker (King), and crucially, gives me the Second Nut Flush Draw (Kd). The board now has three diamonds. Claude checks to me. 

Given the three diamonds on the board, this is a texture where players often check for pot control or fear of the flush. My hand has excellent showdown value but is vulnerable to a check-raise if Claude has a made flush or sets a trap. By betting, I likely fold out his bluffs and only get action from hands that have good equity or beat me. 

Checking back is the superior option here for several reasons: 1) It controls the pot size on a wet board. 2) It realizes my equity for the flush draw for free. 3) It keeps Claude's range wide, potentially inducing bluffs on the river or allowing me to get value on a safe river card. 4) I avoid facing a difficult decision if check-raised. I plan to call a river bet or value bet the river if checked to again.

River 7c completes a relatively dry runout with 3 diamonds. I have K-high with no diamond. Pro checked behind on turn showing weakness, but they could easily have a small flush, Qx, or a medium pair that beats K-high. Bluffing into 3 diamonds OOP against a player who called flop is risky - they have too many hands that beat me and will call. Just check and concede the pot.

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a King kicker on a board of 2d-4d-8h-Qd-7c. The board contains three diamonds, making a flush possible. The opponent (Claude) opened UTG, c-bet the flop, then checked the turn (when the flush draw completed and the Queen hit) and checked the river. His line is consistent with a hand that has some showdown value but is pot-controlling (like 99-JJ, 8x) or a missed draw (Ad high), or perhaps a weak Queen. While I have a strong hand, betting for value is thin because he likely folds hands worse than a Queen (like 99-JJ) on this texture, and strong hands (flushes, sets) might check-raise me. Checking back guarantees I realize my equity, avoids opening myself to a check-raise, and likely wins the pot against his checking range. It is a safe, low-variance play to secure the pot.

I am in UTG position with 3s 5c, which is a very weak hand (offsuit one-gapper, low rank). With 100BB stack depth in a 4-handed game, this hand falls well outside opening ranges. There is no strategic merit to entering the pot. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Six suited (Jc 6c). In a 4-handed game, this is a borderline opening hand. However, the players in the blinds, Elon and Claude, have been extremely active and aggressive throughout the last several hands. Elon, the chip leader, has a tendency to 3-bet light, and Claude also defends frequently. Given that I am the shortest stack at the table with about 68 big blinds, I want to avoid playing a marginalized hand out of position or facing a 3-bet from the aggressive big stacks. I will fold and wait for a more premium hand or a better situation to exploit their aggression.

Preflop in small blind with 2s8h, an extremely weak offsuit hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suited potential. Facing the big blind, completing would be a poor use of chips given the hand's lack of playability postflop and high likelihood of domination. Standard fold to preserve stack for better opportunities in this multi-table tournament dynamic.

In a 4-handed game, King-Ten offsuit (KTo) is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the Under-the-Gun position. My stack size is approximately 68 big blinds, which gives me enough room to play post-flop. I'll open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially thin the field.

Pre-flop on the button with 68dd, a suited connector. Flash raised to 300 UTG (3x BB), pot 450, call 300. Excellent position for speculative hand with good playability, straight/flush potential, and strong implied odds given deep stacks (mine very deep, others decent). Folding too tight from button; 3-bet not strong enough range. Call to see flop multiway or isolate if raised, control pot OOP opponents.

J8o in the SB facing an UTG raise and a call. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position in a multiway pot. While I'm getting decent pot odds (250 to win 750), J8o is easily dominated and will be difficult to play profitably OOP against two opponents. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 3h, facing a raise to 300 from UTG (Flash) and a call from the Button (Elon). The pot is 750 (300 + 300 + 50 SB + 100 BB) and I need to call 200. This gives me pot odds of roughly 21%. My hand is extremely weak (unsuited, low cards) and performs poorly against an UTG opening range and a Button calling range. Being out of position multi-way with a hand that has very low playability and equity below the required pot odds makes this a clear fold.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten kicker on a board of K-J-5. Pre-flop, I raised from UTG and was called only by Elon on the button. This board texture hits my range well, but also connects with a button-calling range which often contains broadway cards and mid-pairs. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 400, which is slightly more than half the pot, to get value from weaker hands like a Jack, straight draws (QT), or middle pocket pairs, while also protecting my hand.

68dd completely misses the KhJc5s rainbow flop. No pair, no flush draw (board rainbow), and only a weak gutshot straight draw at best (to the 7 for 5-6-7-8-9, but unlikely and narrow). Flash, as UTG raiser, has a strong continuing range including top pair (Kx), overpairs, and broadway. C-bet of 400 into 1150 pot is standard. No fold equity if raising due to position but stacks and range make it -EV. Position doesn't help enough with zero equity. Immediate fold preserves stack for better spots.

83s is a weak suited hand from UTG in a 4-handed game. Poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and dominated by better spades or higher cards. Folding preserves stack for better positions and stronger hands to maximize long-term EV in tournament.

JTc suited on the button is a premium hand for this position. Only Elon folded, so I'm facing the blinds. This is an easy raise situation - suited connectors play well postflop with position, and I should be opening wide from the button. Standard raise to 250 (2.5x BB).

I hold a pair of Sevens (7c 7s) in the Small Blind facing a standard 2.5x open from the Button (Claude). Claude has been active and opening wide from late position. Pocket sevens is a strong enough hand to continue with, but 3-betting creates a bloated pot out of position with a hand that is difficult to navigate post-flop if overcards appear and I don't hit a set. Flat calling is the most prudent option to keep the pot control manageable and set mine or evaluate the flop texture. I am getting reasonable odds to call.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Eight of diamonds. The action started with a button raise from Claude to 250 and a call from the Small Blind (Pro). The current pot is 600, and it only costs me 150 more to call. I am getting 5:1 odds on a call, and K8s is a very strong hand to defend with in a multi-way pot. It has great playability and enough raw equity against wide button opening ranges and small blind calling ranges to justify seeing a flop. A 3-bet squeeze is an option, but calling is lower variance and highly profitable given the price.

On a board of 4d 4h 4c, I have Fours full of Sevens. While this is a strong hand, I am currently losing to any higher pocket pair (88+) and any hand containing the fourth 4 (Quads), though the latter is unlikely. As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking is the standard play to defer to the pre-flop raiser (Claude). Leading out (donk betting) on this texture would likely fold out the air in my opponents' ranges that I want to keep in, or isolate me against better hands. I will check and evaluate based on the action behind me, hoping to induce a bluff or a continuation bet from the button.

The flop is 4d-4h-4c, giving everyone at least three-of-a-kind. With Kd8d, my hand is essentially 'Trips with a King kicker.' This is a very strong hand on this specific board texture, as only an Ace kicker or a pocket pair (making a full house) beats me. However, leading out into the pre-flop aggressor on such a dry board is rarely optimal. Checking allows the button (Claude) to continuation bet with a wide range of hands, including air or weaker kickers. I will check and see how the action develops, likely planning to call a standard bet to see a turn card.

Board is 444 - trip fours on the flop. Both opponents checked to me. With this monotone board of three 4s, the only hands that beat me are pocket pairs higher than JT or someone with a 4 (very unlikely). My JT high is decent here. Both checks indicate weakness - likely no 4 and no high pairs. This is a great spot to c-bet for value/thin value and protection. Small sizing around 1/3 pot should suffice to take it down or extract value.

I hold a pair of sevens on a board of 4-4-4, giving me a full house (Fours full of Sevens). The button (Claude) has made a small continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 750 after checked to. This texture is extremely dry, and the small bet size is standard for a wide range of hands, including overcards and air. Raising here would likely fold out all the hands I beat (bluffs, Ace-high) and only get action from better hands (88+). My hand is vulnerable to overcards hitting on the turn or river, but the price to call is excellent. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide and allows the Big Blind (Flash) a chance to come along. I will call to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

The flop is 4d-4h-4c, and I hold Kd-8d. This gives me three-of-a-kind with a King kicker. On this board, only an Ace kicker or a pocket pair (making a full house) is currently beating me. Claude, the button raiser, bet 250 into a 750 pot, and Pro (SB) called. I am getting excellent odds (1250:250 or 5:1) to call. Raising here would likely only get action from hands that beat me (like Ax or pocket pairs) while folding out the air/bluff part of Claude's range. By calling, I keep the pot manageable and allow them to continue with weaker holdings. I will call to see the turn.

The turn is the Nine of Diamonds (9d), making the board 4d 4h 4c 9d. I still hold the Full House (Fours full of Sevens). My hand is currently ahead of all unpaired hands and lower pocket pairs (22, 33, 55, 66), but behind higher pocket pairs (88+) and any 9x that has now filled up (though unlikely for a Button open to just flat the flop with 99, maybe A9/K9 suited). The 9d also brings a backdoor flush draw. As the pre-flop caller out of position, leading out (donk betting) doesn't accomplish much; it folds out the bluffs I want to keep in and isolates me against better hands. Checking is the standard play to allow the aggressor (Claude) to continue his story or to let the Big Blind make a move. I intend to check-call a reasonable bet to get to showdown, as my hand is a bluff-catcher against overcards but strong enough to win at showdown often.

On a board of 4-4-4-9, I hold King-high trips (4-4-4-K-9). My hand is strong, but it is vulnerable to any pocket pair (which makes a full house) or an Ace-high hand (which has a better kicker). Since the Small Blind checked to me and the Button was the aggressor on both the pre-flop and flop streets, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the size of the pot, see what the Button decides to do, and potentially catch bluffs. Betting here would likely only be called by hands that have me beat, such as Ax or a pocket pair.

Board is 444-9. Both opponents checked to me again on the turn. My JT gives me a J-high kicker with the board's trips. The 9d doesn't improve much for anyone. Their double-check shows continued weakness - anyone with a 4, overpair, or even A-high would likely have bet or raised by now. I should continue betting for value/protection. Sizing around half pot to extract thin value from lower pairs or weaker high cards while controlling the pot size.

On the turn, the board is 4d 4h 4c 9d. I hold 7c 7s, giving me a Full House (Fours full of Sevens). On this specific trip-fours texture, any pocket pair constitutes a Full House, while any unpaired hand (like A9 or AK) is merely Three of a Kind (Fours with kickers). Therefore, I am currently ahead of Claude's entire range of unpaired hands (including any 9x that hit the turn) and lower pocket pairs (22, 33, 55, 66). I are only behind higher pocket pairs (88+) and the unlikely case four. Claude's double barrel suggests either an overpair or a bluff. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (88+) and fold out the bluffs I am crushing. Calling is the correct play to keep his bluffing range wide and evaluate the river. The price (700 into 2200) is favorable.

I am holding King-high trips on a board of 4d-4h-4c-9d. While my hand is technically strong, the board texture is extremely dangerous. Any pocket pair (22-AA) held by my opponents has already filled up for a full house, and any Ace-high hand (Ax) currently holds a better kicker than mine. The Button (Claude) has shown consistent aggression by betting both the flop and the turn, and the Small Blind (Pro) has called twice, indicating he likely has a piece of the board, such as an Ace or a pocket pair. Given that I am facing a bet and a call on the turn in a multi-way pot, it is statistically probable that at least one of my opponents has me beat. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

The river brings a second Nine, making the final board 4d-4h-4c-9d-9c. This board texture (Fours full of Nines) counterfeits my pocket Sevens. My best five-card hand is now playing the board itself (4-4-4-9-9). I am currently chopping with any opponent holding a hand worse than TT or without a 9 (e.g., AK, 55-88). I lose to any pocket pair TT+ (making Fours full of Tens or better) and any hand with a 9 (making Nines full of Fours). Since I cannot get value from worse hands and betting only folds out chops, my only option is to check and hope for a showdown to split the pot.

River is 4-4-4-9-9. This is a full house board where the board plays for most hands. My JT gives me 444-99 (board plays). To beat me, Pro needs: a 4 (quads), a 9 (full house 999-44), or a pocket pair higher than 99 (444-XX). Pro has been passive throughout, just calling. They could easily have a 9 in their range, which beats me. Betting here is thin value at best - if called I'm often behind, and worse hands fold. Best to check back and showdown.

Q9o from UTG is a marginal hand that plays poorly from early position. It's easily dominated by better queens and doesn't have great playability. With 3 players left to act, I should fold and wait for a better spot.

On the button with K7o in a 4-handed table, this is a standard spot to raise and attempt to steal the blinds. Although the Big Blind (Elon) is the chip leader and capable of aggression, I have the advantage of position. K7o falls well within a standard GTO button opening range (typically K3o+). I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to apply pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened with a standard raise to 2.5x. While Ace-Six isn't a premium hand, it is strong enough to defend against a Button open, especially since I'm getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 400). Folding is too tight, and 3-betting might be too risky given the weak kicker and the fact that I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand. I will call and see a flop, keeping an eye on the Big Blind (Elon) who has a massive stack and could potentially squeeze.

Preflop in BB with Jd6h offsuit. Facing a button raise to 250 by Pro (wide range likely) and SB flat-call by Flash (speculative hands). Pot is 600, calling 150 more for pot odds of over 4:1. J6o has decent playability and equity against a wide button range, even OOP multiway. BB defense range includes this spot, especially deep-stacked. Folding too tight here; will see flop cheap and can play postflop accordingly. No strong reason to 3-bet or fold given the price.

I have missed the flop completely with Ace-high on a King-high board. In a three-way pot, leading out into the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and the deep-stacked big blind (Elon) is not a profitable strategy with my current hand strength. I will check to see how the action unfolds. If there is a bet, I am likely to fold given that my only real equity is a backdoor straight and one overcard that might not even be clean if Pro has a King or better.

Flop Kc4d7h completely misses Jd6h. No pair, no straight or flush draw (minor backdoor heart), jack-high showdown value at best but OOP multiway. Pro's button raise range likely includes many Kx, Ax, broadways that connect well here; Flash's SB call suggests speculative hands that may have missed too. Flash checked, indicating weakness. Pot small at 750 with deep stacks, but no reason to donk bet as bluff or value. Check to realize equity if somehow ahead vs bluffs or control pot; will fold to any reasonable bet from Pro. Folding preflop was too tight given odds, but postflop play conservatively with air.

I have flopped Top Two Pair (Kings and Sevens) on a dry rainbow board (K-7-4). While this is a very strong hand, the board texture is extremely dry, and significantly, I block the hands that would give me action (Top Pair and Middle Pair). It is unlikely my opponents have a hand strong enough to call a bet unless they have a set (44) or a random King (which is less likely given I hold one). By checking back, I accomplish two things: 1) I protect my checking range, so I am not always weak when I check behind, and 2) I give my opponents a chance to catch up (hit a pair on the turn) or attempt to bluff at the pot on later streets, allowing me to extract more value than betting now and forcing folds from their air. Given it is a 3-way pot and I have position, checking is a strong deceptive play.

On the turn, the board is King-high and everyone checked the flop, indicating a lack of immediate strength from any of the three players involved. While I have Ace-high, I have no real connectivity or draws on this board. Since I'm out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and a very deep-stacked Big Blind (Elon), there is no merit in betting here as a bluff. I will check and hope to see a free river or fold if a significant bet is made, as my hand has very little equity to continue.

On the turn, board is Kc4d7hTs. I have Jd6h, pure jack-high with no pair, no draws (backdoor heart irrelevant). Multiway pot OOP after checking flop. Flash (SB) checked again, indicating weakness. No value to betting jack-high vs Pro's button raising range (likely contains Kx, broadways, pairs) and Flash's speculative calling range. Bluffing has low fold equity multiway deep-stacked; Pro can call/raise light from position. Best play is to check, control pot, realize equity if miraculously ahead vs bluffs, and fold to any bet. Consistent with flop plan to play conservatively with air.

I checked the flop with Top Two Pair to disguise my hand strength and induce action or allow opponents to catch up. The turn brings the Ten of Spades, which is a card that connects somewhat with my opponents' ranges (KT, QT, JT, AT, T9). It also introduces some straight draws (QJ, J9). With two opponents checking to me again, I cannot risk giving another free card that might beat me (e.g., a Jack). Furthermore, since I checked the flop, a bet now looks like a delayed c-bet or a stab at the pot, which might induce light calls from a Ten or a weaker King. I need to start extracting value from these second-best hands and charge the draws. A bet of roughly 50-60% of the pot (400 into 750) achieves this balance.

On the turn, the board is Kc-4d-7h-Ts. I checked the flop and turn, and Elon (BB) also checked both. Pro (BTN), the pre-flop aggressor, has now bet 400 into a pot of 750. Although Ace-high might occasionally be good if Pro is attempting a delayed continuation bet with air, I have no draw and no pair. In a three-way pot, calling with just Ace-high out of position is too loose, especially with Elon still left to act behind me. I have very few clean outs to improve to a winning hand if I am behind, and the risk of being out-kicked even if I hit an Ace is high. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Turn board brings Ts, completing some straight draws like 89 or Q9. My Jd6h is pure jack-high air with no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw, and minimal backdoor potential. Preflop, Pro raised from button (wide range: broadways, Ax, suited connectors), Flash called SB (speculative). Flop checked through, indicating possible weakness but Pro now bets 400 into 1150 pot (~1/3 pot) after Flash folds. This sizing could be value (KT, T7, sets, Kx) or thin value/inducing bluff. Calling OOP with trash gives up position, realizes no equity, and commits to tough river spot deep-stacked. Pot odds ~2.9:1 but equity <20% vs any reasonable range. Standard fold to preserve stack in tournament; better spots ahead with 24975 chips (deep). Consistent with postflop plan to check-fold air.

I am holding Ace-Queen offsuit (AQo) in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is effectively the Cutoff position. AQo is a premium hand that sits well near the top of my range and is a mandatory open for value. With a healthy stack of over 100 big blinds, I want to build the pot. A standard open raise to 2.5bb (250 chips) is appropriate here, consistent with previous play and standard sizing for this tournament depth. It puts pressure on the shorter stack on the button and challenges the deep-stacked blinds.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of threes. In a 4-handed game, a small pocket pair is worth seeing a flop with, especially in position. Pro raised from UTG (which is effectively the cutoff/under-the-gun in this setup), and with my stack size of approximately 65 big blinds, I have sufficient implied odds to set-mine. Folding is too conservative for the button, and 3-betting would likely put me in a difficult spot if I face a 4-bet or a call out of position from the blinds. I will call to see if I can hit a set or navigate the pot using my positional advantage.

Hand #90 pre-flop in SB with J♥8♦ offsuit. Marginal holding with poor connectivity and no suitedness. Facing UTG raise from Pro (likely strong range) and Button call from Flash (widens range but still faces strength). Out of position post-flop, which is disastrous with this hand. Pot odds are ~3.25:1 (650 pot, 200 to call), but implied odds are poor OOP without strong post-flop playability. Large stack (25k effective), no need to speculate in multiway pot early in hand. Claude still to act in BB. Best to fold and wait for better spots with 60 hands left.

4-2 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Even with 4.3:1 pot odds (150 to call into 650), this hand is too weak to profitably continue. I'll be out of position against two opponents and rarely make anything strong. Easy fold.

I opened pre-flop with QdAs and was called by the button. The flop comes 9h-8s-7h, which is an incredibly wet and dynamic board that heavily favors the caller's range (suited connectors, set mining pairs, etc.) over my UTG opening range (high cards, big pairs). My specific hand has missed completely, holding only overcards with no flush draw. C-betting here is generally negative EV as the opponent connects with this board very frequently (straights, two pairs, sets, pairs + draws). I should check to control the pot size and give up on the betting lead, as I am likely behind or facing a range that will not fold.

The flop [9h 8s 7h] is extremely coordinated and connects well with many ranges. With pocket threes, I have an underpair and very little equity on this board. Since Pro checked, I have the option to take a free card. Betting here is risky because the board texture is more likely to have hit an UTG opening range (overpairs, high connectors) or a calling range than my specific holding, and I'm unlikely to fold out better hands. Checking back allows me to see the turn for free and keeps the pot small with a marginal hand.

Pre-flop I raised with AQo and got called by the Button. The flop came 9h-8s-7h, extremely wet, and we both checked. The turn is the Queen of Hearts (Qh). This card improves me to Top Pair, Top Kicker, but it is also a very scary card: it completes the flush draw and adds to the straight texture (JT is now the nut straight, J or T alone are straight draws). Given the opponent checked back the flop on a board where draws and strong hands usually bet, his range is likely capped or contains marginal made hands/weak draws. While I have value against worse Queens or pairs like 9x/8x, betting here is risky because I cannot withstand a raise, and the board favors the calling range's coverage of hearts and straights. Checking is the best option to control the pot size. I intend to check-call a reasonable bet as a bluff-catcher or value-own against a worse Queen, or check-check to realize equity on the river.

The board is now [9h 8s 7h Qh], which is extremely coordinated and dangerous for a small pocket pair like threes. The Queen of Hearts puts three hearts on the board and completes various straight possibilities. Pro has checked twice, indicating a weak hand or a potential trap, but my hand has almost no showdown value and is unlikely to win the pot by betting unless Pro has absolutely nothing. Checking back is the best option to see a free river card and avoid inflating the pot with a hand that is likely beaten.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (QdAs) on a board of 9h-8s-7h-Qh-4c. My opponent, playing from the Button, checked back both the flop (which was very wet with straight/flush draws) and the turn (which completed the flush and possible straights). This passive line strongly suggests my opponent does not have a strong hand like a flush, straight, or set, and is likely holding a marginal made hand (like a weak pair) or completely missed draws. If I bet the river, hands that I beat (like 9x, 8x, or missed draws) will likely fold, and I risk only getting called or raised by better hands. By checking, I induce bluffs from missed draws and allow the opponent to check back worse showdown value that I beat. I have sufficient showdown value to win the pot, and checking minimizes risk while maximizing the chance of capturing bluffs.

On a board of 9h 8s 7h Qh 4c, my pocket threes are quite weak, but they do have some showdown value against missed high-card hands like AK, AJ, or KJ, which Pro might have in his UTG opening range. Pro has checked on every street (flop, turn, and river), which suggests either a complete miss or a marginal hand that is content to reach showdown cheaply. While betting might occasionally force a fold from a slightly better hand (like a weak 7 or 8), the board is highly coordinated, making it more likely he has something that will call a small bet or that my threes are already ahead of his overcards. Since the pot is relatively small and I have a small amount of showdown value, I will check back to see the showdown for free.

I am in the UTG position in a 4-handed game with Jack-Four offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand and does not warrant an opening raise, especially as the shortest stack at the table. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my chips for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with 5c3s, an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity. Even in late position, this is a clear fold. No steal attempt warranted given the hand's lack of potential against likely calling ranges from blinds. Preserve stack for better spots with 60+ BB effective stacks.

Holding Q2o in the SB with only Pro in the BB remaining. This is a weak hand but I'm getting 3:1 odds to complete (50 to win 150). Q2o plays poorly postflop and isn't worth a raise, but completing is reasonable given pot odds heads-up.

In the Big Blind with A9o facing a Small Blind limp. This is a situation where I have the option to raise for value/isolation or check to see a free flop. A9o is a strong hand heads-up, but raising risks facing a limp-reraise which would force me to fold a hand with good equity. Checking keeps the pot small, keeps Claude's range wide (including worse Ax and random weak hands), and allows me to realize my equity in position. Given the stack depths and the desire to control variance while realizing equity, checking is a solid, standard play.

Flop is 6d Tc 6c - paired board. I have Q-high with no pair, no draw. Betting here would be a pure bluff that often gets called by tens, pocket pairs, or club draws. Best to check and see a free card or fold to aggression.

In a limped Big Blind vs Small Blind pot, I hold Ace-high (Ah 9d) on a paired board of [6d Tc 6c]. The Small Blind has checked to me. My hand has significant showdown value in this configuration, as my opponent's range is wide and likely contains many missed hands. However, betting achieves little: I fold out hands I am already beating (like Q7 or J5), and if I am called, I am often behind a Ten, a Six, or a pocket pair, or facing a draw that has equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keep the pot small, and potentially win at showdown or call a bet on later streets if the board runs out safely. I will take the free card.

Turn brought a Queen giving me top pair. Board is 6d Tc 6c Qh. While my kicker is weak (2), I have the best hand often enough to value bet. Pro checked flop and likely has air, a weak ten, or a draw. Betting around half pot for value and protection against club draws.

On the turn, the board is [6d Tc 6c Qh]. The Small Blind leads out for 125 into a pot of 200. My hand is Ace-high (Ah 9d). While the Small Blind's limping range is wide, the Queen is a card that hits that range fairly well (QJ, Q9, KQ, Q8). Additionally, he could have a Ten or a Six. He might also be semi-bluffing with a draw (clubs or straight draws). Facing a bet, my Ace-high is essentially a bluff-catcher with very limited outs to improve (only 3 Aces, and even then, I could be dominated or drawing dead). The pot is small, and investing more chips with marginal showdown value out of position relative to the aggressor's lead is likely -EV. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve the stack.

UTG position in a 4-handed game with 7d4h, a completely uncoordinated trash hand with no high card value, straight potential, or suitedness. Facing a small to-call of 100 into 150 pot (likely just blinds), but raising or calling would be a major mistake as this hand has zero equity against typical ranges and plays terribly postflop. Standard fold to preserve our deep stack (250bb) and wait for better spots, especially late in the tournament (hand 92/150) where discipline maximizes EV.

J5o on the button with it folded to me. This is a standard spot to open wide from the button to steal blinds. J5o is marginal but playable in position against just the blinds. I'll make a standard 2.5x open.

I hold King-Jack suited (KhJh) in the Small Blind. Claude has opened to 250 (2.5bb) from the Button. This is a wide range spot for the Button. KJs is a strong hand that dominates much of a standard Button opening range. While a 3-bet is a valid option to isolate and take initiative, flat calling is also an excellent strategy here to keep the opponent's range wide, avoid getting 4-bet off a playable hand, and leverage the strong post-flop playability of suited broadways. Given the stack depth (~100bb) and my position (OOP), I will flat call to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Claude raised from the button and Pro called from the small blind. While I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call (150 chips to win a total pot of 600), 7-4 offsuit performs very poorly in multi-way pots and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. With a stack of 6125 (approx. 61 BBs), there is no reason to commit chips to such a marginal spot. I will fold and wait for a better hand.

I hold KhJh on a 7s6s3d board. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Claude) on the Button. This low, connected board misses my specific hand, leaving me with just two overcards and backdoor possibilities. Standard strategy dictates checking to the aggressor in this spot. Leading out (donk betting) is generally not effective here as it folds out worse hands and gets action from better ones. I will check and evaluate Claude's action; if he bets, I will have to decide based on sizing whether to peel or fold, but for now, checking is the correct move.

Flop is 3d 6s 7c - a low, disconnected board. I have J-high with a gutshot (need a 4). Pro checked to me. This is a good spot for a continuation bet - I have position, the board is dry and low which doesn't strongly favor the caller's range. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot should take this down often enough.

I hold KhJh on a 3d6s7c board. The opponent (Claude, Button) bet 200 into a pot of 600 (1/3 pot). This is a standard small c-bet size often used with a wide range on dry/low textures. I have two overcards (King and Jack) and while I don't have a flush draw, my hand is strong enough to beat pure bluffs and has about 25% equity against top pair. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win 1000, requiring 20% equity). Folding here against a wide button range for this price is too tight. I will call to see a turn card, hoping to hit a pair or check-fold if the board runs out poorly and facing further aggression.

On the turn, the board is 3d 6s 7c Qh. I hold Kh Jh. The Queen of Hearts is an overcard that likely connects better with the Button's opening range (AQ, KQ, QJ, QTs) than my calling range, although I can have some Qx suited. I have failed to improve to a pair or a strong draw (I have 3 hearts, which is not a draw, and 6 outs to a pair of Kings or Jacks). Being out of position with just King-high against a pre-flop raiser who c-bet the flop, my standard line is to check. If the opponent bets again, I will likely have to fold given I didn't improve, but I will start by checking to see his action.

Turn brought a Queen which is a better card for my range. However, Pro called my flop bet showing some interest. I have J-high with minimal equity (only 4 outs to a gutshot). Betting again risks getting called or raised by pairs/draws that won't fold. Checking back gives me position on the river to either bluff if a good card comes or give up cheaply. Pot control with air is the prudent play here.

I hold Top Pair with a good kicker (Kings with Jack) on a board of 3d-6s-7c-Qh-Kc. The opponent checked back the turn, which caps his range and suggests he likely holds a marginal made hand (like a Queen, middle pair) or air, rather than a strong monster or strong draw. While the River King improves my hand significantly, leading out (donk betting) might cause him to fold the weaker parts of his range (Qx, 88-JJ) that I beat. By checking, I give him an opportunity to bluff with missed draws or value bet a worse King/Queen. If he checks back, I likely have the best hand and win the showdown. Checking maximizes my EV by keeping his range wide and inducing mistakes.

River brought a King, another overcard. Pro has checked every street but called my flop c-bet, indicating some made hand or draw. I have J-high with zero showdown value. While K and Q are good for my perceived range, Pro's check-call line suggests they have a pair somewhere. Bluffing risks getting called by Qx, Kx, or small pairs that won't fold. With 1000 in the pot and J-high, the prudent play is to check behind and surrender the pot rather than compound my losses with a bluff that gets called.

As7s is a suited ace in UTG at a 4-handed table. This is a standard open - suited aces have good playability with flush potential and top pair possibilities. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x BB) as I've been doing throughout the tournament.

I am holding King-Five offsuit (K5o) on the Button. The player in the UTG position (Claude) has opened with a raise to 250 (2.5 BB). In a 4-handed game, facing an UTG open, K5o is a weak hand that is frequently dominated by the opener's range (better Kings, pairs, Aces). Although I have position, calling is risky because the Small Blind or Big Blind could squeeze, and playing a weak offsuit King post-flop is difficult. The standard and most profitable play here is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit. Claude has raised to 250 from UTG in a 4-handed game. While the game is short-handed, making hand values slightly higher, T8o is still a weak hand to play out of position against an open raise. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting this hand as a bluff is unnecessary given my current stack size of 60BB. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Q7o is a marginal hand at best from the BB facing an UTG raise from Claude, who tends to raise strong ranges early position based on history. Pot odds are 400:150 (about 2.67:1), but equity against UTG range is poor (~30-35% vs tight open), with low playability offsuit and dominated by Qx, KQ, etc. OOP postflop is tough with this hand. Deep stacks mean no need to speculate; fold to preserve stack for premium spots in tournament phase (hand 93/150).

I am in UTG position with 4 players remaining. My hand is 2c Ts (Ten-Deuce offsuit), which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em due to its lack of connectivity, suitedness, and high card strength. With a stack of 11,425 (approx 114 BB), I have no incentive to speculate with such poor holdings. The standard and optimal play is to fold immediately to preserve chips.

I am on the button with 9c 4s. This is a very weak hand. In a 4-handed game, I might consider stealing with better marginal hands, but 94o is significantly below the threshold for an opening range or a defend against any aggression. Since Pro has already folded and I have yet to act, I am the first person to decide after the UTG fold. Standard strategy for a junk hand like 94o is to fold and wait for a better spot, especially as a shorter stack compared to Elon and Claude.

Preflop in the small blind with Kh4c after UTG and Button folds. Only BB (Claude) behind with 17575 chips. Pot is 150, to call 50 more for great odds, but raising exploits position and dead money. Kh4o is king-high, blocks AA/KK, has some postflop potential. Standard SB vs BB spot to min-raise or small raise to steal or build pot with initiative. Deep effective stacks (mine 24775), low risk. Claude defends wide based on history, but fold equity exists. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) sizes well for isolation or take it down.

As9s is a solid suited ace that plays well postflop. Facing a min-raise from Elon in the SB, I'm getting good pot odds (150 to win 350). This hand has good playability with nut flush potential. Calling is the standard play here - 3-betting is also viable but calling keeps the pot smaller and lets me realize my equity in position.

Preflop aggressor OOP on a dry, rainbow 3-5-8 flop that favors my wide SB raising range over BB's defending range. Hand has no equity but king-high blocks strong pairs like KK/AA, and board misses most floats/pairs. Standard c-bet to continue pressure, deny equity to his Ax/high card calls. Small sizing builds pot or takes it down vs weak range. Deep stacks, tournament spot to apply pressure with 56 hands left. Plan: if called and turn bricks, consider second barrel; if improves, great. Size 300 (~60% pot) balances value/bluff.

Flopped nothing - no pair, no flush draw (only one spade on board). I have just A-high with backdoor spade potential. Against Elon's c-bet on this dry low board, my A9s has very little equity. Even getting 2.67:1 pot odds, I'm not hitting enough to continue profitably. Easy fold.

Q2o is a very weak hand to play from the first position (UTG) in a 4-handed game. Given my stack size is the shortest at the table, I should wait for a better hand to commit chips. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

On the button with A5o (Ah5d) in a deep-stacked spot (~250BB effective vs blinds). Excellent position against two opponents. Ace-high blocker, decent equity vs calling ranges, and high fold equity to steal the 150 pot. Standard aggressive play from button is to raise for value/steal. Limping or calling is too passive and invites multiway pots OOP. Raising to 2.5x the BB (250 total) is a good size: not minraise exploitable, builds pot when ahead, pressures blinds who defend wide but fold often pre. Postflop playability good with straight potential. History shows frequent small raises around 250, fits.

KJd is a strong suited broadway hand. Elon opened from the button which is typically a wide range. I'm in the small blind with a hand that plays well as a 3-bet - it has good equity against button opens, blockers to big hands, and suited potential. 3-betting to around 800 allows me to take initiative and potentially take down the pot preflop or play a bigger pot with position-control.

I hold 8s 6d in the Big Blind. The action involves a Button raise to 250 by Elon and a 3-bet to 800 by Claude in the Small Blind. 86 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly against a 3-bet range. The cost to call is 700 chips, which is substantial relative to my equity in a multi-way or heads-up pot against strong ranges. There are no pot odds or strategic justifications to continue with this hand in this spot. This is a standard fold.

Facing a 3-bet from Claude in the SB to 800 after my button open to 250 with A5o offsuit. Pot 1150, to call 550 (roughly 3:1), but A5o is marginal at best vs a typical 3-bet range (JJ+, AKo/AQs+, some suited Ax/bluffs). Offsuit reduces playability, vulnerable postflop OOP for opponent but dominated by better aces. Ace blocker helps, position excellent, deep stacks (~250BB), but equity likely <35% needed long-term. 4-betting risks cooler or fold equity not worth variance in tourney with ICM but late stage still chip EV focus. Calling invites tough postflop decisions multiway possible if BB wakes up (but folded). History shows Claude aggressive but profitable to fold weak opens here to preserve stack for premium spots. Standard fold preserves massive stack advantage.

J8o is a weak offsuit hand from UTG in a 4-handed game. Even with widened short-handed ranges, it's not strong enough to open raise or limp, as it plays poorly post-flop, gets dominated by better jacks or eights, and faces position disadvantage against button. With a deep stack of nearly 250BB, folding preserves chips for premium spots to maximize long-term stack growth in the tournament.

AKo on the button is a premium hand. With Elon folding UTG, I'm in excellent position to raise and take control of the pot. Standard raise to 250 (2.5x) to build the pot while not bloating it too much.

I hold 5c 7d in the Small Blind facing a Button raise to 250 from Claude. This is a weak off-suit one-gapper. While I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 400), playing this hand out of position is difficult and likely negative EV. The hand has poor playability and I could easily be squeezed by the Big Blind or dominated post-flop. With a healthy stack of over 110 BB, there is no need to get involved in a marginal spot. Folding is the disciplined and correct choice.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The action folded to Claude on the button, who raised to 250. This is a standard late-position open. My hand is strong enough to defend the big blind against a button raise, especially given the pot odds (calling 150 more to win a total pot that will be 550). I have a healthy stack and Ace-high often retains significant value post-flop or can be used to bluff if the board texture is favorable. I will call to see a flop.

On a flop of 4h-3d-3h, I am first to act after calling a pre-flop raise from the button. This board is relatively dry, although the paired treys and the heart draw are present. My Ace-high (As7h) currently has decent showdown value against a wide button-opening range. I will follow the standard procedure of checking to the pre-flop aggressor to see how he reacts. If he bets small, I likely have the best hand and can consider calling. Checking allows me to keep the pot small while navigating with Ace-high.

I have AK on a low, paired board (4h 3d 3h). Flash called preflop from BB and checked to me. This dry board likely missed their range, and I have two overcards. A small c-bet of about 1/3 pot should take this down often - no need to bet big on such a dry texture.

On this paired and relatively dry board of 4h-3d-3h, Claude has made a small continuation bet of 175 into a 550 pot (roughly 32% of the pot). My Ace-high (As7h) currently has significant showdown value against a button opening range, which includes many overcards and air that missed this flop. I also have a backdoor heart draw. Given the excellent pot odds (calling 175 to win 725), folding would be too exploitable. I will call to see how the turn develops, as my hand is often still the best or has enough equity to continue.

The turn brings a Queen of Spades, which is an overcard to the board. Given that I am out of position against the pre-flop and flop aggressor, I will continue with the strategy of checking to see how Claude reacts to this card. My Ace-high still maintains significant showdown value on this paired board, and checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free river if Claude checks behind.

Turn brought Qs which is a good card for my range as the preflop raiser. Flash called flop with a weak range on a paired low board - they likely have small pairs, gutshots, or backdoor draws. The Queen hits my perceived range well. I should continue betting for value/protection with AK high - around 55% pot to keep pressure and deny equity from hands like flush draws or underpairs.

The Queen on the turn is a scare card that significantly improves the Button's perceived range. Claude's bet sizing increased from roughly 1/3 pot on the flop to over 1/2 pot on the turn, suggesting either a made hand (like a Queen or a pocket pair) or a determined semi-bluff. My Ace-high has limited showdown value and very few outs to improve (only 3 Aces). Continuing out of position with just Ace-high and a weak kicker is likely to lead to a difficult and unprofitable river. I will fold and preserve my stack for better spots.
J3 suited from UTG is too weak to open. Even 4-handed, this hand doesn't make my opening range from the worst position. I have a healthy stack at 182bb - no need to take marginal spots.

I am on the Button with Queen-Four offsuit (Q4o). The action has folded to me. While the Button is a strong position to steal the blinds, Q4o is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity. The Big Blind (Elon) has a very large stack and may defend aggressively. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand. There is no strategic merit in entering the pot with this holding.

I am in the Small Blind with 98 suited, a strong speculative hand. Since the action has folded to me, this is a prime opportunity to raise to attempt to steal the blinds or to build a pot with a hand that flops well. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open from the Small Blind to exert pressure on the Big Blind.

Preflop in BB facing a standard min-raise from SB after two folds. SB range is wide in this steal spot, approximately 40-50% of hands. T7o has around 40% equity against that range, and we get excellent implied odds with position postflop (2:1 pot odds, needing ~25% equity). Hand has straight potential and plays decently multiway if button wakes up, but button folded. Calling is +EV, preserves stack variance while realizing equity. Folding too tight in BB defense; 3-betting marginal without great blockers or value. Will play postflop carefully given offsuit nature.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a significant range advantage on this board, which features two broadway cards (King and Queen). Although my specific hand (8h9h) has missed the flop entirely, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. A bet of 300 (half the pot) allows me to represent a strong King or Queen and puts pressure on the Big Blind to fold their air and weaker draws. If called, I will likely evaluate the turn carefully, but the goal here is to take the pot down immediately.

Flop completely misses: T7o has no pair, no flush draw (only one club), no straight draw against Kc5cQh board. Flash's SB range hits this board frequently (Kx, Qx, sets, clubs). C-bet 1/3 pot OOP, pot odds ~4:1 (25% equity needed) but range equity <20% typically. Preflop call was for implied odds/multiway potential, but heads-up miss warrants fold to avoid difficult OOP spots. Deep stack, no need to speculate. Preserve chips for 53 hands left.

I am in UTG with [Ac Jd] in a 4-handed game. AJo is a strong hand in this configuration, well within the opening range. My stack is deep (over 100BB). Limping is passive and invites multi-way pots which AJo does not play as well in. A standard raise is the best option to build the pot and define ranges. I have been opening to 250 consistently in previous hands with my raising range. I will raise to 250 (2.5BB).

I have a medium pocket pair (77) on the button in a 4-handed game. Pro opened from UTG (effectively the cutoff). 77 is a strong hand in this short-handed context. I am choosing to 3-bet to 750 to take the initiative, squeeze the blinds, and potentially isolate the opener. Having position throughout the hand makes a 3-bet more attractive here than just calling.

62h suited is a very weak hand, purely speculative even in position. Facing an UTG raise from Pro (tight range) and a button 3-bet from Flash (likely strong range), this is an easy fold out of position in the SB. No showdown value, minimal fold equity for a squeeze given stacks and villain tendencies from history (Pro and Flash aggressive but with shown strength). Preserve deep stack (24k) for better spots with 52 hands left. Pot odds don't justify calling 700 into 1150 OOP with trash.

QTs is a decent suited broadway hand facing a 3-bet. I'm getting roughly 2.8:1 pot odds (650 to win 1800). QTs has good playability with flush and straight potential. Flash has a short stack (~5200) so this could play out as a smaller SPR pot. Calling is reasonable here - hand plays well multiway or heads up, and folding suited broadway cards getting these odds seems too tight.

I hold AcJd in UTG. I opened to 250, Flash (BTN) 3-bet to 750, and Claude (BB) cold-called the 3-bet. Although the pot odds are attractive (calling 500 to win 1800, approx 3.6:1), the situation is strategically poor. AJo is often dominated by the 3-betting range (AK, AQ, JJ+) and the cold-calling range (TT-QQ, AQs+). Furthermore, I will be playing post-flop out of position relative to the aggressor (Flash) and 'sandwiched' between the cold-caller and the aggressor. This creates significant reverse implied odds where I may win a small pot or lose a large one if dominated. Consistent with my play in Hand #41 where I folded AJo to a 3-bet, I will exercise discipline and fold to preserve my stack.

Completely missed this flop with QTs on A-A-9 board. No pair, no draws. Flash 3-bet preflop so their range is strong - likely has an ace, big pairs, or premium hands. Checking is the only sensible play. If Flash bets, I fold. If they check behind, I get a free card to potentially hit a Q or T, though even then I'd be behind much of their range.

The flop of As-9c-Ad is quite dry but heavily favors any Ace in the Big Blind's calling range. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet, checking back here is a solid pot-control move with my pocket sevens. I have some showdown value but am likely either way ahead of his missed connectors/smaller pairs or way behind an Ace or a larger pocket pair. Checking back keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see how the turn develops without overcommitting.

Flash 3-bet preflop but checked both flop and turn on this A-A-9-4 board. This weakness suggests they likely don't have an ace. However, I have QTs with zero equity - no pair, no real draws. My Q-high might actually beat some of Flash's air like KJ, KQ that also missed. Betting as a bluff here is risky since Flash only has ~5200 behind and may call light. If they have any pocket pair 22-KK, they'll likely call. Better to check and see a free river - if Flash wanted to bluff me off, they would have bet the flop. I can potentially win at showdown with Q-high or evaluate a river bluff.

On a board with two Aces, my pocket sevens are in a classic 'way ahead or way behind' situation. After the BB checked the turn, checking back remains the best strategy for pot control. I have significant showdown value against the BB's non-Ace range (like smaller pocket pairs or suited connectors that missed), but I am crushed by any Ace. By checking, I keep the pot manageable and ensure I get to see the river for free, maximizing my chance of reaching showdown with a hand that likely wins if the opponent doesn't hold an Ace.

Flash 3-bet pre then checked flop, turn, and now the river on A-A-9-4-2 board. This passive line caps their range to medium pocket pairs (likely 77-JJ) or missed broadway hands like KQ, KJ. With Q-high, I have some showdown value against their air. Bluffing here is unlikely to work since pocket pairs won't fold after checking down this dry board. Best to check and potentially win at showdown against other missed hands.

The board is A-9-A-4-2. I have pocket sevens, which is a mid-pair. Pre-flop, I 3-bet and the Big Blind (Claude) called. Both of us have checked the flop and the turn. On the river, Claude checks for a third time. My pocket sevens have significant showdown value here against missed broadway cards (like K-Q or K-J) and smaller pocket pairs. However, it is unlikely I will get called by a worse hand if I bet, as a 9 or any Ace would have me beaten and would certainly call (or raise). By checking, I guarantee a showdown and minimize risk, which is the most prudent play given the 'way ahead or way behind' nature of the board.

I am dealt pocket fives in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is a strong enough hand to open for a raise to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to define my hand and reduce the field.

Facing an UTG raise to 300 (3x BB) from Flash with 6h8s offsuit on the button. 68o is a weak, gapped offsuit hand with poor playability against a strong UTG opening range (high pairs, broadway, strong suited hands). Pot is 450, calling 300 gives ~1.5:1 odds, but equity against UTG range is low (~25-35%), and postflop I'll be OOP if SB/BB call or raise. SB Claude and BB Pro still to act, risking a squeeze. Deep stacks, no need to speculate; fold preserves stack in late tournament stages.

AJo in SB facing a UTG open of 300 from Flash (6700 stack). This is a strong hand that plays well as a 3-bet. Flash is shorter stacked, so a 3-bet to around 900 puts good pressure and isolates. If Flash shoves their remaining ~6400, I'd have a call with AJo given pot odds.

I am holding Queen-Eight offsuit in the Big Blind. The action has started with a raise from UTG (Flash) and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Claude). Facing a 3-bet cold, my hand is far too weak to continue. Q8o is dominated by the ranges of both the opener and the 3-bettor. Even though I have a healthy stack, investing chips here would be a significant mistake with low equity and reverse implied odds. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I have pocket fives and have been 3-bet by the small blind (Claude) after my UTG opening raise. I am 4-handed and will have position on Claude for the rest of the hand. The price to call is 600 to win a total pot of 1900, giving me decent pot odds (over 3:1). Although my stack-to-call ratio (about 11:1) is slightly low for pure set-mining, the positional advantage and the wider ranges in short-handed play make this a standard call. I will look to hit a set or navigate the post-flop play if the board is low or if Claude checks.

I 3-bet preflop with AJo and got called by Flash. Flop is 9d-2c-5d - dry and low, missed my hand but I have two overcards and backdoor nut flush draw. This is a good c-bet spot against Flash's calling range which likely contains hands like suited broadways, medium pairs. A 1/3 pot c-bet of ~600 should get folds from unpaired hands while keeping the pot manageable if called.

I have flopped a set of fives on a board of 9d-2c-5d. Claude 3-bet pre-flop and has led out on the flop with a small continuation bet of 600 into a 1900 pot. With a set, I have a monster hand, but the board contains a flush draw. I want to build the pot now to ensure I can get my remaining chips in by the river, especially since my stack size is relatively small compared to the pot. Raising also forces Claude to pay for any diamond draws or high-card bluffs (like AK or AQ with a diamond). If he has a large overpair (AA-TT), he is likely to continue or even shove over my raise. I will raise to 1600 total.

Flash check-raised my c-bet on a 9d-2c-5d board. I have AJo with no pair and no real draw (Jd doesn't give me a flush draw since there are only 2 diamonds on board). Flash called my 3-bet preflop and is now raising - this indicates strength like 99, 55, 9x, or a diamond draw. With only 4500 behind after this raise, Flash is essentially committed. I'm getting 4:1 pot odds needing ~20% equity, but with just overcards against a raising range on this board, I'm likely behind. Calling puts me in an awkward spot on the turn when Flash likely jams. Disciplined fold.

3c5h is a complete trash hand offsuit with low, disconnected cards. UTG in 4-handed play requires a tight range, especially with no action yet. Folding preserves my large stack (24k, chip leader) for better spots late in the tournament. No fold equity or implied odds justify entering the pot.

Pocket 7s on the button with only blinds left to act after Elon folded. This is a clear raise spot - 77 is a strong hand in this position. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) to take initiative and potentially take down the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Eight offsuit (K8o), facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Claude). While a Button opening range is wide, K8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Calling would require investing 200 chips into a pot that will likely be contested out of position, potentially multi-way if the Big Blind also calls or squeezes. The hand is often dominated by better Kings or Aces. Without a strong read to 3-bet bluff, the most profitable long-term play is to fold and preserve chips.

With Hand #100, I am in the Big Blind holding Queen of Clubs and Five of Spades. Claude has raised to 250 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. While the pot odds are tempting (calling 150 to win a total pot of 400), Q5o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an active player like Claude. Q5o has poor playability on most flops and is frequently dominated by Claude's opening range. Given the tournament context and my desire to protect my stack of 85 Big Blinds, I will make the disciplined fold here rather than defending with a marginal hand.
AQo is a premium hand in UTG position. Standard open raise to 2.5x BB. This is a clear value raise - AQ plays well postflop and I want to build the pot with a strong holding.

I hold Qc3s on the Button. The player Under The Gun (Claude) has raised to 250. Q3 offsuit is a statistically weak hand with very poor playability and low equity against a typical UTG opening range. Despite having position, there is no strategic justification to call or 3-bet this hand given the stack depths and the strength represented by an early position raise. The correct and standard play is to fold.

In a 4-max game, Claude's UTG opening range is effectively the Cutoff position, and he has shown a high frequency of opening raises from all positions. Ace-Nine offsuit (A9o) is a strong enough hand to defend from the Small Blind against a loose opener. Rather than calling and playing a multi-way pot out of position (and inviting a squeeze from Elon in the Big Blind), I will 3-bet to 850. This allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or isolate Claude while having some blockers to premium hands like AA or AK.

83d is a very weak suited hand, offsuit equivalent in strength due to low cards and large gap. Facing an UTG raise from Claude (tight range) and a 3-bet from Flash in SB (likely strong range). Pot 1200, to call 750 OOP multiway postflop is terrible EV. Late tournament (hand 101/150), preserve stack, fold easily.

AQo facing a 3-bet from SB. Getting 2:1 odds to call (600 into 1200). AQ plays well against typical 3-bet ranges and I have position on Flash postflop. 4-betting is an option but calling keeps their bluffs in and I'm deep enough to navigate postflop. Standard call.

On this King-high flop, I maintain the range advantage as the pre-flop 3-bettor. While my actual hand (Ace-Nine) hasn't connected, I hold the Ace of Clubs, which provides a backdoor nut flush draw and blocks some of Claude's potential continuing range. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot (600) is effective here to put pressure on Claude's medium pocket pairs (88, 99) or weak floating hands, while allowing me to get away cheaply if he shows significant strength. This 'range bet' represents my strong pre-flop perception (AA, KK, AK).

Missed the flop with AQo on K-T-5 with two clubs. I have a gutshot (need J for Broadway) and two overcards. Flash's small c-bet (25% pot) could indicate weakness or value. Getting 5:1 pot odds (need ~17% equity). My gutshot gives ~8% on turn, overcards add some equity. Calling is marginal but reasonable with implied odds if I hit. I can reevaluate on turn.

The turn 4d is a blank card that doesn't significantly change the board texture. After Claude called my 3-bet pre-flop and my continuation bet on the King-high flop, his range likely consists of mid-pocket pairs (JJ, TT, 99), some Kx hands (KQ, KJ), or draws (QJ, club draws). My Ace-high with the Ace of clubs blocker still has some showdown value against his draws, but it's unlikely to fold out a King or a pocket pair with another bet. By checking, I control the size of the pot and potentially induce a check-back from Claude, allowing me to see a free river and potentially hit an Ace or evaluate his action if he bets. Double-barreling here is risky against a player who has shown they are willing to call down on boards where I represent strength.

Flash checked the turn after c-betting small on flop - this often signals weakness or giving up with missed hands. I have AQo with a gutshot to Broadway (need J) and two overcards. While I missed, Flash's check gives me a prime opportunity to take a stab. My hand has some showdown value if A or Q hits, plus the gutshot. A turn bet around 60% pot should get folds from missed flush draws, weak pairs, and air. If called, I can reevaluate river.

After my check on the turn, Claude has leads for 1800 into a 3050 pot. My Ace-Nine offsuit has very little equity against Claude's likely range of Kings (KQ, KJ), Tens (TT, JT), or even pocket pairs like JJ. While I hold the Ace of Clubs which blocks some flush draws and I represent a strong range, Claude calling both the 3-bet pre-flop and the flop continuation bet suggests he has a piece of this board. My hand only beats a pure bluff, and given the board texture, even Claude's draws (like QJ or club draws) have significant equity against me. Saving my remaining 70.5 big blinds is a more prudent strategy than calling a large bet with just Ace-high.

I am dealt AsQs in UTG (4-handed). This is a premium hand, well within the opening range for this position. With 4 players remaining, I am effectively in the cutoff position relative to the blinds. My stack is deep (~109 BB), allowing for standard play. A raise is the best option to build the pot for value, deny equity to random hands in the blinds, and take the initiative. A standard opening size at this table has been around 2.5x to 3x BB. I will open to 250 to balance with my previous opening ranges.

Facing a raise from UTG (Pro), my hand [Jh, 6s] is far too weak to continue. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop out of position relative to the raiser, and a 3-bet is not justified with such low equity. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop in SB with 5d3h offsuit, a complete trash hand with no connectivity or high cards. Facing a UTG raise to 250 from Pro, who has a strong range. Pot odds of calling 200 into 400 are ~3:1, but my equity is negligible against their range (~10-15% at best). No position postflop, deep stacks, better to preserve chips for premium spots. Clear fold to avoid spew.

J2o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. Pro's UTG opening range should be reasonably tight, and J2o plays poorly out of position - it's often dominated and has no connectivity or suited potential. Easy fold.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand for this configuration. My stack is roughly 70 big blinds, making it the shortest but still very deep. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take initiative and build the pot.

3h7c is a trash hand: offsuit, unconnected, low cards with poor post-flop playability. Facing an UTG raise from Flash, who acts first pre-flop and likely has a strong range. Position on button is nice, but this hand doesn't warrant a call even for implied odds given its weakness. Pot odds are about 1.5:1 (450:300), but equity against a typical UTG raising range is too low, and multiway pots would be disastrous. Folding preserves our deep stack (24k) for better spots with 47 hands left.

J8o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. Flash opened from early position which typically indicates a stronger range. J8o plays poorly against this range - it's easily dominated by better jacks and has poor playability. Not strong enough to 3-bet and calling out of position with this hand is -EV. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s9s facing a UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed) raise to 3BB from Flash. The pot odds are offering me a call of 200 into a pot of 450, which is 200/650 = ~30.7%. My hand, a suited one-gapper, has reasonable equity (approx 35-38% vs a typical 4-handed opening range) and playability. Although I will be out of position, the price is good enough to defend and see a flop, especially closing the action. A fold is slightly too tight given the suited nature and 4-handed dynamic.

I have flopped top two pair (Nines and Sixes) on a coordinated board of [6d 9h 8d]. The board texture is wet with straight draws (7 connects, T connects) and a diamond flush draw present. As the pre-flop caller out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Flash), the standard line is to check to the aggressor. This allows Flash to continue with his overpairs, draws, or bluffs. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out his air range and allow him to play perfectly against my value hand. My plan is to check and look to check-raise for value and protection if Flash bets, as my hand is strong but vulnerable to many turn cards.

The flop is 6d 9h 8d, which is quite coordinated and better connects with the Big Blind's calling range than my UTG open range (Ace-Jack offsuit). While I have two overcards and a backdoor straight draw, checking behind is the most prudent play here. Betting into this board often invites check-raises or calls from draws/pairs that leave me in a difficult spot on many turn cards. By checking back, I control the pot size and can see a turn for free, potentially realizing my equity or catching a better card to bluff or value bet later.

I have flopped two pair (9s and 6s) which is a strong hand, but the turn card (Ad) is dangerous. It completes the diamond flush draw and hits the opponent's range hard, as a pre-flop raiser often has Ax hands. Flash checked back the flop on a wet board [6d 9h 8d], which suggests he likely doesn't have a strong made hand like a set or a strong draw, but rather a marginal made hand (TT-KK) or Ace-high. The Ad improves his Ax hands to top pair, which he will likely bet for value if checked to. It also improves flush draws which I now lose to. Leading out (donk betting) might fold out his weaker hands or air that might try to bluff, and only gets called by hands that have good equity or beat me. By checking, I give him a chance to bet his new top pair (which I beat) or bluff. I will check-call to keep the pot manageable and evaluate the river.

I have turned top pair with a good kicker (Jack). Pro has checked both the flop and the turn. While the board is quite connected with straight and flush possibilities, I need to bet for value and protection against draws. An Ace is a scare card for many of Pro's marginal made hands (like 9x or 8x), but those hands plus the numerous draws (diamonds, 7x, Tx) will often call a single bet. Betting around 70% of the pot builds the pot for value while making it expensive for draws to continue.

I hold 6s9s for two pair on a board of [6d 9h 8d Ad]. My opponent, Flash, raised pre-flop and checked back the wet flop. On the turn, the Ace of Diamonds completes a possible flush draw. Flash bets 450 into a pot of 650. Given the flop check, Flash is less likely to have a flush or set, as those hands usually bet wet boards for value/protection. His line is consistent with having an Ace (top pair) that he checked back on the flop and is now betting for value, or a bluff representing the scare card. Raising would likely fold out his bluffs and isolate me against flushes or sets. Calling keeps his range wide, allowing me to extract value from top pair hands or induce further bluffs on the river. I have strong equity against his likely range of Ax and one-pair hands, so calling is the standard and most profitable play.

I hold two pair (Nines and Sixes) on a board of [6d 9h 8d Ad Ks]. The river King completes the board but does not bring a new flush. The Ace and King are overcards to my pairs, making my hand vulnerable to better two pairs or sets, though the opponent's flop check-back limits his likelihood of holding a monster (like a set or strong flush). On the turn, he bet the Ace, which fits his range as a pre-flop raiser. I called. Now on the river, betting has little value because worse hands (like a single pair of 8s or 9s) likely fold, and better hands (Ax, Kx, flushes) call or raise. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply against hands like Ax or Kx that might check back, or induce a bluff from missed straight/flush draws. I will check and evaluate based on his action.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a board that is quite dangerous. The turn brought the third diamond, completing potential flushes, and the flop was already heavily coordinated for straights (6-7-8-9). Pro check-called my turn bet, which suggests they have either a marginal Ace, a pair with a draw, or potentially a made hand they are playing slowly. While I might get value from a weaker Ace like A7 or A5, the risk of being check-raised by a flush or straight is high. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win against missed draws and weaker one-pair hands without inflating the pot further on a scary board.

AKo is a premium hand, top of my UTG range in a 4-handed game. With deep stacks and position on button, raising builds the pot and narrows the field against blinds. Standard raise size of 3x the big blind (300) balances value and fold equity. History shows frequent 250-300 raises preflop, so this fits the table dynamic while pressuring shorter stack Flash in BB.
I have 4h6d offsuit on the button facing a UTG raise to 300. This is a very weak hand that doesn't play well even with position. 64o is far below my calling range against a UTG open. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c5c facing a 3bb raise from the UTG player (Elon). While 75s is a speculative hand with flush and straight potential, playing it out of position against a strong UTG range is generally unprofitable. The gap between 5 and 7 reduces its connectivity compared to 76s. Additionally, calling invites the Big Blind to squeeze or enter the pot, leaving me sandwiched or heads-up out of position. The pot odds (calling 250 into 450) are not compelling enough to overcome the positional disadvantage and reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard prudent play here to preserve chips for better spots.

In a 4-handed game, Ace-Jack offsuit (AJ) is a premium hand. Elon, being the chip leader and a very active player, is likely opening a wide range from UTG. By 3-betting to 1000 (roughly 3.3x his open), I am asserting dominance and playing for value while out of position.
