This action allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately pre-flop or define Elon's range if he decides to call or 4-bet.

AKo remains a premium hand facing a 3-bet from short-stacked Flash in the BB (5300 effective). In 4-handed play, my UTG open was strong, and Flash's 3-bet sizing to 1000 (3.3x) suggests a polarized range including bluffs and value like QQ+, AK, suited broadways. Both blinds folded, isolating vs Flash OOP. Calling 700 is suboptimal OOP with a hand that plays well postflop but faces positional disadvantage. Folding is -EV. 4-betting applies maximum pressure on his short stack; sizing to 2800 (about 2.8x his bet) builds a pot for value while denying equity to his calling range. If he jams (~5300 total), I have ~45-55% equity vs typical jam range (sets, 2-pairs unlikely pre, premiums, bluffs), and pot odds justify. Shoving my full stack is unnecessary with deep money behind. This maximizes fold equity and value extraction late in the tournament.

I am currently holding Ace-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind. While AJ is a strong starting hand in a 4-handed game, the action has escalated significantly. After my 3-bet to 1000, Elon, the chip leader, responded with a 4-bet to 2800. This 4-bet represents a very strong range (typically JJ+, AQ+). If I call the 1800, I will have only 2500 remaining in my stack with a pot of over 5600, making the SPR extremely low and essentially committing me to the hand regardless of the flop. Given that AJ is often dominated by a standard 4-betting range and that I am out of position, the most prudent tournament strategy is to fold and preserve my remaining 4300 chips (43 big blinds) to find a higher-equity spot, rather than risking my tournament life on a marginal hand against the chip leader's show of strength.

Q8o from UTG is a weak hand that doesn't play well from early position. With 3 players left to act including Elon who has been aggressive with a large stack, opening this hand invites 3-bets and puts me in tough spots postflop. Standard fold from UTG.

I am on the Button with 8s 6c (86o). This is a weak off-suit hand that falls outside the standard opening range for the Button in a 4-handed game. While raising to steal the blinds is sometimes an option with marginal hands, the Big Blind (Elon) has a massive stack (over 250 BB) and has shown aggression recently, making it risky to open light as he can easily defend or 3-bet. The Small Blind also has a playable stack. There is little value in getting involved with a hand that plays poorly post-flop and is easily dominated. Conserving chips by folding is the correct strategic decision here.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc5h. The action has folded to me. My stack is relatively short at 5250 compared to the others. Elon is in the Big Blind with a massive stack of 25275. Given my weak hand and the fact that Elon has been active and has a significant chip lead, calling or raising into him with J5o is not profitable. Folding here is the standard and safest play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in UTG position with 9c 6d, which is a very weak off-suit hand. With 117 big blinds, I have plenty of chips and should not be playing trash hands from early position. There are three players left to act, increasing the likelihood someone has a better hand. Folding is the only standard and correct play here.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit. In a four-handed game, this is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play in position. I will raise to 300 to maintain a consistent opening size and build the pot.

6s8h is a weak offsuit gapped hand with poor playability out of position in the small blind against a button raise. Button ranges are wide, but this hand has low equity, gets dominated often, and is hard to play postflop OOP. Pot odds are not sufficient (calling 250 into 450 for ~36% equity needed, but hand realizes poorly). Standard fold to preserve stack for better opportunities late in tournament.
5-3 offsuit is a weak hand to defend against a button raise. Even with 2.25:1 pot odds, this hand plays poorly postflop and I'm out of position. Fold and wait for a better spot.

KJo is a strong starting hand in a four-handed game, functioning similarly to a cutoff open. With a stack of 54 big blinds, I am the shortest at the table but still have plenty of room for post-flop play. I'll open for a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or see a flop against a single opponent.

Pocket eights is a premium hand, well ahead of an UTG opening range from Flash, who has about 51BB. As button with deep stack, excellent position to 3-bet for value and isolation against the shorter stack (Flash 5100). SB and BB will often fold weak hands. A 3x raise to 900 builds the pot while denying equity to worse hands and putting pressure on Flash to commit or fold. Folding or calling would be too passive; set-mining with position unnecessary given strength.

ATs is a solid hand but facing a 3-bet from Elon who has shown aggression. I'm OOP which is concerning, but the hand plays well postflop with nut flush potential and decent high card strength. Calling to see a flop is reasonable - folding is too tight and 4-betting OOP seems marginal.

I hold 7d 2h in the Big Blind, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The action in front of me is heavy: a UTG open to 300, a Button 3-bet to 900, and a cold call from the Small Blind. Although I am getting decent pot odds (calling 800 to win a pot of 3000, roughly 26.6%), my equity against three ranges that include an opener, a 3-bettor, and a cold caller is extremely poor (likely well below 20%). Furthermore, playing this trash hand out of position against three opponents is a losing strategy. The correct play is to fold without hesitation.

I opened KJo for 300, but the action behind me has become very heavy. Elon 3-bet from the button to 900, and more importantly, Claude cold-called that 3-bet from the small blind. A cold-call of a 3-bet usually indicates a very strong range, such as medium-to-high pocket pairs or strong suited broadways. My KJo is frequently dominated by hands like AK, AJ, KQ, or trapped by pocket pairs. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (600 to win a pot of 2200), KJo plays poorly multi-way in a 3-bet pot out of position, and with my stack size, I would be heavily committed to any marginal top pair on the flop. Given the strength shown by two opponents, folding is the disciplined play to preserve my 51 BB stack for better spots.

I flopped top two pair (aces and tens) which is very strong, but the board is monotone hearts (Th Ah 6h) and I have no heart. This is a dangerous texture - any opponent with two hearts has a flush, one heart has a draw. Elon 3-bet preflop so his range includes hands like AKh, KQhh, suited connectors that could have hearts. Checking lets me see what Elon does - if he has a flush he'll likely bet, and I can make a decision. Betting and facing a raise would be brutal. Pot control with a vulnerable hand seems best.

Heads-up on a highly coordinated flop Th Ah 6h after I 3-bet pre-flop from the button and got one caller from SB who checked. Pocket 88 is a strong overpair to the T6 but vulnerable to Ax (which is in Claude's calling range) and any made flush (possible but less likely given pre-flop action). However, my 8h provides a flush blocker, reducing the likelihood of the nut flush draw. Claude's check indicates possible weakness like weak Ax, missed draws, or floats. With position and deep effective stacks (him 16.5k into 2.2k pot), a continuation bet denies equity to flush/straight draws (e.g., 97s, 75s, KJ), extracts value from Ax/Tx/6x, and builds the pot when ahead. ~2/3 pot sizing (1400) balances value and protection while keeping fold equity against bluffs. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing him to rep the flush later. Aggression maximizes EV late tournament to grow stack.

I have top two pair (aces and tens) on a monotone heart board with no heart in my hand. Elon bet 1400 into ~2200 pot. While any two hearts beats me, folding top two to a single bet is too weak. Elon's range includes many non-flush hands (AK, AQ, QQ, KK without hearts, one-heart hands semi-bluffing). Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me re-evaluate on turn - if another heart comes or he fires big again, I can fold. Raising risks getting stacked by a flush.

Fourth heart came on turn (5h). Board is now Th Ah 6h 5h - four hearts. My top two pair with Ac Ts and no heart is essentially worthless now. ANY single heart beats me. Elon 3-bet pre and bet flop, his range has many combos with at least one heart. Checking is mandatory - if he bets I should fold as he'll have a heart most of the time. If he checks back, my hand is likely good.

The turn 5h has transformed the board into an extremely dangerous four-heart flush board (Th Ah 6h 5h), completing numerous flush draws and adding straight draws like 97 or 74. However, my 8h completes the ace-high flush (Ah Th 8h 6h 5h), making this a monster hand strong enough for value despite vulnerability to higher flushes (e.g., KQhh, KJhh) or straight flushes. Claude's pre-flop call of my button 3-bet from SB indicates a strong range (Ax, broadway, sets), but his check-call on the flop (after checking) and now check on turn suggests possible Ax top pair, weaker made hands, or draws that hit lower flushes. With 5000 in the pot, position, and deep effective stacks (~15k), a 2/3 pot bet of 3300 gets value from worse flushes (e.g., 97h, 75h), pair + flush draws, or Ax while denying equity to rivers that improve villain. Checking would be too passive, missing value and granting a free card on a dynamic board. This sizing balances protection and thin value extraction to maximize stack growth late in the tournament.

Four hearts on board (Th Ah 6h 5h) and I have zero hearts. My top two pair is now worthless - ANY single heart beats me. Elon 3-bet pre and has bet both streets aggressively. His range is heavily weighted toward suited hands that would include hearts. Even if he doesn't have a flush, he has significant equity with any single heart for the river. Getting 2.5:1 but my hand is essentially drawing dead against his value range and only beats pure air bluffs with no heart. Clear fold.

K8o is a marginal offsuit hand from UTG in 4-handed play. Opening ranges widen short-handed, but K8o plays poorly post-flop: weak kicker, offsuit, vulnerable to domination by KQ/KJ/KT and sets/pairs. Facing aggressive opponents like Claude and Pro who frequently raise/3-bet, risking difficult spots with 28k effective stack. Pot odds poor to limp (to call 100? Wait, no limpers, to act first, but blinds posted). Standard play is fold to preserve stack for premium spots, maximizing EV long-term over 150 hands.
Q3o on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a standard steal spot - I have position and can apply pressure. Raising to 250 to try to take down the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with K6o facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Claude). While K6o has some raw equity against a wide button opening range, playing it out of position is difficult and often unprofitable due to poor equity realization. Additionally, calling invites the Big Blind to squeeze or call, leading to a multi-way pot where I am out of position. The standard play here is to fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 3h, which is a very weak hand. Claude, on the button, has raised to 250. It only costs me 150 more to call, but playing 7-3 offsuit out of position against a big stack like Claude is generally a losing proposition. My stack is relatively short (50BB) compared to the field, so I should avoid bloating pots with marginal trash hands.
J2 suited from UTG is too weak to open. While suited, the hand lacks connectivity and J-high with a 2 kicker has poor playability postflop. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I hold 7s 3d on the Button. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (7-3 offsuit). Despite having the positional advantage, the hand has almost no playability or equity to justify an open raise, especially with aggressive opponents like Elon in the Big Blind who can easily put pressure on me. Folding is the only standard and correct play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Td 6c. Both Claude and Pro have folded, leaving the action between me and Elon in the Big Blind. Elon is the chip leader and has shown a high level of aggression in recent hands. T6o is a weak hand to play out of position against a big stack who is likely to defend or 3-bet frequently. With nearly 50 big blinds, I have enough of a stack to wait for a more favorable spot rather than risking chips with a marginal hand in a blind vs. blind battle.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with Jack-Ten suited (Jh Th). This is a strong suited connector with excellent playability and is a standard open-raise in this position. The goal is to build the pot, take initiative, and potentially take down the blinds pre-flop. Limping is passive and invites the button to isolate or blinds to realize equity cheaply. A standard raise size of 2.5x (250 chips) balances my range and gives a good price on a steal attempt while keeping the pot manageable if 3-bet.

I am on the button with 3d4d, which is a speculative suited connector. Pro opened for 2.5bb from UTG. While these hands play well in position, 34s is at the very bottom of the range and is easily dominated. With a stack of approximately 50BB, calling a UTG raise with this hand is generally -EV, especially with two active players (including the chip leader Elon) still left to act behind me. Preserving my stack for a more high-equity opportunity is the better play here.

Preflop in the small blind facing an UTG raise to 250 from Pro, who has been aggressive but UTG suggests a strong range including pairs, broadways, and strong aces/kings. My K♦8♣ offsuit is king-high with a weak kicker, poor postflop playability out of position against this range—easily dominated by better kings, pairs, or Ax. Calling commits 200 into a pot of 400 (getting ~3:1 but facing tough spots OOP), and 3-betting lacks fold equity or value. With a deep stack (28k) late in the tournament (hand 110/150), folding preserves chips for better opportunities against a premium range. Standard fold.

Holding 9h5d in the BB facing a UTG raise to 250. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. UTG raises typically represent stronger ranges, and I'm out of position. While pot odds are ~2.7:1, 95o doesn't perform well enough to defend here. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am currently the short stack with approximately 50 big blinds. I am in the Under-The-Gun (UTG) position in a 4-handed game. Holding 7-5 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot, especially against three larger stacks. Folding is the correct play to preserve my chips for a more favorable hand.

On the button in an unopened pot after UTG fold, with T8o, a speculative hand with straight potential and decent playability. Deep stack and position allow me to raise for value, steal blinds, or play post-flop advantageously against blinds who may defend wide. Pro in BB has ~117BB but we've seen aggressive play; standard 3x raise to build pot or take it down preflop. Calling is loose OOP for them; folding too passive in prime spot late in tournament to maximize stack.

5s Th is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Facing a button raise from Elon who has the largest stack, this hand doesn't have the equity or implied odds to justify defending. I should fold and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with [6h 5c] facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Elon). The pot odds are roughly 31% (calling 200 to win a pot of 650). While 65o has some connectivity, it is an off-suit connector at the bottom of the playable range. Being out of position against the chip leader who opened for 3bb makes equity realization difficult. My estimated realized equity is likely below the required pot odds. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold.

Q8o offsuit from UTG in a 4-handed game is a standard fold. It's not strong enough to open-raise, as it plays poorly post-flop in position out of position, gets dominated by better queens frequently, and lacks suited or connected value for speculative play. Limping is suboptimal UTG. With a deep effective stack (mine is deepest), patience is key late in the tournament (hand 112/150) to maximize stack by waiting for stronger spots against these opponents who show aggression. Pot odds don't justify calling. Preserve chips for premium hands.

I have 3d4s on the button. Very weak hand but excellent position with only the blinds left to act. Standard button steal opportunity - I've been actively raising in position throughout this tournament. Raising 2.5x to 250 for the steal attempt.

I am in the Small Blind with J5o, a very weak hand. The Button (Claude) has raised to 250. I am out of position and my hand has poor playability and low equity realization against a button opening range. Calling 200 chips here would be a losing play in the long run due to the positional disadvantage and hand weakness. There is no strategic merit to bluffing (3-betting) with this specific holding. The correct play is to fold.

In Hand #112, I am in the Big Blind with Q5 offsuit. Claude has raised to 250 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. The pot is currently 400 and it costs me 150 to call. While Q5 offsuit is a weak hand, I am getting nearly 3:1 on a call and am closing the action. However, against a Button open, I am out of position and my hand has poor playability and is frequently dominated. Looking at my current stack of 4850, I want to preserve my chips for better spots rather than defend with marginal holdings in a high-variance spot against a significant stack like Claude's. I will fold.
A2 suited in UTG. Suited aces have decent playability with nut flush potential. Standard open raise to 250 is appropriate - not a premium hand but worth playing for initiative.

I hold J2s on the Button. While I have position, facing an open raise from UTG (Claude) indicates a stronger range. J2s is a very weak hand with poor playability and is easily dominated. There are no strategic reasons to call or 3-bet bluff against an UTG open with this holding. Folding is the standard and correct decision.

I am the shortest stack at the table with 48 BBs. Claude raised from UTG in a 4-handed game, which usually represents a decent range. King-Eight offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position, especially with the chip leader (Elon) still to act behind me in the Big Blind. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop without the initiative. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Facing an UTG raise from Claude to 250, with T9o offsuit in the Big Blind. This hand has poor playability out of position, mediocre equity against a strong early-position range, and no strong blockers or suited/connectors to justify a call or 3-bet. Pot odds are decent (150 to call into 400), but post-flop OOP dynamics with this hand are tough—easy to get outdrawn or dominated. With deep stacks and tournament late stage (hand 113/150), better to fold marginal holdings and wait for stronger spots to maximize stack. Folding preserves chips for premium hands.

I am in UTG (Cutoff in 4-handed play) with 6s 5h. This is an off-suit connector. While the game is 4-handed and ranges are wider, 65o is statistically too weak to open profitably from this position. It does not play well post-flop and is dominated by many calling hands. With 116 BB, I should play a solid strategy and fold this trash hand.

I am on the button with 2h 6d, which is an exceptionally weak hand. While the button is a prime position for stealing blinds, this hand has almost no post-flop value and very poor equity if called. With a stack of 48 big blinds, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with garbage hands. I will fold and wait for a more profitable spot.

Jh2d is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or blocking value. Preflop from SB facing only BB, with folds before us, completing would commit us to a multiway pot OOP with a hand that plays terribly postflop. Standard play is to fold weak blinds to preserve stack and avoid speculative plays late in tournament. Deep stack but no reason to risk chips here.

I am in the UTG position with 8c 4h, which is a very weak starting hand. With a stack of 4800 chips (48 big blinds), there is no reason to enter the pot from early position with such a disconnected and low-value hand. Conserving my stack for better opportunities is the optimal strategy here.

K4o is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability, low equity against calling ranges, and gets dominated by better kings frequently. Even on the button with position, the EV of opening is negative due to the hand's trash quality. With a deep effective stack and tournament nearing end (hand 115/150), preserve chips for premium spots rather than spew with marginal holdings. Standard fold preflop.

QTo is a solid hand heads-up against the BB. Flash and Elon folded, so I'm only facing Pro. With position after the flop and a decent holding, I should raise to put pressure and try to take down the blinds or play a pot with initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s7d facing a 2.5x raise (250 chips) from Claude in the Small Blind. The active players are down to 4. While I would have position post-flop, 67o is a weak offsuit connector that is typically outside the profitable defending range against a 2.5x raise, even in a Blind-vs-Blind scenario. The pot odds are roughly 30% (calling 150 to win a pot of 500), and while my raw equity against a wide range is slightly higher (~35%), equity realization is difficult with this hand. Standard GTO strategy usually folds 76o here, preferring to defend 87o+ and suited variants. I will fold to preserve my stack.

UTG with 4s9d offsuit is a complete trash hand. Poor high card strength, offsuit, no connectivity for straights or flushes. Standard fold from early position to preserve stack. Deep effective stack (280BB), plenty of better spots ahead with 35 hands left in tournament. No need to enter pot with this weak holding where I'll be out of position postflop against likely stronger ranges.
JTs suited on the button is a premium hand. With Elon folding UTG, I have great position and should raise to steal blinds or build a pot with this strong suited connector. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Three offsuit (3s Tc), facing a standard raise to 250 from the Button (Claude). This is a very weak hand with poor playability and is certainly dominated by the opener's range. Being out of position post-flop makes calling even less attractive despite the pot odds. 3-betting is not a profitable bluff with this holding. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Claude is opening from the button with a standard 2.5x raise. My hand, K2o, is at the very bottom of any potential defending range. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (requiring about 27% equity), K2o plays extremely poorly post-flop, especially out of position. It is easily dominated and rarely hits a board strongly enough to continue. Given my stack size of 47bb and the fact that I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand against a larger stack, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my tournament life for better opportunities.
J4 suited from UTG is too weak to open. While suited, the hand lacks connectivity and the jack is easily dominated. Standard fold from early position.

I am on the Button with K6o, which is a marginal hand. While blind stealing is possible, the Small Blind has a reshove/3-bet stack size (46 BB), and the Big Blind is the massive chip leader (279 BB) who can call widely or apply significant pressure. With 114 BB myself, I am deep-stacked, and K6o suffers from reverse implied odds (kicker trouble) and playability issues post-flop. It is cleaner to fold this weak offsuit King and wait for a better spot to attack.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h3h. The action has folded to me. While 93 suited is a relatively weak hand, I only need to call 50 more to see a flop in a pot of 150. However, Elon is in the Big Blind and has a very large stack, and players in this tournament have been aggressive pre-flop. Folding here to preserve my stack of 46.5 big blinds is a safe and standard play to avoid getting outplayed out of position with a marginal hand.

I am in the UTG position (which is the Cutoff in this 4-handed game) with [5c 7c]. Suited one-gappers like 75s are on the very edge of an opening range from this position. While playable in a passive game, the table features a very deep-stacked and aggressive chip leader (Elon) in the Small Blind and a solid stack in the Big Blind. Raising risks facing a 3-bet which would force a fold, wasting chips. Given my stack size (3rd of 4), preserving chips and waiting for better spots or better position (Button) is preferable to opening a speculative hand into aggressive blinds.

I am on the button with Queen-Six offsuit. While it is a potential stealing hand, my current stack of 4,650 (approx. 46 BB) is healthy enough that I don't need to take high-variance risks with marginal hands. With a large stack in the Small Blind (Elon) and an active player in the Big Blind (Claude), Q6o is too weak to open-raise or call with. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

9d2h is one of the worst possible hands: offsuit, low cards, large gap, no straight or flush potential. In the small blind facing only the big blind after UTG and Button folds, completing would commit chips to play out of position post-flop with minimal equity against a wide BB defending range. Folding preserves my deep stack (280BB) for premium spots in the late stages of the tournament, avoiding unnecessary risk with trash.

I am UTG with Ten of Clubs and Seven of Spades (T7o) in a 4-handed game. This is a weak off-suit hand and does not justify an opening raise or a call from the first position. With a stack of 4650 (46.5 BBs) and roughly 30 hands remaining in the tournament, I should focus on playing stronger ranges and avoiding marginal spots from early position.

On the button with As8c (ace-high offsuit), I have a strong position to open-raise. Flash has already folded UTG with a short stack, leaving only the blinds to act after me. A8o has good playability post-flop in position, solid equity against calling ranges, and decent fold equity pre-flop to potentially steal the 150 pot. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) is standard: builds the pot without overcommitting deep stacks, discourages weak calls from blinds while inviting action from better hands I want to dominate or play against. Folding or limping is suboptimal with position and this hand strength.

J8o is a weak hand to defend out of position against a button raise. While I'm getting decent pot odds (250 to win 450), I'll be playing a mediocre hand OOP for the rest of the hand. Elon has a big stack and has been active. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 5c (offsuit connectors) facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Elon). The pot odds are 200 to win 450, requiring about 31% equity. While 65o has some connectivity, it plays poorly post-flop from out of position, especially against a larger-than-minimum raise size. My raw equity against a wide button range is roughly 36%, but my equity realization will be low (likely under 75%) due to positional disadvantage and the offsuit nature of the hand. Adjusted equity is likely below the required pot odds. Therefore, folding is the most positive EV decision.

UTG with J7o in late tournament stage (hand 120/150, 4 players left). This is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against ranges, and easily dominated. Folding preserves my deep stack (282BB effective) for better spots. No steal incentive pre-flop UTG with BB short but others covered. Standard fold.

On the button with J7o after UTG folded. This is a standard button steal opportunity - I have position and only need to get through the blinds. J7o is playable here for a standard open raise. Pro has a decent stack and Flash is short-stacked, making this a good spot to apply pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Deuces (22) facing a standard 2.5x open from the Button (Claude). Claude has been opening very frequently from late position, so his range is wide. We are playing deep-stacked (over 100 BB effective), which makes set-mining with low pocket pairs mathematically profitable due to the high implied odds; if I hit a set (approx. 12% chance), I can likely extract significant value from Claude's wide range. Pocket Deuces play poorly post-flop without a set, and 3-betting would turn my hand into a bluff that forces me to fold to a 4-bet or play an awkward pot out of position. Therefore, the most prudent play is to flat call, looking to hit a set on the flop and check-fold if I miss.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 2d, one of the weakest starting hands. The Button (Claude) raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Pro) called. Although I only need to call 150 more into a pot of 600, giving me decent pot odds, the hand has very poor playability post-flop, and I will be out of position against two opponents for the rest of the hand. As the shortest stack at the table, I prefer to conserve my chips for better opportunities rather than defending with a marginal hand in a multi-way pot.

I hold Pocket Deuces (22) on a paired board of [4d, Qs, Qd]. I missed my set, which was the primary goal of calling pre-flop. The board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Claude) who has a wide range containing many Queens and higher pairs. My hand is currently beating only Ace-high or complete air, but has very low playability and is vulnerable. Leading out serves no purpose. My best course of action is to check and fold to a bet, as I cannot profitably continue without significant improvement or specific reads that justify a hero call this early in the hand.

Flop is 4d Qs Qd - a paired board that favors the preflop raiser. Pro checked to me. With the preflop aggression and position, this is a standard c-bet spot. The paired queen board is hard for Pro to connect with, and I can represent a queen or better. A small c-bet of around 1/3 pot should be sufficient to take this down most of the time.

I hold Pocket Deuces on a paired board of [4d, Qs, Qd]. Pre-flop, my plan was to set-mine, which failed. Claude (Button) has a significant range advantage on this board and has made a standard small continuation bet (1/3 pot) of 200 into 600. Although the price to call is excellent (requiring only 20% equity), my hand has very poor playability out of position. Almost any turn card will be an overcard to my pair, making it extremely difficult to realize equity or reach a showdown. I am currently behind any pair in Claude's range and flipping against his high-card bluffs. Sticking to the discipline of 'no set, no bet' (or call) in this spot is the correct long-term strategy.

J8o from UTG in a 4-handed game is a weak holding. While ranges open up short-handed, J8o is still too marginal to profitably open from first position. With 161 BBs, I don't need to take unnecessary risks with mediocre hands. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold Ten-Six offsuit on the Button. While being on the Button is advantageous for stealing blinds, my hand is too weak (T6o) to profitably open-raise, especially against the Big Blind (Elon) who is the deep-stacked chip leader and has shown aggression. The risk of being 3-bet or called by a better range is too high, and the hand has poor post-flop playability. Therefore, folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit. Both Claude and Pro have folded, leaving only Elon in the Big Blind. A9o is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot. Given my 45 BB stack, this is a standard open to either win the blinds now or play a pot with a hand that is likely ahead of Elon's defending range.

A9c suited is an excellent defending hand from the BB against an SB raise to 300 (3x). SB ranges are typically very wide here after folds from UTG and Button, including many weaker aces, suited hands, broadways, and pairs. We get great pot odds (pot will be 600 after call, investing 200 more into ~400 current pot), position postflop, and excellent playability with nut flush draw potential, straight draws, and ace blocker. Flash's stack of 4250 (~42BB) is short-ish but sufficient for implied odds if we hit top pair or better. Raising risks a shove from their wide value/light range where equity is closer to coinflip; calling realizes full equity and controls pot size deepstacked. Folding is far too tight given the spots.

Pre-flop I raised A9o from the Small Blind and Elon called from the Big Blind. The flop comes 8d-2s-5d, which is quite low and often connects better with a Big Blind's defending range (containing many small pairs and connectors) than with my high-card-heavy range. Since I have only Ace-high with no significant draws, checking is a solid play to control the pot size and see how Elon reacts. This prevents me from bloating the pot out of position against the chip leader while I have little equity.

Preflop call was correct with suited Ace from BB vs wide SB open. Flop 852 rainbow (two diamonds) completely misses my range, giving me ace-high with no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw. Board is relatively dry but features a diamond flush draw and minor gutshots. Flash checks SB, which is standard with weak/marginal hands or traps but likely weak given wide preflop range including Ax weaker than mine, small pairs, suited connectors that missed. As OOP with no equity realized and pure air, checking back realizes equity, controls pot size (~600), avoids bloating OOP where I'm vulnerable on turns bringing draws/completing hands. Betting risks folding out better equity realization vs calls from draws/pairs and facing raises/shoves from Flash's 42BB stack. Pot control prioritizes seeing turn cheap in position battle postflop; my deep stack (280BB effective) allows patience. Will re-evaluate turn based on Flash's range.

I have Ace-high and have picked up a flush draw with the 9d on a board of 8d-2s-5d-7d. Elon checked back the flop, suggesting he doesn't have a very strong hand. Checking again allows me to see the river for free or evaluate a bet from Elon. With my current equity (overcards plus the flush draw), I am comfortable checking to see what develops, avoiding bloating the pot out of position against a much larger stack.

Turn board 7d completes the diamond flush (board now has 8d,5d,7d), adding a third diamond and making the board extremely draw-heavy with possible straight draws (e.g., 36, 46) and completed flushes for any opponent holding two diamonds, which is plausible in SB's wide opening range (Ax suited/weak, suited connectors, broadway). My Ac9c remains pure ace-high with no pair, no flush draw (clubs), no straight draw, and the ace blocker reduces some strong Ax but not flush combos. Flash's check-check sequence OOP is consistent with a missed draw, weak Ax (worse than mine), small pocket pairs below 8, or total air from their polarized/wide range, while trapping nuts twice OOP into BB is unlikely given dynamic ranges. Pot is small at 600 (~6BB), and with Flash's 42BB effective stack vs my deep 280BB, checking back controls the pot, realizes my equity vs worse hands cheaply, denies free cards selectively, and avoids bloating OOP where a bet could face calls from pairs/draws/flushes or shoves from polarized range, turning my hand into a bluff with poor equity realization. Preflop/flop checks were pot control; continuing this to showdown maximizes EV with semi-bluff catcher value in position battle. River will re-eval based on action/board, prioritizing thin value or large bluffs only if clear.

On the river, the board is 8d-2s-5d-7d-Jc. I have Ace-high (As 9d). Elon has checked back both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates a weak range, possibly air or a low pair looking for a cheap showdown. While my Ace-high has some showdown value against his missed high-card hands, it is unlikely to fold out a pair if I bet, and a bet would only be called by better hands. Since the pot is small and I am out of position against the chip leader, checking is the most prudent play to either win at showdown for free or evaluate a small bet from Elon.

River board completes the diamond flush and adds Jc, making it very coordinated with possible straights (e.g., 46 for 4-5-6-7-8, 9T for 7-8-9-T-J) and flushes. Ac9c is pure ace-high (no club flush draw hit, no pair/straight). Flash's check-check-check sequence OOP from wide SB range (post UTG/Button folds) is consistent with missed draws, weak Ax (worse than mine), unpaired broadways, or small pairs below 7 that don't want to bloat pot. Trapping monsters OOP three streets unlikely. Ace-high loses to any pair (22-66, JJ, or board-pairing overpairs) but beats pure air/bluffs/missed draws (~20-30% of range est.). Pot tiny at 600 (my 47BB stack vs Flash's 71BB effective? Wait no, Flash 4250=71BB? Blinds 50/100 so 42.5BB), deep overall. Betting as bluff risks calls from pairs/value and stacks off unnecessarily; thin value impossible. Checking realizes equity vs worse cheaply, controls pot, minimizes variance late tournament (hand 121/150), wins at showdown frequently vs polarized weak range. Consistent with prior pot control.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with AJo, a strong starting hand that is well within the standard opening range for this position. My stack depth is comfortable at over 110 BB. A standard open-raise is the best play to isolate opponents and build the pot. I have consistently been opening to 2.5bb (250) in previous hands, so I will maintain that sizing to balance my range and apply pressure.

I am on the button with 8-7 offsuit, which is a weak hand. Pro, acting from under-the-gun in this 4-handed game, has opened for 2.5x the big blind. While I am on the button, my stack is the shortest at the table (45.5 BB). Calling here with a marginal, dominated hand against an early-position raiser is not profitable, especially since the two larger stacks in the blinds have yet to act and could easily squeeze or force me out of the pot post-flop. I will preserve my stack and look for a better spot.

9s3d is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. Pro's UTG raise indicates a strong range, and calling 200 into 400 pot from SB gives poor implied odds even deepstacked. No position post-flop, BB acts after. Folding maximizes EV long-term.

K7o in the BB facing a UTG raise. This is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly post-flop. Even though I'm getting ~2.7:1 pot odds, K7o is too dominated by UTG's range which includes stronger kings and pairs. With 29 hands left and a healthy 16000 stack, no need to defend light here.

I am holding Ace-Jack suited (Ad Jd) in the UTG position in a four-handed game. This is a very strong starting hand, well within the opening range for this position. My stack size is approximately 45.5 big blinds, which gives me enough room to play a standard game. I will open with a raise to 300, which is consistent with my previous opening sizes and serves to build the pot while narrowing the field.

47o is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or high cards. Facing an UTG raise from Flash, who has a short stack of 4250 but still represents strength from early position. Calling 300 into 450 pot gives poor implied odds postflop OOP if others fold, and position doesn't help enough with this trash hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 123/150).

Pocket 3s facing a UTG raise of 300 from Flash who has a short stack (~4250). Set mining is viable here - calling 250 into a 450 pot gives decent immediate odds, and if I hit a set I can potentially stack Flash. 3-betting with 33 isn't great as I'd hate to get 4-bet and have to fold. Calling to see a flop is the standard play with small pocket pairs in position to close the action (after Pro acts).

I am in the Big Blind with A4o facing a 3x raise from the UTG player (Flash) and a call from the Small Blind (Claude). Although the pot odds are roughly 3.5:1 (requiring ~22% equity), A4o performs poorly against a strong UTG opening range and is often dominated by better Aces. Additionally, playing out of position against two opponents with a hand that has poor playability and reverse implied odds (often winning small pots or losing big ones when dominated) makes calling marginal to negative EV. The 3x sizing (300 chips) is large enough to deter a loose defense compared to a min-raise. Preserving my stack of ~111bb is preferable to bleeding chips in a low-equity spot.

Missed my set completely on Ac Qh 8d. My pocket 3s have almost no value here - it's an underpair to the entire board. This board texture connects well with Flash's UTG raising range (Ax hands, broadways). No reason to bluff into a range that likely hit. Check behind and re-evaluate on turn.

I am heads-up against Claude on a board of Ac-Qh-8d. I have top pair with a Jack kicker (Ad Jd). This board significantly favors my pre-flop raising range from UTG. Claude checked to me from the small blind, which is a standard play. I will bet for value and protection, as I want to get called by weaker Aces (A2s-ATs), Queens (KQ, QJ), or draws like KJ or JT. A bet of approximately 65% of the pot is appropriate here.

I have pocket 3s on Ac Qh 8d - complete miss. Flash c-bet 450 from UTG on a board that heavily favors their opening range (Ax, broadways). My underpair has almost no equity here - only 2 outs to a set. No draws, no reason to bluff-raise as Flash is fairly committed with ~3800 behind. Easy fold.

UTG position with Jc2s offsuit, a complete trash hand lacking playability, suitedness, or straight potential. Early position requires tight range to avoid difficult post-flop spots multiway. With 4 active players and stacks allowing for ICM considerations near tournament end (hand 124/150), folding preserves my deep stack of 28k and avoids spew. Opponents' stacks show Flash short but irrelevant here as hand too weak to open.

Pocket Aces on the button - the premium hand. Elon folded UTG. I should raise for value. Standard button raise of 3x to 300 looks normal and builds the pot. Don't want to raise too big and scare everyone off, but want to get value from this monster.

I am in the Small Blind with 4c Tc facing a 3x raise (300) from Claude on the Button. T4s is a weak hand, even suited, to play out of position against a raise. The pot odds are not compelling enough to call with this hand strength OOP, and a 3-bet bluff is risky given the hand's poor removal and playability. The standard and disciplined play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey of hearts. Claude has raised to 300 from the Button. While the hand is suited, it is very weak (T3s) and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. With a stack of 4850 (about 48.5 BBs), I am currently the shortest stack among the remaining four players. I should prioritize protecting my stack and looking for higher-equity spots to commit chips, rather than defending with marginal suited hands that are easily dominated or difficult to play post-flop.
6h5s from UTG is a weak offsuit hand. Not suited, poor position, and against 3 opponents. This is a standard fold - no reason to open with this holding.

I am on the Button with T9o, which is a marginal hand. The action folded to me. While the Button allows for a wide opening range, T9o is on the weaker side of playable hands, particularly off-suit. The Big Blind (Elon) is the chip leader with over 280 BBs and has shown aggression recently (e.g., Hand #107, #104). Opening light against an aggressive deep-stacked player invites a 3-bet which would force me to fold, wasting chips. Given my stack is healthy (111 BB) and we are late in the tournament (Hand 125/150), avoiding high-variance spots with marginal holdings against the chip leader is prudent. I will fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. Action has folded to me. This is a standard opening situation in a 4-handed game. I have an Ace blocker, which makes it less likely Elon has an Ace. I will raise to 300 to try and take down the blinds or play the hand with the initiative if called.

Preflop in the big blind facing a raise to 300 (needing 200 to call into 400 pot) from the small blind after UTG and button folded. Hand is 7h2d offsuit, one of the worst possible starting hands with no connectivity, low cards, and offsuit. Offers no postflop playability, dominated by villain's likely calling range. Pot odds ~2:1 require ~33% equity, but equity realization vs SB open-raise range (which includes many better hands even if stealing) is insufficient, especially multipot potential with stacks allowing further action. Deep effective stack (Flash 45BB), but folding trash preserves stack late in tournament (hand 125/150). Standard fold to avoid speculative defense.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 4-handed game with Queen-Four suited (Qs4s). While suited Queens have some playability, Q4 is a weak hand due to the low kicker and poor connectivity. Opening from UTG puts me at a range disadvantage against calling ranges, which will often dominate me (better Queens, better flush draws). With a stack of over 100 BBs, there is no need to force action with marginal holdings out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with JTo in a 4-handed game. This is a standard opening range hand. Opening from the button allows me to put pressure on the blinds and ensures I have position post-flop if called. I'll stick to my established opening size of 300 chips.

Pocket Aces is the best starting hand in poker. Flash raised from the button after UTG fold, likely a wide range including suited connectors, broadway cards, and speculative hands. With position disadvantage in SB but deep stacks (mine 28k effective vs Flash's 4.6k and Claude's 15.7k), I need to 3-bet for value to build the pot, protect my hand, and potentially isolate. Standard sizing is 3x the open to 900 total. Late in tournament (hand 126/150), maximizing stack with premium hands is key. Calling is too passive OOP; folding impossible.

I have 2c5d - a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a 3-bet to 900 from Elon, I'd need to call 800 more with a hand that has almost no playability. This is an easy fold - the hand is too weak to defend against a 3-bet, even from the big blind.

I opened with JTo from the button, which is a standard play. However, Elon (the chip leader) has 3-bet me from the small blind. JTo does not play well against a 3-bet range, even with the advantage of position. Calling would commit a significant portion of my stack to a pot where I am likely dominated or at best flipping. With only 25 hands left in the tournament and my stack being the shortest at the table, I should avoid high-variance situations with marginal offsuit hands against the chip leader. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my chips.

I am holding Ah3h under the gun in a 4-handed game. With a stack of 46.5 big blinds, this is a strong hand to open-raise with. I have been consistently opening for 300 chips in recent hands, and I will maintain that sizing to apply pressure and potentially win the blinds or play a pot with a hand that has good post-flop equity and nut potential (flush).

83o is one of the worst possible hands: offsuit, unconnected, low cards with no showdown value or playability. Facing a UTG raise from short-stacked Flash (4350 behind), who often raises aggressively per history, but calling or raising risks our deep stack (28k) on a hand that rarely improves enough to win. Button position allows stealing blinds sometimes, but not with trash like this. Pot odds poor (300 into 450, ~2:1 but implied odds minimal). Standard fold to preserve chips for premium spots with 23 hands left in tournament.
45o is a weak hand. Flash raised from UTG (indicating strength) and I'm out of position in the SB. This hand doesn't play well enough to call 250 here - easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from Flash in the UTG (CO equivalent in 4-handed). My hand is J6o, which is very weak. The pot odds are roughly 3.25:1, requiring about 31% equity to call. However, J6o plays very poorly out of position, often dominated or failing to connect. With 110 BBs, I should preserve my stack and avoid defending with trash hands OOP. The standard play is to fold.

Qh7h is a suited queen with playability for straights and flushes, decent speculative hand in 4-handed play where ranges are wider. As UTG with deep stacks (284 BB), this falls in the open-raising range to take initiative, build the pot, and potentially steal blinds or play post-flop effectively. Standard sizing 2.5x BB isolates weak blinds (especially short-stacked Flash at 47 BB) and button. Avoid limping to prevent multiway pots without initiative. Folding too tight late in tournament with 23 hands left.

I have pocket 6s on the button facing a min-raise from Elon in UTG. This is a good spot to call and try to set mine - I have position, implied odds against a deep stack (Elon has 28k), and pocket pairs play well postflop. 3-betting is an option but calling is more standard here to keep the pot small and see if I hit a set.

I am in the Small Blind with T3o, a very weak hand. Facing a raise from UTG and a call from the Button, I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 850), but playing a trash hand out of position against two players is a losing strategy. The potential for being dominated or failing to realize equity is high. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 5d, a very weak starting hand. Elon (UTG) raised to 250 and Claude (Button) called. I need to call 150 to win a pot of 650, which provides decent pot odds of approximately 18.75%. However, 95o is a poor hand that plays poorly out of position, especially in a three-way pot. With 4,700 chips (47 big blinds), I have enough stack depth to wait for a more profitable spot rather than defending with a sub-optimal hand against the chip leader and a button caller. Preserving my stack for better equity situations is the better play here.

Preflop raise with Qh7h took initiative and got a fold from blinds, isolating vs Claude's button call range, which is wide but capped somewhat OOP to a UTG raise. Flop 2s2dKh is a good c-bet board for my range: hits AK/AQ/KQ/KJ, mid pairs, and overpairs; low paired board reduces straight/flush draws. Board rainbow, no low cards for his suited connectors much. My Qh7h is queen-high with backdoor flush/straight potential, ~35-40% equity vs his float/calling range. Deep stacks (282BB), late tournament (23 hands left), but positionally OOP now. C-bet 1/2 pot (325) for value from weaker Kx/Ax/high cards and fold equity from air/misses. Protects against his floats and builds pot if called. Check risks giving free card or facing bet where I fold anyway; betting denies equity and reps strength well. Avoid overbetting paired board.

I have 66 on a 2-2-K board. My pocket sixes are an overpair to the deuces but under the king. Elon c-bet half pot after raising preflop. I'm getting good odds (325 to win 975) and my hand beats unpaired hands and lower pocket pairs. The board is very dry. Raising seems risky as I'd only get called by better. Folding is too weak given pot odds. Calling to see the turn with position is the best play.

Turn 4c is a blank card that doesn't complete obvious draws (no flush, limited straight possibilities like 3-5 or A5, but flop paired low). Board remains dry: 2s2dKh4c. My Qh7h is queen-high, no pair/draw, low equity (~15-25%) vs Claude's OOP flop-call range to UTG c-bet (Kx+, QQ+, sets, floats like AQo/QJo/JTs). Previous flop call suggests strength or float; unlikely pure air given sizing. With position (acting first heads-up), checking achieves pot control with marginal hand, induces bluffs/bets from weaker Ax/Qx/floats, and showdowns profitably if checked behind (queen often good). Double-barrel bluff suboptimal: thin value from worse unlikely, reps sets/two-pair but vulnerable to hero-calls/raises from Kx; stacks deep (150BB+ eff), preserve for better spots late (23 hands left). Check-fold/raise if he bets huge.

Elon c-bet flop but checked turn on 2-2-K-4 board. His check signals weakness or pot control. My 66 has showdown value - beats all unpaired hands and lower pairs. Betting only folds out worse hands and gets called by better (Kx, trips). Checking back protects my showdown value and keeps the pot small. I can evaluate the river based on his action.

River Jh is a somewhat scary card: brings a second heart (with Kh already on board), possible flush for heart draws, pairs the J for possible two-pair (KJ, J2 unlikely), and adds straight possibilities (Q-T but unlikely given action). Board now 2s2dKh4cJh remains relatively dry but more coordinated. My Qh7h is still queen-high (no improvement; hearts don't make flush with only four total). Vs Claude's OOP calling range to UTG open + c-bet on paired K-high flop (Kx, QQ+, sets, some Ax/floats that checked turn), queen-high has ~20-30% equity, often second-best to Kx or pairs but beats pure bluffs/A-high. Heads-up, acting first post-check-check turn, deep effective stacks (~150BB Claude), late tournament (22 hands left). Betting out risks folding out better bluffs and getting raised by value; thin value from Ax unlikely as he'd bet turn or check-raise river. Checking controls pot, induces bluffs from missed draws/A-high/weak Kx, allows cheap showdown if checked behind (queen live often), and check-fold to big bets preserves stack for better spots. No fold equity needed; positionally vulnerable but reps showdown value well. Check to realize equity profitably.

Elon checked river after checking turn. His line (bet flop, check turn, check river) suggests a weak hand that gave up - probably missed draws or weak showdown hands like ace-high. My 66 beats unpaired hands and lower pairs. Betting would only fold out worse and get called by better (Kx, Jx, trips). Checking back to realize my showdown value is optimal.

A5o from UTG in a 4-handed game is marginal but playable. I have a comfortable stack at 164bb. Standard open to 250 to apply pressure and potentially take down the blinds. If 3-bet, I can fold easily.

I am on the Button with JsTs, a strong suited connector that plays well post-flop. Claude raised from UTG, indicating a potentially strong range. I have position and deep enough stacks (approx 110 BB) to play this hand for implied odds and playability. Folding is too tight, and 3-betting might isolate me against a range I'm behind or face a 4-bet. Flat calling allows me to see a flop in position and keep Claude's range wider while investing a small amount relative to my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Claude raised from UTG and Pro called from the button. Even with decent pot odds to call the remaining 200, this hand performs poorly multi-way and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given my stack size of 4650, there is no reason to risk chips with such a weak holding.

Pre-flop in the big blind with Jd8c offsuit, facing an UTG raise from Claude to 250 and a button call from Pro. J8o is a marginal hand with poor playability multiway out of position. UTG range is strong, button call adds pressure. Pot odds are ~4.3:1 but equity realization OOP is low against likely strong ranges. Folding preserves stack late in tournament (hand 129/150) for better spots.

I flopped bottom pair with an ace kicker on T53 rainbow. This is a fairly dry board that doesn't connect well with a button calling range. I should c-bet small (around 1/3 pot) for value and protection - I can get called by overcards and weaker hands while also protecting against them improving. If raised, I can reassess.

I hold Top Pair with a Jack kicker (JsTs) on a dry rainbow board of Th-5d-3c. Claude opened UTG and c-betted 225 (approx 1/3 pot). My hand is well ahead of his bluffs (AK, AQ, lower pairs) but behind his premium value range (Overpairs, Sets, AT). Raising would likely fold out worse hands and isolate me against better ones. Calling is the best option to keep his range wide, use my position to control the pot size, and evaluate the turn. I have sufficient equity to continue.

Turn brought 7h, adding a heart flush draw to the board. I have bottom pair with ace kicker. Pro called my flop c-bet which usually indicates some kind of made hand (ten, pocket pair 66-99) or a draw. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value against Pro's continuing range. Betting again risks getting raised off my hand or inflating the pot with a marginal holding. Best play is to check for pot control and see a cheap showdown.

On the Turn, the board is Th-5d-3c-7h. I hold Top Pair (Tens) with a Jack kicker. Claude (UTG) checked after c-betting the flop. This check often signifies pot control with a hand like a weaker Ten, an underpair (99, 88), or missed overcards (AK, AQ). It could also be a trap with a set or overpair, though betting is more common to charge draws. Betting here isolates me against better hands (AT, Overpairs, Sets) and folds out hands I beat (AK, AQ). By checking back, I control the pot size, avoid facing a check-raise, and keep his range wide, potentially inducing a bluff on the river or getting a cheaper showdown. My hand has good showdown value but is vulnerable to being out-kicked by AT or beaten by overpairs in an UTG range.

The river 5h gave me trip fives with ace kicker - a very strong hand. Pro checked back turn showing weakness, likely holding a ten, small pair, or missed draw. I need to value bet. With the flush completing, I shouldn't go too big as Pro might fold medium hands. A bet around 60% pot should get called by tens and pocket pairs.

I hold TsJs (Top Pair, Jack kicker) on a board of Th-5d-3c-7h-5h. The river brings a second 5 and completes a backdoor flush draw. Claude (UTG) c-betted the flop, checked the turn, and now bets 650 into a pot of 1100 (~60%). His line is polarized. The turn check followed by a river bet on this runout usually indicates value (Overpairs, Trips, Flushes, or strong Tens like AT/KT) or a bluff. Given his UTG opening range, his value combos are plentiful (JJ+, ATs+, A5s, Heart flushes). My hand is merely a bluff catcher that loses to all his value hands and splits with JT. I have better hands in my range to call with (Flushes, Trips, AT). The price (27% equity required) is tempting, but the likelihood of him bluffing enough on a board that favors his range is low. I fold to preserve my stack.

I am in UTG position with A4o. Even in a 4-handed game, A4o is a marginal hand to open from the earliest position. My stack is healthy (105 BB), but there is a short stack on the button who might shove, and two large stacks in the blinds who cover me. A4o plays poorly post-flop out of position and is often dominated. Given the tournament stage (Hand 130/150) and my comfortable stack relative to the short stack, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with a weak ace. Folding is the prudent play.

I am on the button with 2d-6c, which is a bottom-tier hand. While the button is a prime position for stealing blinds, 2-6 offsuit is too weak to justify a raise, especially given that I am the shortest stack and don't want to waste chips on speculative steals against the larger stacks in the blinds. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

QQ is a premium hand, top of our range preflop. With UTG and Button folded, it's heads-up vs BB. Raising builds the pot for value, takes initiative, and denies equity to BB's calling range. Standard raise size from SB vs single opponent is 3x the BB (300 total). Deep stacks allow for postflop play if needed, but QQ performs well OOP. No history suggests BB is overly tight or loose in this spot.

AQo is a premium hand in the BB facing a min-raise from Elon. This is a standard 3-bet spot for value. Elon has been active and has the biggest stack. I'll 3-bet to around 900 to extract value and narrow his range.

Facing a 3-bet from the BB after my SB open-raise with QQ, a premium hand. BB's defending range includes AA/KK/QQ/AK and some bluffs suited broadways/connectors given position and folds before. Calling OOP is suboptimal as QQ plays poorly postflop out of position against a strong 3-bet range, vulnerable to sets and overcards realizing equity. 4-betting extracts value from his worse hands (TT-JJ, AK, bluffs) that fold or call lighter, builds a bigger pot when ahead, and denies equity to his calling range. Sizing to 2500 (2.78x his 3-bet, ~2/3 pot after) is standard deepstack vs BB 3-bet: large enough to pressure but not committed. If he 5-bets shove, QQ is still crushing his polarized range. Late tournament with deep effective stacks (Claude ~160BB), but initiative crucial OOP. History shows Claude aggressive but folds to pressure (e.g., Hand 26, 33). Folding QQ here is too tight.

Elon 4-bet to 2500 after my 3-bet. Against a 4-bet, AQo is in a tough spot - often dominated by AK or flipping against pairs like JJ/TT. Against a tighter 4-bet range (QQ+, AK), I'm in bad shape. The pot odds are ~2:1 requiring ~33% equity, which is marginal at best. With 16k stack at hand 130/150, I'm healthy and don't need to take this marginal spot. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am dealt 6c 2d in the Under-the-Gun (UTG) position. This is statistically one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em. With a stack of 4650 (46.5 big blinds) and being the shortest stack at the table, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this hand, especially from early position. I will fold and conserve my chips for a more favorable opportunity.

Preflop on the button in an unopened pot (Flash UTG folded, blinds posted creating pot of 150, to call 100). Holding 3d5h suited connectors, which have good implied odds and playability in position deep-stacked vs blinds. Late tournament stage (hand 131/150), stacks deep (287BB effective vs Pro's 104BB, Claude 161BB). Raising wide from button to steal blinds or build pot in position if called. Standard open size 3x BB to 300 total.

2s4s is a very weak hand. Even though it's suited, it has poor connectivity and low card values. Facing a raise from Elon who has position and a large stack, this is an easy fold. Not worth defending with such marginal holdings.

Opponent on the Button (Elon) opens to 3BB (300). While Button ranges are wide, K7o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a larger 3x sizing compared to a min-raise. The pot odds are roughly 31%, but equity realization will be difficult. Defending K7o is usually reserved for BvB or smaller sizing. Against the chip leader opening 3x, folding is the disciplined and prudent play to preserve the stack.

2s Td is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low card value. Acting first UTG in a 4-handed game, the correct play is to fold to avoid playing out of position with marginal equity. My stack is deep at nearly 300BB, no need to speculate. Preserves chips for better spots late in the tournament.

J8 suited on the button with only the blinds to act behind. This is a standard steal spot - suited broadway with a connector is plenty strong enough to open here. I'll raise to 250 to put pressure on the blinds and play in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s 6h facing a Button raise to 250 from Claude. My hand is a weak unsuited one-gapper with very low equity and poor playability. Playing this hand out of position is expected to be -EV. The cost to call is 200, requiring ~33% equity realization which is difficult to achieve OOP with this hand. I should fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 2.5bb. While the pot odds are tempting (150 to call into a pot of 400), Queen-Seven offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a large stack like Claude's. With only 18 hands remaining in the tournament and a stack of roughly 45bb, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips where I have a stronger range or initiative.

K8o from UTG in 4-handed play. This is a borderline hand but playable 4-handed. With 162 BBs I have a healthy stack. Standard open to 2.5x is fine - the king provides some equity and I've been actively opening from early position throughout the tournament.

I hold A5o on the Button facing a 2.5x raise from Claude in UTG. A5o is a marginal hand that is often dominated by an UTG opening range (which includes strong Aces and pairs). Calling is risky due to reverse implied odds and the potential for a squeeze from the blinds. 3-betting is too aggressive given the position of the opener and my hand quality. The disciplined play at this stage (Hand 133/150) is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am holding 7-6 offsuit in the small blind. Claude has raised from under-the-gun to 250. This is a very weak hand to play out of position against an UTG opening range, and calling would put me in a difficult spot for the rest of the hand with the shortest stack at the table. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my chips for better opportunities.

K3o is a very weak hand offsuit with poor playability and low equity against an UTG raising range from Claude, who has been aggressive but selective in early position. Facing a 2.5BB raise into a 4BB pot, calling 150 more (1.5BB) offers insufficient odds given the likelihood of domination (e.g., KQ+, Axs). Postflop position is an advantage, but reverse implied odds are terrible with this hand. With 287BB effective stack late in the tournament (hand 133/150), folding preserves chips for premium spots to maximize stack growth. No value in 3-betting bluff here as ranges don't support it.

I am in UTG position with 7d 3d. This is a very weak hand (7-high suited gapper) that is not suitable for opening from early position, even in a 4-handed game. I have a comfortable stack of over 100 BBs and there is no strategic reason to get involved with such poor hole cards, especially with aggressive opponents behind. The standard play is to fold.

I am on the button with a premium hand (Ad Qs) in a three-handed situation after the UTG player folded. With a stack of 4500 (45 big blinds), I should be raising to build the pot and take control of the hand. A standard 3x open to 300 is appropriate here to exert pressure on the blinds while maintaining a healthy stack-to-pot ratio.

Facing a standard button raise to 3x from Flash with a shortish 42BB stack. My 5h4s is a suited connector but very low, with poor postflop playability OOP in SB. Defending SB requires tighter range vs button opens due to position disadvantage. Pot offers ~1.8:1 direct odds (250 to call into 700 effective pot), but implied odds limited by hand strength, multiway potential with BB, and Flash's stack. Late tournament (134/150), preserve deep stack for better spots. Folding is GTO-correct.
2s9h is a very weak hand with poor playability. Against a button raise, I'm getting 200 to win 450, but 92o plays terribly postflop and is dominated by most opening ranges. Easy fold.

I am UTG with Jack-Five offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Given my stack size and position, there is no strategic reason to play this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity in the remaining 15 hands.

Preflop on the button with A8o after UTG fold. Excellent position with deep stacks. A8o is a strong opening hand here: ace-high blocker, good playability for straights/flushes, and high equity against calling ranges. Standard to raise for value and fold equity against blinds, who may be wide but can fold weak hands. Raising to 3x BB (300) builds the pot while maintaining fold equity. Late in tournament (hand 135/150), stealing blinds increases stack without showdown risk. If called, position allows postflop control.

K3o in the SB facing a button raise. This is a weak king that plays poorly out of position. Against a standard button opening range, I'll often be dominated by better kings and the 3 kicker provides little value. Not strong enough to 3-bet and calling OOP with this hand is -EV. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 4h, facing a 3x raise from the Button (Elon). The pot odds are approximately 30.7% (calling 200 to win 650). My hand is unsuited, disconnected, and very weak (bottom 10-15% of hands). While Button ranges are wide, 84o has poor equity and extremely poor playability out of position. The 3x sizing makes the price significantly worse than a min-raise, making a defense with trash hands unprofitable. I will fold to preserve my stack.

9s2d is an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low card value. From UTG in a 4-handed tournament spot late in the event (hand 136/150), standard play is to fold trash like this to avoid speculative situations out of position. My stack is deep at 28,850, while opponents have shallower stacks, but there's no reason to enter the pot with such a hand early position. Preserve chips for premium spots to maximize stack.

As8s on the button is a solid raising hand. With Elon folding UTG and only the blinds to act, I have position and a suited ace. Standard button open to 2.5x to pick up blinds or play in position postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Kc 6s (K6 offsuit). The Button (Claude) has raised to 250 (2.5BB). This exact scenario occurred in Hand #108 where I held the same cards against the same action and folded. K6o is a weak hand to play out of position, even against a wide button range. It lacks playability and dominates very few hands in the opener's range. Calling would require realizing decent equity out of position, which is difficult. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind with Seven of Spades and Six of Diamonds. Claude, the chip leader at the table, has raised to 250 from the button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win a pot of 400), 76o is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive big stack. With only 15 hands remaining in the tournament, I prefer to preserve my stack of 45.5 big blinds for better spots rather than entering a potentially difficult post-flop situation out of position.
3d4d from UTG is too weak to open. Suited low connectors play poorly out of position and from early position. Standard fold.

I am on the Button with Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o). With action folded to me, this is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds or play the pot in position. A7o is well within the top range of hands to open from the button 4-handed. I will use a standard sizing of 2.5x BB (250), consistent with my previous successful opens. If the big stack in the BB 3-bets, I can easily fold, but I expect to generate fold equity often.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Trey offsuit. Pro (Button) has raised to 250. K3o is significantly below the strength required to profitably call or 3-bet from the Small Blind against a Button open, especially with the chip leader (Elon) still to act in the Big Blind. Folding is the standard and most prudent play to conserve my stack as the tournament nears its end.

In the big blind facing a button raise to 250 after folds, with 8cTc suited connectors. Excellent speculative hand with strong playability, straight and flush potential. Pot odds are favorable: additional 150 to call into a pot that will be 550 (pot odds ~3.67:1), requiring only ~21% equity, which T8s exceeds against a wide button opening range (~35-40% equity). Deep effective stacks (Pro 9950, me 28750) provide great implied odds if we hit. Defend wide vs button, plan to play postflop aggressively on good boards or fold to heavy aggression. Calling is optimal over raising (not premium) or folding (too strong).

On this flop of Ac7h2h, first to act heads-up OOP vs button raiser with T8cc. Hand has zero equity: no pair, no flush draw (backdoor clubs at best), no straight draw (gutshot unlikely). Board is draw-heavy with heart flush draw and possible straight draws (e.g., 43, 54, 65). Villain's button raising range (Ax, broadways, suited gappers, suited connectors) hits this board frequently: top pair+, sets, strong draws. Pure bluff bet OOP risks stack with no fold equity necessary, and villain can easily call/raise light. Pot odds irrelevant as acting first. Optimal: check-fold to any reasonable bet, preserving stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 137/150, 4 players left). No value, no semi-bluff.

I have flopped Top Two Pair (Aces and Sevens) on a board with a flush draw (two hearts). This is a very strong hand, and my primary goal is to extract value from weaker Aces (A2-A6, A8-AT, etc.) and charge flush draws. Checking back risks giving a free card that could complete a flush or kill my action. A small to medium continuation bet is appropriate to keep the opponent's calling range wide while building the pot. The pot is 550, so a bet of 225 (approx. 40% pot) is a good size to get called by worse hands.

Facing a button bet of 225 into 775 on Ac7h2h flop with T8cc OOP heads-up. Hand is complete air: no pair, no straight draw (T8 needs specific cards not on board), backdoor club flush/spade straight draws at best but irrelevant. Pot odds ~4.4:1 require ~18% equity, but against Pro's wide button raising range (frequently Ax top pair, sets, heart flush draws, str8 draws), T8cc has <15% equity typically. Board favors villain's range heavily. OOP with no playability, calling commits to tough river spots or folding later anyway. Late tournament (137/150), preserve deep stack (28600) for premium spots vs shorter stacks. Check-fold optimal to avoid spew.

I am in UTG (which is also Cutoff in 4-handed play) with 9d 8h, an off-suit connector. While ranges are wider in 4-handed play, 98o is still too weak to open profitably from the first position, especially with deep-stacked opponents in the blinds and a player on the button who has position on me. I have a healthy stack of over 100 BBs and should wait for better spots to accumulate chips in the final 12 hands.

I am on the button with ATo, which is a very strong hand in a 4-handed game. Since the UTG player (Pro) folded, I have a standard opening opportunity. I want to raise to build the pot and potentially take the blinds or isolate the action. A raise to 3x the big blind is a standard opening size.

Js4s is a marginal suited hand at best, but very weak jack-high with poor connectivity. Facing a button raise from Flash (short stack at 42BB), who opens wide, but I'm OOP in SB. Calling gives poor pot odds considering multiway potential with BB behind (deep stack), and postflop playability is terrible without position or strong equity (est. ~30% vs wide button range, worse multiway). Raising has no fold equity with this hand and commits too much OOP. Deep stacked (285BB), late in tournament (hand 138/150), better to fold and wait for premium spots to maximize stack.

Q7o in the BB facing a button raise of 300. I need to call 200 into a pot of 450, getting about 2.25:1 odds. Q7o is a weak hand that plays poorly postflop - often dominated when I hit the queen, and the 7 rarely helps. Against a button steal, this is marginal at best. With 16300 chips I'm healthy and don't need to defend with weak hands. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am dealt Qd 2s in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. With a stack of 4,650 chips (46.5 BBs) and only 11 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no reason to play such a weak, disconnected hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

A2s on the button is a strong speculative hand with excellent position against the blinds. Deep stacks allow for post-flop playability, where the nut flush draw potential and ace blocker are valuable. Raising builds the pot, isolates weaker ranges from blinds, and applies fold equity to steal the 150 pot. Standard sizing of 3x the BB (300 total) is appropriate late-stage with few hands left to maximize stack by accumulating blinds/chips.
T7o is a weak hand facing a button raise. Not worth defending from the small blind - poor playability and will be out of position postflop. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d 4d facing a standard 3x open from the Button. The pot odds are favorable; I need to call 200 to win a pot of 450, requiring approximately 31% equity. Suited connectors like 54s perform well against a wide button range and have good playability post-flop despite being out of position. My stack depth (104 BBs) allows me to defend here comfortably without risking tournament life. A call is the standard play to realize equity and potentially hit a strong draw or disguised hand.

I am out of position on a paired board (K-8-8) that heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (who has more Kx and stronger 8x hands). My hand (5d 4d) has completely missed, with only very weak backdoor straight and flush equity. Leading out (donk betting) makes no sense here as I fold out nothing better and get called by everything that beats me. The standard play is to check to the raiser and likely fold to a continuation bet given my lack of equity.

Heads-up on a paired flop (88Kcc with one diamond) after villain checks from the BB. My button raising range contains few strong hands that hit this board hard (few sets, two-pair combos), but villain's BB calling range is wide and often weak. Villain's check indicates possible weakness, missing K, Ax, or suited connectors. A2s is a strong bluff candidate: ace-high showdown value, position for free cards if check-raised, and low equity but fold equity high here. With deep effective stack (Pro 10k) but late tournament (hand 139/150), accumulating small pots builds stack without high variance. Bet sizing ~60% pot (400 into 650) balances value (thin Kx, sets) and bluffs, maintaining playable SPR (~20) post-bet. Check would give free card to draws/strength, suboptimal with position.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d 4d on a flop of 8c Kc 8d. I have missed the flop completely, holding only 5-high with no immediate draws on a paired board that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Facing a continuation bet of 400 into a pot of 650 (total pot now 1050), I have no equity to justify a call. The only reasonable play is to fold.

Pocket 3s is a strong hand from UTG in a 4-handed game. Small pairs perform well by setting mining or winning unpaired hands. As chip leader with a deep stack, open-raising standardly to 3x the BB (300 total) builds the pot, takes initiative, and isolates weaker ranges from blinds. Flash is short-stacked and may shove, but 33 has good equity against shoving ranges. Raising denies dead money and positions us well post-flop.
