Limp/fold is too passive; min-raise too small for value/protection.

KTd suited on the button facing a 3x open from UTG. This is a solid suited broadway hand with position. Elon has been active but UTG opens tend to be stronger. I'll call to see a flop in position rather than 3-bet, as 3-betting would commit more chips against his strong range.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Aces, the best starting hand. Elon opened to 300 from UTG and Claude called on the Button. This is an ideal spot to squeeze (3-bet) for value. Flat calling would invite the Big Blind in and leave me playing a multi-way pot out of position, which significantly decreases the equity of Aces. A 3-bet builds the pot immediately while I have a massive equity advantage and helps isolate a single opponent or provokes a 4-bet shove. Given the open of 300 and one caller, the pot is currently 750. A standard squeeze size out of position is roughly 4x the open plus the caller adjustment, or a pot-sized raise. A raise to 1400 puts significant pressure on the opponents, charges draws heavily, and extracts maximum value from second-best hands like KK, QQ, or AK.

I am currently the shortest stack at the table with 4,550 chips (45.5 BB) at Hand 140. I am facing an UTG open from Elon and a large 3-bet squeeze from Pro in the Small Blind. Pro's 3-bet to 1,400 into two players is a show of significant strength, likely representing a range that dominates my King-Ten suited (hands like AK, KQ, or high pairs). Calling would commit nearly 30% of my stack out of position, and shoving into three players, including the chip leader who opened UTG, is too risky given the high probability of being dominated. I will fold to preserve my stack and look for a more favorable spot to commit chips in the final 10 hands.

Pocket threes is a marginal set-mining hand UTG, but facing a sizable 3-bet to 1400 from Pro in the SB after my open and Claude's button call, the range is polarized towards strong value (QQ+, AKo) or bluffs, but SB squeezes are decent here with dead money. Calling 1100 OOP commits ~4% of my deep stack into a pot where I'll be OOP postflop, potentially multiway if Claude continues, with limited implied odds given Pro's 88BB effective stack (7400 behind). Equity realization is low without a set (~12% vs tight range), and fold equity on a 4-bet is minimal as Pro can easily call/shove. Late in tournament (hand 140/150), folding avoids speculative risk and preserves chip lead for better spots. Standard fold.

Facing a 3-bet squeeze from Pro in the SB after I flatted Elon's UTG open. Pro's squeeze range from SB is typically strong (premium pairs, AK, AQ). KTs is at the bottom of my continuing range here - I'm often dominated by AK, KQ or racing against pairs. With 1100 to call into a 2100 pot, the pot odds are decent but not great. Pro has about 8800 behind so SPR would be ~2.3 if I call - we'd be pretty committed on most flops. Given the strength of a SB squeeze after UTG open + button flat, folding is the disciplined play.

Q5o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak offsuit hand that doesn't play well from early position. With 160BB effective stack and 9 hands remaining in the tournament, I should maintain tight opening ranges from UTG. No need to get involved with marginal holdings here.

The current hand is Q8s (Queen-Eight of Hearts) on the Button in a 4-handed game. This is a standard steal position. With a playable suited connector/gapper and position, raising puts pressure on the blinds. Although the Big Blind (Elon) is the chip leader and defends frequently, I have the advantage of position post-flop. A standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is appropriate to attempt to win the blinds or play a pot in position.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Seven offsuit. Pro opened from the Button for 2.5x. While King-high has some raw value, K7o is a weak hand to play out of position against a Button raise, especially with Elon (the chip leader) still to act in the Big Blind. Elon is very active and could easily squeeze or outplay me post-flop. With 4,500 chips (45 BBs) and only 10 hands remaining in the tournament, I should avoid marginal spots where I'll likely be forced to play a bloated pot out of position with a dominated or mediocre hand. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for a better opening.

T6o is a very weak offsuit hand with low playability, especially out of position in the big blind facing a button raise. Pro's button raise is likely wide, but T6o has poor equity against their range and will be difficult to play post-flop OOP. Pot odds to call 150 into 400 are about 2.7:1, but this hand doesn't realize enough equity long-term. With only 10 hands left, folding marginal hands preserves stack for better spots. Standard fold.

Current hand is 142/150, playing 4-handed. I am UTG with K9o and a stack of 11,050 (approx 110 BB). While 4-handed play loosens opening ranges significantly, K9o is still a marginal hand to open from first position. The player to my left (Flash) is short-stacked (45bb) and could easily shove over an open, forcing a fold. The players in the blinds (Elon and Claude) are deep-stacked and capable of defending or 3-betting, which would put me out of position with a weak King. Having raised the button in the previous hand, tightening up slightly from UTG is prudent to maintain a credible image. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Q9o. The player in UTG (Pro) has folded. The pot is currently 150 (50 from Elon, 100 from Claude). I have a stack of 4500, which is 45 big blinds. Q9o is a reasonable hand to open from the button in a 4-handed game to steal the blinds or play in position. However, given the tournament's late stage (Hand 142 of 150) and my stack size relative to the others, I want to be selective but aggressive. Raising here puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to take control of the hand. I will raise to 300.

Preflop in SB with 5s8c offsuit, a weak hand with poor playability OOP. Flash raised from button (standard position), short stack at 42BB but no fold equity needed. Pot 450, to call 250 for ~5.5:1 but postflop position terrible, hand unlikely to improve well. Late tournament, preserve stack, fold trash hands facing raises OOP. No history suggests Flash weak; standard fold.

I have pocket 6s in the BB facing a button raise of 300. Flash has ~4200 stack and opened from button, likely a wide range. Pocket 6s are decent for defending - I'm getting good pot odds (200 to win 450) and have set mining potential. 3-betting is an option but with a short-stacked opponent, calling to see a flop is more prudent.

Flop came Q-T-4 rainbow, completely missing my pocket 6s. I have an underpair with two overcards on board. Flash raised from button and will likely c-bet this board frequently. Checking is standard here - I can check-call a small bet or check-fold to heavy aggression. No need to lead into the preflop aggressor with a marginal hand.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen. The board is Q-T-4 rainbow, which is relatively safe though there are some straight draws like KJ or J9 possible. Claude checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. I have 3,900 chips remaining and the pot is 650. This is a clear value betting situation to protect my hand against draws and get called by worse hands like middle pair (Ten) or smaller pocket pairs. I will bet 350, which is slightly more than half the pot, to build the pot and maintain initiative.

I have pocket 6s on a Q-T-4 board - a complete whiff with two overcards. Flash c-bet 350 into 650 (about 54% pot). My underpair has very little equity against Flash's continuing range which hits this board well (Qx, Tx, JJ+, draws). I'd need ~35% equity to call and I'm likely around 15-20% at best. No implied odds worth chasing since Flash only has ~3850 behind. Clean fold.

I am holding King-Nine of clubs (suited) in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament and currently being the shortest stack at the table (48.5 big blinds), I need to look for opportunities to increase my stack without taking unnecessary risks. K9s is a strong starting hand in a short-handed game and comfortably falls within a standard opening range from this position. I will raise to 300 to take initiative and potentially pick up the blinds or narrow the field.

Ad9d is a premium suited ace hand on the button with excellent position and playability. Facing a UTG raise from Flash, who has a relatively short stack of 4550 (~45BB), his range is likely strong but not uncallable. A 3-bet isolates him, applies pressure, and leverages my deep stack (28450) and position. Calling is fine but allows multiway pots; raising builds the pot OOP for him while denying equity to blinds. Sizing to 900 (3x) is standard, potentially folding out worse aces or inducing shoves from premium hands where I have FE and position postflop.
Q6o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet from Elon. I'm out of position and would need to call 850 into a 1350 pot. Q6o doesn't play well postflop and is dominated by most 3-betting ranges. Clear fold.

I am holding 8s 3d in the Big Blind, which is a very weak hand. The action pre-flop has been significant: Flash raised from UTG and Elon 3-bet from the Button. Facing a 3-bet with trash cards makes this a trivial decision. The pot odds do not justify calling 800 chips with such poor equity against strong ranges, and I would be out of position. Preserving my stack of 10,950 is the priority.

I opened K9s from UTG in a 4-handed game, and Elon has 3-bet me from the button. While K9s is a decent hand 4-handed, Elon has shown a pattern of 3-betting and has a massive stack that allows him to pressure me as the shortest stack at the table. K9s is likely dominated by his value range (AK, KQ, 99+) and does not play particularly well out of position in a 3-bet pot. With only 8 hands remaining and a healthy 42 BB stack if I fold, I will choose to wait for a better spot rather than committing more chips with a marginal hand against the chip leader.

4-handed pre-flop UTG with 6c3c. This is a weak suited gapped connector. Even short-handed, UTG open range prefers stronger suited connectors like 65s+, higher broadways. Low equity when called or 3-bet, difficult post-flop playability with reverse implied odds. Deep stack (289BB), no need to gamble with marginal hand late in tournament. Opponents have varying stacks, Flash short in BB but still fold to preserve for better spots with 6 hands left. Standard fold.
On the button with T5 suited after UTG folds. This is a marginal hand but suited with position against just the blinds. Standard button steal opportunity - I'll raise to 2.5x.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four offsuit (Qs 4d). The Button (Claude) has raised to 250. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Even though I am getting decent pot odds to call, the reverse implied odds and the disadvantage of being out of position against an aggressive opponent make this a clear fold. My stack is healthy (over 100 big blinds), so there is no need to get involved in a marginal spot near the end of the tournament (Hand 144/150).

I'm in the big blind with Jd 8h. Claude, on the button, has raised to 250. It's 150 more to me to call into a pot that will be 550. This is a reasonable price to defend my blind, but J8o is a relatively weak hand. However, given we are deep into the tournament (hand 144 of 150) and my stack is currently the shortest at 4450, I need to be cautious about bleeding chips. Claude has been active. Since I am out of position and the hand is mediocre, I will fold to preserve my stack for better spots in the remaining few hands.
Ad5c in UTG, 4-handed with 6 hands left. A5o is a reasonable open in a short-handed game. I have a solid stack (15,750) and should continue my standard opening strategy. Standard raise to 2.5x.

Current situation: 4-handed game, holding ATo on the Button. Claude (UTG) has raised to 250 (2.5bb). Claude has been observed opening a reasonably wide range from UTG in this 4-handed format (including hands like 77 and JTs), against which ATo has decent equity and dominates some of his broadway/Ax hands. 3-betting isolates but risks bloating the pot against a range that might dominate me (AJ+). Folding is too tight given the position and active dynamics. Calling allows me to use position to navigate post-flop, keeping Claude's wider range involved while controlling the pot size. I am aware of the stacks in the blinds (Flash short, Elon deep), but flatting is standard here.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 4d. Claude (UTG) has raised to 250, and Pro (Button) has called. While the pot odds are attractive (200 to call into a pot of 650), 6-4 suited is a weak speculative hand to play out of position against an UTG raiser and a button caller. With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament and being the shortest stack at the table (4400 chips), I should prioritize preserving my stack for a better opening or a late-position steal rather than entering a multi-way pot out of position with a marginal hand.

9d4c is a terrible hand: low offsuit cards with a large gap, no straight or flush potential. Facing an UTG raise from Claude (tight range) and a button call from Pro, this is a clear fold from the BB. Pot odds are insufficient (150 to call into 650, ~4:1, but equity vs ranges is poor). Late in tournament (hand 145/150), better to preserve deep stack for premium spots rather than spew chips multiway.

Flopped top pair with A5 on AK4ss board. I have the Ace of diamonds, no spade draw. Against Pro's button calling range, I likely have the best hand much of the time, but I'm vulnerable to better aces and kings. A small c-bet (~1/3 pot) is appropriate for value and protection against draws.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a Ten kicker on a board of [As, Kd, 4s]. The opponent (Claude), who opened from UTG, has c-bet small (225 into 650, roughly 1/3 pot). This texture hits a UTG range hard (AK, AQ, Sets), but the small sizing suggests a range bet or a merged range including draws and weaker made hands. Raising would likely only get action from better hands (AQ+, Sets) while folding out bluffs or weaker hands like Kx or underpairs. Calling allows me to keep his range wide, use my position to evaluate future streets, and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal kicker. The price is excellent to see a turn card.

Turn brought the 3s, putting three spades on board. I have top pair with Ad5c but no spade for a flush draw. This is a very dangerous board - any two spades now has me beat. Pro called both preflop and flop, which could indicate a flush draw that just got there, or a stronger ace/king. With my weak kicker and no backdoor equity, I should check to control pot size and try to get to showdown cheaply. Betting here only gets called by hands that beat me.

Current hand is As Kd 4s 3s. I hold Ac Td (Top Pair). Claude (UTG) opened, c-bet flop small, and now checks the turn. The 3s brings a possible flush (3 spades on board). With the As on board, the nut flush is not possible for anyone, but Ks-high flushes are. My Top Pair is vulnerable and likely only ahead of bluffs or weaker pairs (Kings) which might fold to a bet anyway or call and leave me in a tough spot on the river. Betting opens me up to a check-raise which would force a fold of a hand with showdown value. By checking back, I exercise pot control, realizing my equity and inducing bluffs on the river or getting to showdown cheaply. I have showdown value but not enough to value bet comfortably 3 streets on this texture.

River completed 4 spades on board (As Kd 4s 3s 2s). I have Ad5c with no spade - my top pair is now worthless against any hand containing a single spade. Betting accomplishes nothing since I only get called by better hands. Check and hope Pro also missed the flush for a showdown win.

The board has run out [As, Kd, 4s, 3s, 2s], putting four spades and a four-card straight (A-2-3-4) on the board. I hold [Td, Ac] for top pair. With four spades on the board, any spade in the opponent's hand makes a flush, and any 5 makes a straight. Since I hold neither a spade nor a 5, my hand is merely a bluff-catcher or a chop candidate against other non-spade Ax hands. Claude checked to me. There is very little value in betting: worse hands (like Kx without a spade) will almost certainly fold given the scary board texture, and better hands (flushes, straights, better kickers, or sets) will call or raise. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and get to showdown cheaply, hoping Claude has a hand like KQ/KJ without a spade or a smaller pair.

I am in UTG (first to act) in a 4-handed game with [As 5h]. With only 4 hands remaining after this, and sitting comfortably in 3rd place (well ahead of the short stack Flash, but far behind the leaders), my priority is to preserve my stack rather than take marginal spots. A5o is a weak Ace that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Opening invites aggressive responses from the large stacks (Elon, Claude) or a shove from the short stack (Flash), putting me in a difficult spot. Folding is the standard and prudent play here.

With only 5 hands remaining and a relatively short stack of 4400 (44 BB), I need to be selective about the hands I play to maximize my finishing position. Jack-Seven offsuit is a weak hand even from the button. While I could attempt a steal, both Elon and Claude are deep-stacked and have shown a willingness to defend their blinds or 3-bet. I will fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity in the final few hands to commit my chips.

Folded to me in the small blind holding pocket threes (3c3d), a strong speculative hand with set-mining potential and decent playability. Standard play here is to raise to isolate the big blind, steal the dead money (150 in pot), and take the initiative. BB (Claude) has been aggressive historically but folds to raises often enough. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) is a good size: builds the pot without overcommitting, pot becomes 400, BB faces ~200 to call. My deep stack (28k) supports this; equity vs calling range is solid (~50%+ if called). Late tournament (hand 146/150), goal to maximize stack favors aggression in positionally advantaged spot. Will fold to 3bet unless light.

J2o is a weak hand in the BB facing a min-raise from Elon (who has been active). Getting 2.3:1 odds but being out of position with a dominated hand makes this a clear fold. Not worth defending with this weak holding against an aggressive big stack.

I am dealt 8s 2h in the UTG position (which is the cutoff in this 4-handed game). This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em and holds very little value for opening or calling, especially with a stack of 44 big blinds and only a few hands remaining in the tournament. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities in the final hands.

Hand 147 of 150, very late in the tournament with deep stacks (289 BB effective). On the button with 3d6d suited connectors after UTG fold, this is a standard spot to raise for value, position, and blind steals. 3-6 suited plays well post-flop in position against likely calling ranges from blinds. Raising to 2.5x (250) balances fold equity and pot-building while keeping it small given deep stacks. Folding too tight, limping invites multiway pots bad for speculative hand.
J4o is a weak hand to defend out of position against a button raise. I'll be playing a marginal hand OOP for the rest of the hand - easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with KJo facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Elon). The pot odds are very favorable, requiring me to call 150 into a pot of 400 (approx 27% equity needed). KJo is well ahead of a wide Button opening range. Given the late stage of the tournament (Hand 147/150) and being out of position, I prefer to flat call to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable rather than 3-betting and potentially facing a 4-bet or playing a bloated pot OOP against a strong range.

I am out of position in the Big Blind against the Button opener. The flop comes A-10-4 rainbow. This texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, as they hold all the strong Ax hands, whereas my range is capped as a caller. I hold KJo, giving me a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (Broadway with a Queen) and backdoor possibilities. Leading out (donk betting) makes little sense here as I am unlikely to fold out better hands (like Ax or Tx) and I don't have a value hand strong enough to build the pot. The standard play is to check to the aggressor to see if they continue. I will check and evaluate based on their action.

Flop of 4s Ad Tc hits my 3d6d perfectly with the nut diamond flush draw (Ad on board kills some draws but mine is nutted) plus a gutshot straight draw to the 5 for a 3-4-5-6 straight. Pro (BB caller) checked, pot 550, my position with ~286BB effective vs Pro's ~102BB. Strong semi-bluff spot: betting builds the pot for when I hit, charges opponent's draws/pairs, has fold equity vs weak Ax/pairs that checked. Deep stacks late in tournament (147/150), can't check-fold equity here; betting maximizes EV by denying equity and applying pressure. Size to ~half pot for balance, protecting hand while keepingSPR reasonable.

The board texture [4s Ad Tc] heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Elon on the Button), as the Ace connects well with his opening range. I hold King-Jack offsuit, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen) and King-high. Facing a bet of 300 into a pot of 550, I am being offered pot odds of roughly 3.8:1 (requiring ~26% equity to call). My direct equity with a gutshot is only about 8.5% to hit on the turn. Without a flush draw or a pair to go with it, and playing out of position, I do not have sufficient equity or implied odds to justify a call. Floating out of position on this texture is generally a losing play. The disciplined move is to fold and preserve my stack.

Late in the tournament (hand 148/150), chip leader with 292 BB effective. 4-handed, UTG with 9sTh suited connector, strong speculative hand with good playability postflop. Wide opening ranges appropriate short-handed. Raise to standard 3x BB size (300) to steal blinds/antes (none shown but pot 150), apply pressure on shorter stacks (Flash 43BB, Pro ~100BB). High fold equity vs blinds, continue aggression if flat-called, fold to shoves from tight ranges. Maximizes stack growth by winning dead money or playing strong postflop.

67 offsuit on the button facing an UTG raise of 300. This is a marginal hand - it's a suited connector type hand but offsuit which reduces its playability significantly. Elon has been aggressive and has the biggest stack. While I have position, 67o doesn't play well enough against an UTG opening range. With only 3 hands left and a solid stack of 16,225, no need to take marginal spots. Fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s5s. The player in the UTG position (Elon), who is the chip leader and has been aggressive, opens to 300. Although we are 4-handed and my stack is deep (100BB), playing a suited one-gapper from the Small Blind against an UTG open is generally a losing play in the long run due to positional disadvantage and difficulty realizing equity. Additionally, the Big Blind is somewhat short and could squeeze. Given there are only 3 hands left, preserving chips by avoiding marginal out-of-position spots is preferred.

In the Big Blind with 9d 7d, I am facing a 3x open from Elon in the UTG (Cutoff) position. With only 3 hands left in the tournament and being the short stack, I need to be careful, but the pot odds are very favorable. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me 3.25:1. 9d 7d has sufficient equity against a wide opening range in a 4-max game to justify a call. I will see a flop out of position and look for opportunities to realize my equity or check-fold if I miss completely.

I am the Big Blind in a heads-up pot against the pre-flop aggressor (Elon). The flop of Qc-Jh-4s is very favorable for an UTG opening range, which often contains high broadway cards like AQ, KQ, QJ, and JJ. My hand, 9d-7d, has completely missed this flop and offers very little equity. Since I am out of position and have no piece of the board, my standard play is to check to the aggressor. If Elon bets, I will most likely fold as I have no reason to contest this pot with only three hands left in the tournament and a relatively short stack.

Late tournament (148/150), massive chip leader at 289BB vs Flash's 41BB heads-up OOP on QcJh4s flop with 9sTh: open-ended straight draw (8 outs to 8 or K, ~32% equity). Took initiative preflop with suited connector raise, Flash defended wide from BB and checked flop, indicating weakness or trapping but unlikely given history of calling stations/check-folds. Standard c-bet to deny equity to his floats/draws/弱 pairs, build pot for nutted hands if we improve, high fold equity vs short stack pressure. Pot-sized bet charges max his draws (e.g., Ks, 8x suited), extracts from Qx/Jx/sets. Protects draw OOP, realizes equity. Fold equity high as villain often folds to aggression postflop when behind (per hand history). Avoid check-back which gives free cards/draws and loses dead money.

I am in the Big Blind and missed the flop completely with 9d 7d on a Qc-Jh-4s board. This texture heavily favors Elon's opening range, which includes many strong broadway cards and pairs. I have no pair, no draw, and very little equity. With only 150 hands in the tournament and being the shortest stack with three hands remaining, preserving my remaining 3,800 chips to find a better spot to double up in the final two hands is the optimal strategy. Calling here would be a significant waste of chips given the lack of potential for my hand to improve.

K3 suited from UTG is too weak to open, even in a 4-handed game. This hand has poor playability and will often be dominated by hands that call. With 16225 chips and a healthy stack, no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with 5c 3h, which is a very weak hand (trash). With only 2 hands left in the tournament and sitting in 3rd place, there is no incentive to play this hand. The risk of losing chips to the chip leader in the BB or the short stack in the SB outweighs any minimal fold equity I might generate with a steal. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for the final hand.

With only 7-6 offsuit in the Small Blind and the tournament reaching its final hands, I am facing the chip leader (Elon) in the Big Blind. 7-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a large, aggressive stack. I will fold to preserve my remaining 4,000 chips for the final hand of the tournament.

This is the final hand of the tournament (150/150). My goal is to maximize my final stack. I am holding Ace-Queen offsuit (AsQd) in UTG (first to act in 4-handed play). This is a premium starting hand, well above the average strength, especially in a short-handed game. I should open the pot to build value and potentially steal the blinds. Shoving 100BB would be an overbet that likely only gets called by better hands, so a standard open raise is the best play to keep weaker hands in the pot or induce re-raises from aggressive players like the chip leader (Elon). I will raise to 250, consistent with standard sizing observed in this game.

As it is the final hand of the tournament and I am holding Pocket Queens on the button, I have a premium hand that I must play aggressively to maximize my final stack. Pro opened to 250 from UTG. I will 3-bet to 850, which is a standard re-raise size given my stack. This allows me to build a larger pot immediately and set up a potential shove on any safe flop, while still leaving room for opponents to call or 4-bet with a wider range of hands than they would against a direct all-in.

9cJd is offsuit and mediocre, not suited, connected only marginally. Facing UTG open from Pro (9875 stack) and button 3-bet to 850 from Flash (3200 stack). OOP in SB, pot odds to call 800 into 1250 (~1.56:1) but poor implied odds with this hand that rarely makes nutted hands. Multiway pot likely if BB calls, postflop OOP disastrous for speculative hand. Raising as bluff risky with large stack on final hand; better preserve chips as chip leader (29550 stack). Standard fold to avoid unnecessary risk.

Last hand of tournament. 8h5h is a weak suited hand facing a 3-bet from Flash. Even though suited, 85s plays poorly against a 3-bet range. Flash has a short stack (3200) and 3-bet, indicating strength. I have a healthy stack at 16125 and don't need to gamble here. Folding is the disciplined play - no need to risk chips with a speculative hand on the final hand when I'm in good position.

It is the final hand of the tournament. I hold AsQd in UTG. I opened to 250 and the Button (Flash) 3-bet to 850. Flash started the hand with 3200 chips (32BB), leaving him with 2350 behind. The pot is currently 1250. Calling costs 600, offering roughly 2:1 odds. A 4-bet shove would put Flash all-in, but given his 3-bet size relative to his stack, his range is likely strong (88+, ATs+, KQs+, AK), against which AQo is often flipping or dominated. Shoving forces me to realize my equity immediately with high variance. Calling allows me to see a flop cheaply. With an SPR of ~1.2 post-flop, the plan is simple: play fit-or-fold. If I hit an Ace or Queen, I likely stack him. If I miss, I can fold and preserve the remaining chips, avoiding a large swing on the final hand. This line likely yields higher EV than shoving into a strong range.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I defended a 3-bet out of position with AsQd and the flop comes Js-Jc-8c. This is a paired board with a flush draw. As the pre-flop caller, this texture favors the 3-bettor's range (pairs AA-99, AK, strong broadways). With an SPR of ~1.2 (Pot 1850, Opponent Stack 2350), the opponent is pot-committed. Leading out with Ace-high serves no purpose as I only get called by better hands or raised all-in. Checking is the standard line to see what the opponent does. If he checks back, I realize equity for free. If he jams, I can easily fold my missed hand and preserve my stack of ~9275 chips, having lost only the pre-flop call.

I have Pocket Queens on a paired board [Js Jc 8c]. My overpair is very strong. Given the low stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of approximately 1.27, I am committed to this hand. I will bet 850, which is roughly 46% of the pot, to extract value from middle pairs (8x, 99, TT) and draws, while keeping the door open for Pro to call with a wider range than a shove would allow.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am out of position in a 3-bet pot with Ace-Queen high on a Jack-Jack-Eight board. The opponent, who started the hand short-stacked, has bet 850 (about 45% of the pot), leaving themselves with very little behind. Given the board texture which favors pairs and the opponent's strong line indicating commitment, my Ace-high is likely behind. Drawing to an Ace or Queen is thin (approx. 12% for the turn), and calling would bleed chips unprofitably. To maximize my final stack, the correct play is to fold and preserve my remaining 9275 chips.
